nodes.description: The number of times your video was played for 2 continuous seconds or more. Most 2-second continuous video views will have at least 50% of the video pixels in view.
nodes.formula: The number of times your video was played for 2 continuous seconds
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking the 2-Second Continuous Video Plays metric in a Facebook ad account is important because it reflects the initial engagement and viewer interest in your video content. It indicates that viewers found your video compelling enough to watch for at least 2 seconds. This data helps advertisers gauge the effectiveness of their video content and make decisions about ad creative, audience targeting, and ad placement to improve engagement and overall campaign performance.
nodes.questions: 1. What is the rate of conversion for users who watch the video for at least 2 seconds, and how does it compare to those who don't watch for that long? 2. Are there any particular points in the video where most users stop watching or become more engaged, and how can this be used to improve the video content? 3. Are there any particular audience segments (demographics, interests, locations) that are more likely to watch the video for at least 2 seconds, and how can we adjust our targeting strategies accordingly? 4. How does the position of the video ad placement on the Facebook platform (e.g., in-feed, Stories, right column) affect the 2-Second Continuous Video Plays metric, and can we optimize placement for better results? 5. Are there any significant differences in the 2-Second Continuous Video Plays metric across different devices (mobile, desktop), and how can we optimize the video for various device types? 6. Can we analyze the impact of video length on the 2-Second Continuous Video Plays metric, and determine whether shorter or longer videos perform better in terms of engagement? 7. Are there any particular times of day or days of the week when the 2-Second Continuous Video Plays metric tends to perform better or worse, and how can we adjust ad scheduling to take advantage of these patterns?
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Ad Format - Audience Segment - Geo - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Device Type - Content Type - Ad Placement

nodes.description: The number of times your video played for at least 3 seconds, or for nearly its total length if it's shorter than 3 seconds. For each impression of a video, video plays are counted separately and exclude any time spent replaying the video.
nodes.formula: The number of times video played for 3 seconds, or for 97% of its total length if it's shorter than 3 seconds.
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking the 3-Second Video Plays metric in a Facebook ad account is important because it indicates how engaging your video content is in capturing the audience's initial attention. It helps advertisers understand if their video content is compelling enough to keep viewers watching beyond the first few seconds. By monitoring this metric, advertisers can make informed decisions about video content, ad creative, and audience targeting to optimize their ad campaigns for better engagement and retention.
nodes.questions: 1. What is the connection between the '3-Second Video Plays' metric and conversion rates? Is there a link between users who watch at least 3 seconds of the video and those who become customers? 2. How does the video's content, style, or message affect the '3-Second Video Plays' metric? Are there particular elements within the video that result in higher engagement? 3. Are there any differences in the '3-Second Video Plays' metric across different ad placements on Facebook (e.g., in-feed, Stories, right column)? How can we maximize ad placement for better results? 4. Can we divide the data by demographic information (age, gender, location) to identify which audience groups are more likely to engage with the video for at least 3 seconds? How can this data inform targeting strategies? 5. Does the day of the week or time of day have an effect on the '3-Second Video Plays' metric? Are there certain periods when the video performs particularly well or poorly, and how can we adjust ad scheduling accordingly? 6. How does the video's length influence the '3-Second Video Plays' metric? Are shorter or longer videos more successful in capturing and retaining viewer attention for at least 3 seconds? 7. Can we analyze the impact of ad creative elements, such as the thumbnail image or headline, on the '3-Second Video Plays' metric? Are there certain creative variations that lead to higher engagement?
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Ad Format - Audience Segment - Geo - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Device Type - Content Type - Ad Placement

nodes.description: The total amount of money you've spent on your campaign, ad set or ad during its schedule. This metric is used as the numerator for calculating all cost per action or cost per result metrics. If your ads are currently running, these numbers may be an estimate, since it can take up to 48 hours for ad results to be processed.
nodes.formula: The amount of money you've spent
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: Amount spent lets you see how much you've spent against your maximum budget during the time period you're looking at. It may include amounts already invoiced as well as billable amounts that haven't been invoiced yet.
nodes.questions: 1. What can we learn from the variations in the 'Amount Spent' metric across different ad campaigns, ad sets, or individual ads in terms of budget allocation and performance? 2. Is there a correlation between the 'Amount Spent' metric and certain business outcomes, such as return on ad spend (ROAS) or revenue generated? Are there ad sets or campaigns that offer a better return on investment? 3. What is the cost trend over time? Are there certain days, weeks, or months when ad spend is higher or lower? How can we adjust our budget to align with these trends? 4. Can we segment the 'Amount Spent' data by ad placement (e.g., in-feed, Stories, right column) to determine which placements are the most cost-effective for our e-commerce products? 5. Are there differences in the 'Amount Spent' metric when comparing various audience segments (demographics, interests, locations)? How can we optimize our targeting to reduce costs while preserving reach? 6. How does the ad schedule affect the 'Amount Spent' metric? Are there certain times of day or days of the week when ad spend is more efficient, and how can we adjust our ad scheduling accordingly? 7. Can we calculate the cost per result (e.g., cost per click, cost per conversion) for different campaigns and ad sets to identify which ones are delivering the best results relative to their ad spend?
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Ad Format - Campaign Objective - Audience Segment - Geo - Time Period - Device Type - Ad Placement

nodes.description: The number of clicks, taps or swipes on your ads.The metric counts multiple types of clicks, taps or swipes on your ad, including certain types of interactions with the ad container, links to other destinations, and links to expanded ad experiences. It can include:
nodes.formula: The number of multiple types of clicks
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: It provides insights into the ad's effectiveness in driving user engagement and can help advertisers understand the quality of their ad content, audience targeting, and ad placement. This information is valuable for optimizing ad campaigns and improving the return on ad spend by focusing on the most effective ad elements and strategies.
nodes.questions: 1. What is the breakdown of click types within the 'Clicks (All)' metric (e.g., link clicks, call-to-action button clicks, clicks on the ad's headline or description)? Are there any clicks that are particularly common or successful? 2. How does the 'Clicks (All)' metric relate to conversion metrics (e.g., purchases, sign-ups, lead generation)? Can we measure the effect of these clicks on actual business results? 3. Can we divide the data to determine if certain audience demographics or interests are more likely to generate clicks? Are there any particular audience segments that should be prioritized for targeting? 4. What is the click-through rate (CTR) associated with 'Clicks (All)' and how does it differ across different ad campaigns, ad sets, or ads? Are there any particular creatives or targeting strategies that yield higher CTR? 5. Are there any seasonal or time-specific patterns in the 'Clicks (All)' metric? Does the metric experience changes on certain days or during certain times of the year? 6. How do different ad placements (e.g., in-feed, Stories, right column) affect the 'Clicks (All)' metric, and can we optimize ad placement for better engagement and clicks? 7. Can we calculate the cost per click (CPC) for various campaigns, ad sets, or ads to identify which ones are the most cost-effective in generating clicks?
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Ad Format - Campaign Objective - Audience Segment - Geo - Time Period - Device Type - Ad Placement

nodes.description: Average number of clicks made by one unique account
nodes.formula: Clicks (All) / Unique clicks (all)
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: It helps assess user engagement and the effectiveness of your ad campaign. By understanding how many clicks, on average, a unique account generates, you can gauge the overall ad performance and identify potential areas for improvement. A high Clicks per Account score can be an indicator that your ad campaign is reaching the same audience repeatedly rather than expanding to new audiences. This can lead to ad fatigue and decreased effectiveness over time. It's essential to strike a balance between engagement and audience expansion for a successful ad campaign.
nodes.questions: 1. To what extent does the 'Clicks per Account' metric differ between different ad campaigns, ad sets, or individual ads? Are there any campaigns or ad sets that are particularly successful in getting clicks from a single user account? 2. Are there any particular audience segments (demographics, interests, locations) that tend to have higher 'Clicks per Account'? How can this information be used to inform our targeting strategies? 3. What is the relationship between 'Clicks per Account' and conversion metrics (e.g., purchases, sign-ups, lead generation)? Can we measure the effect of multiple clicks from a single account on actual business results? 4. Does the ad format or creative elements (e.g., images, headlines, descriptions) affect the 'Clicks per Account' metric? Are there any ad creatives that encourage more interaction from a single user? 5. Can we analyze the seasonal or time-specific patterns in the 'Clicks per Account' metric? Are there any particular days or times when users are more likely to engage with the ad multiple times? 6. How does the ad schedule influence the 'Clicks per Account' metric? Are there any specific times of day or days of the week when multiple clicks from a single account are more common? 7. Can we identify any outliers or anomalies in the 'Clicks per Account' data that might indicate unusual ad performance or user behavior, and how can we respond to these insights?
nodes.slices: - Ad Placement - Device Type - Geo - Demographic Segment - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Audience Segment - Ad Set

nodes.id: Cost per 1,000 Accounts Center Accounts Reached
nodes.description: The average cost to reach 1,000 Accounts Center accounts.
nodes.formula: The amount spent / Reach * 1,000.
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Online advertisers typically look at CPM as a performance measurement for the cost-efficiency and value of an ad campaign. On Meta, reach can be a more insightful metric than impressions, because it gives you a measure of how many Accounts Center accounts were exposed to your message and how efficiently you reached them. It's affected by how well your bid performed in the ad auction, your budget and the effectiveness of your audience targeting.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the 'Cost per 1,000 Accounts Center Accounts Reached' metric compare to the overall cost per 1,000 impressions (CPM) in our ad campaigns? Are there any ad sets or campaigns where reaching Accounts Center accounts is more cost-efficient than targeting the general audience? 2. Is there a connection between reaching these accounts and business outcomes when we analyze the effectiveness in terms of conversion metrics, such as purchases, sign-ups, or lead generation? 3. Are there any particular audience segments that are more cost-effective for reaching Accounts Center accounts? Can we identify any demographics, interests, or locations that perform exceptionally well in this regard? 4. How does ad creative and messaging affect the 'Cost per 1,000 Accounts Center Accounts Reached' metric? Are there any specific ad elements that encourage better engagement with these accounts? 5. Are there any seasonal or time-specific patterns in this metric? Are there any particular times of the year or days of the week when reaching Accounts Center accounts is more efficient or important for our e-commerce business? 6. What is the distribution of the 'Cost per 1,000 Accounts Center Accounts Reached' metric across different ad placements on Facebook (e.g., in-feed, Stories, right column)? How can we optimize ad placement for reaching these accounts? 7. Can we calculate the cost per result (e.g., cost per click, cost per conversion) specifically for Accounts Center accounts to determine the cost-effectiveness of targeting this segment?
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Campaign Objective - Audience Segment - Geo - Time Period - Device Type - Ad Placement

nodes.description: The average cost for each 2-second continuous video view.
nodes.formula: The amount spent / 2-second continuous video plays.
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: It provides insights into the cost associated with retaining viewers for at least 2 seconds, indicating the cost-effectiveness of your video content in capturing and retaining audience attention. Advertisers can use this metric to optimize their ad creative, audience targeting, and budget allocation, ultimately improving the return on investment and the overall performance of their video ads.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the 'Cost per 2-Second Continuous Video Play' metric differ between ad campaigns, ad sets, or individual ads? Are there any campaigns or ad sets that are more cost-efficient in achieving 2-second continuous video views? 2. Is there a link between 'Cost per 2-Second Continuous Video Play' and conversion metrics (e.g., purchases, sign-ups, lead generation)? Does video engagement have an effect on actual business outcomes? 3. What is the cost trend over time for achieving 2-second continuous video views? Are there any particular days, weeks, or months when the cost is notably higher or lower? 4. Can we divide the data to determine if certain audience demographics or interests lead to a lower 'Cost per 2-Second Continuous Video Play'? How can this data inform our targeting strategies? 5. How does the ad format, video length, or creative elements influence the 'Cost per 2-Second Continuous Video Play' metric? Are there any video attributes that lead to more cost-effective engagement? 6. Are there seasonal or time-specific patterns in the 'Cost per 2-Second Continuous Video Play' metric? Does the cost vary on certain days or during certain times of the year? 7. Can we compare the 'Cost per 2-Second Continuous Video Play' metric across different ad placements on Facebook (e.g., in-feed, Stories, right column) to identify which placements are the most cost-effective for achieving this engagement?
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Ad Format - Campaign Objective - Audience Segment - Geo - Time Period - Device Type - Ad Placement

nodes.description: The average cost of each lead. In some cases where lead events cannot be measured directly due to partial or missing data, statistical modeling may be used to account for some events.
nodes.formula: The amount spent / Leads.
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: It helps advertisers evaluate the efficiency and affordability of their lead generation efforts. It provides insights into how much it costs to acquire a potential customer's contact information or other relevant data, enabling advertisers to assess the return on investment for their lead generation campaigns. By monitoring this metric, advertisers can make informed decisions about ad content, targeting, and budget allocation to optimize their campaigns for more cost-effective lead generation.
nodes.questions: 1. What is the relationship between 'Cost per Lead' and conversion metrics such as lead-to-purchase rate or lead-to-customer rate? Is there a correlation between lead generation and actual business outcomes? 2. How does the 'Cost per Lead' metric vary across different ad campaigns, ad sets, or individual ads? Are there certain campaigns or ad sets that are more cost-effective in generating leads? 3. What is the trend of 'Cost per Lead' over time? Are there certain days, weeks, or months when lead generation is more or less expensive? 4. Can we segment the data to determine if certain audience demographics or interests are more likely to convert into leads at a lower cost? How can this information inform your targeting strategies? 5. How does the ad creative, messaging, or content type affect the 'Cost per Lead' metric? Are there specific elements that encourage more cost-effective lead generation? 6. Are there seasonal or time-specific patterns in the 'Cost per Lead' metric? Does the cost vary on particular days or during specific times of the year? 7. Can you compare the 'Cost per Lead' metric across different ad placements on Facebook (e.g., in-feed, Stories, right column) to identify which placements are the most cost-effective for lead generation?
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Campaign Objective - Audience Segment - Geo - Time Period - Device Type - Ad Placement

It provides information on how much is spent to gain each new follower, helping advertisers evaluate the cost-effectiveness of their content, targeting, and ad strategy. By monitoring this metric, advertisers can make informed decisions to optimize their campaigns for better audience engagement and growth.

Are there certain days, weeks, or months when getting likes is more or less expensive? 4. It provides insights into how much is spent to generate likes, comments, shares, and other forms of engagement, allowing advertisers to evaluate the effectiveness of their content and make informed decisions about what types of posts, topics, or creative elements resonate most with their audience. This information is valuable for optimizing content strategy, improving engagement, and ultimately achieving better results from your Facebook ad campaigns.

nodes.description: The average cost per result from your ads. The metric is calculated as the total amount spent during the selected time period, divided by the number of results. In some cases where results cannot be measured directly due to partial or missing data, statistical modeling may be used to account for some results.
nodes.formula: The amount spent / The number of results
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Cost per result indicates how cost-efficiently you achieved the objectives you set in your ad campaign. You can use it to compare performance among different campaigns and identify areas of opportunity. This metric can help you determine your bid for future ad sets. This metric can be affected by many factors, such as your auction bid, target audience, optimization type, ad creative and messaging, and schedule.
nodes.questions: 1. What are the components of the 'Cost per Result' metric, and how does the cost vary for different types of results (e.g., purchases, sign-ups, clicks, impressions)? 2. How does the 'Cost per Result' metric differ between different ad campaigns, ad sets, or individual ads? Are there campaigns or ad sets that are more cost-effective in achieving certain results? 3. Is there a correlation between the 'Cost per Result' and key performance indicators (KPIs), such as return on ad spend (ROAS) or overall revenue generated? 4. What is the trend of the 'Cost per Result' metric over time? Are there certain days, weeks, or months when the cost is notably higher or lower? 5. Can you segment the data to determine if certain audience demographics, interests, or locations lead to a lower 'Cost per Result'? How can this information be used to inform targeting strategies? 6. How does the ad creative, messaging, or content type affect the 'Cost per Result' metric? Are there specific ad elements that lead to more cost-effective results? 7. Are there seasonal or time-specific patterns in the 'Cost per Result' metric? Does the cost vary on particular days or during certain times of the year?
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Campaign Objective - Audience Segment - Geo - Time Period - Device Type - Ad Placement

nodes.questions: 1. What is the variation of 'CPC (All)' across different ad campaigns, ad sets, or individual ads? Are there any campaigns or ads that have lower CPC (All) values, and what tactics make them more cost-efficient? 2. Is there a link between 'CPC (All)' and conversion metrics (e.g., purchases, sign-ups, lead generation)? Is there a connection between click costs and actual business results? 3. What is the trend of 'CPC (All)' over time? Are there any particular days, weeks, or months when click costs are notably higher or lower, and how can you adjust your budget allocation accordingly? 4. Can you divide the data to determine if certain audience demographics, interests, or locations lead to lower CPC (All)? How can this information inform your targeting strategies? 5. How does the ad creative, messaging, or content type affect the 'CPC (All)' metric? Are there any ad elements that encourage more cost-effective clicks? 6. Are there seasonal or time-specific patterns in the 'CPC (All)' metric? Does the cost per click differ on certain days or during certain times of the year, and how can you modify your ad strategy accordingly? 7. Can you compare the 'CPC (All)' metric across different ad placements on Facebook (e.g., in-feed, Stories, right column) to identify which placements are the most cost-effective for generating clicks?
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Campaign Objective - Audience Segment - Geo - Time Period - Device Type - Ad Placement

nodes.description: The average cost for each link click. It shows how much, on average, each link click costs you. CPC is a metric used in the online advertising industry for benchmarking ad efficiency and performance.
nodes.formula: The amount spent / Link clicks.
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: It helps advertisers assess the cost-effectiveness of driving users to click on links within their ads, which often lead to important actions like website visits or conversions. This metric provides insights into the efficiency of ad targeting, content relevance, and budget allocation, guiding decisions to optimize these aspects for better results. Advertisers can use this metric to improve the ROI of their ad campaigns by ensuring they're getting the most value for their advertising spend.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the 'CPC (Cost per Link Click)' metric differ between ad campaigns, ad sets, or individual ads? Are there any campaigns or ads that have lower CPC values, and what tactics make them more cost-efficient? 2. Is there a connection between 'CPC (Cost per Link Click)' and conversion metrics (e.g., purchases, sign-ups, lead generation)? Is there a relationship between link click costs and actual business results? 3. What is the trend of 'CPC (Cost per Link Click)' over time? Are there any particular days, weeks, or months when link click costs are notably higher or lower, and how can you adjust your budget accordingly? 4. Can you divide the data to determine if certain audience demographics, interests, or locations lead to lower CPC (Cost per Link Click)? How can this information inform your targeting strategies? 5. How does the ad creative, messaging, or content type affect the 'CPC (Cost per Link Click)' metric? Are there any ad elements that encourage more cost-effective link clicks? 6. Are there seasonal or time-specific patterns in the 'CPC (Cost per Link Click)' metric? Does the cost per link click vary on certain days or during certain times of the year, and how can you modify your ad strategy accordingly? 7. Can you compare the 'CPC (Cost per Link Click)' metric across different ad placements on Facebook (e.g., in-feed, Stories, right column) to identify which placements are the most cost-effective for driving link clicks?
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Campaign Objective - Audience Segment - Geo - Time Period - Device Type - Ad Placement

nodes.description: The average cost for 1,000 impressions.
nodes.formula: The amount spent / Impressions * 1,000
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: CPM is a common metric used by the online advertising industry to gauge the cost-effectiveness of an ad campaign. It's often used to compare performance among different ad publishers and campaigns.
nodes.questions: 1. What is the variation of 'CPM (Cost Per 1,000 Impressions)' across different ad campaigns, ad sets, or individual ads? Are there any campaigns or ads that have lower CPM values, and what tactics make them more cost-efficient? 2. Is there a link between 'CPM (Cost Per 1,000 Impressions)' and engagement metrics (e.g., clicks, conversions, shares)? Is there a correlation between ad exposure costs and user engagement? 3. What is the trend of 'CPM (Cost Per 1,000 Impressions)' over time? Are there any particular days, weeks, or months when CPM is notably higher or lower, and how can you optimize your budget allocation accordingly? 4. Can you segment the data to determine if certain audience demographics, interests, or locations lead to lower CPM (Cost Per 1,000 Impressions)? How can this information inform your targeting strategies? 5. How does the ad creative, messaging, or content type affect the 'CPM (Cost Per 1,000 Impressions)' metric? Are there any ad elements that encourage more cost-effective ad exposure? 6. Are there seasonal or time-specific patterns in the 'CPM (Cost Per 1,000 Impressions)' metric? Does the cost per 1,000 impressions vary on particular days or during certain times of the year, and how can you adjust your ad strategy accordingly? 7. Can you compare the 'CPM (Cost Per 1,000 Impressions)' metric across different ad placements on Facebook (e.g., in-feed, Stories, right column) to identify which placements are the most cost-effective for achieving ad exposure?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Increased Competition. Rising competition in the e-commerce sector can lead to higher CPM as more advertisers vie for limited ad inventory, driving up the cost per impression. 🔹 Seasonal Demand. Seasonal fluctuations in e-commerce demand can lead to increased competition during peak times, resulting in higher CPM. 🔹 Ad Creative Quality. Poorly designed or unattractive ad creatives may lead to lower click-through rates, which, in turn, can increase CPM as it takes more impressions to get clicks. 🔹 Ad Relevance Score. Lower ad relevance scores can lead to higher CPM as less relevant ads may require more impressions to generate engagement. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Narrow Audience Targeting. Overly specific or niche targeting can reduce the potential audience size, increasing the CPM as you may need more impressions to reach them. 🔹 Ad Fatigue. Repeatedly showing the same ad to the same audience can lead to ad fatigue, requiring more impressions and increasing CPM. 🔹 Ad Placement Strategy. Changes in your ad placement strategy may affect CPM, as different placements have varying costs associated with impressions. 🔹 Changes in Bidding Strategy. Adjusting your bidding strategy can impact CPM, especially if you're bidding higher to prioritize impressions. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Ad Content Changes. Frequent changes in ad content may influence user engagement and the cost per impression. 🔹 Mobile Optimization. Lack of mobile optimization can lead to higher CPM from mobile users. 🔹 Ad Scheduling. The timing of your ad delivery can affect CPM, as specific hours or days may have higher demand and competition. 🔹 Social Trends and Events. External events and social trends can influence user behavior and the cost per impression. 🔹 Ad Frequency. Showing the same ad to the same audience too frequently can lead to increased ad costs, including CPM.
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Campaign Objective - Audience Segment - Geo - Time Period - Device Type - Ad Placement

nodes.description: The percentage of impressions where a click (all) occurred out of the total number of impressions.
nodes.formula: Clicks (all) / Impressions.
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: It indicates the effectiveness of your ad in generating clicks or user interactions relative to the total number of times it was displayed. This metric provides insights into the relevance of your ad content and targeting, helping advertisers make informed decisions about how to optimize their campaigns for better engagement and click-through rates. By monitoring CTR (All), advertisers can improve their ad's performance and efficiency, ultimately enhancing the return on ad spend.
nodes.questions: 1. Analyze the variation of 'CTR (All)' across different ad campaigns, ad sets, or individual ads. Are there any campaigns or ads that usually have higher CTR (All) values, and what tactics are behind their success? 2. Examine the correlation between 'CTR (All)' and conversion metrics (e.g., purchases, sign-ups, lead generation). Is there a link between click-through rate and actual business results? 3. Investigate the trend of 'CTR (All)' over time. Are there any particular days, weeks, or months when the CTR is notably higher or lower, and what external factors may be influencing these trends? 4. Segment the data to determine if certain audience demographics, interests, or locations lead to higher CTR (All). How can this information inform your targeting strategies? 5. Assess the impact of ad creative, messaging, or content type on the 'CTR (All)' metric. Are there any ad elements that usually encourage higher click-through rates? 6. Analyze seasonal or time-specific patterns in the 'CTR (All)' metric. Does the click-through rate vary on particular days or during specific times of the year, and how can you adjust your ad strategy accordingly? 7. Compare the 'CTR (All)' metric across different ad placements on Facebook (e.g., in-feed, Stories, right column) to identify which placements are the most effective for achieving higher CTR (All).
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Audience Mismatch. If the ad content doesn't resonate with the target audience, it can lead to reduced clicks. 🔹 Ad Creative Quality. Poorly designed or unattractive ad creatives can result in lower CTR as they may not resonate with the audience. 🔹 Increased Competition. Rising competition in the e-commerce sector can lead to lower CTR as more advertisers vie for the same audience's attention. 🔹 Ad Relevance Score. Lower ad relevance scores can lead to lower CTR as less relevant ads may not generate as many clicks. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Narrow Audience Targeting. Overly specific or niche targeting can reduce the potential audience size, leading to a lower CTR. 🔹 Ad Fatigue. Repeatedly showing the same ad to the same audience can lead to ad fatigue, resulting in a lower CTR over time. 🔹 Ad Placement Strategy. Changes in your ad placement strategy may affect CTR, as different placements have varying click-through rates. 🔹 Changes in Bidding Strategy. Adjusting your bidding strategy may impact CTR, especially if you're bidding lower and getting fewer clicks. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Ad Content Changes. Frequent changes in ad content may influence user engagement and the CTR. 🔹 Mobile Optimization. Lack of mobile optimization can lead to a lower CTR from mobile users. 🔹 Ad Scheduling. The timing of your ad delivery can affect CTR, as specific hours or days may have different user engagement rates. 🔹 Social Trends and Events. External events and social trends can influence user behavior and the CTR. 🔹 Ad Frequency. Showing the same ad to the same audience too frequently can lead to ad fatigue and lower CTR.
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Campaign Objective - Audience Segment - Geo - Time Period - Device Type - Ad Placement

nodes.description: The percentage of impressions that received a link click out of the total number of impressions.
nodes.formula: Link clicks / Impressions.
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: CTR (link click-through rate) indicates how many link clicks your ad received compared to the number of impressions it received. It is a common metric used by online advertisers to understand how ads drive traffic to websites and other destinations.

Is there a connection between 'CTR (Link Click-Through Rate)' and conversion metrics (e.g., purchases, sign-ups, lead generation)? Are there any days, weeks, or months when the click-through rate is notably higher or lower, and what external factors may be causing this? 4. Can you divide the data to determine if certain audience demographics, interests, or locations lead to higher CTR (Link)? How can this data be used to inform your targeting strategies? 5. How does the ad creative, messaging, or content type affect the 'CTR (Link Click-Through Rate)' metric? Are there any ad elements that usually result in higher click-through rates on links? 6. Are there any seasonal or time-specific patterns in the 'CTR (Link Click-Through Rate)' metric? Does the click-through rate on links vary on certain days or during certain times of the year, and how can you adjust your ad strategy accordingly? 7. Can you compare the 'CTR (Link Click-Through Rate)' metric across different ad placements on Facebook (e.g., in-feed, Stories, right column) to identify which placements are the most effective for achieving higher CTR (Link)?

Rising competition in the e-commerce sector can lead to lower CTR as more advertisers vie for the same audience's attention. 🔹 Ad Relevance Score. Adjusting your bidding strategy may impact CTR, especially if you're bidding lower and getting fewer clicks. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Ad Content Changes. Frequent changes in ad content may influence user engagement and the CTR. 🔹 Mobile Optimization. Lack of mobile optimization can lead to a lower CTR from mobile users. 🔹 Ad Scheduling. The timing of your ad delivery can affect CTR, as specific hours or days may have different user engagement rates. 🔹 Social Trends and Events. nodes.description: The average number of times each Accounts Center account saw your ad. This metric is estimated.
nodes.formula: Impressions / Reach.
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Frequency helps to build awareness and recall by showing your message to Accounts Center accounts in your target audience multiple times. Frequency may average 1 to 2 per ad set or may be much higher, depending on your budget, audience size and schedule. However, it's important to monitor frequency along with your results and ad relevance diagnostics to make sure the same Accounts Center accounts aren't seeing your ads too often during a campaign. If performance begins to drop as your frequency numbers rise, your target audience may be experiencing ad fatigue, and it may be wise to change your ad creative or targeting.
nodes.questions: 1. Analyze the correlation between 'Frequency' and other ad performance metrics, such as click-through rate (CTR), engagement, or conversion rates. Is there a link between frequency and ad effectiveness? 2. Examine how the 'Frequency' metric varies across different ad campaigns, ad sets, or individual ads. Are there certain campaigns or ads with higher or lower frequency values, and what strategies contribute to these differences? 3. Investigate the trend of 'Frequency' over time. Are there certain days, weeks, or months when frequency increases or decreases, and how can you adjust your ad scheduling accordingly? 4. Segment the data to determine if certain audience demographics, interests, or locations are more likely to encounter ads with higher frequency. How can this information inform your targeting strategies? 5. Assess how ad creative, messaging, or content type impacts the 'Frequency' metric. Are there specific ad elements that contribute to higher or lower frequency values? 6. Analyze seasonal or time-specific patterns in the 'Frequency' metric. Does the frequency of ad views vary on particular days or during specific times of the year, and how can you adjust your ad strategy accordingly? 7. Compare the 'Frequency' metric across different ad placements on Facebook (e.g., in-feed, Stories, right column) to identify which placements result in higher or lower ad frequency.
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Ad Scheduling. Poor ad scheduling can lead to increased frequency, especially if ads are shown too frequently during certain hours or days. 🔹 Limited Audience Size. Having a small target audience can result in a higher frequency as the ad is shown to the same people more often. 🔹 High Budget. Allocating a high budget to a small audience can also lead to increased frequency, as there's more room for impressions. 🔹 Ad Fatigue. Repeatedly showing the same ad to the same audience can result in increased frequency and ad fatigue. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Ineffective Ad Creative. Ads that don't capture the audience's attention may require more impressions to achieve desired results, leading to higher frequency. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Ad Content Changes. Frequent changes in ad content may influence ad performance and the frequency at which ads are shown. 🔹 Changes in Ad Placement Strategy. Adjusting your ad placement strategy may impact frequency, especially if it leads to higher impressions in specific locations. 🔹 Social Trends and Events. External events and social trends can influence user behavior and impact ad frequency. 🔹 Seasonal Demand. Seasonal fluctuations can affect frequency, with peak seasons potentially leading to higher ad frequency. 🔹 Narrow Audience Targeting. Overly specific targeting can limit the potential audience size and result in higher frequency.
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Campaign Objective - Audience Segment - Geo - Time Period - Device Type - Ad Placement

nodes.description: The number of times your address or the Get Directions button was clicked on your Meta listing (such as a Marketplace listing), attributed to your ads.
nodes.formula: The number of time someone taps on the get directions button
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking the Get Directions Clicks metric in a Facebook ad account is important for businesses with physical locations because it measures the engagement of users interested in visiting their stores or venues. This metric provides valuable insights into the effectiveness of location-based ad campaigns and can help businesses gauge the impact of their advertising efforts on driving foot traffic. By monitoring Get Directions Clicks, businesses can make informed decisions to optimize their ad targeting, creative elements, and budget allocation to increase in-store visits and enhance the return on their advertising investment.
nodes.questions: 1. Analyze the variation of 'Get Directions Clicks' across different ad campaigns, ad sets, or individual ads. Are there any campaigns or ads that consistently generate more 'Get Directions Clicks,' and what tactics are behind their success? 2. Investigate the correlation between 'Get Directions Clicks' and offline conversions, foot traffic, or in-store sales. Is there any connection between these clicks and physical business outcomes? 3. Examine the trend of 'Get Directions Clicks' over time. Are there any particular days, weeks, or months when these clicks are notably higher or lower, and how can you optimize your ad scheduling accordingly? 4. Segment the data to determine if certain audience demographics, locations, or interests are more likely to click for directions to your business location. How can this information inform your targeting strategies? 5. Assess the impact of ad creative, messaging, or content type on the 'Get Directions Clicks' metric. Are there any ad elements that encourage users to request directions to your business location? 6. Analyze seasonal or time-specific patterns in the 'Get Directions Clicks' metric. Does the number of clicks for directions vary on particular days or during specific times of the year, and how can you adjust your ad strategy accordingly? 7. Compare the 'Get Directions Clicks' metric across different ad placements on Facebook to identify which placements are the most effective for driving users to request directions to your business.
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Campaign Objective - Audience Segment - Geo - Time Period - Device Type - Ad Placement

nodes.description: An impression is counted as the number of times an instance of an ad is on screen for the first time. (Example: If an ad is on screen and someone scrolls down, and then scrolls back up to the same ad, that counts as 1 impression. If an ad is on screen for someone 2 different times in a day, that counts as 2 impressions.) This method of counting video impressions differs from industry standards for video ads. With the exception of ads on Audience Network, impressions are counted the same way for ads that contain either images or video. This means that a video is not required to start playing for the impression to be counted.
nodes.formula: The number of times your ads were on screen.
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: It provides insights into how many times your ad is displayed on users' screens, helping advertisers assess the visibility and potential awareness of their ad campaigns. By monitoring impressions, advertisers can make informed decisions about ad frequency, targeting, and budget allocation to optimize their campaigns for better brand recognition and audience engagement.
nodes.questions: 1. Analyze the correlation between 'Impressions' and other key performance metrics, such as click-through rate (CTR), conversion rate, or ad engagement, to determine if there is a relationship between impressions and ad effectiveness. 2. Examine the trend of 'Impressions' over time to determine if there are specific days, weeks, or months when impression counts are notably higher or lower, and what external factors may be influencing these trends. 3. Segment the data to investigate if certain audience demographics, interests, or locations result in higher or lower impression counts, and how this information can inform targeting strategies. 4. Investigate how the ad creative, messaging, or content type impacts the 'Impressions' metric, and if there are specific ad elements that consistently lead to higher visibility and impression counts. 5. Compare the 'Impressions' metric across different ad placements on Facebook (e.g., in-feed, Stories, right column) to identify which placements offer the highest visibility for your ads. 6. Analyze seasonal or time-specific patterns in the 'Impressions' metric to determine if the number of impressions vary on particular days or during specific times of the year, and how you can adjust your ad strategy accordingly.
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Campaign Objective - Audience Segment - Geo - Time Period - Device Type - Ad Placement

nodes.description: The number of clicks on your ad that open an Instant Experience. This metric is only available for playable ads.
nodes.formula: The number of times an Instant Experience opened as a result of people clicking on your ad.
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: It measures the effectiveness of playable ads in engaging users and encouraging them to interact with the ad's immersive experience. This metric provides insights into how well your ad captures user interest and leads them to explore the Instant Experience, which is valuable for assessing the ad's creative and user experience quality. By monitoring this metric, advertisers can make informed decisions about playable ad design and targeting strategies to improve user engagement and overall campaign performance.
nodes.questions: 1. Analyze the correlation between 'Instant Experience Clicks to Open' and conversion metrics. Are there any connections between users engaging with Instant Experiences and actual business results? 2. Examine the trend of 'Instant Experience Clicks to Open' over time. Are there any particular days, weeks, or months when this metric is notably higher or lower, and how can you optimize your ad scheduling accordingly? 3. Segment the data to determine if certain audience demographics, interests, or locations are more likely to click to open Instant Experiences in your playable ads. How can this information inform your targeting strategies? 4. Investigate how the content and design of the Instant Experience itself affects the 'Instant Experience Clicks to Open' metric. Are there any particular elements within the Instant Experience that encourage more user interactions? 5. Analyze seasonal or time-specific patterns in the 'Instant Experience Clicks to Open' metric. Does user engagement with Instant Experiences vary on particular days or during specific times of the year, and how can you adjust your ad strategy accordingly? 6. Compare the 'Instant Experience Clicks to Open' metric across different ad placements and devices to identify which placements and devices are most effective for generating user interactions with Instant Experiences.

nodes.description: The number of clicks on links in an Instant Experience that lead to destinations outside Meta technologies. It only includes clicks on links that appear inside the Instant Experience. Destinations include: websites, product page in an app store, app deep links
nodes.formula: The number of clicks that take people from an Instant Experience to other destinations outside Meta technologies.
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: It measures the effectiveness of your Instant Experience in driving users to take actions that lead them outside of Facebook's ecosystem. This metric provides insights into the success of your ad campaign in driving traffic to external destinations, such as websites or app stores. By monitoring this metric, advertisers can gauge the impact of their Instant Experience and make informed decisions about ad content, design, and user experience to optimize their campaigns for specific objectives like website visits or app downloads.

nodes.description: The average percentage of the Instant Experience that was viewed. An Instant Experience is a screen that opens after someone interacts with your ad on a mobile device. It may include a series of interactive or multimedia components, including video, images, products and more.
nodes.formula: The number of components viewed / Total number of components It counts the views of the Instant Experience that opened from your ad, but not views of other Instant Experiences that were linked from the original.
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: It provides insights into how engaging and effective your Instant Experience is at retaining user attention. It measures the portion of the Instant Experience that users engage with, indicating the quality of the ad's content and the user experience. By monitoring this metric, advertisers can make informed decisions about optimizing the design and content of their Instant Experiences to improve engagement and ensure that more users interact with their ad's multimedia components, ultimately leading to better campaign results.
nodes.questions: 1. What is the relationship between 'Instant Experience View Percentage' and key performance metrics such as click-through rate (CTR), conversion rates, or ad engagement? Is there a correlation between view percentage and ad effectiveness? 2. How does the 'Instant Experience View Percentage' vary across different ad campaigns, ad sets, or individual ads? Are there certain campaigns or ads that consistently achieve higher view percentages, and what strategies contribute to their success? 3. What is the trend of 'Instant Experience View Percentage' over time? Are there certain days, weeks, or months when view percentages are notably higher or lower, and what external factors may be influencing these trends? 4. Can you segment the data to determine if certain audience demographics, interests, or locations result in higher view percentages for your Instant Experiences? How can this information inform your targeting strategies? 5. How does the content and design of the Instant Experience affect the 'Instant Experience View Percentage' metric? Are there particular elements or features within the Instant Experience that encourage users to view a higher percentage of the content? 6. Are there seasonal or time-specific patterns in the 'Instant Experience View Percentage' metric? Does user engagement with Instant Experiences differ on particular days or during certain times of the year, and how can you adjust your ad strategy accordingly? 7. Can you compare the 'Instant Experience View Percentage' metric across different ad placements and devices to identify which placements and devices offer the best viewing experience for your Instant Experiences?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Instant Experience Content Quality. If the content within the Instant Experience is not engaging or relevant, users may not view a high percentage of it. 🔹 Mobile Optimization. Lack of mobile optimization within the Instant Experience can lead to user frustration and lower view percentages on mobile devices. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Ad Targeting. Changes in ad targeting can impact the relevance of the Instant Experience to the audience, affecting view percentage. 🔹 Ad Fatigue. Repeatedly showing the same Instant Experience to the same audience can lead to ad fatigue and lower view percentages. 🔹 Ad Creative Quality. The quality and creativity of the ad's design and content play a significant role in capturing user attention within the Instant Experience. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 User Behavior. Seasonal or external factors may influence user behavior and their willingness to engage with Instant Experiences. 🔹 Competition. The presence of competing ads or content that draws users' attention away from your Instant Experience can reduce view percentages. 🔹 Instant Experience Length. The length of the Instant Experience may affect how much of it users engage with. Extremely long or short experiences may impact view percentages. 🔹 Technical Issues. Technical issues, such as slow loading times or errors within the Instant Experience, can deter users from engaging with the content.

By monitoring this metric, advertisers can make informed decisions about optimizing the design and content of their Instant Experiences to ensure users spend more time interacting with the ad, leading to a more immersive and successful ad campaign.

Segment the data to determine if certain audience demographics, interests, or locations result in longer view times for your Instant Experiences. How can this information be used to inform your targeting strategies? 5. Assess the impact of the content and design of the Instant Experience on the 'Instant Experience View Time' metric. Are there any elements or features within the Instant Experience that encourage users to spend more time engaging with the content? 6. Analyze seasonal or time-specific patterns in the 'Instant Experience View Time' metric. Does user engagement with Instant Experiences vary on particular days or during specific times of the year, and how can you adjust your ad strategy accordingly? 7. Compare the 'Instant Experience View Time' metric across different ad placements and devices to identify which placements and devices offer the best environment for users to spend more time in Instant Experiences.
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Instant Experience Content Quality. The quality and relevance of content within the Instant Experience significantly affect how long users stay engaged. 🔹 Mobile Optimization. Inadequate mobile optimization may lead to user frustration and shorter view times on mobile devices. 🔹 Ad Fatigue. Repeatedly showing the same Instant Experience to the same audience can lead to ad fatigue, reducing view time. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Ad Creative Quality. Engaging and creative ad design and content can encourage users to spend more time in the Instant Experience. 🔹 Audience Relevance. Changes in ad targeting may affect the relevance of the Instant Experience to the audience, impacting view time. 🔹 Technical Issues. Technical problems such as slow loading times or errors within the Instant Experience can discourage users from spending time in it. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Competition. The presence of competing ads or content that draws users' attention away from your Instant Experience can reduce view time. 🔹 User Behavior. Seasonal or external factors may influence user behavior and the time they are willing to spend engaging with content. 🔹 Instant Experience Length. The length of the Instant Experience can influence how much time users are willing to invest in engaging with it. Extremely long or short experiences may affect view time.
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Campaign Objective - Audience Segment - Geo - Time Period - Device Type - Ad Placement

nodes.description: The number of likes of your Facebook Page, attributed to your ads. It counts both likes that happened directly on the Page and likes that happened on the Page Likes button in your ad.
nodes.formula: The number of Facebook Page likes attributed to ads.
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Facebook Page lets you promote your business and engage with people on Facebook. When people like your Facebook Page, they can follow your Page posts to stay informed about your business. You can also use geographic and demographic insights about people who like your Page to make marketing decisions.
nodes.questions: 1. Analyze the variation of 'Likes' across different ad campaigns, ad sets, or individual ads. Are there any campaigns or ads that usually get more likes, and what tactics are behind their success? 2. Examine the correlation between 'Likes' and other engagement metrics (e.g., comments, shares, clicks). Is there a link between likes and overall engagement with your ads? 3. Investigate the trend of 'Likes' over time. Are there any particular days, weeks, or months when the number of likes is notably higher or lower, and what external factors may be influencing these trends? 4. Segment the data to determine if certain audience demographics or interests are more likely to like your Page as a result of your ads. How can this information inform your targeting strategies? 5. Assess the impact of ad creative, messaging, or content type on the 'Likes' metric. Are there any ad elements that usually encourage more Page likes? 6. Look for seasonal or time-specific patterns in the 'Likes' metric. Does the number of likes vary on particular days or during specific times of the year, and how can you adjust your ad strategy accordingly? 7. Compare the 'Likes' metric across different ad placements on Facebook (e.g., in-feed, Stories, right column) to identify which placements are the most effective for generating likes?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Decreased Ad Spend. Reducing your ad spend can lead to fewer impressions and, consequently, fewer likes attributed to your ads. 🔹 Ad Creative Quality. Poorly designed or unattractive ad creatives may result in fewer likes, as they may not resonate with the audience. 🔹 Changes in Targeting Strategy. Altering your targeting strategy to a less relevant audience may lead to a decline in likes attributed to your ads. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Ad Relevance Score. Lower ad relevance scores can result in fewer likes, as less relevant ads may not perform well. 🔹 Ad Fatigue. Repeatedly showing the same ad can result in ad fatigue, leading to a decrease in likes over time. 🔹 Changes in Ad Frequency. Adjusting ad frequency can affect the number of likes attributed to your ads, especially if frequency is too high. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Ad Content Changes. Frequent changes in ad content may influence user engagement and the number of likes attributed to ads. 🔹 Mobile Optimization. Lack of mobile optimization can lead to fewer likes from mobile users. 🔹 Ad Scheduling. The timing of your ad delivery can affect the number of likes, as specific hours or days may have different user engagement rates. 🔹 Social Trends and Events. External events and social trends can influence user behavior and the number of likes attributed to your ads.
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Campaign Objective - Audience Segment - Geo - Time Period - Ad Placement - Content Type

nodes.description: The number of clicks on links within the ad that led to advertiser-specified destinations, on or off Meta technologies. This metric counts link clicks, taps or swipes on the ad's text, media or call-to-action, that link to destinations or experiences specified by the advertiser. This metric excludes clicks on content or links in the comments section of a post.
nodes.formula: The number of clicks on links
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Link clicks are one way to measure the interest that your ad generates among your audience. Digital advertisers have often considered click-through rate as a measure of success for an online advertising campaign. To better understand what happens after someone clicks on your ad, you can compare link clicks to other metrics. For example, you can compare link clicks to landing page views to see how often people who clicked on your ad reached your landing page.
nodes.questions: 1. Analyze the correlation between 'Link Clicks' and conversion metrics such as purchases, sign-ups, and lead generation. Is there a connection between link clicks and actual business results? 2. Examine the trend of 'Link Clicks' over time. Are there certain days, weeks, or months when link click counts are notably higher or lower, and what external factors may be influencing these trends? 3. Segment the data to determine if certain audience demographics, interests, or locations are more likely to click on links in your ads. How can this information inform your targeting strategies? 4. Investigate how the ad creative, messaging, or content type affects the 'Link Clicks' metric. Are there specific ad elements that consistently encourage users to click on the links within your ads? 5. Analyze seasonal or time-specific patterns in the 'Link Clicks' metric. Does the number of link clicks vary on particular days or during specific times of the year, and how can you adjust your ad strategy accordingly? 6. Compare the 'Link Clicks' metric across different ad placements on Facebook (e.g., in-feed, Stories, right column) to identify which placements are the most effective for driving link clicks.
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Campaign Objective - Audience Segment - Geo - Time Period - Device Type - Ad Placement

nodes.description: The number of leads submitted on Meta technologies (including forms, Messenger and Instagram chats) and attributed to your ads.
nodes.formula: The number of leads
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: It measures the success of your ad campaigns in generating leads and valuable customer interactions. It provides insights into how effective your ads are at driving user actions like form submissions, Messenger chats, and Instagram interactions. By monitoring this metric, advertisers can assess the ROI of their campaigns, make informed decisions about lead generation strategies, and optimize their ad content and targeting to improve results and grow their customer base.
nodes.questions: 1. What are the differences in 'Meta Leads' across different ad campaigns, ad sets, or individual ads? Are there any campaigns or ads that consistently generate more leads, and what strategies are driving their success? 2. Is there a correlation between 'Meta Leads' and ad spend or budget allocation? Are there campaigns or ad sets that generate a higher number of leads more efficiently, and can you determine the budget allocation that optimizes lead generation? 3. What is the trend of 'Meta Leads' over time? Are there any particular days, weeks, or months when lead generation is notably higher or lower, and what external factors may be influencing these trends? 4. Can you segment the data to determine if certain audience demographics, interests, or locations result in more leads? How can this information be used to inform your targeting strategies for lead generation? 5. How does the ad creative, messaging, or call-to-action affect the 'Meta Leads' metric? Are there any ad elements that consistently encourage users to submit more leads? 6. Are there any seasonal or time-specific patterns in the 'Meta Leads' metric? Does lead generation vary on particular days or during certain times of the year, and how can you adjust your ad strategy accordingly? 7. Can you compare the 'Meta Leads' metric across different placements on Facebook, including forms, Messenger, and Instagram chats, to identify which channels are the most effective for lead generation?
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Campaign Objective - Audience Segment - Geo - Time Period - Device Type - Ad Placement

nodes.description: The total value of Meta lead. The metric is calculated from value parameters when you set up your Meta leads event.
nodes.formula: The sum of the value of all Meta leads.
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: It quantifies the total value generated from the leads acquired through your ad campaigns. It allows advertisers to measure the monetary impact of their lead generation efforts, providing insights into the return on investment and the value of individual leads. By monitoring this metric, advertisers can make informed decisions about their lead generation strategies, allocate resources effectively, and focus on acquiring higher-value leads to maximize their advertising results.
nodes.questions: 1. What is the relationship between 'Meta Leads Conversion Value' and ad campaigns, ad sets, or individual ads? Are there any campaigns or ads that consistently generate higher conversion values, and what strategies are associated with their success? 2. Is there a correlation between 'Meta Leads Conversion Value' and ad spend or budget allocation? Are there campaigns or ad sets that generate higher conversion values more efficiently, and can you determine the budget allocation that optimizes the value generated from lead conversions? 3. What is the trend of 'Meta Leads Conversion Value' over time? Are there any particular days, weeks, or months when conversion values are notably higher or lower, and what external factors may be influencing these trends? 4. Can you segment the data to determine if certain audience demographics, interests, or locations result in higher conversion values from leads? How can this information be used to inform your targeting strategies for lead generation? 5. How does the quality of leads affect the 'Meta Leads Conversion Value' metric? Are there any lead generation strategies or sources that consistently result in higher value per conversion? 6. Are there any seasonal or time-specific patterns in the 'Meta Leads Conversion Value' metric? Does the value generated from lead conversions vary on particular days or during specific times of the year, and how can you adjust your ad strategy accordingly? 7. Can you compare the 'Meta Leads Conversion Value' metric across different lead generation events or forms to identify which lead sources or events provide the highest conversion value?

nodes.description: This metric is calculated by adding the approximate sales occurred on Meta technologies such as Shops, Marketplace, Pages or Messenger. This includes the amount provided for orders marked as paid in Messenger and Instagram.
nodes.formula: The total value of Meta purchases, attributed to your ads.
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: It provides a clear indication of the monetary value generated by your ad campaigns across various Meta platforms. It quantifies the revenue directly attributed to your advertising efforts, helping you assess the return on investment and the effectiveness of your campaigns in driving sales. By monitoring this metric, you can make informed decisions about ad strategy, budget allocation, and optimization to maximize the revenue generated by your ads.
nodes.questions: 1. Analyze the correlation between 'Meta Purchase Conversion Value' and ad spend or budget allocation. Are there campaigns or ad sets that generate higher conversion values more efficiently, and can you determine the budget allocation that optimizes sales and revenue? 2. Examine the trend of 'Meta Purchase Conversion Value' over time. Are there particular days, weeks, or months when conversion values are notably higher or lower, and what external factors may be influencing these trends? 3. Segment the data to determine if certain audience demographics, interests, or locations result in higher purchase conversion values through Meta technologies. How can this information inform your targeting strategies to drive more sales? 4. Investigate how different sales channels within Meta technologies (e.g., Shops, Marketplace, Pages, Messenger) contribute to the 'Meta Purchase Conversion Value' metric. nodes.description: This metric counts purchase events that occurred on a Meta technologies, and are attributed to your ads. Meta purchases can occur on Facebook, Marketplace, Messenger and Instagram and can include product sales, travel bookings, event and movie ticket sales. This metric also counts the number of times your business marked orders as paid in Messenger and Instagram or in third-party apps or websites, and are attributed to your ads.
nodes.formula: The number of purchases made within Meta technologies (such as Pages or Messenger) and attributed to your ads.
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: It provides valuable information about the effectiveness of your ad campaigns in driving sales and conversions across various Meta platforms and third-party sources. It quantifies the impact of your advertising efforts on revenue generation, enabling you to measure the return on ad spend and make informed decisions about ad content, targeting, and budget allocation to optimize your campaigns for better sales and conversions. This metric is essential for understanding the direct impact of your ads on your business's bottom line.
nodes.questions: 1. What trends or patterns can be observed in the 'Meta Purchases' metric over time, and how can you adjust your advertising strategy to capitalize on them? 2. Can you analyze the data to determine which Meta technologies are most effective in driving purchases, and do certain platforms excel in particular product categories? 3. What are the common characteristics of the audience segments that generate the highest number of 'Meta Purchases,' and how can this information be used to target potential customers? 4. Are there any external sources or third-party apps and websites that lead to 'Meta Purchases,' and if so, which ones are the most successful in driving sales and revenue? 6. nodes.description: The metric counts clicks that lead to other destinations outside Meta technologies. The metric includes clicks on links that appear in the displayed ad, as well as links in extended experiences such as Instant Experience, collections or Facebook forms. It includes clicks to:
nodes.formula: The number of clicks on links that lead to destinations outside Meta technologies.
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Outbound clicks provide a measure of the amount of traffic your ads help send to your website or app. They are different than link clicks, which specifically count clicks on links in the displayed ad that include both external destinations and certain Meta experiences. While some traffic might drop off between an outbound click and a webpage view, outbound clicks give you a closer approximation of the traffic intended for your website or app.
nodes.questions: 1. Are there any trends in the 'Outbound Clicks' metric that can be observed over time, and how can you use this information to adjust your ad strategy? 2. Can you segment and analyze the data to determine which links or ad experiences are most successful in driving 'Outbound Clicks'? 3. What are the common characteristics of the audience segments that generate the most 'Outbound Clicks'? 4. How can the 'Outbound Clicks' metric be used to measure the effectiveness of your ad campaigns in sending traffic to your e-commerce website or app? 5. Can you identify the sources of external destinations where outbound clicks lead? 6. How can the 'Outbound Clicks' metric help you track the volume of traffic intended for your e-commerce website or app more accurately?
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Campaign Objective - Audience Segment - Geo - Time Period - Device Type - Ad Placement

nodes.description: This metric counts interactions with your Facebook Page, Instagram profile or your content and its posts, attributed to your ads. Engagement from ads can include actions such as liking your Instagram Page, following your Instagram profile, loving a post, replying to a story, checking in to your location, clicking a link and more.
nodes.formula: The number of actions taken on your Facebook Page, Instagram profile or any of your content, attributed to your ads.
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: This metric allows you to see how people interacted with your Facebook Page, Instagram profile or any of your content because of your ads, which can indicate how relevant your ads were to your audience.
nodes.questions: 1. To what extent does 'Page Engagement' differ between different ad campaigns, ad sets, or individual ads, and can you spot campaigns or ads that usually generate higher engagement with your Facebook Page and Instagram profile? What tactics are behind their success? 2. Are there any particular engagement activities (e.g., liking, following, loving posts, checking in, clicking links) that are more frequent in 'Page Engagement'? Can you analyze the variations in the engagement activities associated with your ads? 3. Can you divide and analyze the data to understand the demographics, interests, or locations of the audience segments that contribute the most to 'Page Engagement'? How can this data inform your targeting strategies to engage the most pertinent audience? 4. What are the shared characteristics of ads or ad creative elements that lead to higher 'Page Engagement'? Are there certain ad components that regularly motivate users to engage with your Facebook Page and Instagram profile? 5. How can the 'Page Engagement' metric help you assess the relevance of your ads to your target audience? What knowledge can you gain about the efficiency of your ad campaigns in fostering interactions with your brand's content? 6. Can you detect any time-specific or seasonal trends in the 'Page Engagement' metric? Are there certain days or periods when engagement with your Facebook Page and Instagram profile is notably higher, and how can you adjust your ad strategy accordingly? 7. How can you use the 'Page Engagement' metric to measure the overall brand awareness and affinity generated by your ad campaigns? Are there approaches you can implement to encourage even higher engagement and build a stronger connection with your audience?

nodes.description: This metric is counted each time someone taps your phone number or the call button on the details page of your Meta listing when using their mobile phone. This metric excludes clicks on the phone number shown on desktop listings because it does not lead to a direct phone call. The metric also excludes clicks on phone numbers in the listing's description.
nodes.formula: The number of times your phone number or Call Now button was clicked on your Meta listing (such as a Marketplace listing), attributed to your ads.
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: It provides insights into how effective your ads are at driving phone inquiries and interactions. It allows advertisers to gauge user interest in contacting your business directly via phone calls, helping assess the success of your ad campaigns in generating leads or inquiries. By monitoring this metric, you can make informed decisions about ad content, call-to-action strategies, and targeting to optimize your campaigns for better engagement and customer interactions.
nodes.questions: 1. Analyze the correlation between 'Phone Number Clicks' and ad spend or budget allocation to identify campaigns or ad sets that generate more phone inquiries more efficiently. 2. Examine the trend of 'Phone Number Clicks' over time to determine if there are specific days, weeks, or months when phone inquiries and interactions are notably higher or lower. 3. Segment and analyze the data to understand which audience demographics or ad targeting strategies contribute to higher 'Phone Number Clicks'. 4. Evaluate the impact of ad creative and call-to-action strategies on the 'Phone Number Clicks' metric. 5. Analyze any external factors or seasonal patterns that influence the 'Phone Number Clicks' metric. 6. Utilize the 'Phone Number Clicks' metric to assess the success of your ad campaigns in generating leads and inquiries.

nodes.description: The number of views of photos on your Page or posts that are attributed to your ads. Photo views are counted when people click on photos to view them.
nodes.formula: The number of views of photos
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: It helps advertisers assess the level of engagement and interest in the visual content within their ads. It indicates how many users are clicking to view photos, providing insights into the effectiveness of your ad's visual elements and content. By monitoring this metric, advertisers can make informed decisions about ad design, creative assets, and targeting strategies to optimize their campaigns for better user engagement and overall ad performance.
nodes.questions: 1. Can you analyze the correlation between 'Photo Views' and other engagement metrics, such as link clicks or post engagement, to identify patterns that indicate which types of engagement are more likely to lead to photo views? 2. What are the common characteristics of the audience segments that generate the most 'Photo Views', and how can this information guide your targeting strategies to reach potential customers who are more likely to engage with visual content? 3. How does the placement of photos within ad creative affect the 'Photo Views' metric, and are there specific ad placements or formats that lead to higher photo views that you can optimize for? 4. Are there certain product categories or offerings that receive more photo views, and can you identify which types of products generate the most interest through visual content to inform your product promotion strategies? 5. Can you determine the impact of ad frequency on 'Photo Views', and is there an optimal frequency of ad exposure that maximizes user engagement with photos without causing ad fatigue? 6. How can you leverage the 'Photo Views' metric to assess the level of engagement and interest in the visual content within your ads, and are there specific adjustments to ad design, creative assets, or targeting strategies that can optimize your campaigns for better user engagement and overall ad performance?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Ad Relevance and Quality. Highly relevant and visually appealing ad content can lead to increased photo views. 🔹 Creative Quality. The quality of the images and visuals in your ads significantly influences photo views. 🔹 Audience Targeting. Precise audience targeting ensures that your ads reach users more likely to engage with photos. 🔹 Ad Format and Design. The design of ad formats, such as carousel ads or image galleries, can impact photo views. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 User Behavior Changes. Shifts in user behavior and preferences can affect how they engage with ad visuals. 🔹 Seasonal Variations. Seasonal trends and holidays can influence user interactions with visual content. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Ad Frequency. Overexposure to the same ad content can lead to ad fatigue and reduced engagement. 🔹 Technical Issues. Technical problems, such as slow-loading images, can discourage photo views. 🔹 Competitive Landscape. Increased competition within your industry can affect the performance of your ads. 🔹 External Factors. External factors, like industry trends or global events, can influence ad engagement. 🔹 Content Variety. Providing diverse visual content can keep users engaged and encourage more photo views.

nodes.description: This metric counts all comments attributed to your ads, including ones that were deleted.

nodes.questions: 1. Analyze the variation of the 'Post Comments' metric across different ad campaigns, ad sets, or individual ads, and determine which campaigns or ads generate more comments? Are there any changes to ad content, messaging, or targeting strategies that can optimize your campaigns for better user engagement and encourage more discussions and interactions with your brand or products?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Ad Content Relevance. Relevant ad content can encourage users to comment. 🔹 Call-to-Action (CTA) Clarity. A clear CTA can prompt users to engage and comment. 🔹 Audience Engagement. A highly engaged audience is more likely to comment. 🔹 Content Authenticity. Authentic and relatable content often leads to comments. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Ad Copy and Messaging. The tone and message in your ad copy can impact comments. 🔹 Ad Targeting.
