Current events or trends may affect audience behavior. 🔹 Technical Issues. Technical problems, such as slow-loading ads, can deter comments. 🔹 Changes in User Behavior. Shifts in how users interact with ads can influence comments. 🔹 Variability in Ad Creatives. Ad creatives' diversity can impact the number of comments.

nodes.description: Post engagement includes all actions that people take involving your ads while they're running. Post engagements can include actions such as reacting to, commenting on or sharing the ad, claiming an offer, viewing a photo or video, or clicking on a link.

nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.

When people see ads that are relevant to them, they're more likely to interact with those ads. This metric lets you measure these interactions and compare them to engagement from other ads or campaigns.

Can you analyze the distribution of post engagements across different types of actions, such as reactions, comments, shares, offer claims, photo or video views, and link clicks, and determine if there are any particular actions that are more likely to generate post engagements? nodes.description: The reactions button on an ad allows people to share different reactions to its content: Like, Love, Care, Haha, Wow, Sad or Angry. This metric counts all reactions attributed to your ads, including ones that were removed.

nodes.why: Post reactions indicate that your ads are relevant to your target audience, which helps your ads perform better. When people react to a post, they automatically start following further reactions and comments, which can engage them in an ongoing conversation on your business Page. It's important to measure how your ads help to influence these reactions.
nodes.questions: 1. Analyze how the different types of post reactions (Like, Love, Care, Haha, Wow, Sad, Angry) affect the 'Post Reactions' metric, and can you recognize particular reactions that are more common in your ad campaigns? How do these reactions influence the overall performance of your ads? 2. Examine the trends and changes in the 'Post Reactions' metric over time? Are there certain days, weeks, or months when reactions are notably higher or lower, and can you link these trends with particular ad campaigns or external events? 3. Investigate the relationship between the 'Post Reactions' metric and the ad content, creative elements, or messaging used in your campaigns? Are there certain ad components that regularly generate more reactions, and how can you modify your ad content to motivate user engagement? 4. Determine the correlation between the volume of 'Post Reactions' and other engagement metrics, such as comments, shares, or link clicks? Can you detect patterns where certain forms of engagement lead to more post reactions? 5. Segment and analyze the data to understand the demographics and interests of the audience segments that contribute the most to post reactions? How can this information direct your targeting strategies to engage the most relevant audience? 6. Utilize the 'Post Reactions' metric to assess the relevance of your ads to your target audience and their likelihood to interact with those ads? Are there particular adjustments to ad content, call-to-action strategies, or targeting that can optimize your campaigns for better user engagement and overall ad performance? 7. Are there any remarkable cases of reactions being removed, and can you identify any patterns or trends related to the removal of reactions from your ads? How can you use this information to maintain positive interactions and engagement with your audience?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Ad Relevance. Relevant ads are more likely to generate reactions. 🔹 Emotive Content. Content that elicits emotions tends to get more reactions. 🔹 Seasonal Relevance. Seasonal promotions or content can influence reactions. 🔹 Cultural Sensitivity. Ads that consider cultural nuances can impact reactions. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Ad Copy and Messaging. Ad copy and messaging can impact user reactions. 🔹 Audience Engagement. An engaged audience is more likely to react to ads. 🔹 Ad Targeting. Precise ad targeting ensures relevance to users' interests. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Competition. Increased competition may affect ad performance. 🔹 Technical Issues. Technical problems like slow-loading ads can deter reactions. 🔹 External Events. Current events or trends may affect audience behavior. 🔹 Changes in User Behavior. Shifts in how users react to ads can impact post reactions. 🔹 Variability in Ad Creatives. Ad creative diversity can impact the number of reactions.
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Campaign Objective - Audience Segment - Geo - Time Period - Device Type

nodes.description: This metric counts all saves attributed to your ads, including ones that were removed.
nodes.formula: The total number of times your ad has been saved.
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Post saves counts the number of times your ad was saved by people who want to revisit it at a later time. This metric lets you measure your saves and compare them to saves from other ads and campaigns.
nodes.questions: 1. Identify which ad campaigns, ad sets, or individual ads have the highest number of 'Post Saves' and what characteristics of these ads contribute to their success in encouraging users to save them. 2. Investigate the correlation between 'Post Saves' and other engagement metrics, such as likes, comments, shares, or link clicks, and if certain forms of engagement lead to more post saves. 3. Analyze the relationship between the timing of ad delivery and the 'Post Saves' metric to optimize ad scheduling. 4. Segment and analyze the data to understand the demographics and interests of the audience segments that are most likely to save your ads. 5. Leverage the 'Post Saves' metric to assess the long-term impact of your ads on audience engagement and retention. 6. Track instances of 'Post Saves' being removed and identify any patterns or trends related to the removal of saves from your ads. 7. Monitor the 'Post Saves' metric over time to identify trends or changes in user behavior related to saving ads.
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Ad Relevance. Highly relevant ads are more likely to be saved. 🔹 Valuable Content. Content that provides value or useful information is saved more. 🔹 Seasonal Promotions. Seasonal and timely content is more likely to be saved. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Call to Action (CTA). Clear and enticing CTAs can encourage users to save ads. 🔹 Audience Engagement. Engaging content is more likely to be saved by users. 🔹 Targeting Precision. Precise targeting ensures that ads are relevant to users. 🔹 Ad Copy and Messaging. The ad copy can influence whether an ad gets saved. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Competition. Increased competition may lead to a drop in saves. 🔹 Technical Issues. Technical problems like slow-loading ads can deter saves. 🔹 Changes in User Behavior. Shifts in user behavior can affect post saves. 🔹 Frequency of Ads. High ad frequency may deter users from saving the same ad repeatedly. 🔹 Ad Design Variability. Consistent ad design may result in fewer saves.
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Campaign Objective - Audience Segment - Geo - Time Period - Device Type

nodes.description: People can share your ads or posts on their own or friends' Timelines, in groups and on their own Pages. The metric counts shares of your ads while they were running. It may also include Instagram shares sent to people's inboxes. It doesn't count engagement with the post after it's shared on another person's or Page's timeline.
nodes.formula: The number of shares of your ads.
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: It indicates how often your ad content is being shared and distributed, expanding its reach beyond your initial audience. It helps advertisers assess the virality and the ability of their ads to resonate with users to the point that they want to share the content with their own networks. By monitoring this metric, advertisers can make informed decisions about ad content, targeting, and audience engagement strategies to enhance the organic reach of their campaigns and achieve broader exposure for their brand or message.
nodes.questions: 1. Identify which ad campaigns, ad sets, or individual ads have the highest number of 'Post Shares' and explore what elements of these ads contribute to their success in encouraging users to share them. 2. Investigate the correlation between 'Post Shares' and other engagement metrics, such as likes, comments, link clicks, or reactions, and determine if certain forms of engagement lead to more shares. 3. Analyze the demographics and interests of the audience segments that are most likely to share your ads and use this information to guide your targeting strategies. 4. Examine the relationship between the timing of ad delivery and the 'Post Shares' metric to optimize ad scheduling for higher sharing rates. 5. Leverage the 'Post Shares' metric to assess the virality and organic reach of your ad campaigns and make adjustments to ad content, messaging, or targeting to optimize for better user engagement and sharing behavior. 6. Track and analyze the 'Post Shares' metric over time to identify trends or changes in user behavior related to sharing ads and gain insights to inform your ad strategy and campaign planning. 7. Assess the impact of 'Post Shares' on the overall performance of your ads, including reach, brand exposure, and conversions, and measure the extended reach of your campaigns beyond your initial audience.
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Shareable Content. Content that's highly shareable tends to get more shares. 🔹 Incentives and Contests. Offering incentives for sharing can boost this metric. 🔹 Emotional Appeal. Content that evokes strong emotions is more shareable. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Timely and Trending Topics. Content related to trending topics can get more shares. 🔹 Interactive Content. Polls, quizzes, and interactive content is often shared. 🔹 Ad Placement. Ad placement can influence shareability. 🔹 Call to Action (CTA). A clear CTA encouraging sharing can positively impact shares. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Frequency of Ads. Overexposure to the same ad can reduce shares. 🔹 Technical Issues. Technical problems like slow-loading ads can deter shares. 🔹 Ad Copy and Messaging. The ad copy and message can influence sharing behavior. 🔹 Audience Engagement. Engaging content tends to get more shares. 🔹 Targeting Precision. Precise targeting can lead to more shares from the right audience.
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Campaign Objective - Audience Segment - Geo - Time Period - Device Type

nodes.description: The number of Accounts Center accounts that saw your ads at least once. Reach is different from impressions, which may include multiple views of your ads by the same Accounts Center accounts. This metric is estimated.
nodes.formula: The number of Accounts Center accounts that saw your ads at least once.
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Reach gives you a measure of how many Accounts Center accounts were exposed to your message during an ad campaign. Accounts Center accounts may not always click on your ads, but they may be more likely to engage with your business when they see your message. Your reach can be affected by your bid, budget and audience targeting.
nodes.questions: 1. To gain insight into the distribution of ad exposure, can you segment the 'Reach' data based on factors such as age, gender, location, or interests? How does the reach vary for these different segments? 2. What is the connection between 'Reach' and other metrics, such as impressions, clicks, and conversions? Are there patterns that suggest certain types of ads or campaigns are more effective at turning reach into engagement or sales? 3. How does budget allocation affect the 'Reach' metric? Are there budget thresholds or strategies that consistently result in higher reach, and how can this information be used to inform budget planning? 4. Are there any trends or seasonality in the 'Reach' metric? Are there particular times of the year or events that lead to increased reach, and how can you use this information for campaign planning? 5. What is the impact of reach on your brand awareness and recall? How can you use the 'Reach' metric to measure the exposure of your brand's message to a wider audience and evaluate its effectiveness in creating brand recognition? 6. Are there certain ad formats or creative elements that tend to result in higher reach rates? How can you optimize your ad content and design to increase reach without compromising other metrics like engagement or conversion rates? 7. Can you compare the 'Reach' metric across different ad objectives (e.g., awareness, consideration, conversion) and determine which objectives are most successful at maximizing reach, and how does this align with your campaign goals?
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Campaign Objective - Audience Segment - Geo - Time Period - Device Type

nodes.description: The percentage of results that occured out of the number of impressions.
nodes.formula: Results / Impressions
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: It measures the effectiveness of your ad in converting impressions into desired results or actions, such as clicks, conversions, or engagements. It provides insights into how well your ad is performing in terms of user response and can help you assess the relevance and impact of your ad content, targeting, and overall strategy. By monitoring this metric, advertisers can make informed decisions to optimize their ad campaigns, improve results, and allocate their ad spend more effectively.
nodes.questions: 1. Analyzing the 'Result Rate' for different types of results, such as clicks, conversions, and engagements, how does the effectiveness of your ad vary and what factors contribute to the differences? 2. Examining the 'Result Rate' across various ad placements and platforms within Facebook's ad network, are there placements or platforms where your ads are more successful in converting impressions into results, and how can you use this information for future ad strategies? 3. Are there certain audience segments that have a higher 'Result Rate' than others? Can you identify the characteristics of these high-performing segments and adjust your targeting strategies to reach them more effectively? 4. What is the relationship between 'Result Rate' and other key performance metrics, such as click-through rate (CTR), conversion rate, and return on ad spend (ROAS)? How can you use these correlations to refine your ad campaigns? 5. Assessing the impact of ad creative, messaging, and ad copy on the 'Result Rate' metric, which creative elements tend to result in higher result rates, and how can you optimize your ad content accordingly? 6. Investigating the 'Result Rate' metric over time, are there specific trends or patterns that coincide with changes in ad content or strategy? What can you learn from these temporal fluctuations to improve your campaigns? 7. Establishing benchmark 'Result Rate' goals based on historical data and industry standards, and then measuring your campaigns against these benchmarks to evaluate their success, how can you use these benchmarks to guide your ad optimization efforts?
nodes.slices: - Overall Campaign - Ad Set - Individual Ad - Campaign Objective - Audience Segment - Geo - Time Period - Device Type

nodes.description: The metric counts the number of results you received based on the settings for your campaign. For example: If you selected sales as your campaign objective, the results metric may show the number of purchases that happened as a result of someone seeing your ad. For results where time may pass between when someone sees your ad and when they take action, Meta uses a default attribution setting of 7-day click to count results. If you select a different attribution setting for an ad set, results will be counted with the attribution setting that has been selected. In some cases where results cannot be measured directly due to partial or missing data, statistical modeling may be used to account for some results.
nodes.formula: The number of times your ad achieved an outcome, based on the objective and settings you selected.
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: The results metric shows how well your ad campaign performed based on the business objectives and settings you chose. You can use it to compare performance among similar campaigns and identify areas of opportunity to get better results.
nodes.questions: 1. To what extent does the 'Results' metric differ between different campaign objectives, and what can be learned from these variations? Are there any objectives that consistently generate better results for your e-commerce business? 2. Can you analyze the 'Results' metric based on the attribution window used (e.g., 7-day click attribution, 1-day view attribution) to understand how different attribution models affect the measurement of campaign performance? What is the effect of these attribution settings on result counts? 3. Are there any ad sets or ad creatives that consistently produce better results than others? How can you replicate the strategies and elements that lead to these high-performing ads? 4. Can you examine the relationship between the 'Results' metric and other key performance indicators, such as the budget allocated, ad reach, and engagement metrics? How can you optimize your ad campaigns based on these correlations? 5. What is the influence of seasonality and external factors on the 'Results' metric for your e-commerce business? How can you adjust your ad strategies to take into account these fluctuations and maximize results during peak periods? 6. Can you compare the 'Results' metric with the cost metrics (e.g., cost per result, cost per conversion) to assess the efficiency of your ad campaigns? Are there possibilities to reduce costs while maintaining or improving results? 7. How can you use the 'Results' metric to set meaningful performance benchmarks for future ad campaigns and make data-driven decisions to enhance the effectiveness of your advertising efforts?
nodes.slices: - Campaign Objective - Ad Set - Ad Placement - Attribution Window - Ad Creative - Audience Segment - Time Period - Ad Schedule - Device Type - Geo

nodes.description: This metric is calculated as the sum of available interactions including impressions, clicks and visits that happened before a conversion based on data received from your tracking connections, pixel, app or offline event set. Impressions and clicks are reported for ad platforms that you've set up tracking connections for, and are automatically reported for Facebook and Facebook-owned platforms.
nodes.formula: The total number of impressions, clicks and visits that happened before a conversion
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Touchpoints give you a measure of how many times people interacted with your ads in any way. If your goal is to generate traffic, consider clicks or visits. If your goal is reach, consider impressions.
nodes.questions: 1. Can you determine the most frequent sequences of touchpoints that result in conversions? Are there any particular ad platforms, ad types, or user interactions that are regularly seen in these sequences? 2. How does the attribution window for touchpoints (e.g., how many days prior to conversion are taken into account) affect the distribution of touchpoints? What can you learn from this about the ideal attribution setting for your campaigns? 3. Are there certain combinations of touchpoints that are more likely to lead to higher-value conversions or longer-term customer value? How can you modify your campaigns to generate more of these touchpoint sequences? 4. How does the cost of acquiring each touchpoint (e.g., cost per click, cost per impression) differ for different types of conversions and campaign objectives? Are there areas where you can reduce costs while still preserving touchpoint effectiveness? 5. Can you evaluate how the number of touchpoints needed for a conversion varies across different segments of your audience, such as new visitors versus returning customers? What strategies can you put in place to shorten the conversion journey for certain segments? 6. What are the temporal patterns of touchpoints? Are there certain days of the week or times of the day when touchpoints are more likely to occur? How can this information be used to optimize your ad delivery schedule and timing?
nodes.slices: - Conversion Event - Ad Placement - Time Period - Device Type - Geo - Audience Segment - Ad Set

nodes.description: The number of Accounts Center accounts that performed a click (all). This metric is estimated. The metric counts Accounts Center accounts, not actions. This metric uses sampled data.
nodes.formula: The number of Accounts Center accounts that performed a click (all).
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: It helps advertisers gauge the unique engagement of Accounts Center accounts with their ads. It provides insights into the number of individual users who have taken actions like clicking on ads. This data is valuable for understanding the reach and impact of your ad campaigns and can inform decisions about ad targeting, creative elements, and strategy to optimize campaigns for better engagement and user interactions.
nodes.questions: 1. To what extent does the number of unique clicks differ between different ad types and formats (e.g., image ads, video ads, carousel ads)? Are there any ad types that tend to generate more unique clicks? 2. Are there any particular demographics or audience segments that are responsible for the majority of unique clicks? What can be learned from the most engaged user groups, and how can you adjust your targeting to reach them more effectively? 3. What is the temporal pattern of unique clicks? Are there any particular days or times of the week when unique clicks are more likely to occur? How can this information be used to inform your ad delivery schedule? 4. Is there a correlation between unique clicks and conversion rates? Do higher unique clicks lead to better conversion performance, and if so, how can you optimize your campaigns to take advantage of this relationship? 5. Are there any particular creatives, ad copy, or call-to-action elements that tend to generate more unique clicks? How can you use these insights to enhance the engagement of your ad campaigns? 6. Can you analyze the cost associated with acquiring unique clicks? Are there cost-effective strategies to acquire more unique clicks while staying within your budget constraints? 7. Does the number of unique clicks on an ad affect ad relevance scores and ad auction performance? What are the implications for your ad quality and overall campaign effectiveness?
nodes.slices: - Ad Placement - Device Type - Geo - Demographic Segment - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Audience Segment - Ad Set

nodes.description: The percentage of Accounts Center accounts that saw your ad and performed a unique click (all).
nodes.formula: Unique clicks (all) / Reach
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: It helps advertisers assess the unique click-through rate of Accounts Center accounts, which provides insights into the effectiveness of ad campaigns in driving clicks from individual users. This data is valuable for understanding the engagement and relevance of your ad content and targeting. By monitoring this metric, advertisers can make informed decisions about ad optimization, creative elements, and audience targeting to improve the unique click-through rate and overall campaign performance.
nodes.questions: 1. To what extent do different ad types and formats influence Unique CTR (All)? Are there certain ad formats that are more successful in generating individual user clicks? 2. Are there any particular audience segments that tend to have a higher Unique CTR (All)? What demographics or interests are associated with better engagement, and how can you adjust your targeting accordingly? 3. What is the effect of ad frequency on Unique CTR (All)? Does too much exposure to the same ad reduce the unique click-through rate of individual users, and how can you optimize ad frequency? 4. How does Unique CTR (All) compare to other performance metrics, such as conversion rates or return on ad spend (ROAS)? Does a higher Unique CTR (All) lead to better overall campaign results? 5. Are there any particular ad creative elements or ad copy strategies that usually result in a higher Unique CTR (All)? How can you apply these insights to improve your ad campaigns? 6. Does Unique CTR (All) vary depending on the device used (e.g., mobile vs. desktop)? How can you adapt your ad strategy to cater to the device preferences of your audience? 7. Can you analyze the cost associated with achieving a higher Unique CTR (All)? Are there cost-effective strategies for increasing unique click-through rates while staying within budget constraints?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Ad Relevance. Irrelevant ad content may reduce click-through rates. 🔹 Audience Targeting. Poorly targeted ads may not resonate with users. 🔹 Ad Creative Quality. Low-quality creatives may discourage clicks. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Ad Placement. Ad placements vary in effectiveness. 🔹 Ad Frequency. High ad frequency can lead to ad fatigue and lower CTR. 🔹 Seasonal Trends. Seasonal changes can affect user behavior and CTR. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Ad Scheduling. Ad timing can influence user availability for clicks. 🔹 External Events. Events beyond your control may impact ad performance. 🔹 A/B Testing Changes. Changes in ad variants can affect click-through rates. 🔹 Algorithm Updates. Facebook's algorithm updates can influence performance.
nodes.slices: - Ad Placement - Device Type - Geo - Demographic Segment - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Audience Segment - Ad Set

nodes.description: The percentage of Accounts Center accounts that saw your ad and performed a link click. It counts Accounts Center accounts, not actions. This metric uses sampled data.
nodes.formula: Unique link clicks / Reach.
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: It provides insights into the unique engagement of Accounts Center accounts with your ad, specifically focusing on link clicks. It helps advertisers understand how effective their ad campaigns are at driving clicks from individual users. By monitoring this metric, advertisers can make informed decisions about ad content, targeting, and creative elements to improve the unique click-through rate and overall campaign performance.
nodes.questions: 1. To what extent does Unique CTR (Link Click-Through Rate) differ between different ad placements (e.g., Facebook, Instagram, Audience Network)? Are there certain placements that are more successful in generating unique link clicks from individual users? 2. Are there particular audience segments that tend to have a higher Unique CTR (Link Click-Through Rate)? What characteristics, interests, or behaviors are associated with better engagement, and how can you adjust your targeting accordingly? 3. What is the role of ad scheduling in achieving a higher Unique CTR (Link Click-Through Rate)? Are there certain times or days when individual users are more likely to interact with your ads by clicking on links? 4. How does the uniqueness of ad creatives, such as images, videos, or ad copy, affect Unique CTR (Link Click-Through Rate)? Are there creative elements that consistently drive higher link click engagement from individual users? 5. Does the Unique CTR (Link Click-Through Rate) vary depending on the device used (e.g., mobile vs. desktop)? How can you modify your ad content and design to cater to the device preferences of your audience? 6. Can you analyze the relationship between Unique CTR (Link Click-Through Rate) and conversion rates or return on ad spend (ROAS)? Does a higher Unique CTR (Link Click-Through Rate) lead to better overall campaign results? 7. How does ad frequency influence the Unique CTR (Link Click-Through Rate)? Does overexposure to the same ad affect the click-through rate of individual users, and how can you optimize ad frequency to maintain engagement?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Ad Relevance. Irrelevant ad content may reduce link click-through rates. 🔹 Audience Targeting. Poorly targeted ads may not resonate with users. 🔹 Ad Creative Quality. Low-quality creatives may discourage link clicks. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Ad Placement. Ad placements vary in effectiveness. 🔹 Ad Frequency. High ad frequency can lead to ad fatigue and lower CTR. 🔹 Seasonal Trends. Seasonal changes can affect user behavior and CTR. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Ad Scheduling. Ad timing can influence user availability for clicks. 🔹 External Events. Events beyond your control may impact ad performance. 🔹 A/B Testing Changes. Changes in ad variants can affect link click rates. 🔹 Algorithm Updates. Facebook's algorithm updates can influence performance.
nodes.slices: - Ad Placement - Device Type - Geo - Demographic Segment - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Audience Segment - Ad Set

nodes.description: The number of Accounts Center accounts that performed a link click. The metric counts Accounts Center accounts, not actions. This metric is estimated.
nodes.formula: The number of Accounts Center accounts that performed a link click.
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: It provides insights into the number of distinct users who have clicked on links within the ads. This data is valuable for understanding the impact and relevance of your ad campaigns and can inform decisions about ad targeting, creative elements, and strategy to optimize campaigns for better engagement and user interactions.
nodes.questions: 1. How has the number of distinct users clicking on ad links changed over time? Are there any seasonal patterns or trends that can be observed? 2. Does the ad format (e.g., image, video, carousel) have an effect on Unique Link Clicks? Are there any formats that attract more distinct users to click on links, and how can this be optimized? 3. Are there any differences in Unique Link Clicks between various audience segments? Can you identify any demographics or interests that lead to a higher number of distinct users clicking on links? How can this be used to refine targeting strategies? 4. What is the impact of ad copy, call-to-action buttons, and link descriptions on Unique Link Clicks? Are there any messaging elements that are more effective in engaging distinct users? 5. Does the use of retargeting or custom audience segments result in more Unique Link Clicks compared to broader targeting? How can this be used to improve campaign performance? 6. Is there a correlation between Unique Link Clicks and other key metrics, such as conversion rates, click-through rates, or return on ad spend (ROAS)? Do higher Unique Link Clicks generally lead to better overall campaign results? 7. What is the relationship between ad frequency and Unique Link Clicks? Does showing the same ad to users multiple times affect the likelihood of distinct users clicking on links?
nodes.slices: - Ad Placement - Device Type - Geo - Demographic Segment - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Audience Segment - Ad Set

nodes.description: The average time a video was played, including any time spent replaying the video for a single impression. This metric is calculated as the amount of time spent playing your video (including replays for a single impression), divided by the total number of video plays. Because this metric includes replays for a video, the reported average video watch time could be longer than the length of the video.
nodes.formula: The amount of time spent playing your video
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This data can help advertisers assess the effectiveness of their video content, make informed decisions about ad creative, audience targeting, and campaign optimization to improve video engagement and overall ad performance.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the average playtime of videos differ between ad formats such as in-stream video ads, carousel ads, or stories? Are there any ad formats that tend to have longer or shorter average playtimes, and how can you adjust your video content accordingly? 2. Is there a distinction in average playtime between short videos (under 15 seconds) and longer videos (over 60 seconds)? Does video duration have an effect on user engagement, and can you determine an ideal video length for your audience? 3. What effect do video thumbnails, captions, and opening scenes have on average playtime? Are there certain elements in the video that contribute to longer viewer engagement? 4. Are there any patterns or trends in video playtime based on the time of day, day of the week, or season? Do users watch videos for longer durations during certain periods? 5. How does the targeting of your ad campaigns affect video average playtime? Do videos have different levels of resonance with various audience segments, and how can you refine your targeting strategies based on playtime data? 6. Does the use of storytelling or a narrative approach in your video content lead to longer average playtimes compared to purely promotional content? How can you use storytelling techniques to increase engagement? 7. Are there any correlations between video average playtime and other key performance metrics, such as click-through rates (CTR), conversions, or return on ad spend (ROAS)? Does longer playtime usually result in better overall campaign results?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Video Content Quality. Poor-quality or unengaging videos result in shorter playtimes. 🔹 Audience Targeting. Inaccurate audience targeting may lead to disinterest. 🔹 Video Length. Longer videos may have shorter average play times. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Ad Placement. Ad placements can influence video engagement. 🔹 Platform and Device Compatibility. Videos might not be optimized for all devices/platforms. 🔹 Creative Fatigue. Overexposure to the same video can lead to shorter playtimes. 🔹 Loading Speed and Buffering Issues. Technical problems can discourage video views. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Competition and Market Trends. Market changes and increased competition can impact engagement. 🔹 Ad Scheduling. Timing of video ad placements can influence playtime. 🔹 Content Relevance. The relevance of the video content to the audience matters. 🔹 User Behavior Changes. Shifts in user behavior can affect video performance.
nodes.slices: - Ad Placement - Video Length - Audience Segment - Video Type - Interactive Elements - Device Type - Geo - A/B Test

nodes.description: This metric counts when a video starts to play after an impression for the video is counted. Video plays can occur either when a video starts to play automatically or because someone clicked to play the video. Replays of a video, such as when someone pauses a video and starts playing it again, are not counted. In the case of an ad format with multiple videos, such as a carousel ad, video plays will be counted separately for each video shown.
nodes.formula: The number of times your video starts to play
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: It helps advertisers understand the level of interest and engagement their video ads are generating. By monitoring this metric, advertisers can make informed decisions about the effectiveness of their video content, ad format, and audience targeting to optimize their campaigns for better video engagement and overall ad performance.
nodes.questions: 1. What differences exist in video plays between single videos, carousel ads, and stories? Are there certain formats that tend to generate more video plays, and how can you use this information to optimize your ad format choices? 2. Does the audience segment have an effect on video plays? Are certain demographics or interests more likely to engage with video content, and how can you adjust your targeting to reach these users effectively? 3. Are there any patterns in video plays based on the time of day or day of the week? Does the timing of ad delivery influence video plays, and how can you time your ads for maximum engagement? 4. Does the length of the video affect the number of video plays? Are shorter videos more likely to be played in their entirety, or do longer videos tend to generate more plays? 5. Can you recognize trends in video plays over time? Do certain types of video content, seasonal events, or promotions lead to increased video plays, and how can you use this data to plan future campaigns? 6. How does the placement of your video ads (e.g., Facebook feed, Instagram stories, or audience network) affect video plays? Are there particular placements that drive higher engagement? 7. Are there any correlations between video plays and other performance metrics like click-through rates (CTR) or conversions? Does a higher number of video plays result in better overall campaign results and return on investment (ROI)?
nodes.slices: - Ad Placement - Video Type - Device Type - Video Length - Audience Segment - Geo - Video Thumbnail - Ad Format

nodes.description: The metric counts when the 100% point in your video was played. This may include plays from people who played all of your video from the beginning, and may also include plays from people who skipped to the 100% point.
nodes.formula: The number of times your video was played at 100% of its length, including plays that skipped to this point.
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Your video ad was shown to 5,000 people. Out of those 5,000 viewers, 800 watched the video all the way to the 100% point, and an additional 200 viewers skipped directly to the 100% point of the video. Video Plays at 100% = The number of times your video was played at 100% of its length, including plays that skipped to this point. Video Plays at 100% = 800 (full views) + 200 (skipped views) Video Plays at 100% = 1,000
nodes.questions: 1. Is there a connection between the content of the video and the number of plays at 100%? Are there certain topics or messages that lead to higher viewer retention and more plays at 100%? 2. Does the demographic and interests of the audience have an effect on the percentage of viewers who finish the video? Are certain groups more likely to watch the entire video, and how can you adjust your targeting accordingly? 3. What is the average video length that usually results in the most plays at 100%? Is there an ideal video duration for maximizing viewer engagement, and does this differ by ad format? 4. Can you recognize patterns in the device types (mobile, desktop, tablet) used by viewers who complete the video at 100%? Does device type have an effect on viewer engagement, and how can you optimize your ad content accordingly? 5. Are there external factors such as the time of day or day of the week that are associated with higher completion rates? Does the timing of ad delivery influence the probability of viewers watching the video to completion? 6. How do plays at 100% compare to other key performance metrics like click-through rates (CTR) or conversions? Does a higher rate of video completion lead to better overall campaign results and return on investment (ROI)?
nodes.slices: - Ad Placement - Video Length - Audience Segment - Device Type - Video Type - Geo - Ad Format - Video Thumbnail

nodes.description: The metric counts when the 25% point in your video was played. This may include plays from people who played a quarter of your video from the beginning, and may also include plays from people who skipped to the 25% point and played your video from there.
nodes.formula: The number of times your video was played at 25% of its length, including plays that skipped to this point.
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Your video ad was shown to 8,000 people. Out of those 8,000 viewers, 1,500 watched the video all the way to the 25% point, and an additional 300 viewers skipped directly to the 25% point of the video. Video Plays at 25% = The number of times your video was played at 25% of its length, including plays that skipped to this point. Video Plays at 25% = 1,500 (full views) + 300 (skipped views) Video Plays at 25% = 1,800
nodes.questions: 1. Is there a connection between the percentage of video plays at 25% and other completion points (e.g., 50%, 75%, 100%) in your videos? Are there any patterns that suggest viewers are more likely to engage up to the 25% mark or another point, and how can you optimize your video content accordingly? 2. Does the content of the video have a major effect on the number of plays at 25%? Are there certain video themes, opening sequences, or hooks that lead to higher engagement up to the 25% point? 3. Are there particular audience segments that show a stronger tendency to engage with the video up to 25%? Do certain demographics, interests, or behavior patterns correlate with this level of engagement? 4. What is the ideal video length for maximizing viewer retention up to the 25% mark? Are there trends suggesting that shorter or longer videos are more effective in achieving this milestone? 5. Can you identify external factors such as time of day, day of the week, or seasonality that influence the likelihood of viewers reaching the 25% point? How can you adjust your ad delivery schedule to take advantage of these patterns? 6. How do video plays at 25% relate to other key performance metrics like click-through rates (CTR) or conversion rates? Is there a correlation between reaching the 25% point and improved overall campaign performance and ROI? 7. Does the ad format (e.g., single video, carousel, slideshow) have an impact on the percentage of viewers engaging up to 25%? Are some ad formats more effective at retaining viewer attention up to this milestone?

nodes.description: The amount of money you set manually
nodes.formula: Let's say you normally spend $304 per month on advertising. To figure out your average daily budget, you'd divide $ 304 USD by 30.4 (the average number of days in a month - 365/12) to get an average daily budget of $ 10 USD. Using this example, here's how you'd figure out your average daily budget: 304/30.4 = $ 10 USD per day (Monthly budget / Average number of days per month = average daily budget) You can also create shared budgets, which let you set a budget across multiple campaigns.
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: Tracking the Average Daily Budget in a Google ad account is essential as it provides insights into whether the budget aligns with campaign goals and allows advertisers to make informed decisions on adjustments, ensuring that they are allocating their budget effectively across campaigns for optimal performance without exceeding financial constraints.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the Average Daily Budget affect the total monthly expenditure, and are there particular days or weeks where modifications could optimize budget utilization? 2. Are there recognizable patterns or trends in the connection between Average Daily Budget and click-through rates (CTR), and how do these trends vary across product categories? 3. Does the Average Daily Budget match the seasonality of the e-commerce business, and are there chances to strategically adjust budgets during peak shopping times? 4. How does the Average Daily Budget correlate with the return on ad spend (ROAS) for different campaigns, and are there particular campaigns that could benefit from budget reallocation? 5. Are there cases where the Average Daily Budget is consistently used up before the end of the day, potentially limiting ad visibility during key hours? 6. How does the Average Daily Budget affect the impression share, and are there missed opportunities for increased visibility due to budget restrictions? 7. Is there a noticeable difference in the effectiveness of the Average Daily Budget for various targeting criteria, such as demographics, locations, or devices?
nodes.slices: - Product Category - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Geo - Device Type - Campaign Type - Audience Segment - Seasonal or Event-Based Trends

nodes.description: Impressions / Unique Reach
nodes.formula: In Google Ads, you want to look at the avg. impr. freq. per user (7 days) from January 29, 2020-January 30, 2020 for a selected campaign. Google Ads will look at the sum of total impressions in the lookback windows for January 29, 2020 and January 30, 2020 and divide the amount by the sum of total unique users for each day in the date range. Let’s say you have the following impressions and unique users for your campaign: 1,400 impressions and 700 unique users in the past 7 days from January 29, 2020. 1,000 impressions and 200 unique users in the past 7 days from January 30, 2020. Your avg. impr. freq. per user in the past 7 days would be 2.7 (or [1,400+1,000] / [700+200] = 2.7).
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking the Average Impression Frequency per user is important as it provides advertisers with insights into how often an individual is exposed to their video ads over a specified period, either 7 or 30 days. This metric is crucial for managing ad fatigue, avoiding overexposure, and making informed decisions about campaign optimization, ensuring a balanced and effective delivery of video content to the target audience.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the Average Impression Frequency per user differ across different product categories or campaigns within your e-commerce company, and what can be learned about the best frequency for different types of products or services? 2. Are there particular audience segments or demographics where adjusting the Average Impression Frequency per user could lead to more effective ad delivery and improved campaign performance? 4. How does seasonality or promotional events affect the Average Impression Frequency per user, and how can this data be used to plan strategic adjustments to ad exposure during peak periods? 5. Can the correlation between ad creative variations and Average Impression Frequency per user be examined to identify which creatives are more resistant to ad fatigue and remain effective over time? 6. What can be learned by comparing the Average Impression Frequency per user for different ad formats, such as in-stream versus bumper ads, and how can this information inform the overall video ad strategy? 7. How does the Average Impression Frequency per user contribute to brand recall and awareness, and what strategies can be implemented to find the right balance between exposure and avoiding audience fatigue in the context of an e-commerce business?

nodes.description: Clicks / Impressions
nodes.formula: For example, if you had 5 clicks and 100 impressions, then your CTR would be 5%.
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: A high CTR is a good indication that users find your ads and listings helpful and relevant. CTR also contributes to your keyword's expected CTR, which is a component of Ad Rank. Note that a good CTR is relative to what you're advertising and on which networks. You can use CTR to gauge which ads, listings, and keywords are successful for you and which need to be improved. The more your keywords, ads, and listings relate to each other and to your business, the more likely a user is to click on your ad or listing after searching on your keyword phrase.
nodes.questions: 1. Are there any patterns in the Clickthrough Rate (CTR) across different product categories in the e-commerce business, and do certain categories have consistently higher or lower CTRs? 2. Does optimizing for different devices (desktop, mobile, tablet) have an effect on the CTR, and can improvements in CTR be seen by optimizing for specific devices? 3. What is the correlation between ad position on the search results page and CTR, and how do changes in bids or ad quality affect clickthrough rates? 4. Are there any geographical locations or regions that have notably higher or lower CTRs, which could provide insight into regional preferences or market dynamics? 5. How does seasonality or special events affect CTR, and is it possible to tailor ad campaigns to temporal trends to maximize engagement? 6. Are there any patterns in the relationship between CTR and subsequent conversions, which could help to understand the effectiveness of ads in driving user actions on the e-commerce site? 7. What is the impact of ad extensions, such as sitelink extensions or callout extensions, on CTR, and how can the use of extensions be optimized for better performance?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Ad Relevance Decline. If the relevance between your ad copy and the user's search intent decreases, it may result in a lower CTR. Ensure that ad content aligns closely with targeted keywords and user expectations. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Seasonal Trends or Events. Changes in user behavior due to seasons or specific events might impact CTR. For example, a decrease in interest during off-seasons can influence the clickthrough rate. 🔹 Ad Fatigue or Overexposure. Displaying the same ad frequently to the same audience may lead to ad fatigue, causing a decline in CTR. Rotate ad creatives and optimize targeting to combat fatigue. 🔹 Competitor Actions. Increased competition or changes in competitor strategies can affect CTR. Stay aware of competitor activities and adjust your ad content and bidding strategy accordingly. 🔹 Changes in Ad Position. Movement in ad position, especially a drop below the fold, can impact visibility and CTR. Monitor average position metrics and adjust bids if necessary. 🔹 Ad Copy Relevance and Creativity. Poorly crafted ad copy that lacks relevance or fails to capture attention can lead to a decrease in CTR. Regularly test and optimize ad copy for maximum engagement. 🔹 Landing Page Issues. If the landing page doesn't match user expectations set by the ad, it can result in a higher bounce rate and lower CTR. Ensure landing pages are relevant and optimized. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Changes in Search Trends. Shifts in user search behavior or the popularity of certain keywords can impact CTR. Stay updated on industry trends and adjust keyword targeting accordingly. 🔹 Ad Format or Extension Changes. Modifications to ad formats or extensions can influence user interaction. Ensure that new formats align with user preferences and contribute positively to CTR.
nodes.slices: '- Keyword-Level - Campaign or Ad Group - Device Type - Ad Position - Geo - Network (Search vs. Display) - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Competitor Benchmarking

nodes.description: Average time from click to conversion.
nodes.formula: For example, if the majority of clicks yield conversions within 2 days, the system would adjust conversion performance in approximately 2 days.
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking the Conversion Cycle metric is crucial because it provides insights into the time it takes for clicks to translate into valuable conversions. This information helps advertisers understand the efficiency of their conversion process, enabling them to make informed decisions on campaign optimizations, budget allocations, and strategic adjustments to enhance the overall effectiveness and timeliness of their advertising efforts.
nodes.questions: 1. Are there any differences in the average conversion cycle across different product categories in e-commerce, and are there any products with particularly short or long conversion cycles? 2. Does optimizing for different types of devices (desktop, mobile, tablet) affect the conversion cycle, and can changes be made to campaigns to reduce the time it takes for clicks to result in conversions? 3. What impact do marketing channels or campaigns have on the conversion cycle, and can strategies be adjusted to shorten the time it takes for users to convert? 4. Are there any geographical locations or regions where the conversion cycle is shorter or longer, and what does this tell us about regional consumer behavior and decision-making processes? 5. How does seasonality or specific events affect the conversion cycle, and can advertising strategies be tailored to take advantage of temporal trends to speed up the conversion process? 6. Is there a pattern between the ad position on the search results page and the conversion cycle, and can this help us understand how the visibility of ads affects the time it takes for users to convert? 7. What role does the type of keywords play in the conversion cycle, and can keyword selection be optimized to influence the speed of users moving through the conversion funnel?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Landing Page Issues. If the landing page experience is poor or the page doesn't align with user expectations, it can increase the time it takes for a click to convert. Optimize landing pages for relevance and user experience. 🔹 Complex Purchase Process. A lengthy or complicated checkout process can extend the conversion cycle. Simplify the purchasing steps to make it easier for users to complete transactions quickly. 🔹 Slow Website Load Times. Slow website loading can lead to user frustration and abandonment, delaying conversions. Regularly optimize website speed for a smoother user experience. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Ineffective Ad Messaging. If the ad messaging doesn't accurately represent the product or service, it may attract clicks from users who are less likely to convert. Align ad content with landing page information. 🔹 Seasonal Trends or Events. Changes in user behavior due to seasons or events may impact the time it takes for conversions. For example, holiday seasons might experience longer conversion cycles. 🔹 Competitor Actions. Increased competition can lead to delayed conversions as users compare options. Monitor competitor strategies and adjust ad content or promotions accordingly. 🔹 External Economic Factors. Economic conditions may influence consumer spending habits and the decision-making process, impacting the conversion cycle. Consider economic trends when planning campaigns. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Ad Campaign Changes. Significant changes to ad campaigns, such as targeting, messaging, or bidding strategy, can affect user response time and conversion cycles. Implement changes gradually and monitor results. 🔹 Product Availability or Inventory Issues. If there are issues with product availability or inventory, users may delay conversions. Ensure that product information is accurate and transparent to manage user expectations. 🔹 User Behavior Shifts. Changes in user preferences or behavior patterns may affect the time it takes for them to convert. Stay informed about industry trends and adapt marketing strategies accordingly.
nodes.slices: - Device Type - Ad Placement - Keyword-Level - Campaign or Ad Group - Geo - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week

nodes.description: The number of conversions / The number of interactions
nodes.formula: For example, if you had 50 conversions from 1,000 interactions, your conversion rate would be 5%, since 50 ÷ 1,000 = 5%.
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking the Conversion Rate metric is essential as it indicates the effectiveness of your ad campaigns in turning interactions into valuable conversions. This metric helps advertisers assess the performance of their ads, allowing them to make data-driven decisions on optimizing ad content, targeting strategies, and overall campaign efficiency to maximize the return on investment and achieve business objectives.
nodes.questions: 1. nodes.description: Total cost / Number of conversions
nodes.formula: Let's say you are running a Google Ads campaign, and you've incurred a total cost of $500 for the campaign. During the same period, the campaign generated 20 conversions. Cost per Conversion = Total Cost/Number of Conversions Cost per Conversion = 500/20 Cost per Conversion = 25

nodes.description: The total cost / The total number of clicks
nodes.formula: Let's say your ad gets two clicks, one costing $0.20 and one costing $0.40, for a total cost is $0.60. You'd divide $0.60 (your total cost) by 2 (your total number of clicks) to get an average CPC of $0.30.

nodes.description: Total cost of impressions / Total number of impressions * 1000
nodes.formula: Let's say you are running a Google Ads campaign, and the total cost incurred for impressions (views) is $500. During the same period, your ads received a total of 100,000 impressions. CPM=Total Cost of Impressions/Total Number of Impressions × 1000 CPM = 500/100,000 ×1000 CPM = 5
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: The information about Tracking the Cost-per-Thousand Impressions (CPM) metric is crucial for evaluating the efficiency of ad campaigns in terms of brand exposure and awareness, helping advertisers make informed decisions about budget allocation and strategy adjustments to maximize the impact of their advertising efforts.
nodes.questions: 1. What is the variation in Cost-per-Thousand Impressions (CPM) across different product categories in the e-commerce business, and are there any categories with higher or lower costs? 2. Is it possible to evaluate the effectiveness of targeting options such as demographics or interests based on CPM variations, to identify the optimal audience segments for cost-efficient impressions? 3. What is the effect of ad placement on CPM, and are there any differences between placements on the Google Display Network, YouTube, or other platforms, to help decide where to allocate budget for better cost efficiency? 4. How does seasonality or specific events affect CPM, and are there any patterns that can be used to adjust advertising strategies during peak seasons or promotional periods? 5. Can trends in CPM be identified based on the geographic location of users, to gain insights into regional advertising efficiency and opportunities for targeted adjustments? 6. Are there any ad formats that result in higher or lower CPM, and how can this information be used to guide decisions on creative design and format selection to optimize cost efficiency? 7. How does the Quality Score influence CPM, and can improvements in ad relevance, expected clickthrough rate, and landing page experience lead to lower CPM and better overall campaign performance?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Increased Competition. Higher competition in the advertising space can drive up CPM as more advertisers vie for ad space. This is particularly impactful in highly competitive industries or during peak seasons. 🔹 Ad Placement Quality. Poor placement choices or placements with low engagement rates can result in higher CPM. Evaluate and adjust ad placements to ensure they align with your target audience and campaign goals. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Changes in Ad Formats or Sizes. Modifications to ad formats or sizes may impact the cost of impressions. Experimenting with different formats can inadvertently lead to increased costs. Test and optimize ad formats carefully. 🔹 Ad Quality and Relevance. Low-quality ads or those with low relevance may experience higher CPM as platforms may charge more for less engaging content. Regularly review and enhance ad creatives. 🔹 Targeting Changes. Changes in targeting parameters, such as broadening the audience, can influence CPM. Ensure that your targeting aligns with your campaign objectives and audience relevance. 🔹 Ad Scheduling and Timing. Poorly timed ads or running ads during periods of low user activity may result in higher CPM. Optimize ad scheduling based on when your target audience is most active. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Economic Conditions. Economic fluctuations can impact CPM, especially if advertisers adjust their budgets in response to economic uncertainties. Monitor market conditions and adjust strategies accordingly. 🔹 Ad Auction Dynamics. Changes in the auction environment, such as modifications to bidding algorithms, may influence CPM. Stay informed about platform updates and adjust bidding strategies accordingly. 🔹 Industry Events or Trends. Industry-specific events or trends may temporarily impact CPM. Stay aware of industry happenings and adjust strategies as needed. 🔹 Ad Creative Fatigue. Displaying the same ad creative frequently may lead to ad fatigue, resulting in higher CPM. Rotate creatives regularly to maintain user interest and engagement.

nodes.description: Total cost of impressions / Total number of video views
nodes.formula: Let's say you are running a Google Ads video campaign, and the total cost incurred for video impressions is $200. During the same period, your video ads received a total of 1,000 views (where a view is counted when a viewer watches 30 seconds of the video ad). CPV = Total Cost of Impressions/Total Number of Video Views ​CPV = 200/1,000 CPV = 0.20

nodes.description: The frequency you set manually
nodes.formula: Frequency Capping is not typically calculated using a specific formula; rather, it is a setting that advertisers manually configure based on their campaign objectives and user experience goals. The calculation involves setting a predefined frequency limit for the number of times an individual user is allowed to see ads within a specific time period. For example: In a Display campaign, an advertiser may set a frequency cap of 3 impressions per day for a specific user. In a Video campaign, an advertiser may set a frequency cap of 5 impressions or views per week for a particular user.
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric is important for avoiding ad fatigue, enhancing user experience, and optimizing campaign performance by controlling the frequency of ad impressions. Advertisers can use frequency capping to strike a balance between reaching their audience and preventing overexposure, ultimately improving the effectiveness of their advertising campaigns.

How does the effectiveness of Frequency Capping differ across different audience segments, and are there certain demographics or user behaviors that need special frequency limits to improve targeting accuracy? 5. nodes.description: The number of video views

nodes.description: Impressions on the absolute top / Impressions
nodes.formula: Let's say your ad appears at the absolute top of the search results 300 times, and overall, your ad receives 1,000 impressions. Impr. (Abs. Top) % = Impressions on the absolute top/Total Impressions × 100 Impr. (Abs. Top) % = 300/1,000 × 100 Impr. (Abs. Top) %=30%
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: You can use these metrics to understand changes in click-through rate (CTR) caused by a change in the location of your ads on the search result page. We don’t recommend you use these metrics as a target to set your bids because, sometimes, they may decrease as bids increase. This happens as higher bids may allow you to enter more competitive auctions in a worse location.

Top) % metric relate to changes in click-through rates (CTRs) for e-commerce ads, and what can be learned about the effect of ad placement on user engagement and interaction? 2. Top) % over time to detect patterns or changes, giving advertisers insights into the dynamic nature of ad placement and potential chances for strategic adjustments? 3. Top) % metric, and how can advertisers adjust bidding and ad strategies to align with seasonal trends and take advantage of peak periods of user engagement and conversion opportunities?

nodes.description: Impressions on top / Impressions

nodes.description: Impressions / Total eligible impressions
nodes.formula: Let's consider a Google Ads campaign that, based on various factors such as targeting settings, approval statuses, and quality, has a total of 10,000 eligible impressions. During a specific time period, your ads received 7,000 impressions. Impression Share (IS)=Impressions/Total Eligible Impressions×100 Impression Share (IS)= 7,000/10,000×100 Impression Share (IS)=70%

nodes.description: The amount of money
nodes.formula: Let's say an advertiser's monthly expenses for their Google Ads campaigns include: Campaign spend: $5,000 Taxes and fees: $500 Adjustments and promotional credits: -$200 Net cost=Campaign spend+Taxes and fees+Adjustments and promotional credits Net cost = $5,000 + $500 + (-$200) Net cost = $5,300

nodes.description: The number of on-target impressions / The total number of impressions
nodes.formula: Suppose you run a campaign targeting males 18-34. Your ad is shown 5 times on single-viewer devices, such as smartphones and tablets. The users include: 1 Male 35-39 2 Males 25-29 2 Females 25-29 The total number of impressions is 5, which includes 2 impressions that are on-target. The OTP is 2 / 5 = 40%.

nodes.description: The number of ad clicks collected from organic traffic to the publisher site.
nodes.formula: Suppose you have recieved 100 visitors who came to you from the publisher’s website. And out of these 100 users, 50 came to the publisher’s website through a paid channel, 30 through an organic channel, and 20 through an unknown channel. Organic clicks = Total Clicks - (Unknown Clicks + Purchased Clicks) Organic clicks = 100 - (20 + 50) Organic clicks = 30
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking "Organic clicks" is important because it helps advertisers to understand the performance of their ads within the context of unpaid and owned traffic. This metric provides insights into the natural interaction of users with ads on a publisher's site, aiding in decision-making regarding ad placements, content strategy, and overall user experience optimization.

Understanding the relative impact of organic clicks can guide strategies for maximizing the value from both organic and paid sources. 4. Are there particular products or categories that receive a higher proportion of organic clicks, and how can this information inform product placement, content strategy, or promotional efforts to take advantage of organic engagement? 5. How do organic clicks vary across different devices (desktop, mobile, tablet), and what implications does this have for responsive design, mobile optimization, or device-specific advertising strategies? 6. Can a comparison of organic clicks with paid clicks provide insights into user behavior, preferences, or intent, and how can this knowledge be used to refine targeting and messaging in paid advertising efforts? 7. What role do organic clicks play in the overall customer journey, and are there ways to align organic and paid strategies to create a consistent and unified user experience from initial engagement to conversion?
