nodes.description: The number of plays your video ad receives / Impressions (the number of times your video ad is shown).
nodes.formula: Let's go through an example of calculating Play rate: Number of plays your video ad receives: 500 Impressions (the number of times your video ad is shown): 10,000 Play rate=Number of plays/Impressions Play rate=500/10,000 Play rate=0.05
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Use play rate to gauge user engagement with your opening image (for Click-to-Play video ads) or your selected video thumbnail (for YouTube-specific video ad formats). If your ad has a low play rate, try creating a more engaging opening image or video thumbnail with a clear and attractive call-to-action
nodes.questions: 1. What is the trend in the play rate of our video ads over the past several campaigns, and are there any campaigns or periods where the play rate significantly differed from the average? 2. How does the play rate vary between different ad formats (Click-to-Play, YouTube-specific, etc.), and are there any formats that consistently perform better than others in terms of user engagement? 3. Does the analysis of play rate provide any insights into the effectiveness of different opening images or video thumbnails, and are there any common characteristics of high-performing visuals that can be replicated in future creatives? 4. What correlation exists between play rate and other engagement metrics, such as click-through rate (CTR) or conversion rate? Understanding these relationships can help identify overall patterns in user behavior. 5. Are there any particular products or categories that have higher play rates, and how can this information guide content strategy or promotional efforts to increase engagement in certain areas? 6. How does play rate vary across different audience segments or demographics, and are there any opportunities to tailor video content to better appeal to specific target audiences? 7. Can a comparison of play rates on different devices (desktop, mobile, tablet) provide any insights into user preferences or behaviors on various platforms, guiding device-specific video ad optimizations?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Low-Quality Opening Image/Thumbnail. If the opening image or thumbnail is unappealing or unclear, it may result in a low play rate. Enhance the visual elements to capture user attention and encourage engagement. 🔹 Mismatch Between Ad Content and Audience Expectations. If the actual video content does not align with what users expect based on the opening image or thumbnail, it can lead to a disinterest, impacting play rate. Ensure consistency in messaging and content. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Technical Issues with Video Playback. Technical glitches affecting video playback, such as slow load times or buffering, can discourage users from watching the video, contributing to a lower play rate. Regularly test and optimize for technical performance. 🔹 Ad Placement Quality. Poor ad placement choices, such as locations with low visibility, may result in lower play rates. Assess and optimize ad placements to maximize visibility to the target audience. 🔹 Competitive Ad Environment. Increased competition for ad space can impact play rates. Evaluate the competitive landscape and adjust bidding or targeting strategies accordingly. 🔹 Video Length and Content Relevance. Lengthy videos or content that is not relevant to the target audience may lead to disengagement and a decrease in play rates. Ensure videos are concise and align with audience interests. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Changes in User Behavior or Trends. Shifts in user behavior or trends may influence the types of content users engage with. Stay informed about evolving trends and adjust video content accordingly. 🔹 Seasonal Variations. Seasonal changes in user preferences or behaviors may impact play rates. Adjust video content and messaging to align with seasonal trends. 🔹 Ad Creative Fatigue. Displaying the same ad creative frequently may lead to ad fatigue, resulting in lower play rates. Rotate creatives regularly to maintain user interest and engagement. 🔹 External Events or Cultural Sensitivity. External events or cultural nuances may impact user receptivity to certain ad content, affecting play rates. Ensure that ad content is culturally sensitive and relevant.
nodes.slices: - Ad Format - Device Type - Campaign or Ad Group - Video Length - Geo - Keyword-Level - Audience Segment

nodes.description: The total number of people in your target demographics and location
nodes.formula: The Population metric is not something that is typically calculated within the Google Ads platform. Instead, it is derived from various data sources like census data, digital behavior, TV viewership, and YouTube reach.
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking the Population metric is crucial as it provides advertisers with insights into the potential reach and audience size within their target demographics and location. This information helps advertisers make informed decisions about the scale and scope of their advertising campaigns. By understanding the total population across different channels and platforms, advertisers can optimize their strategies to effectively reach and engage with their desired audience.
nodes.questions: 1. How has the population in our target demographics and location changed over certain time periods, and are there any patterns or changes that coincide with our advertising campaigns or external factors? 2. Is there a connection between changes in the digital population and changes in online consumer behavior, and how can this information help our digital marketing strategies? 3. What can we learn by comparing the census population with the YouTube population, and are there certain demographics where YouTube ads have a higher or lower reach than the overall population? 4. How does the TV population compare to the viewership trends for our TV advertising campaigns, and are there chances to improve ad content based on TV-watching habits within our target audience? 5. Are there certain sub-locations or audience segments where population data is not available, and how can this affect our targeting strategies or adjustments in certain regions? 6. Can changes in population metrics give us early signs of changes in market dynamics, allowing us to adjust our advertising strategies ahead of time to take advantage of new opportunities or avoid potential problems? 7. What role does population data play in deciding the potential reach and scale of our advertising efforts, and how can this information be used to set realistic and achievable campaign goals?
nodes.slices: - Demographic Segment - Geo - Campaign or Ad Group - Seasonal or Event-Based Trends

nodes.description: The number of ad clicks collected from paid traffic to the publisher site.
nodes.formula: Let's say a publisher's website, XYZ.com, runs Google ads, and they purchase traffic through a third-party advertising network. In a given month: Number of ad clicks collected from paid traffic to XYZ.com: 5,000 clicks So, Publisher Purchased Clicks = 5,000
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking Publisher Purchased Clicks is important as it provides insights into the effectiveness and impact of paid third-party traffic on a publisher's site. By monitoring this metric, advertisers and publishers can assess the performance of externally sourced traffic, helping them make informed decisions about the value and efficiency of their paid advertising efforts. This information aids in optimizing strategies, allocating budgets effectively, and maximizing the return on investment for ad campaigns.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the presence of publisher-purchased clicks affect the overall click-through rate (CTR) of our ads, and are there any campaigns or ad placements that show a higher concentration of these clicks? 2. Are there any sources of paid third-party traffic that generate publisher-purchased clicks, and how does the quality of this traffic compare to other acquisition channels in terms of conversion rates and user engagement? 3. Are there any seasonal or temporal patterns in the occurrence of publisher-purchased clicks, and how can this information help us to decide our budget allocation and bidding strategies during certain periods? 4. What effect do publisher-purchased clicks have on the return on investment (ROI) of our advertising campaigns, and how does the cost per acquisition (CPA) for these clicks compare to clicks from other channels? 5. How does the performance of ads with publisher-purchased clicks vary across different demographics or geographic regions, and are there any possibilities to adjust our messaging or targeting strategies accordingly? 6. Are there any trends or anomalies in the data related to publisher-purchased clicks, and how can these insights help us to improve our fraud detection and prevention measures to ensure the integrity of our advertising efforts? 7. What is the role of publisher-purchased clicks in the overall user journey, and how can we optimize our ad creatives or landing pages to enhance the experience for users coming from this traffic source?
nodes.slices: - Campaign or Ad Group - Geo - Device Type - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Ad Format

nodes.description: This score given by Google
nodes.formula: Let's consider three hypothetical ads for the keyword "running shoes," each with a different Quality Score: Ad A has a Quality Score of 7/10. Ad B has a Quality Score of 5/10. Ad C has a Quality Score of 9/10. In this example: Ad C has the highest Quality Score, indicating that Google perceives it as highly relevant and valuable to users searching for "running shoes." Ad A has a decent Quality Score, suggesting good relevance but with room for improvement. Ad B has a lower Quality Score, indicating potential issues with relevance or user experience.
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: You can use the Quality Score diagnostic tool to identify where it might be beneficial to improve your ads, landing pages, or keyword selection.
nodes.questions: 1. How does our e-commerce campaigns' average Quality Score compare to industry standards, and are there any campaigns or product categories that have higher or lower Quality Scores? 2. What is the relationship between Quality Score and KPIs such as CTR, conversion rate, and ROAS? How can we use this information to improve our campaigns' performance? 3. Are there any patterns or trends in Quality Score changes over time, and how can we use these insights to optimize our campaigns, including ad copy, landing page, and keyword adjustments? 4. What effect does Quality Score have on ad position and CPC? Can we identify cases where a higher Quality Score leads to better ad placements and more cost-effective clicks? 5. How does Quality Score vary across different devices (desktop, mobile, tablet), and what strategies can we use to maintain a high Quality Score on all platforms? 6. Can we identify any keywords or ad groups with low Quality Scores, and what targeted improvements can we make to increase the relevance and quality of our ads in these areas? 7. Are there any components of the Quality Score algorithm, such as ad relevance, expected CTR, or landing page experience, where our campaigns are particularly successful or have difficulties?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Low Ad Relevance. If the content of your ad is not closely aligned with the user's search intent, it can result in a lower Quality Score. Regularly review and optimize ad copy to ensure relevance to targeted keywords. 🔹 Poor Landing Page Experience. An unoptimized or irrelevant landing page can contribute to a lower Quality Score. Ensure that landing pages provide a seamless and valuable experience for users, aligning with ad content. 🔹 Keyword Relevance and Match Type. Irrelevant or broad keyword selection, or mismatches with the chosen match type, may impact Quality Score negatively. Choose relevant keywords and use appropriate match types to improve alignment. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Historical Click-Through Rate (CTR). A history of low CTR can signal to Google that your ads are not resonating with users. Improve ad creatives, targeting, and relevance to boost CTR. 🔹 Ad Format and Extensions. The use of inappropriate ad formats or neglecting ad extensions may affect Quality Score. Ensure that ad formats and extensions enhance the overall ad experience. 🔹 Mobile Optimization. With an increasing number of users on mobile devices, an unoptimized mobile experience can impact Quality Score. Ensure mobile-friendly ads and landing pages. 🔹 Ad Bid Competitiveness. If your bid is significantly lower than competitors, it may impact ad position and, consequently, Quality Score. Evaluate bid competitiveness within the industry. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Ad Performance on the Display Network. If your ads are part of the Google Display Network and are not performing well, it can affect Quality Score. Optimize ad creatives for the display context. 🔹 Changes in Search Trends or User Behavior. Shifts in user behavior or changes in search trends may influence the relevance of certain keywords, affecting Quality Score. Stay informed about evolving trends. 🔹 Ad Creative Innovation. Lack of innovation in ad creatives may contribute to ad fatigue and lower engagement, impacting Quality Score over time. Regularly refresh and test ad creatives.
nodes.slices: '- Campaign or Ad Group - Time Period - Device Type - Network (Search vs. Display) - Geo

nodes.description: An estimated amount of money for your average daily budget
nodes.formula: Let's say you have a campaign with highly competitive keywords. Based on historical data and auction dynamics, Google recommends an average daily budget of $100 to achieve optimal exposure and minimize lost impression share due to budget constraints. Choosing a budget lower than this recommendation might result in fewer impressions and a lower ad share throughout the day.
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: The Recommended Average Daily Budget metric helps advertisers understand the minimum budget required to avoid losing impressions due to budget constraints. Adhering to or adjusting your budget based on this recommendation can enhance your ad performance, ensuring that your ads appear more consistently and potentially improving your overall campaign effectiveness.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the current average daily budget compare to the recommended average daily budget across our e-commerce campaigns, and are there any campaigns or product categories where the recommended budget is notably higher or lower? 2. What is the relationship between the average daily budget and key performance indicators (KPIs) such as impression share, click-through rate (CTR), and conversion rate? How can changes to the budget affect overall campaign performance? 3. Are there certain times or days of the week where the recommended average daily budget is usually higher, suggesting potential opportunities for budget adjustments to get more impressions during peak times? 4. How does the recommended average daily budget vary across different devices (desktop, mobile, tablet)? Are there insights that can guide device-specific budget allocations to maximize ad visibility? 5. What is the connection between the recommended average daily budget and ad position? Can we optimize budgets to make sure our ads appear in better positions on the search results page? 6. Are there particular keywords or ad groups where the recommended average daily budget is significantly affecting impression share due to budget limitations? How can we strategically allocate budget to important areas of our campaigns? 7. How does seasonality affect the recommended average daily budget, and are there historical patterns that can inform budget adjustments during peak sales periods or promotional events?
nodes.slices: - Keyword-Level - Time Period - Campaign Type - Geo - Device Type - Seasonal or Event-Based Trends

What strategies can be implemented to improve Relative CTR compared to competitors?

nodes.description: Average CPM you set manually
nodes.formula: Let's say you set a Target CPM (tCPM) of $5.00 for your Google Ads campaign. Google Ads will then try to optimize your bids to ensure that, on average, you don't exceed $5.00 for every one thousand impressions. Example Calculation: Impressions: 100,000 Cost: $500.00 Average CPM = Cost / (Impressions / 1000) Average CPM = $500.00 / (100,000 / 1000) Average CPM = $5.00
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking the Target Cost-Per-Thousand Impressions (tCPM) in Google Ads is important because it allows advertisers to control and optimize the cost of impressions for their campaign. By setting a specific tCPM, advertisers can manage their budget efficiently, gain unique reach, and ensure that the average CPM aligns with their cost objectives. Monitoring this metric provides insights into the effectiveness of bid strategies, helping advertisers make informed decisions to achieve their campaign goals while maintaining cost control.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the actual CPM compare to the target cost-per-thousand impressions (tCPM) across our ad campaigns, and are there any campaigns or audience segments where the actual CPM differs from the target? 2. Can we analyze the relationship between the tCPM and KPIs such as CTR, conversion rate, and ROAS to determine if the bidding strategy meets our e-commerce goals? 3. Are there patterns or trends in the difference between the tCPM and actual CPM at certain times of the day, days of the week, or seasons? How can we adjust the tCPM bid strategy to align with these patterns? 4. What effect does ad creative and messaging have on the performance of tCPM bidding? Can we identify trends in ad performance that suggest opportunities for creative optimization to improve the bidding strategy? 5. How does the tCPM bidding strategy affect the distribution of ad impressions across different devices? Are there device-specific insights that can inform changes to the tCPM bid for better reach? 6. Can we evaluate the historical performance of tCPM bidding during promotional events or sales periods? Are there strategies or adjustments that can be implemented to maximize the impact of tCPM bidding during peak sales times? 7. What role does audience targeting play in the effectiveness of tCPM bidding? Are there audience segments where tCPM performs particularly well or areas for improvement in targeting to enhance the bidding strategy?

nodes.description: Estimated CPC bid
nodes.formula: Let's say you have a keyword, "digital cameras," and the estimated Top of Page Bid is $3.00. In this example, the estimated Top of Page Bid of $3.00 means that, to have a higher likelihood of your ad appearing at the top of the first page of search results for the keyword "digital cameras," you should set your cost-per-click (CPC) bid at $3.00 or higher. This bid reflects the competition and the expected cost to secure a top position. Adjusting your bid in line with or above this estimate can increase the visibility of your ad in the prime location on the search results page.
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking the Top of Page Bid Estimate is crucial for advertisers to understand the competitive landscape and optimize their bidding strategy. This metric provides insights into the estimated CPC bid needed for prime ad placement at the top of search results. By monitoring this estimate, advertisers can make informed decisions about adjusting their bids to enhance the visibility and positioning of their ads, ultimately improving the likelihood of reaching their target audience and achieving campaign objectives.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the actual CPC bid compare to the top of page bid estimate across our various keywords and ad groups? Are there any keywords or product categories where the actual CPC differs from the estimated bid for the top of the page? 2. Can we analyze the effect of the top of page bid estimate on key performance indicators (KPIs) such as click-through rate (CTR), conversion rate, and return on ad spend (ROAS)? 3. Are there any patterns or trends in the difference between the top of page bid estimate and actual CPC during certain times of the day, days of the week, or seasons? How can we adjust our bidding strategy to match these patterns and maximize visibility? 4. What role does ad relevance and quality score play in the top of page bid estimate? Can improvements in ad quality increase the chance of achieving top-of-page placement without increasing the bid too much? 5. How does competitor activity and bid landscape influence the top of page bid estimate? Are there strategies we can use to maintain or improve our ad position without overbidding? 6. Can we evaluate the historical performance of top-of-page bids during promotional events or sales periods? Are there specific strategies or bid adjustments that can be used to ensure optimal visibility and performance during peak sales times? 7. What impact does device targeting have on the top of page bid estimate, and are there device-specific insights that can help us adjust the bidding strategy for achieving top-of-page placement?

nodes.description: The sum of conversion values
nodes.formula: Let's say you have an e-commerce website, and you're tracking two types of conversions: "Purchase" and "Sign-up." For the "Purchase" conversion, a customer buys a product worth $50, and the conversion value assigned is $50. For the "Sign-up" conversion, a user signs up for a newsletter, and the conversion value assigned is $5. Total Conversion Value = Sum of Conversion Values Total Conversion Value = $50 (Purchase) + $5 (Sign-up) Total Conversion Value = $55
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Conversion values help you measure and optimize the true business impact of your ad campaigns more accurately. If you assign values to your conversions, you'll be able to learn the total value driven by your advertising across different conversions, rather than simply the number of conversions that have happened. You'll also be able to identify and focus on high-value conversions.
nodes.questions: 1. How do different types of conversions (e.g. purchases, sign-ups, etc.) affect the Total Conversion Value? Are there any conversion types that have a particularly large impact on the Total Conversion Value, and how can this be used to optimize campaigns? 2. Are there any trends or patterns in the Total Conversion Value over time, such as monthly or quarterly fluctuations? Are there any times of the year where the Total Conversion Value changes significantly, and how can this be used to plan for the future? 3. Which products or services have the biggest impact on the Total Conversion Value? 4. How does the Total Conversion Value compare to the total advertising spend? 5. Are there any regional variations in the Total Conversion Value? 6. How does the Total Conversion Value differ across different devices (desktop, mobile, tablet)? 7. Is there a correlation between the Total Conversion Value and ad position on the search results page?
nodes.slices: - Campaign or Ad Group - Conversion Type - Time Period - Device Type - Geo - Attribution Model

nodes.description: The total number of people who were shown an ad
nodes.formula: For example, if someone sees your ad on their mobile, their desktop, and their tablet, your ad will receive 3 impressions but Unique Reach can tell you that each of those impressions came from a single user (your ad reached a single user 3 different times). If someone sees your ad on their mobile device and then sees it again with another person on a connected TV (CTV) device, your ad will receive 3 impressions (co-viewed) and Unique Reach can tell you that those impressions came from 2 users.
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: The Unique Reach metric goes beyond traditional measurements and helps advertisers understand the reach of their campaigns across various platforms and devices. By knowing the unique number of people reached, advertisers can make more informed decisions about their targeting strategies and optimize campaigns to maximize audience coverage effectively.
nodes.questions: 1. What is the reach of each advertising channel (e.g., display, search, video)? 2. How does Unique Reach vary across different devices (desktop, mobile, tablet)? 3. Are there any differences in Unique Reach based on geographic locations? 4. Are there any changes in Unique Reach at different times of the day? 5. How does the type of ad format affect Unique Reach? 6. Is there a pattern in ad exposure, and how does it influence Unique Reach?

nodes.description: The number of ad clicks collected from unknown traffic to the publisher site.
nodes.formula: Suppose a publisher's website receives 1,000 ad clicks in a given period. Out of these clicks, 600 are definitively identified as organic, 300 are identified as purchased, and the remaining 100 cannot definitively be categorized as either organic or purchased. The calculation for Unknown Clicks would be: Unknown Clicks = Total Clicks - (Organic Clicks + Purchased Clicks) Unknown Clicks = 1,000 - (600 + 300) Unknown Clicks = 1,000 - 900 Unknown Clicks = 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking Unknown Clicks in a Google ad account is important because it helps publishers understand the nature of clicks that cannot be definitively categorized as either organic or purchased. This metric can provide insights into the source and authenticity of website traffic, enabling publishers to identify potential areas for improvement in click attribution and ad performance. By gaining clarity on the origin of unknown clicks, publishers can make informed decisions to optimize their ad strategies and enhance the overall quality of user engagement on their site.
nodes.questions: 1. How do Unknown Clicks compare to organic and purchased clicks? 2. Are there variations in conversion rates between Unknown Clicks and identified sources? 3. What is the geographic spread of Unknown Clicks? 4. Are there differences in Unknown Clicks across different devices? 5. Are there particular times of the day when Unknown Clicks are more frequent? 6. Does the type of content or ad creative affect the number of Unknown Clicks? 7. What landing pages do Unknown Clicks lead to?

nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking the View Rate in a Google ad account is important as it provides insights into the effectiveness of video ads. This metric indicates the ratio of paid views to eligible impressions, helping advertisers assess the engagement level of their video content. By understanding the View Rate, advertisers can make informed decisions about the performance of their video ads, optimize content, and refine targeting strategies to enhance overall campaign success.
nodes.questions: 1. What is the impact of video ad content or themes on View Rate? 2. Are there any significant View Rate variations when segmented by audience demographics, interests, or behaviors? 3. Is there a difference in View Rate across different devices (desktop, mobile, tablet)? 4. Does the placement of video ads (e.g., YouTube, Display Network) affect the View Rate? 5. How does the View Rate change over the course of a video ad campaign? 6. Are there any regional variations in View Rate? 7. Is there a correlation between View Rate and audience retention throughout the video?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Unoptimized Targeting. Inaccurate targeting can result in impressions being shown to an audience less likely to engage with the content, leading to a lower view rate. Refine targeting parameters to reach a more relevant audience. 🔹 Low-Quality Video Content. If the video content is uninteresting, unclear, or of poor quality, viewers are less likely to engage, contributing to a decreased view rate. Invest in high-quality, compelling video content. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Ad Relevance to Audience. Irrelevant ad content to the target audience may lead to disengagement, impacting the view rate. Ensure that video content is aligned with the preferences and interests of the target audience. 🔹 Ad Placement Issues. Poor ad placement choices, such as low-traffic locations, may result in lower impressions and, consequently, a lower view rate. Optimize ad placements for better visibility. 🔹 Ad Creative Length. Overly long or short video ads may impact the view rate. Ensure that video length is suitable for the platform and aligns with user preferences. 🔹 Ad Position and Ad Rank Changes. Changes in ad position and Ad Rank can influence the view rate. Optimize ad relevance and targeting to maintain a competitive position. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Technical Issues with Video Playback. Technical glitches affecting video playback, such as slow load times or buffering, can discourage users from watching the video, contributing to a lower view rate. Regularly test and optimize for technical performance. 🔹 Changes in User Behavior or Preferences. Shifts in user behavior may influence the types of video content users engage with, affecting the view rate. Stay informed about evolving trends and adjust video content accordingly. 🔹 Competitive Ad Environment. Increased competition for ad space can impact the view rate. Evaluate the competitive landscape and adjust bidding or targeting strategies accordingly. 🔹 Ad Creative Innovation. Lack of innovation in video creatives may contribute to ad fatigue and lower engagement, impacting the view rate over time. Regularly refresh and test video creatives.
nodes.slices: - Ad Placement - Device Type - Demographic Segment - Geo - Video Length - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Audience Segment - Ad Creative - Keyword-Level - Cross-Channel Integration - Competitor Benchmarking

nodes.description: The period of time after an impression during which a view-through conversion will be recorded. View-through conversions occur after an ad impression, if the user doesn’t interact with the ad, then later converts. You can set your view-through conversion window when you create or edit conversion actions.
nodes.formula: Time period between impression and conversion
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: The View-through Conversion Window metric allows advertisers to assess the impact of ad impressions on user behavior and conversions, providing valuable insights into the effectiveness of display campaigns. By adjusting the view-through conversion window, advertisers can make informed decisions on the attribution model that aligns with their campaign goals and user interaction patterns.
nodes.questions: 1. What is the most effective length of time for view-through conversions in e-commerce campaigns? 2. How does the view-through conversion window vary for different types of e-commerce campaigns (e.g., product launches, promotions, seasonal campaigns)? 3. Are there any industry-specific trends that affect the view-through conversion window? 4. Is there a difference in the view-through conversion window across different devices (desktop, mobile, tablet)? 5. How does the type of ad content affect the view-through conversion window? 6. Are there any regional variations in the view-through conversion window? 7. How does the view-through conversion window vary depending on where the ads are placed (e.g., Display Network, YouTube)?
nodes.slices: - Time Period - Ad Placement - Device Type - Geo - Campaign or Ad Group - Ad Creative - Audience Segment - Cross-Channel Integration - Frequency - Seasonal or Event-Based Trends - Competitor Benchmarking

nodes.description: “View-through conversions” tells you when customers see, but don’t interact with your ad, and then later complete a conversion on your site. View-through conversions are a helpful way to track the value of your display or video ad campaigns. For display campaigns, for example, they measure the conversions where a customer saw—but didn't click—an ad before completing a conversion.
nodes.formula: The number of view-through conversions
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: VTC helps you understand the ad's influence on conversions even if there was no direct interaction. Tracking VTC is essential for fine-tuning your ad strategy to maximize its impact on customer behavior and drive more conversions.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the inclusion of view-through conversions affect the attribution of conversions to different touchpoints? 2. What is the average time between an ad view and a conversion? 3. What ad creatives tend to generate the most view-through conversions? 4. How does the purpose of a campaign (e.g., awareness, promotion) influence view-through conversions? 5. What is the view-through conversion rate compared to click-through conversion rates? 6. How often do users switch devices between viewing an ad and completing a view-through conversion? 7. Does increasing ad frequency eventually lead to fewer view-through conversions?
nodes.slices: - Attribution Model - Time Period - Ad Format - Frequency - Ad Creative - Audience Segment - Device Type - Competitor Benchmarking - Seasonal or Event-Based Trends - Custom Conversion Windows - Ad Placement - Content Type

nodes.description: The count of unique users who have successfully completed the activation process, such as creating an account or performing an initial login.
nodes.formula: Number of Activated Users
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric helps identify the success of the activation process, enabling businesses to gauge the efficiency of their user acquisition efforts and make informed decisions to improve the overall user experience, marketing campaigns, and retention strategies based on the data gathered from user activations.
nodes.questions: 1. What is the trend in Activated Users over the past six months, and are there any notable spikes or declines? 2. How does the distribution of Activated Users vary across different marketing channels? 3. Can you identify any patterns or trends in the time it takes for users to complete the activation process? 4. What impact does the activation process have on subsequent user engagement and conversion rates? 5. Are there specific demographics or user segments that show a higher or lower rate of activation? 6. How does the activation rate differ between new users and returning users? 7. Is there a correlation between the number of steps in the activation process and the dropout rate?
nodes.slices: - Activation Time - Referral Source - Geo - User Type - Device Type

nodes.description: The percentage of total users who have successfully completed the activation process relative to the total user base.
nodes.formula: (Activated Users / Total Users) * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking the Activation Rate is essential for e-commerce stores as it provides a clear measure of the effectiveness of onboarding processes and user engagement strategies. A high Activation Rate indicates a successful conversion of users into active participants, while a low rate may suggest areas for improvement in the onboarding experience.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the Activation Rate vary during promotional periods, and what factors contribute to these fluctuations? 2. Are there specific user segments that consistently exhibit higher or lower activation rates? 3. What impact does the complexity of the activation process have on the overall Activation Rate? 4. Can you identify any correlation between the Activation Rate and customer satisfaction scores? 5. How does the Activation Rate compare across different devices and platforms? 6. What strategies can be implemented to improve the Activation Rate based on historical data? 7. Are there any external factors, such as industry trends, that influence the Activation Rate?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Slow Onboarding Process. Lengthy or slow onboarding processes may discourage users from completing activation. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Technical Issues. Glitches or errors during the activation process can hinder successful completion. 🔹 Lack of User Guidance. Insufficient guidance may result in users being unsure of how to activate their accounts. 🔹 Poor Communication. Inadequate communication about activation benefits or value may lead to lower engagement. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Limited Device Compatibility. Activation difficulties on certain devices may impact the overall activation rate. 🔹 Invasive Permissions Requests. Excessive requests for permissions during activation can lead to user discomfort and a lower completion rate. 🔹 Lengthy Email Verification Processes. Complex email verification steps may contribute to user fatigue and a decline in activation rates. 🔹 Low Motivation to Activate. If users see little value in completing the activation process, they may postpone or skip it altogether.
nodes.slices: - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Traffic Source - Audience Segment - Device Type - Campaign or Ad Group

tactics.tactic: A/B Testing Variations of Activation Pages

nodes.description: The number of distinct users who visited your website or application. An active user is any user who has an engaged session or when Analytics collects: the first_visit event or engagement_time_msec parameter from a website the first_open event or engagement_time_msec parameter from an Android app the first_open or user_engagement event from an iOS app
nodes.formula: Number of Active Users
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: By monitoring this metric, businesses can understand user behavior, identify popular features or content, and optimize marketing efforts. This information aids in decision-making related to user experience enhancements, targeted promotions, and overall strategies to retain and grow the user base.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the number of Active Users correlate with the frequency of product updates or new feature releases? 2. What is the average session duration for Active Users, and are there outliers that indicate particularly engaged users? 3. Can you identify any geographical patterns in the distribution of Active Users? 4. How do different traffic sources contribute to the pool of Active Users, and which sources are most effective? 5. Are there specific times of the day or days of the week when Active Users peak or dip? 6. What is the correlation between user engagement metrics and the overall number of Active Users? 7. How do Active Users relate to conversion rates, and are there opportunities to optimize this relationship?
nodes.slices: - Geo - User Behavior - Device Type - Marketing Channel - Loyalty Program Level

nodes.description: The mean number of users actively using the app or website simultaneously during a specific time period.
nodes.formula: Sum of Concurrent Users at each time point / Number of time points
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric helps businesses make informed decisions about server capacity, infrastructure scalability, and resource allocation to ensure a seamless user experience, especially during high-traffic periods. Monitoring ACU allows e-commerce platforms to optimize their technology infrastructure to meet user demand and avoid performance issues that could impact customer satisfaction and sales.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the ACU fluctuate during peak and off-peak hours, and what insights can be gained from these usage patterns? 2. Are there specific features or events that consistently lead to a higher ACU, and how can these be leveraged for growth? 3. Can you identify any correlation between marketing campaigns and spikes in ACU, indicating campaign effectiveness? 4. What is the impact of server performance on the ACU, and how can infrastructure adjustments optimize the user experience? 5. Are there geographical differences in ACU, and how can localization efforts enhance user engagement in specific regions? 6. How does the ACU vary between different device types, and are there opportunities for platform-specific optimizations? 7. What is the relationship between user demographics and the average concurrent usage of the app or website?
nodes.slices: - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Geo - Device Type - Traffic Source - Audience Segment

nodes.description: The number of times users added items to their shopping carts.
nodes.formula: Number of times users added to cart
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: Monitoring this metric helps businesses understand which products are attracting interest and allows for optimization of the shopping experience. By analyzing patterns in "Add to Cart" actions, e-commerce platforms can make informed decisions on inventory management, pricing strategies, and website design to enhance the overall conversion rate and improve sales.
nodes.questions: 1. What types of products are most frequently added to the shopping cart, and how can this information guide inventory management? 2. How does the frequency of 'Add to Cart' events correlate with overall conversion rates and subsequent purchases? 3. Are there seasonal trends or events that significantly impact the number of items added to the shopping cart? 4. Do users who add items to their cart exhibit different browsing behavior than those who do not, and how can this inform UX? 5. What is the average value of items in the shopping cart, and are there opportunities to encourage users to increase this value? 6. How does the 'Add to Cart' metric differ between new users and returning users, and what strategies can be tailored accordingly? 7. Can you identify any patterns in user behavior leading up to an 'Add to Cart' action, indicating potential upselling opportunities?
nodes.slices: - Product Category - User Type - Traffic Source - Promotion Type - Device Type

nodes.description: The percentage of sessions where users have added items to the shopping cart, relative to the total number of sessions.
nodes.formula: (Sessions with item added to cart / Total Sessions) * 100
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: A high Add to Cart rate indicates effective product appeal and website usability, while a low rate may suggest areas for improvement. This metric aids in decision-making by helping businesses optimize product displays, marketing strategies, and overall user experience to increase the likelihood of converting browsing sessions into actual sales.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the 'Add to Cart' rate change during limited-time promotions or sales events, and what factors influence these changes? 2. Are there specific product categories that consistently have higher or lower 'Add to Cart' rates, and what marketing strategies can be applied? 3. How does the 'Add to Cart' rate vary across different traffic sources, and which sources contribute most to this metric? 4. What impact do changes in website or app design have on the 'Add to Cart' rate, and how can user experience enhancements optimize this metric? 5. Are there external factors, such as industry trends or competitor actions, that influence the 'Add to Cart' rate? 6. How does the 'Add to Cart' rate differ between mobile and desktop users, and what responsive design improvements can be made? 7. What is the relationship between the 'Add to Cart' rate and user reviews or ratings of products?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Slow Website Loading. A slow website can frustrate users, impacting their willingness to add items to the cart. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Unintuitive User Interface. Confusing interfaces may make it difficult for users to find and use the "Add to Cart" option. 🔹 Limited Product Information. Incomplete or unclear product details may discourage users from adding items to their cart. 🔹 Technical Glitches. Technical issues during the add-to-cart process can result in a lower conversion rate. 🔹 Unexpected Costs. Additional costs revealed during the checkout process may deter users from adding items to the cart. 🔹 Lack of Trust in Payment Security. Users may hesitate to add items to the cart if they perceive the payment process as insecure. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Limited Social Proof. A lack of reviews or social proof regarding product popularity may lead to a decline in add-to-cart rates. 🔹 Overwhelming Product Choices. An excessive number of product options can overwhelm users, reducing the likelihood of adding items to the cart.
nodes.slices: - Marketing Channel - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Product Category - Audience Segment - Device Type

tactics.tactic: One-Click Add to Cart for Returning Users

nodes.description: The total instances where users have marked products as favorites or added them to a wishlist.
nodes.formula: Number of times users added to favorite
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Monitoring the "Add to Favorite" metric is essential for e-commerce stores because it offers insights into customer preferences and potential buying intentions. By tracking this metric, businesses can tailor marketing strategies, provide personalized recommendations, and optimize inventory management to improve customer satisfaction and increase sales.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the 'Add to Favorite' metric correlate with long-term user engagement, and what strategies can be employed to boost this correlation? 2. Are there specific demographics or user segments that are more likely to use the 'Add to Favorite' feature, and how can targeted marketing capitalize on this information? 3. What impact does the availability of discounts or promotions have on the frequency of users adding items to their favorites? 4. How does the 'Add to Favorite' metric change during product launches or feature updates, and what insights can be derived from these fluctuations? 5. Can you identify any correlation between the 'Add to Favorite' metric and subsequent purchases, indicating potential cross-selling opportunities? 6. Are there patterns in user behavior leading up to an 'Add to Favorite' action, and how can this information guide user experience improvements? 7. How does the 'Add to Favorite' metric differ between users who have connected social media accounts and those who haven't?
nodes.slices: - Product Category - Loyalty Program Level - Promotional Periods - Seasonal or Event-Based Trends - Device Type

nodes.description: The percentage of sessions where users have added items to their favorites, relative to the total number of sessions.
nodes.formula: (Sessions where an item was added to favorites / Total Sessions) * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: A high Add to Favorite rate suggests that customers find products appealing, providing insights into popular items. This metric informs decision-making by helping businesses tailor marketing strategies, optimize product placement, and enhance the overall user experience to increase customer satisfaction and drive sales.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the 'Add to Favorite' rate vary across different product categories, and what strategies can be implemented to increase this rate for specific categories? 2. Are there external events or trends that significantly impact the 'Add to Favorite' rate, and how can marketing efforts align with these trends? 3. How does the 'Add to Favorite' rate differ between new users and users who have been using the platform for an extended period? 4. What role does user engagement (e.g., time spent on the platform) play in influencing the 'Add to Favorite' rate? 5. How does the 'Add to Favorite' rate change in response to changes in the user interface or app design, and what improvements can be made? 6. Can you identify any patterns in the timing of 'Add to Favorite' actions, and how can this information inform push notification strategies? 7. What is the impact of personalized recommendations on the 'Add to Favorite' rate, and how can recommendation algorithms be optimized?
nodes.causes: 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Poor Visibility of Favorites Feature. If the "Add to Favorites" option is not prominent, users may overlook it, reducing the add to favorite rate. 🔹 Lack of Incentives. Absence of incentives, such as personalized offers, may decrease motivation to add items to favorites. 🔹 Unresponsive or Slow Interaction. Slow or unresponsive interactions can frustrate users and reduce the likelihood of adding items to favorites. 🔹 Limited Product Variety. A narrow product selection may lead to fewer opportunities for users to add items to their favorites. 🔹 Complex Wishlist Management. Difficulty in managing or accessing the wishlist may discourage users from adding items to favorites. 🔹 Limited Personalization. Lack of personalized recommendations in the wishlist may reduce its attractiveness to users. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Insufficient Social Sharing Options. If users cannot easily share their favorite items on social media, the add-to-favorite rate may decrease. 🔹 Unattractive Wishlist Design. An unappealing or poorly designed wishlist interface may deter users from utilizing the feature.
nodes.slices: - Audience Segment - Device Type - Traffic Source - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Product Attributes

nodes.description: The average number of times users add products to their shopping cart in a single session.
nodes.formula: (Add to Cart / Number of Sessions)
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking the "Adds to Cart per Session" metric is important for e-commerce stores as it provides insights into user engagement and the effectiveness of the shopping experience. A higher average indicates strong interest in products, helping businesses tailor marketing strategies and optimize website features to encourage more conversions.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the 'Adds to Cart per Session' metric differ between users accessing the platform via mobile apps and those using a web browser? 2. Are there specific user journeys or pathways through the app or website that lead to a higher average number of 'Adds to Cart per Session'? 3. What is the impact of user onboarding processes on the 'Adds to Cart per Session' metric, and how can onboarding be optimized for higher engagement? 4. How does the 'Adds to Cart per Session' metric change during promotional periods, and what promotional strategies are most effective in driving this metric? 5. Can you identify any correlation between the 'Adds to Cart per Session' metric and customer satisfaction scores or feedback? 6. How does the time of day or day of the week influence the 'Adds to Cart per Session' metric, and how can this information inform marketing schedules? 7. What is the relationship between the 'Adds to Cart per Session' metric and user demographics, and how can this information guide targeted advertising?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Poor Website Navigation. If users find it difficult to navigate, they may be less likely to add items to their cart. 🔹 Slow Page Load Times. Slow-loading pages can frustrate users, leading to decreased engagement and cart additions. 🔹 Unoptimized Mobile Experience. A subpar mobile experience can discourage users from adding products to their carts. 🔹 Complicated Checkout Process. A complex or lengthy checkout process can deter users, impacting the number of items added to the cart. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Misleading Product Descriptions. Inaccurate product descriptions can lead to cart abandonment as users may expect something different. 🔹 Limited Product Availability. Insufficient stock or unavailability may hinder users from adding items to their carts. 🔹 Ineffective Call-to-Action Buttons. Poorly designed or unclear CTA buttons may result in lower interaction and reduced cart additions. 🔹 Lack of Personalization. Users are more likely to engage with personalized recommendations, and a lack of personalization may decrease cart additions.
nodes.slices: - Audience Segment - Product Category - Traffic Source - Promotion Periods - Device Type

tactics.tactic: Seamless One-Click Add to Cart for Returning Users
Countdown Timer for Limited Stock Items

nodes.description: The average number of times users add products to their favorites or wishlist in a single session.
nodes.formula: (Add to Favorite / Number of Sessions)
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: By monitoring the "Adds to Favorite per Session" metric, e-commerce stores can gain specific insights into which products users find most appealing or wishlist-worthy during a single session. A higher average in this metric suggests that users are actively expressing interest in products, allowing businesses to focus marketing efforts on those items, provide targeted promotions, and enhance the overall shopping experience.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the 'Adds to Favorite per Session' metric change based on the frequency of content updates or new product releases? 2. Are there specific user segments that consistently exhibit a higher average number of 'Adds to Favorite per Session'? 3. What role does the ease of use of the 'Add to Favorite' feature play in influencing the 'Adds to Favorite per Session' metric? 4. How does the 'Adds to Favorite per Session' metric differ between users who have completed the activation process and those who haven't? 5. Can you identify any correlation between the 'Adds to Favorite per Session' metric and the overall session duration? 6. What impact do limited-time promotions or exclusive offers have on the 'Adds to Favorite per Session' metric? 7. How does the 'Adds to Favorite per Session' metric vary between users who access the platform via different devices?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Limited Product Visibility. If key products are not prominently displayed, users may not discover or add them to their favorites. 🔹 Cumbersome Wishlist Management. A complicated or unfriendly interface for managing wishlists may discourage users from adding items to favorites. 🔹 Technical Glitches in Adding to Favorites. Technical issues such as slow response times or errors during the favorite addition process can frustrate users and reduce engagement. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Ineffective Product Recommendations. Poorly curated or irrelevant product recommendations can reduce user interest in adding items to their favorites. 🔹 Lack of Incentives for Wishlist Activity. Users may be less motivated to add products to their favorites if there are no rewards, discounts, or personalized incentives. 🔹 Uninspiring Product Descriptions. Bland or uninformative product descriptions may fail to capture user interest, leading to fewer additions to the favorites list. 🔹 Insufficient Social Integration. Users often enjoy sharing their favorite products on social media. If the integration is lacking, it may impact the desire to add items to favorites. 🔹 Limited Wishlist Customization Options. Users appreciate the ability to organize and customize wishlists. A lack of such features may result in decreased favorite additions.
nodes.slices: - Audience Segment - Product Category - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Promotion Periods - Device Type

tactics.tactic: Seamless One-Click Wishlist for Returning Users
Countdown Timer for Limited Wishlist Items

nodes.id: App Page Views from Organic Sources
nodes.description: The count of views on app page in the app store originating from organic (non-paid) sources.
nodes.formula: Number of App Page Views from Organic Sources
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric provides insights into the app's organic visibility, user engagement, and the effectiveness of its app store presence. By monitoring these views, businesses can make informed decisions about optimizing their app store listings, enhancing discoverability, and improving the overall organic performance of the app.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the number of App Page Views from Organic Sources correlate with changes in app store optimization strategies? 2. Are there specific keywords or phrases that consistently drive organic traffic to the app page, and how can these be leveraged for greater visibility? 3. How do changes in the app store algorithms or policies impact the number of App Page Views from Organic Sources? 4. Can you identify any seasonal or event-based patterns in the organic visibility of the app page, and how can marketing efforts align with these patterns? 5. What is the correlation between the quality of app store reviews and the number of App Page Views from Organic Sources? 6. How does the geographic location of users influence the number of App Page Views from Organic Sources, and are there opportunities for localized optimization? 7. Are there specific features or content on the app page that consistently attract organic traffic, and how can these be emphasized for greater user engagement?
nodes.slices: - Demographic Segment - Geo - App Version - Competitor Benchmarking - Seasonal or Event-Based Trends

nodes.id: App Page Views from Paid Sources
nodes.description: The count of views on app page in the app store originating from paid sources.
nodes.formula: Number of App Page Views from Paid Sources
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric provides insights into the return on investment from advertising campaigns, allowing businesses to make informed decisions about the allocation of marketing budgets, optimization of paid strategies, and overall enhancement of app store visibility to drive user acquisition.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the number of App Page Views from Paid Sources correlate with changes in the allocated budget for paid marketing campaigns? 2. Are there specific keywords or ad creatives that consistently drive paid traffic to the app page, and how can these be optimized for better results? 3. How does the geographic targeting of paid campaigns influence the distribution of App Page Views from Paid Sources, and are there opportunities for localization? 4. Can you identify any seasonality or event-driven patterns in the effectiveness of paid sources in driving app page views? 5. What is the correlation between the conversion rate of paid sources and the number of App Page Views from Paid Sources? 6. How does the App Page Views from Paid Sources metric differ between different app versions, and how can this inform version-specific marketing strategies? 7. What impact does the timing and frequency of paid campaigns have on the App Page Views from Paid Sources, and how can campaign schedules be optimized?
nodes.slices: - Ad Platforms - Audience Segment - Keyword Effectiveness - Conversion Funnel Stage

nodes.description: The total number of reviews provided by users on the app.
nodes.formula: Number of App Reviews
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking "App Reviews" is important for e-commerce stores as it offers direct feedback from users, providing insights into their satisfaction, concerns, and overall app experience. This metric helps businesses make informed decisions about app improvements, feature enhancements, and customer service adjustments, ultimately contributing to the optimization of user experience and the overall success of the e-commerce app.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the sentiment expressed in App Reviews correlate with changes in app features, updates, or customer support interactions? 2. Are there specific app versions or releases that consistently receive higher or lower numbers of reviews, and what factors contribute to these patterns? 3. Can you identify any correlation between the timing of user surveys or feedback requests and the number of App Reviews received? 4. How do App Reviews differ between users who have recently completed the activation process and those who have been using the app for an extended period? 5. What is the impact of personalized responses to user reviews on the overall number of App Reviews and user satisfaction? 6. How does the number of App Reviews change during limited-time promotions or exclusive offers, and what insights can be gained from these fluctuations? 7. Can you identify any correlation between the number of App Reviews and user engagement metrics, such as session duration and frequency?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Unresolved Technical Issues. Frequent technical problems in the app may frustrate users, discouraging them from leaving positive reviews. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Lack of Incentives. Users may not be motivated to leave reviews if there are no incentives, such as discounts, rewards, or recognition. 🔹 Cumbersome Review Process. A complicated or time-consuming review process may discourage users from providing feedback. 🔹 Limited User Engagement. Users who are not actively engaged with the app may be less inclined to leave reviews about their experiences. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Absence of Prompting. If users are not prompted or reminded to leave a review, they may forget or choose not to provide feedback. 🔹 Fear of Privacy Invasion. Concerns about how personal information will be used may lead users to refrain from leaving reviews. 🔹 Limited Social Integration. Lack of seamless social media integration may result in fewer users sharing their experiences through reviews. 🔹 Competitor Loyalty. Users loyal to competitors' apps may be less likely to try, engage with, or positively review a new app. 🔹 Short Time Since Launch. Newly launched apps may have fewer reviews simply due to the short period available for users to provide feedback.
nodes.slices: - App Version - Feature Usage - Audience Segment - Competitor Benchmarking

nodes.description: A ratio indicating how well an app retains users, expressed as the proportion of DAU to MAU (Daily Active Users to Monthly Active Users).
nodes.formula: (DAU / MAU) * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: A higher stickiness ratio (DAU to MAU) indicates a more engaged user base, while a lower ratio may signal potential challenges in retaining users. This metric helps businesses assess the effectiveness of their app's features, content, and overall user experience, guiding decision-making related to user retention strategies, app updates, and marketing efforts to foster long-term user loyalty.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the App Stickiness ratio vary between different user segments, and what strategies can be implemented to improve stickiness for specific segments? 2. Are there specific app features or functionalities that contribute more to or detract from the overall App Stickiness ratio? 3. What role do onboarding processes and tutorials play in influencing App Stickiness, and how can these be optimized for better user retention? 4. How does the App Stickiness ratio change during major app updates or redesigns, and what factors contribute to these changes? 5. Can you identify any correlation between the App Stickiness ratio and user demographics, such as age, location, or device type? 6. How do external factors, such as industry trends or changes in user behavior, impact the App Stickiness ratio, and how can the app adapt accordingly? 7. What strategies can be employed to enhance App Stickiness during specific seasons or events, and how does user engagement contribute to stickiness?

nodes.description: The average score assigned by users in the app store or rating system.

Can you identify any correlation between the Average App Rating Score and user engagement metrics, such as session duration and frequency? 4. How does the Average App Rating Score differ between users who have completed the activation process and those who haven't, and what insights can be derived? 5. nodes.description: The average duration (in seconds) of users' sessions.
nodes.formula: Sum of all session durations / Number of Sessions
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Monitoring the "Average Session Duration" is essential for e-commerce stores as it offers insights into user engagement and satisfaction levels on the website. A lengthier average session duration suggests more meaningful interactions, guiding decisions on optimizing content, improving user experience, and implementing strategies to boost engagement for increased conversions and customer contentment.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the Average Session Duration correlate with the type of content users engage with during sessions, and how can content optimization impact this metric? 2. Are there specific entry points or landing pages that result in longer or shorter average session durations, and how can these entry points be enhanced or redesigned? 3. Can you identify any correlation between the Average Session Duration and the timing of promotional campaigns or limited-time offers? 4. How do user demographics, such as age or location, influence the Average Session Duration, and what strategies can be implemented to cater to diverse user preferences? 5. What is the impact of app stability and loading times on the Average Session Duration, and how can performance optimizations enhance the overall user experience? 6. How does the Average Session Duration differ between users who access the app through different devices, and how can responsive design improve this metric? 7. What role does personalization play in influencing the Average Session Duration, and how can personalized content recommendations be optimized for longer engagement?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Technical Issues. Frequent technical problems or slow loading times can frustrate users, leading to shorter session durations. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Irrelevant Content. If users find the content irrelevant or unengaging, they may spend less time in each session. 🔹 Poor Navigation. Complex or confusing navigation can result in users spending less time within the app during each session. 🔹 Lack of Personalization. A lack of personalized content may reduce user interest and contribute to shorter session durations. 🔹 Limited Interactive Features. Users may spend less time in the app if there are limited interactive features or engaging activities. 🔹 External Distractions. External factors such as notifications or interruptions may divert users' attention, leading to shorter sessions. 🔹 Seasonal Trends. Seasonal variations in user behavior may impact session duration, especially during holidays or special events. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Device-Specific Issues. Compatibility issues on certain devices may lead to a suboptimal user experience and shorter sessions.
nodes.slices: - Traffic Source - Product Category - Audience Segment - Seasonal or Event-Based Trends - Conversion Funnel Stage - Content Type - Technical Performance

nodes.description: The percentage of sessions that were not engaged sessions. Bounce rate is the inverse of engagement rate.

nodes.description: CEI measures how efficiently your e-commerce website or platform converts visitors into paying customers. It's a composite metric that takes into account various factors affecting conversion rates.
nodes.formula: Total Paid Orders / (Total Users * Views per Session * Average Session Duration) * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: It provides a comprehensive measure of the efficiency in converting visitors into paying customers, considering multiple influencing factors. This metric helps businesses identify areas for improvement in the conversion process, guiding decisions on website optimization, marketing strategies, and user experience enhancements to increase overall conversion rates and drive revenue.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the Conversion Efficiency Index (CEI) change during A/B testing of different website layouts or user interface designs, and what insights can be gained from these tests? 2. Are there specific user segments or demographics that consistently exhibit higher or lower CEI values, and how can targeted marketing strategies be adjusted accordingly? 3. Can you identify any correlation between changes in product pricing or discounts and fluctuations in the Conversion Efficiency Index? 4. How does the CEI vary across different product categories, and what strategies can be implemented to improve conversion efficiency for specific categories? 5. What is the impact of changes in the registration process on the Conversion Efficiency Index, and how can the onboarding process be optimized for better conversions? 6. How do external factors, such as industry trends or economic conditions, influence the Conversion Efficiency Index, and how can marketing strategies adapt to these conditions? 7. What role do customer reviews and ratings play in influencing the Conversion Efficiency Index, and how can positive reviews be leveraged to improve conversions?
nodes.slices: - Audience Segment - Traffic Source - Device Type - Geo - Product Category - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Customer Journey Stage

nodes.id: Conversion from Product Card View to Add to Cart
nodes.description: The percentage of users who viewed a product card and subsequently added the product to the shopping cart.
nodes.formula: (Add to Cart / Items Viewed) * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Monitoring the "Conversion from Product Card View to Add to Cart" is vital for e-commerce stores as it gauges the success of product engagement in transitioning users from viewing a product card to adding items to the cart. This metric offers insights into the effectiveness of product presentation, guiding decisions on product recommendations, marketing approaches, and website optimization to enhance the overall conversion process and increase sales.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the Conversion from Product Card View to Add to Cart metric vary between different product categories, and what factors contribute to these variations? 2. Are there specific product features or details on the product card that consistently lead to higher or lower conversion rates to adding to the cart? 3. Can you identify any correlation between the timing of limited-time promotions or discounts and changes in the Conversion from Product Card View to Add to Cart? 4. How does the Conversion from Product Card View to Add to Cart differ between new users and returning users, and what strategies can be tailored accordingly? 5. What is the impact of user reviews and ratings on the Conversion from Product Card View to Add to Cart, and how can positive reviews be highlighted for better conversions? 6. How do changes in the user interface or app design influence the Conversion from Product Card View to Add to Cart, and what improvements can be made for better user experience? 7. What is the relationship between the Conversion from Product Card View to Add to Cart and the overall user satisfaction scores?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Unattractive Product Presentation. If product details are not compelling or visually appealing on the product card, users may hesitate to click "Add to Cart." 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Insufficient Product Information. If the product card lacks key details or specifications, users may be reluctant to proceed with adding items to the cart. 🔹 Ambiguous Call-to-Action. If the "Add to Cart" button is not clear or prominent, users might overlook or delay clicking, affecting the conversion rate. 🔹 Cumbersome Product Card Navigation. If navigating within the product card is challenging or confusing, users may face difficulties in initiating the checkout process. 🔹 Limited Trust Indicators. Lack of trust elements, such as reviews or security badges, may lead to user hesitancy in taking the next step to add items. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Mobile Responsiveness Issues. If the product card is not optimized for mobile users, it can lead to frustration and a decrease in conversions on mobile devices. 🔹 Slow Loading Product Images. Slow-loading images may impede the user experience, reducing the likelihood of users proceeding to add items to the cart.
nodes.slices: - Promotional Events - Device Type - Product Category - Audience Segment - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Traffic Source - Seasonal or Event-Based Trends - Loyalty Program Level - Cross-Sell and Upsell Opportunities - Social Proof

nodes.id: Conversion from Product Card View to Favorite
nodes.description: The percentage of users who viewed a product card and subsequently added the product to favorites or a wishlist.
nodes.formula: (Add to Favorite / Items Viewed) * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric provides valuable insights into user preferences and interests, guiding decisions on personalized marketing, product recommendations, and website optimization to enhance the overall customer experience and potentially increase sales through targeted promotions.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the Conversion from Product Card View to Favorite vary across different product categories, and what insights can be gained from these category-specific variations? 2. Are there specific user segments or demographics that consistently exhibit higher or lower conversion rates from product card views to adding to favorites? 3. Can you identify any correlation between the availability of discounts or promotions and changes in the Conversion from Product Card View to Favorite? 4. How does the Conversion from Product Card View to Favorite differ between users who have completed the activation process and those who haven't? 5. What role does the ease of use of the 'Add to Favorite' feature play in influencing the Conversion from Product Card View to Favorite, and how can this feature be optimized? 6. How do changes in the user interface or app design impact the Conversion from Product Card View to Favorite, and what improvements can be made for better user experience? 7. What is the correlation between the Conversion from Product Card View to Favorite and user engagement metrics, such as session duration and frequency?
nodes.causes: 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Hidden or Unappealing Favorites Feature. If the "Add to Favorites" option is not easily visible or lacks visual appeal on the product card, users may overlook it. 🔹 Lack of Incentives. Users may be less motivated to add products to their favorites if there are no associated incentives or benefits highlighted. 🔹 Cumbersome Favorite Management. Difficulty in managing or accessing the wishlist on the product card may discourage users from adding products to favorites. 🔹 Ineffective Call-to-Action. If the "Add to Favorites" button lacks clarity or a compelling call-to-action, users may not feel encouraged to use this feature. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Limited Social Sharing Options. If users cannot easily share their favorite items on social media directly from the product card, it may impact engagement. 🔹 Unattractive Wishlist Design. An unappealing or poorly designed wishlist interface on the product card may deter users from utilizing the "Add to Favorites." 🔹 Lack of Prompts or Reminders. Users may forget to add products to their favorites without clear prompts or reminders on the product card.

nodes.description: The percentage of users who completed the registration process relative to the total number of users.
nodes.formula: (Registrations / Total Users) * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric helps businesses understand user engagement levels and can guide decisions related to optimizing the registration experience, implementing targeted marketing campaigns, and improving overall user acquisition strategies to increase the conversion of visitors into registered users.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the Conversion to Registration metric vary between different user demographics, and what strategies can be implemented to improve registration rates for specific segments? 2. Are there specific onboarding processes or tutorials that consistently lead to higher or lower conversion rates to registration, and how can onboarding be optimized accordingly? 3. Can you identify any correlation between changes in the registration process and fluctuations in the Conversion to Registration metric? 4. How does the Conversion to Registration differ between users accessing the app through different devices, and what responsive design improvements can be made for better conversion rates? 5. What impact do limited-time promotions or exclusive offers have on the Conversion to Registration, and how can these promotions be optimized for higher registration rates? 6. How does the Conversion to Registration correlate with user satisfaction scores, and what improvements can be made based on user feedback to enhance the registration experience? 7. What role does the simplicity and clarity of the registration process play in influencing the Conversion to Registration, and how can the process be streamlined for better conversions?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Lengthy or Complicated Registration Process. A complex or time-consuming registration process may lead to user abandonment, reducing the overall conversion rate. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Lack of Incentives for Registration. Users may be less inclined to complete the registration process if there are no clear incentives or benefits offered. 🔹 Concerns About Data Privacy. Users may be reluctant to register if they have concerns about how their personal information will be handled and stored. 🔹 Limited Accessibility to Registration. If the registration process is not easily accessible or well-promoted, users may not be aware of or engaged in registering. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Optional Registration. If the app allows users to explore and use basic features without registering, the conversion rate to registration may be lower. 🔹 Lack of Social Media Integration. Absence of seamless social media integration for registration may result in fewer users signing up through this method. 🔹 User Perceived Lack of Value in Registration. If users do not see clear value or benefits in registering, they may choose to use the app without completing the process.

nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Shift in audience preferences or behavior leading to decreased interaction levels. Changes in what resonates with the audience may result in lower engagement levels, impacting the overall engagement rate. 🔹 Decreased relevance or quality of content. Content that is perceived as less relevant or lower in quality may fail to elicit high levels of engagement, resulting in a decrease in the engagement rate. 🔹 Decreased trust or credibility in brand messaging. Poorly executed or unclear CTAs may reduce user participation, resulting in lower engagement rates across content pieces. 🔹 Technical issues affecting content accessibility or usability.
