nodes.why: Tracking the Event Count in e-commerce is crucial as it provides insights into user interactions and engagement with specific actions on the platform, such as "Add to Cart" or "Product View." Analyzing event counts helps businesses understand user behavior, identify popular features, and optimize the user experience.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the Event Count vary between different user actions or features within the app, and what insights can be gained for prioritizing feature improvements? 2. Are there specific user segments or demographics that trigger a higher or lower number of events, and how can targeted marketing address these variations? 3. Can you identify any correlation between the timing of push notifications or in-app messages and changes in the Event Count? 4. How does the Event Count change during promotional periods, and what strategies are most effective in encouraging users to trigger more events? 5. What is the impact of app stability and loading times on the Event Count, and how can performance optimizations enhance overall event triggering? 6. How do changes in user interface design or app layout impact the Event Count, and what design enhancements can be made for encouraging more user interactions? 7. What is the correlation between the Event Count and user satisfaction scores, and how can user feedback be used to address issues affecting event triggering?
nodes.slices: - Audience Segment - Device Type - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Traffic Source - Geo - Product Category - Loyalty Program Level - Session Depth - Event Type Distribution - OS - Promotion Effectiveness - Content Type - Seasonal or Event-Based Trends - Customer Journey Stage - Demographic Segment

nodes.description: The average number of events triggered per user.
nodes.formula: Event count / Active users
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: A higher Event Count per User suggests increased user activity and interest, indicating a positive user experience. Monitoring this metric helps in optimizing the platform, identifying popular features, and tailoring marketing strategies to enhance overall user satisfaction and drive business growth.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the Event Count per User vary between different user segments or demographics, and what insights can be gained for prioritizing feature improvements? 2. Are there specific features or content types that consistently lead to a higher average Event Count per User, and how can these be emphasized for better user interaction? 3. Can you identify any correlation between the timing and frequency of push notifications or in-app messages and changes in the Event Count per User? 4. How does the Event Count per User differ between users who have completed the activation process and those who haven't? 5. What impact do limited-time promotions or exclusive offers have on the Event Count per User, and how can these promotions be optimized for increased user interaction? 6. How does the Event Count per User vary between different app versions, and what insights can be gained for optimizing marketing efforts for specific versions? 7. What role does personalization play in influencing the Event Count per User metric, and how can personalized content recommendations be optimized for better user interaction?
nodes.slices: - Audience Segment - Device Type - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Traffic Source - Geo - Product Category - Loyalty Program Level - Session Depth - Event Type Distribution - OS - Promotion Effectiveness - Content Type - Seasonal or Event-Based Trends - Customer Journey Stage - Demographic Segment

nodes.description: The sum of all value parameters supplied with an event. You can use this context-sensitive metric to capture data that's important to you (e.g., revenue, time, distance). Event value differs from purchase revenue in that event value combines realized and unrealized values. Realized values come from purchase events, and unrealized values come from other events (e.g., a gem_referral event with an event value of 5.). Event value isn't currency converted.
nodes.formula: Sum of values associated with each tracked event
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Event Values Metrics allows businesses to capture diverse data such as revenue, time, or distance associated with various events, enabling nuanced insights into user behavior and preferences. By considering both realized and unrealized values, businesses can make informed decisions on optimizing engagement strategies, improving user experience, and tailoring marketing efforts based on a holistic view of user interactions.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the Event Values metric vary between different types of user interactions or events within the app, and what insights can be gained for prioritizing value-driven features? 2. Are there specific user segments or demographics that contribute more to the overall Event Values, and how can targeted marketing address these variations? 3. Can you identify any correlation between changes in the app description or promotional materials and fluctuations in the Event Values? 4. How does the Event Values metric differ between users who have completed the activation process and those who haven't? 5. What impact do limited-time promotions or exclusive offers have on the Event Values metric, and how can these promotions be optimized for increased overall value? 6. How does the Event Values metric vary between different app versions, and what insights can be gained for optimizing marketing efforts for specific versions? 7. What role does personalization play in influencing the Event Values metric, and how can personalized content recommendations be optimized for better overall value?
nodes.slices: - Event Type Breakdown - Audience Segment - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Conversion Funnel Stage - Device Type - Geo - Referral Source

nodes.description: The average number of events per session.
nodes.formula: Event Count / Number of Engaged Sessions
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: A higher average number of events per session typically indicates increased user activity, helping businesses assess the effectiveness of features, user experience, and content. This metric guides decisions on optimizing website elements, enhancing user engagement, and tailoring marketing strategies to improve overall website performance and user satisfaction.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the average number of Events per Session change between different types of sessions, and what insights can be gained for tailoring features to specific session types? 2. Are there specific user segments or demographics that trigger a higher or lower average number of events during sessions, and how can targeted marketing address these variations? 3. Can you identify any correlation between the timing and frequency of push notifications or in-app messages and changes in the Events per Session metric? 4. How does the Events per Session metric differ between users who have completed the activation process and those who haven't? 5. What impact do limited-time promotions or exclusive offers have on the Events per Session metric, and how can these promotions be optimized for increased event triggering? 6. How does the Events per Session metric vary between different app versions, and what insights can be gained for optimizing marketing efforts for specific versions? 7. What role does personalization play in influencing the Events per Session metric, and how can personalized content recommendations be optimized for better user interaction during sessions?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Lack of Engaging Features. An absence of engaging features or activities within the app may result in fewer user interactions and lower event counts. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Ineffective User Guidance. Users may not know how to interact with events if there is insufficient guidance or education on their functionalities. 🔹 Technical Issues or Bugs. Frequent technical problems or bugs may disrupt the user experience, leading to a decrease in overall events per session. 🔹 Limited Content Relevance. If the app content is not relevant to users, they may be less likely to engage with events, resulting in lower event counts. 🔹 Unappealing Design. A poorly designed interface or unattractive visuals may discourage users from exploring and interacting with various events. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Insufficient Notifications or Reminders. Lack of notifications or reminders about ongoing events may contribute to lower user engagement and event participation. 🔹 External Distractions. External factors such as notifications from other apps may divert users' attention, reducing their engagement with events.
nodes.slices: - Audience Segment - Device Type - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Geo - Conversion Funnel Stage - Referral Source - Event Type Breakdown - Loyalty Program Level

nodes.description: The total count of times app listings are displayed in the app catalog or store.
nodes.formula: Number of Impressions in App Catalog
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking Impressions in App Catalog is important because it provides insights into the visibility and exposure of your app listings within the store. A higher number of impressions indicates greater visibility, potentially leading to increased awareness and user engagement. Monitoring this metric helps in assessing the effectiveness of your app's placement and optimizing strategies to enhance visibility, ultimately influencing user acquisition and downloads.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the number of Impressions in App Catalog correlate with changes in app store optimization strategies, and what insights can be gained for optimizing visibility in the app catalog? 2. Are there specific keywords or phrases that consistently drive impressions in the app catalog, and how can these be leveraged for greater visibility? 3. How do changes in the app store algorithms or policies impact the number of Impressions in App Catalog, and how can marketing efforts align with these changes? 4. Can you identify any seasonal or event-based patterns in the visibility of app listings in the app catalog, and how can marketing efforts align with these patterns? 5. What is the correlation between the quality of app store reviews and the number of Impressions in App Catalog, and how can positive reviews be leveraged for increased visibility? 6. How does the geographic location of users influence the number of Impressions in App Catalog, and are there opportunities for localized optimization? 7. Are there specific features or content in app listings that consistently attract impressions in the app catalog, and how can these be emphasized for greater visibility?

nodes.description: The number of users who selected a promotion divided by the number of users who viewed the same promotion.
nodes.formula: Items Clicked in Promotion / Promotion Views * 100
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: A higher click-through rate indicates that the promotion successfully captures user interest, providing valuable insights for optimizing promotional strategies, enhancing product placements, and tailoring marketing efforts to increase overall conversion rates and maximize the impact of promotional campaigns.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the Item Promotion Click Through Rate vary between different types of promotions, and what insights can be gained for prioritizing promotion types that drive higher click-through rates? 2. Are there specific user segments or demographics that consistently exhibit higher or lower Item Promotion Click Through Rates, and how can targeted marketing address these variations? 3. Can you identify any correlation between changes in the design or placement of promotions and fluctuations in the Item Promotion Click Through Rate? 4. How does the Item Promotion Click Through Rate differ between users who have completed the activation process and those who haven't? 5. What impact do limited-time promotions or exclusive offers have on the Item Promotion Click Through Rate, and how can these promotions be optimized for higher click-through rates? 6. How does the Item Promotion Click Through Rate vary between different app versions, and what insights can be gained for optimizing marketing efforts for specific versions? 7. What role does user feedback and reviews play in influencing the Item Promotion Click Through Rate, and how can positive sentiments be leveraged for more effective promotion click-through?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Unattractive or Misleading Promotions. If promotions lack visual appeal or are perceived as misleading, users may be less likely to click through to explore them. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Lack of Clarity in Promotion Details. Unclear or insufficient details about promotions may result in users hesitating to click through, impacting the click-through rate. 🔹 Limited Promotion Exposure. If promotions are not prominently displayed or easily accessible, users may miss the opportunity to click through. 🔹 High Loading Times for Promotions. Slow loading times for promotions can frustrate users, leading to a decline in the click-through rate. 🔹 Ineffective Call-to-Action. If promotions lack a compelling call-to-action, users may not feel motivated to click through and explore further. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Overly Frequent or Intrusive Promotions. Users may be deterred from engaging with promotions if they find them intrusive or if promotions are displayed too frequently. 🔹 Seasonal Trends. Seasonal variations in user interests may affect the click-through rate for promotions, especially during specific times.
nodes.slices: - Promotion Type - Audience Segment - Device Type - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Seasonal or Event-Based Trends - Product Category - Geo - Promotion Placement - Promotion Duration

nodes.description: Average number of items clicked by users for each view of a list.
nodes.formula: (Items Clicked in List / Item-list View Events)
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking "Items Clicked per List View" is important for e-commerce stores as it provides insights into user engagement with product lists, helping businesses understand the effectiveness of list layouts and item placements. A higher average indicates greater user interest and interaction, guiding decisions on optimizing list designs, enhancing product visibility, and tailoring marketing strategies to improve overall user experience and increase click-through rates for listed items.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the average number of Items Clicked per List View vary between different types of lists or categories, and what insights can be gained for prioritizing list types that drive higher engagement? 2. Are there specific user segments or demographics that consistently exhibit a higher or lower average number of Items Clicked per List View, and how can targeted marketing address these variations? 3. Can you identify any correlation between the timing of app updates or changes in the app catalog structure and fluctuations in the Items Clicked per List View metric? 4. How does the average number of Items Clicked per List View change during promotional periods, and what strategies are most effective in encouraging users to interact with items in lists? 5. What is the impact of app stability and loading times on the average number of Items Clicked per List View, and how can performance optimizations enhance overall item clicking in lists? 6. How do changes in user interface design or app layout impact the average number of Items Clicked per List View, and what design enhancements can be made for encouraging more user interactions with items in lists? 7. What is the correlation between the average number of Items Clicked per List View and user satisfaction scores, and how can user feedback be used to address issues affecting item clicking in lists?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Limited Product Variety or Appeal. A lack of diverse or appealing products in list views may result in users clicking on fewer items within those views. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Poorly Curated Lists. If lists are not well-curated or do not align with user preferences, users may be less motivated to explore and click on items. 🔹 Unresponsive or Slow Interaction. Slow or unresponsive interactions within list views can frustrate users and reduce the likelihood of clicking on items. 🔹 Limited Filtering and Sorting Options. Inadequate options for users to filter or sort items within lists may hinder their ability to find items of interest. 🔹 Lack of Personalization. Absence of personalized recommendations in list views may result in lower user interest and interaction with listed items. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Cumbersome List Navigation. If navigating through lists is challenging or cumbersome, users may be less likely to explore and click on multiple items. 🔹 Seasonal or Trend Misalignment. If the content within lists does not align with seasonal trends or user preferences, it may impact the click count.
nodes.slices: - List Type - Audience Segment - Device Type - List Placement - List Length - List Content - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Seasonal or Event-Based Trends - List Diversity - List Personalization - List Sorting - Cross-List Engagement - List Refresh Frequency - List Interaction History - Geo

nodes.id: Items in List Click Through Rate
nodes.description: The percentage of items clicked in a list relative to the total number of items viewed in that list.
nodes.formula: (Items Clicked in List / Items Viewed in List) * 100
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: Monitoring the "Items in List Click Through Rate" is crucial for e-commerce stores as it assesses the success of item presentation and user engagement within lists. A heightened click-through rate signifies user interest in specific products, offering valuable insights for optimizing list designs, improving product visibility, and tailoring marketing strategies to enhance overall user experience and potentially drive more conversions for individual items.

How does the Items in List Click Through Rate vary between different types of lists or categories, and what insights can be gained for prioritizing list types that drive higher engagement? 2. Are there specific user segments or demographics that consistently exhibit a higher or lower Items in List Click Through Rate, and how can targeted marketing address these variations? 3. How do changes in user interface design or app layout impact the Items in List Click Through Rate, and what design enhancements can be made for encouraging more user interactions with items in lists? 7. What is the correlation between the Items in List Click Through Rate and user satisfaction scores, and how can user feedback be used to address issues affecting item clicking in lists?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Limited Visual Appeal of List Items. If items within a list lack visual appeal, attractive images, or engaging presentation, users may skip clicking through. 🔹 Poor Quality or Insufficient Product Images. Low-quality or insufficient images may fail to showcase products effectively, impacting the click-through rate in lists. 🔹 Irrelevant Items Displayed. If the list displays items that are not relevant to user preferences or the search query, users may avoid clicking through. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Lack of Clear and Relevant Descriptions. Inadequate or unclear product descriptions may hinder users' understanding and discourage them from clicking on items. 🔹 Limited Filtering and Sorting Options. If users cannot easily filter or sort items within lists, it may lead to frustration and a reduced click-through rate. 🔹 Cumbersome List Navigation. If navigating through lists is challenging or cumbersome, users may be less likely to explore and click on multiple items. 🔹 Ineffective Personalization. Absence of personalized recommendations in list views may result in lower user interest and interaction with listed items. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Seasonal or Trend Misalignment. If the content within lists does not align with seasonal trends or user preferences, it may impact the click-through rate.

nodes.description: Average number of items viewed by users during a single session.
nodes.formula: (Items Viewed / Number of Sessions)
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Monitoring "Items Viewed per Session" is vital for e-commerce stores as it directly reflects user interaction with product catalogs in a single session. A higher average suggests a deeper engagement with offerings, aiding businesses in optimizing website layouts, refining personalized recommendations, and tailoring marketing strategies to improve user satisfaction and potentially drive higher conversion rates within each session.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the average number of Items Viewed per Session vary between different types of user sessions, and what insights can be gained for tailoring features to specific session types? 2. Are there specific features or content types that consistently lead to a higher average number of Items Viewed per Session, and how can these be emphasized for better user interaction? 3. Can you identify any correlation between the timing and frequency of push notifications or in-app messages and changes in the Items Viewed per Session metric? 4. How does the Items Viewed per Session metric differ between users who have completed the activation process and those who haven't? 5. What impact do limited-time promotions or exclusive offers have on the Items Viewed per Session metric, and how can these promotions be optimized for increased item viewing during sessions? 6. How does the Items Viewed per Session metric vary between different app versions, and what insights can be gained for optimizing marketing efforts for specific versions? 7. What role does personalization play in influencing the Items Viewed per Session metric, and how can personalized content recommendations be optimized for better user interaction during sessions?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Limited User Engagement. Users who are not actively engaged with the app may view fewer items during each session, reducing overall item views. 🔹 Unappealing or Uninteresting Content. If users find the content unappealing or irrelevant, they may be less motivated to explore and view multiple product pages. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Technical Glitches or Slow Loading Times. Frequent technical issues or slow loading times can disrupt the user experience, resulting in fewer items viewed per session. 🔹 Lack of Personalization. A lack of personalized recommendations may lead to users seeing fewer items of interest during each session. 🔹 Ineffective Content Recommendations. If content recommendations are not well-matched to user preferences, users may be less likely to explore and view suggested items. 🔹 Limited Interactive Features. Users may view fewer items if the app lacks interactive features or engaging activities to keep them exploring. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 External Distractions or Interruptions. External factors such as notifications or interruptions may divert users' attention, reducing their engagement with viewing items.

nodes.description: The percentage of users who opt-in for push notifications.
nodes.formula: (Number of Opt-ins / Total Users) * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking the "Opt-In Rate" is vital for e-commerce stores as it indicates user interest in receiving real-time updates. A substantial opt-in rate allows businesses to customize notification content, optimize marketing strategies, and enhance communication for increased user engagement and potential sales growth through targeted promotions.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the Opt-In Rate vary between different user segments or demographics, and what insights can be gained for tailoring push notification opt-in requests to specific user preferences? 2. Are there specific app features or incentives that consistently lead to a higher Opt-In Rate, and how can these be emphasized for better user engagement with push notifications? 3. Can you identify any correlation between the timing and context of push notification opt-in requests and changes in the Opt-In Rate? 4. How does the Opt-In Rate differ between users who have completed the activation process and those who haven't? 5. What impact do limited-time promotions or exclusive offers have on the Opt-In Rate, and how can these promotions be optimized for increased opt-ins to push notifications? 6. How does the Opt-In Rate vary between different app versions, and what insights can be gained for optimizing push notification strategies for specific versions? 7. What role does user education play in influencing the Opt-In Rate, and how can informative messages be crafted to increase user understanding and willingness to opt-in to push notifications?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Lack of Incentives for Push Notifications. Users may be less inclined to opt-in if there are no clear incentives or benefits associated with receiving push notifications. 🔹 Unclear or Intrusive Opt-In Requests. If the opt-in requests are unclear in their purpose or feel intrusive, users may hesitate or decline, affecting the overall opt-in rate. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Poorly Timed Opt-In Requests. Timing matters; poorly timed opt-in requests, such as interrupting critical user tasks, can lead to lower opt-in rates. 🔹 Limited Explanation of Notification Benefits. If users are not adequately informed about the benefits of receiving push notifications, they may be less likely to opt-in. 🔹 App Lacks Compelling Notification Content. Users may opt-out if the app fails to deliver compelling and valuable content through push notifications. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Lack of Customization Options. Users prefer control; if the app lacks options for customizing notification preferences, it may result in a lower opt-in rate. 🔹 Users' Privacy Concerns. Apprehensions about data privacy and security may lead users to opt-out of push notifications, impacting the overall opt-in rate.
nodes.slices: - Device Type - Geo - App Version - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - User Engagement Level - Campaign Type - App Permission Requests - Demographic Segment - App Usage Frequency - Content Type - Social Media Integration

nodes.description: The percentage of app installations that originated from organic (non-paid) sources relative to the app page views from organic sources
nodes.formula: (Organic Installs / App Page Views from Organic Sources) * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Monitoring the "Organic App Install Rate" is crucial for e-commerce stores as it offers insights into the success of organic user acquisition. A higher rate signifies effective organic visibility and user engagement, informing decisions on optimizing app store presence, refining organic marketing strategies, and improving overall app discoverability to maximize non-paid app installations and foster organic growth of the user base.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the Organic App Install Rate vary between different user segments or demographics, and what insights can be gained for tailoring app store optimization efforts to specific user preferences? 2. Are there specific app features or updates that correlate with changes in the Organic App Install Rate, and what insights can be gained for future development and marketing efforts? 3. Can you identify any correlation between the timing and frequency of organic app install campaigns or promotions and changes in the Organic App Install Rate? 4. How does the Organic App Install Rate differ between users who have completed the activation process and those who haven't? 5. What impact do limited-time promotions or exclusive offers have on the Organic App Install Rate, and how can these promotions be optimized for increased organic app installations? 6. How does the geographic location of users influence the Organic App Install Rate, and are there opportunities for localized optimization? 7. What is the correlation between the Organic App Install Rate and user satisfaction scores, and how can improvements in user satisfaction contribute to higher organic app installations?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Low Visibility or Prominence of Organic Sources. If organic sources are not prominently featured or visible, users may not discover the app through organic channels, impacting install rates. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Ineffective App Store Optimization (ASO). Poor ASO practices can result in lower visibility in organic search results, reducing the chances of users installing the app. 🔹 Weak Organic User Acquisition Strategies. If the app lacks effective strategies for organic user acquisition, it may struggle to convert organic app page views into installations. 🔹 Limited User Engagement with Organic Content. Low engagement with organic content may lead to fewer app installations, even if the organic sources generate page views. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Seasonal Variations or Trends. Seasonal fluctuations or changes in user trends may impact the effectiveness of organic user acquisition, affecting install rates. 🔹 Unattractive App Store Presentation. Aesthetically unappealing app store pages may deter users from installing the app, even if they find it through organic sources. 🔹 Inconsistency in Organic Content Delivery. Inconsistent delivery of organic content may result in lower engagement and conversion rates, impacting the organic install rate.
nodes.slices: - Keyword - Geo - Device Type - App Version - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Audience Segment - Traffic Source - Seasonal or Event-Based Trends - Referral Source

nodes.description: The speed at which web pages or app screens load, often measured in seconds.
nodes.formula: Measurement specific to the chosen page speed metric
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Monitoring the "Page Speed" metric is vital for e-commerce stores as it significantly influences user satisfaction and engagement. Faster loading times contribute to lower bounce rates and potentially higher conversion rates, making it essential for decision-making related to optimization strategies to enhance overall user experience and the success of the e-commerce platform.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the Page Speed metric influence user engagement and retention, and what improvements can be made to enhance the overall user experience based on page speed optimization? 2. Are there specific app features or content types that are more sensitive to variations in Page Speed, and how can these be optimized for better performance and user satisfaction? 3. Can you identify any correlation between the timing and frequency of app updates or changes in content structure and fluctuations in the Page Speed metric? 4. How does the Page Speed metric differ between users who have completed the activation process and those who haven't? 5. What impact do limited-time promotions or exclusive offers have on the Page Speed metric, and how can these promotions be optimized for a seamless user experience during peak traffic? 6. How does the geographic location of users influence the Page Speed metric, and are there opportunities for localized optimization? 7. What is the correlation between the Page Speed metric and user satisfaction scores, and how can user feedback be used to address issues affecting overall page speed performance?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Slow Server Response Times. Slow server response times can significantly increase page loading times, leading to a poor user experience and negatively impacting page speed. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Large or Unoptimized Media Files. Heavy media files, such as images or videos, can slow down page loading times, affecting the overall speed and user satisfaction. 🔹 Inefficient Code or Scripting. Poorly optimized or inefficient code can contribute to slow page loading times, undermining the overall speed and responsiveness. 🔹 Limited Content Delivery Network (CDN) Utilization. Lack of or underutilization of CDNs may result in increased latency and slower page loading, affecting the overall page speed. 🔹 High Number of HTTP Requests. Excessive HTTP requests can prolong page loading times, especially if the app or website requires multiple resources to load. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Unoptimized Browser Caching. Ineffective or insufficient browser caching strategies can lead to repeated resource fetching, negatively impacting page speed. 🔹 Device or Network Limitations. Users on devices with limited processing power or slow network connections may experience slower page loading times.
nodes.slices: - Geo - Device Type - Browser - Page Type - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Audience Segment - Product Category - Page Complexity - Seasonal or Event-Based Trends - Conversion Funnel Stage - Landing Page - A/B Test - Customer Journey Stage

A higher rate indicates successful engagement with the target audience through paid channels, guiding decisions on optimizing advertising strategies, allocating marketing budgets effectively, and maximizing the return on investment for paid campaigns to boost app installations and user acquisition.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the Paid App Install Rate vary between different user segments or demographics, and what insights can be gained for optimizing paid marketing strategies to specific user preferences? 2. Are there specific app features or updates that correlate with changes in the Paid App Install Rate, and what insights can be gained for future development and marketing efforts? 3. Can you identify any correlation between the timing and frequency of paid app install campaigns or promotions and changes in the Paid App Install Rate? 4. How does the Paid App Install Rate differ between users who have completed the activation process and those who haven't? 5. nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Ineffective Paid Marketing Strategies. If paid marketing strategies are not well-executed or targeted, they may fail to convert app page views into paid installations. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Misalignment Between Ads and App Content. If the content in paid advertisements does not align with user expectations from the app, it may lead to lower conversion rates. 🔹 High Competition in Paid Channels. Increased competition in paid channels may make it more challenging to acquire users through paid installations, affecting rates. 🔹 Low Quality or Misleading Ad Creatives. Ad creatives that are of low quality or are perceived as misleading may result in lower trust and conversion rates from paid sources. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Insufficient Budget Allocation. If the budget allocated for paid marketing is insufficient, it may limit the reach and effectiveness of paid user acquisition efforts. 🔹 Users' Resistance to Paid Installations. Some users may resist paid installations, preferring to discover and install apps through organic channels, affecting the paid install rate. 🔹 Ad Fatigue. If users repeatedly encounter the same ads, it may lead to ad fatigue, reducing the effectiveness of paid marketing efforts.

nodes.description: The percentage of users who are paying customers out of the total user base.
nodes.formula: (Paying Users / Total Users) * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking the "Paying Share" is crucial for e-commerce stores as it provides a clear measure of the customer base that contributes revenue. Monitoring this metric helps businesses gauge the success of their monetization strategies, informs decisions on marketing approaches, customer retention initiatives, and pricing models, ultimately aiding in optimizing overall revenue generation and business sustainability.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the Paying Share metric vary between different user segments or demographics, and what insights can be gained for tailoring monetization strategies to specific user behaviors? 2. Are there specific app features or updates that correlate with changes in the Paying Share metric, and what insights can be gained for future development and monetization efforts? 3. Can you identify any correlation between the timing and frequency of in-app promotions or changes in pricing models and fluctuations in the Paying Share metric? 4. How does the Paying Share metric differ between users who have completed the activation process and those who haven't? 5. What impact do limited-time promotions or exclusive offers have on the Paying Share metric, and how can these promotions be optimized for increased user conversion to paying customers? 6. How does the geographic location of users influence the Paying Share metric, and are there opportunities for localized monetization strategies? 7. What is the correlation between the Paying Share metric and user satisfaction scores, and how can improvements in user satisfaction contribute to a higher paying user conversion rate?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Limited Product Variety and Appeal. If the app's product range is limited or lacks appeal, users may not find products compelling enough to make a purchase, impacting the paying share. 🔹 Complicated Checkout Process. A complex or confusing checkout process can lead to user frustration and abandonment, reducing the likelihood of successful transactions. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Ineffective Product Recommendations. If product recommendations do not align with user preferences, users may be less likely to discover and purchase relevant items. 🔹 Lack of Incentives for Repeat Purchases. Insufficient incentives for repeat purchases, such as loyalty programs or exclusive offers, may result in lower user retention and paying share. 🔹 Poorly Optimized Product Pages. Product pages that lack optimization, including clear images and descriptions, may fail to engage users and encourage purchases. 🔹 Inadequate Customer Support. Poor customer support can lead to dissatisfaction, impacting users' trust and willingness to make repeat purchases, affecting paying share. 🔹 Limited Payment Options or Payment Issues. If there are restrictions on payment options or if users encounter payment issues, it can lead to abandoned transactions and reduced paying share. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Uncompetitive Pricing or Lack of Discounts. If pricing is uncompetitive or lacks attractive discounts, users may explore alternative platforms, impacting the paying share. 🔹 Insufficient Marketing of Premium Features. If premium features that enhance the shopping experience are not effectively marketed, users may miss out on added value, affecting paying share. 🔹 Slow Loading Times for Checkout or Payment Pages. Slow loading times during the checkout process can lead to user frustration and increased chances of abandoning the transaction. 🔹 Limited Social Proof or User Reviews. The absence of social proof, including user reviews, may impact users' confidence in making purchases, affecting the paying share.
nodes.slices: - Product Category - Subscription Type - Audience Segment - Referral Source - Geo - Device Type - Promotion Engagement - Loyalty Program Level - Time Since Registration - Payment Method - Customer Support Interaction - Cross-Sell Opportunities

nodes.description: The maximum number of users concurrently using the app or website during a specific time period.
nodes.formula: Measurement specific to the chosen method for determining peak concurrent users
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: Tracking Peak Concurrent Users (PCU) is crucial for e-commerce stores as it helps identify the maximum load on the app or website infrastructure at a specific time. This metric is essential for capacity planning, ensuring that the system can handle peak user demand without performance issues or downtime. By understanding the peak usage, businesses can optimize server resources, enhance user experience, and make informed decisions to scale infrastructure when necessary.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the PCU metric correlate with changes in user engagement strategies or major app updates, and what insights can be gained for optimizing server infrastructure to accommodate peak usage? 2. Are there specific app features or events that consistently lead to higher PCU, and how can these be emphasized for better user experience during peak concurrent usage periods? 3. nodes.description: The number of sessions that began on your website or application. A session is a period of time during which a user interacts with your website or app. A session initiates when a user either: Opens your app in the foreground Views a page or screen and no session is currently active (e.g. their previous session has timed out) By default, a session ends (times out) after 30 minutes of user inactivity. There is no limit to how long a session can last.
nodes.formula: Number of Sessions
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: By understanding how many sessions are initiated, you can assess the effectiveness of your app or website in capturing user attention. This information helps in making decisions to optimize user experience, content, and overall performance.
nodes.questions: 1. How do the number of Sessions correlate with changes in user engagement strategies or major app updates, and what insights can be gained for optimizing user sessions over time? 2. How do Sessions differ between users who have completed the activation process and those who haven't? 5. What impact do limited-time promotions or exclusive offers have on the number of Sessions, and how can these promotions be optimized for increased user engagement? 6. How does the geographic location of users influence the number of Sessions, and are there opportunities for localized optimization of user engagement strategies? 7. What is the correlation between the number of Sessions and user satisfaction scores, and how can improvements in user satisfaction contribute to higher session counts?
nodes.slices: - Session Duration - Time-of-Day and Day-of-Week - Traffic Source - Device Type - Geo - New vs. Returning Sessions - Session Depth - Seasonal or Event-Based Trends - Referral Source - Demographic Segment - Session Quality Score

nodes.description: The average amount of time users spend viewing a product card.
nodes.formula: Total Time on Product Cards / Number of Product Card Views
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking the "Time on Product Card" is important for e-commerce stores as it provides insights into user engagement with specific products. A higher average time on product cards indicates increased interest and engagement, influencing decisions on product presentation, content optimization, and overall user experience to enhance the chances of conversion and improve the effectiveness of product displays.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the Time on Product Card metric correlate with changes in the product presentation or additional product information provided, and what insights can be gained for optimizing the product card experience? 2. Are there specific app features or updates that correlate with changes in the Time on Product Card metric, and what insights can be gained for future development and user engagement efforts? 3. Can you identify any correlation between the timing and frequency of in-app promotions or changes in the app catalog structure and fluctuations in the Time on Product Card metric? 4. How does the Time on Product Card metric differ between users who have completed the activation process and those who haven't? 5. What impact do limited-time promotions or exclusive offers have on the Time on Product Card metric, and how can these promotions be optimized for increased engagement with product details? 6. How does the geographic location of users influence the Time on Product Card metric, and are there opportunities for localized optimization of the product card experience? 7. What is the correlation between the Time on Product Card metric and user satisfaction scores, and how can improvements in user satisfaction contribute to a higher engagement with product details?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Limited Engagement Features. If the product card lacks interactive features or engaging content, users may spend less time viewing the product details. 🔹 Unattractive Product Presentation. If the product card does not present products in an appealing way, users may lose interest quickly, resulting in shorter viewing times. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Insufficient Product Information. A lack of comprehensive details or specifications on the product card may lead to quicker decision-making and shorter viewing times. 🔹 Slow Loading Product Images. Slow-loading images may lead to delays in the user's exploration of the product card, impacting the overall time spent. 🔹 Ambiguous or Hidden Navigation Elements. If navigation within the product card is unclear or hidden, users may struggle to explore further, affecting time spent. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Limited User Interest in Product Category. If users are generally less interested in the product category, it may result in shorter viewing times on product cards. 🔹 External Distractions or Interruptions. External factors, such as notifications or interruptions, may divert users' attention, leading to shorter viewing times.

nodes.why: Tracking the User Engagement Rate is crucial for e-commerce stores as it provides insights into the effectiveness of your app or website in retaining and involving users. A high engagement rate indicates a positive user experience and may suggest that your content, features, or promotions are resonating well. This metric helps in making informed decisions about optimizing user experience, content strategy, and marketing efforts to enhance overall user satisfaction and retention.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the User Engagement Rate metric vary between different user segments or demographics, and what insights can be gained for tailoring app features or content to specific user preferences? 2. Are there specific app features or updates that correlate with changes in the User Engagement Rate, and what insights can be gained for future development and marketing efforts? 3. Can you identify any correlation between the timing and frequency of user engagement campaigns or promotions and fluctuations in the User Engagement Rate? 4. How does the User Engagement Rate differ between users who have completed the activation process and those who haven't? 5. What impact do limited-time promotions or exclusive offers have on the User Engagement Rate, and how can these promotions be optimized for increased user engagement? 6. How does the geographic location of users influence the User Engagement Rate, and are there opportunities for localized optimization of user engagement strategies? 7. What is the correlation between the User Engagement Rate and user satisfaction scores, and how can improvements in user satisfaction contribute to a higher user engagement rate?
nodes.slices: - Traffic Source - Device Type - Geo - Demographic Segment - Product Category - User Type

nodes.description: The percentage of unique visitors who do not return to the website after their initial visit.
nodes.formula: (Churned Users / Total Users) * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking the "Users Churn Rate" is crucial for e-commerce stores as it provides insights into the effectiveness of retaining unique visitors. A high churn rate suggests potential issues with user engagement or satisfaction, guiding decisions on website improvements, marketing strategies, and customer retention initiatives to enhance the overall user experience and encourage repeat visits.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the Users Churn Rate metric vary between different user segments or demographics, and what insights can be gained for tailoring retention strategies to specific user behaviors? 2. Are there specific app features or updates that correlate with changes in the Users Churn Rate, and what insights can be gained for future development and retention efforts? 3. Can you identify any correlation between the timing and frequency of user engagement campaigns or personalized interactions and fluctuations in the Users Churn Rate? 4. How does the Users Churn Rate differ between users who have completed the activation process and those who haven't? 5. What impact do limited-time promotions or exclusive offers have on the Users Churn Rate, and how can these promotions be optimized for reducing user churn? 6. How does the geographic location of users influence the Users Churn Rate, and are there opportunities for localized optimization of retention strategies? 7. What is the correlation between the Users Churn Rate and user satisfaction scores, and how can improvements in user satisfaction contribute to a lower rate of user churn?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Poor Onboarding Experience. If the onboarding process is confusing or unengaging, users may leave the platform early on, contributing to a higher churn rate. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Ineffective User Engagement Strategies. Lack of strategies to keep users engaged over time may result in boredom or disinterest, contributing to higher churn rates. 🔹 Frequent Technical Issues or Crashes. Repeated technical issues or app crashes can frustrate users, leading to a negative experience and a higher likelihood of churn. 🔹 Lack of Timely and Relevant Content Updates. If the content or features within the app do not evolve over time, users may lose interest, contributing to a higher churn rate. 🔹 Limited Personalization and Customization Options. Users may churn if they cannot personalize their experience or if the app lacks customization options to suit their preferences. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Unresponsive Customer Support. If customer support is unresponsive or unsatisfactory, users may feel neglected and more likely to churn. 🔹 Changes in User Preferences or Needs. If the app fails to adapt to evolving user preferences or needs, users may find alternatives, contributing to a higher churn rate.
nodes.slices: - Device Type - Geo - Traffic Source - Demographic Segment - Marketing Channel - Product Category - Landing Page - Promotional Periods

nodes.description: The percentage of users who continue to use the app or website over an extended period relative to the total number of users.
nodes.formula: (Returning Visitors / Total Users) * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Users Loyalty Rate provides insights into the effectiveness of your platform in retaining a consistent user base over time. A high Users Loyalty Rate suggests satisfied and loyal customers, influencing decisions related to marketing strategies, user experience enhancements, and customer relationship management.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the Users Loyalty Rate metric vary between different user segments or demographics, and what insights can be gained for tailoring loyalty programs or rewards to specific user behaviors? 2. Are there specific app features or updates that correlate with changes in the Users Loyalty Rate, and what insights can be gained for future development and loyalty program initiatives? 3. Can you identify any correlation between the timing and frequency of loyalty program promotions or changes in reward structures and fluctuations in the Users Loyalty Rate? 4. How does the Users Loyalty Rate differ between users who have completed the activation process and those who haven't? 5. What impact do limited-time promotions or exclusive offers have on the Users Loyalty Rate, and how can these promotions be optimized for increased user loyalty? 6. How does the geographic location of users influence the Users Loyalty Rate, and are there opportunities for localized optimization of loyalty programs? 7. What is the correlation between the Users Loyalty Rate and user satisfaction scores, and how can improvements in user satisfaction contribute to a higher rate of user loyalty?

nodes.description: The number of app screens or web pages your users viewed per session. Repeated views of a single page or screen are counted.
nodes.formula: (Screen_view events + Page_view events) / Sessions
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking the "Views per Session" metric is important for e-commerce stores as it provides insights into user engagement and content consumption patterns. A higher views per session indicates active exploration of the platform, guiding decisions on content organization, layout optimization, and overall user experience improvements to enhance engagement and potentially drive higher conversion rates.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the Views per Session metric vary between different app screens or web pages, and what insights can be gained for optimizing the layout and content of high-traffic pages or screens? 2. Are there specific app features or updates that correlate with changes in the Views per Session metric, and what insights can be gained for future development and content presentation? 3. Can you identify any correlation between the timing and frequency of content updates or changes in navigation structures and fluctuations in the Views per Session metric? 4. How does the Views per Session metric differ between users who have completed the activation process and those who haven't? 5. What impact do limited-time promotions or exclusive offers have on the Views per Session metric, and how can these promotions be optimized for increased user interaction with app content? 6. How does the geographic location of users influence the Views per Session metric, and are there opportunities for localized optimization of content for better user engagement? 7. What is the correlation between the Views per Session metric and user satisfaction scores, and how can improvements in user satisfaction contribute to higher views per session?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Limited App Content Engagement. Users may view fewer screens or pages per session if the app lacks engaging content or if the content fails to capture their interest. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 User Interface Complexity. A complex or confusing user interface may discourage users from exploring multiple screens during a single session. 🔹 Ineffective Navigation Design. Poorly designed navigation may hinder users' ability to smoothly transition between screens, impacting views per session. 🔹 Lack of Content Discoverability. If users struggle to discover relevant content easily, it may result in a lower number of screens viewed per session. 🔹 Limited In-App Promotions or Features. Absence of in-app promotions or exciting features may reduce users' motivation to explore and view multiple screens. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 External Distractions or Interruptions. External factors, such as notifications or interruptions, may divert users' attention, leading to fewer screens viewed per session. 🔹 Slow Loading Times for Screens or Pages. Slow loading times for screens or pages may result in user frustration, contributing to a decrease in views per session. 🔹 Limited User Engagement Prompts. If there are no prompts or suggestions encouraging users to explore more screens, they may not actively seek out additional content.
nodes.slices: - Device Type - Geo - Traffic Source - Demographic Segment - Marketing Channel - Product Category - Landing Page - Promotional Periods

nodes.formula: Number of unique users engaging with the app or website during the week

This metric aids decision-making by offering a snapshot of the active user base, informing strategies to enhance user experience, optimize content, and tailor promotions to retain and attract users on a weekly basis.

How does the geographic location of users influence the WAU metric, and are there opportunities for localized optimization of weekly engagement strategies? 7. nodes.description: The number of recipients who reported email as spam. Recipients who report email as spam are automatically unsubscribed from all emails.
nodes.formula: Number of Abuse Complaints
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Abuse Complaints are crucial as they indicate the level of dissatisfaction among recipients. High numbers can harm sender reputation and deliverability. Tracking this metric helps identify and address content or targeting issues, ensuring better engagement and reducing the risk of being marked as spam.
nodes.questions: 1. How do abuse complaints impact overall email deliverability? 2. What strategies can be employed to minimize abuse complaints in email marketing? 3. How do abuse complaints relate to the quality of email content and targeting? 4. What is the trend in abuse complaints over the last few email campaigns? 5. How can a high number of abuse complaints be addressed to prevent future issues? 6. Are there specific demographics or segments more prone to reporting emails as spam? 7. What measures can be implemented to automatically identify and address abuse complaints?

nodes.description: The number of emails that weren’t delivered to recipients’ inboxes.
nodes.formula: Soft Bounces + Hard Bounces
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Monitoring "Bounces" is essential to maintain a clean and active email list. High bounce rates can negatively impact sender reputation, affecting deliverability. By addressing bounces promptly, marketers can enhance list quality and optimize future campaigns.

Are there patterns in bounce rates that correlate with specific types of email content? 4. How can bounce rates be used to improve the cleanliness of the email list? 5. What strategies can be employed to reduce the occurrence of both soft and hard bounces? 6. How do bounces influence the sender's reputation, and how can this be managed? 7. What measures can be taken to distinguish between temporary and permanent email delivery issues?
nodes.slices: - Bounce Type - Email Content Category - Sender Reputation - Geographical Location - Device or Email Client

nodes.description: The percentage of delivered emails that registered at least one click.
nodes.formula: (Clicked / Successful deliveries) * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Click Rate measures the effectiveness of email content. A higher click rate indicates engaged recipients. Tracking this metric helps optimize content, design, and calls-to-action, providing insights into audience preferences. It guides marketers in creating more compelling emails and improving overall campaign performance.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the click rate vary across different types of email content or campaigns? 2. What role does the email subject line play in influencing the click rate? 3. Are there specific times or days when click rates are typically higher? 4. How can A/B testing be utilized to optimize the click rate in email campaigns? 5. What is the correlation between click rate and overall conversion rates? 6. How do external factors, such as industry trends, impact the average click rate? 7. What strategies can be employed to encourage higher click rates in email marketing?

nodes.description: The number of recipients who clicked any tracked link.
nodes.formula: Number of Recipients who clicked any tracked link

nodes.why: Clicked represents direct engagement with email content. Identifying the number of recipients who interacted provides insights into the appeal of the message. This metric aids in understanding the effectiveness of specific elements, allowing marketers to tailor future campaigns for increased click-through rates and better user engagement.

nodes.description: Clicks per Unique Open used in email marketing to measure the effectiveness of a campaign in terms of user engagement after opening an email. This metric calculates the average number of clicks on links within an email for each unique open.
nodes.formula: (Total clicks / Opened)
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Clicks per Unique Open measures post-open engagement. A higher ratio indicates a compelling email experience. Monitoring this metric helps refine content to resonate with the audience, enhancing user satisfaction and encouraging further interaction. It provides valuable insights into the relevance of content after the initial open.

Are there specific segments of the audience that consistently show higher clicks per unique open? 5. What is the correlation between clicks per unique open and subsequent conversion rates? 6. How does the clicks per unique open metric vary across different stages of the customer journey? 7. Can the clicks per unique open metric help in predicting long-term customer engagement?

nodes.description: Confirmed opt-in means that someone has to take an extra step to get on email list (double opt-in). After they initially sign up, they'll get an email asking them to confirm their subscription by clicking a link. This helps make sure that the people actually want to be there, and it can improve email deliverability and engagement down the line.
nodes.formula: Number of Confirmed Opt-ins
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Confirmed Opt-ins ensure a qualified and engaged subscriber base. By requiring confirmation, marketers filter out disinterested or mistyped email addresses. This metric contributes to improved deliverability, list quality, and subscriber engagement. It also helps build trust, ensuring that emails reach an audience genuinely interested in the content.

What is the relationship between confirmed opt-ins and subsequent engagement levels? 3. How can the double opt-in process influence the confirmed opt-ins metric? 4. How does the timing and frequency of confirmation emails impact the confirmed opt-ins rate? 6. Can the confirmed opt-ins metric be used as an indicator of the effectiveness of lead generation strategies? 7. What measures can be implemented to encourage more subscribers to complete the confirmed opt-in process?
nodes.slices: - Sign-up Source - Time Since Initial Sign-up - Confirmation Email Content - Offer or Incentive - Device Type

nodes.description: The price paid every time someone clicks on your link in email.

nodes.why: Cost per Email Click evaluates the efficiency of marketing spend in driving clicks. Tracking this metric aids in optimizing budget allocation and assessing the cost-effectiveness of campaigns. Marketers can refine targeting strategies and content to enhance engagement while managing costs, ultimately improving the overall return on investment (ROI).

How does the cost per email click metric compare to industry benchmarks, and what insights can be gained from this?

nodes.description: Cost per Open measures the average cost associated with each open in an email marketing campaign.
nodes.formula: (Email Marketing Costs / Total Opens)
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking "Cost per Open" metric helps marketers evaluate the cost-effectiveness of their content and targeting strategies. It guides decisions on refining email elements to maximize engagement while controlling costs. This insight contributes to overall campaign efficiency and resource utilization.

What strategies can be employed to improve the cost per open while maintaining engagement levels? 3. How does the cost per open metric relate to the overall effectiveness of email subject lines? 4.
