nodes.description: It's the percentage of people who opened your email who then clicked a link within that email.
nodes.formula: (Clicked / Opened) * 100
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: A higher CTOR suggests compelling content that motivates further interaction. Monitoring this metric helps marketers optimize both open and click-through rates, ensuring a seamless user journey and enhancing the overall impact of email campaigns on user engagement and conversion.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the CTOR metric vary between different types of email campaigns (e.g., promotional, informational)? 2. What impact does the length and complexity of email content have on the CTOR metric? 3. How can A/B testing be utilized to optimize the CTOR and improve overall campaign performance? 4. Are there specific segments of the audience that consistently show higher CTOR values? 5. How does the CTOR metric align with the overall goals and objectives of the email marketing strategy? 6. What role does the clarity of calls-to-action play in influencing the CTOR metric? 7. Can the CTOR metric be used to assess the alignment between email content and recipient expectations?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Poor Email Design and Content. Unappealing design or irrelevant content may discourage recipients from clicking on links, reducing the click-through rate. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Misleading Subject Lines. If subject lines promise something that the email content doesn't deliver, recipients may be less likely to click through, affecting CTOR negatively. 🔹 Technical Issues. Broken links, slow loading, or other technical problems can frustrate users, leading to a decline in the click-through open rate. 🔹 Email Fatigue. Overwhelming subscribers with too many emails can lead to fatigue, diminishing interest and lowering the likelihood of clicking through. 🔹 Inadequate Personalization. Lack of personalized content may result in recipients feeling less engaged, reducing their motivation to click through. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Targeting the Wrong Audience. Sending emails to an audience not interested in the content or product can lead to a decrease in click-through rates. 🔹 Competing External Factors. External events, such as industry changes or global happenings, might shift priorities for users, impacting their engagement with emails and lowering CTOR.
nodes.slices: '- Email Content Type - Time Since Email Delivery - Target Audience - Email List Segment - Call to Action (CTA) Clarity

nodes.description: The percentage of emails that were successfully delivered to recipients’ inboxes. Deliverability rates can be impacted by the sending server, email content, and who you’re sending it to.
nodes.formula: (Successful deliveries / Emails sent) * 100
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: High delivery rates are vital for effective communication. Tracking this metric helps identify and address issues affecting email deliverability, ensuring that messages are reaching the intended audience. It provides valuable insights into the health of email infrastructure and sender reputation.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the delivery rate metric impact the overall success of an email marketing campaign? 2. What strategies can be implemented to improve delivery rates and ensure emails reach recipients' inboxes? 3. Are there correlations between delivery rates and specific types of email content or audience segments? 4. How can fluctuations in delivery rates be used to identify potential issues with email content or distribution channels? 5. What role does sender reputation play in influencing the delivery rate, and how can it be managed effectively? 6. How do industry benchmarks for delivery rates compare, and what insights can be gained from benchmarking? 7. Can the delivery rate metric be used to assess the impact of changes in email infrastructure or sending practices?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Spam Triggers in Email Content. Email content triggering spam filters can result in a lower delivery rate as emails are more likely to be flagged as spam and diverted from inboxes. 🔹 Poor Sender Reputation. A bad sender reputation due to past spam complaints or low engagement can harm deliverability, causing a decline in the delivery rate. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Inaccurate Email List. Sending emails to outdated or inaccurate email addresses may lead to bounces, negatively impacting the delivery rate. 🔹 Email Server Issues. Technical problems with the email server, such as misconfigurations or downtime, can prevent successful email deliveries, affecting the delivery rate. 🔹 Changes in ISP Policies. If internet service providers update their policies, it can affect email delivery algorithms, influencing the delivery rate. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Email Content Quality. Low-quality or poorly crafted content may not pass spam filters, impacting the likelihood of emails being delivered to inboxes. 🔹 Unoptimized Sending Schedule. Sending emails at times when recipients are less likely to check their inboxes may lead to lower deliverability rates.
nodes.slices: - Sending Server - Email Content Type - Subscriber Engagement Level - List Segmentation - Time of Day

nodes.description: Domain reputation is like a scorecard for email domain. It tells email servers how trustworthy your emails are, based on past behavior like spam complaints or bounces. A good reputation means emails are more likely to end up in the inbox instead of the spam folder. It's something you build over time by following email best practices and keeping your audience engaged.
nodes.formula: No specific formula (Score based on past behavior like spam complaints or bounces)
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Domain Reputation serves as a scorecard for email domains, influencing email server trust. A positive reputation enhances inbox placement, highlighting the importance of maintaining good practices. Tracking this metric guides marketers in building trust over time, aligning with best practices, and fostering engaged audiences for improved deliverability and email performance.
nodes.questions: 1. How does domain reputation contribute to the overall deliverability of email campaigns? 2. What specific actions can negatively impact domain reputation, and how can they be avoided? 3. How long does it typically take to build and rebuild a positive domain reputation? 4. What measures can be taken to proactively improve and maintain a good domain reputation? 5. How does domain reputation affect email placement in spam folders, and what strategies can mitigate this impact? 6. Can domain reputation be influenced by factors beyond email content, such as website security or overall online presence? 7. How does domain reputation impact different types of email communications, such as newsletters versus promotional emails?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Increase in Spam Complaints. A sudden surge in spam complaints can damage domain reputation, indicating that recipients find the content unwanted or misleading. 🔹 High Bounce Rates. Consistently high bounce rates signal to email servers that the sender is not maintaining a clean and updated email list, affecting domain reputation. 🔹 Blacklist Placement. Being placed on email blacklists due to spamming activities can significantly damage domain reputation, impacting email deliverability. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Frequent Email List Changes. Regularly changing the email subscriber list without proper validation can confuse email servers, negatively impacting domain reputation. 🔹 Lack of Engagement. Low engagement, measured by opens and clicks, can signal to email servers that the content is not relevant or engaging, affecting domain reputation. 🔹 Use of Misleading Subject Lines. Deceptive subject lines can lead to increased spam complaints, harming domain reputation as it indicates a lack of transparency and trustworthiness. 🔹 Failure to Authenticate Emails. Lack of proper authentication, such as SPF and DKIM, can result in emails being treated with suspicion, affecting domain reputation negatively.
nodes.slices: - Sending Domain - Email Authentication - Industry Benchmark

nodes.description: The percentage of emails that weren't successfully delivered to your recipients’ inboxes. We calculate this number by taking the number of hard and soft bounces divided by the total number of emails sent. Bounces / Emails sent
nodes.formula: (Bounces / Emails sent) * 100
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: Monitoring Email Bounce rate is essential for maintaining a clean email list and preserving sender reputation. A high bounce rate may lead to deliverability issues, making this metric crucial for identifying and resolving potential problems. It guides decisions on list management and content optimization to enhance overall email campaign success.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the email bounce rate influence the overall effectiveness of an email campaign? 2. What is the distinction between hard and soft bounces, and how does each affect the bounce rate differently? 3. How can the bounce rate be used to identify and rectify issues with the email subscriber list? 4. Are there industry benchmarks for acceptable bounce rates, and how does the organization compare? 5. How do bounce rates correlate with the frequency of email communications and the type of content shared? 6. Can the bounce rate be used as an early indicator of potential deliverability issues in future campaigns? 7. What measures can be taken to reduce both hard and soft bounces and improve the overall bounce rate?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Outdated Email Lists. Sending emails to outdated or invalid email addresses leads to a higher bounce rate as they no longer exist or are inactive. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Technical Server Issues. Server problems, such as misconfigurations or insufficient resources, can result in bounced emails, impacting the bounce rate. 🔹 Overly Aggressive Spam Filters. Aggressive spam filters may wrongly categorize legitimate emails as spam, leading to higher bounce rates as emails are not delivered successfully. 🔹 Changes in Email Regulations. Updates in email regulations may cause emails that were once compliant to bounce due to changes in standards or requirements. 🔹 High Volume of Email Sends. Sending a large volume of emails in a short period can strain servers and increase the likelihood of bounces, affecting the bounce rate. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Lack of List Maintenance. Failing to regularly clean and maintain email lists can result in a higher bounce rate as outdated or incorrect addresses accumulate. 🔹 Inadequate Email Validation. Insufficient validation processes for new subscribers can lead to higher bounce rates as email addresses may be mistyped or invalid.
nodes.slices: - Bounce Type - Email Content Category - Sender Reputation - Geographical Location - Device or Email Client

nodes.description: Email Ignore Rate is the percentage of sent emails that are ignored or deleted from the recipient’s inbox before being opened. It can be an indication that your email content isn’t engaging enough to be opened, or that your emails are being flagged as spam.
nodes.formula: ((Emails sent - Opened) / Emails sent) * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Email Ignore Rate indicates the effectiveness of email content in capturing recipients' attention. A high ignore rate suggests possible content issues or spam flags. Tracking this metric guides marketers in refining content strategies to boost engagement and reduce the likelihood of emails being ignored or flagged. It contributes to creating more compelling and relevant email campaigns.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the email ignore rate correlate with the engagement levels of email campaigns? 2. What role does email content play in influencing the email ignore rate, and how can it be optimized? 3. Are there patterns in the email ignore rate based on the timing or frequency of email communications? 4. How does personalization impact the email ignore rate, and what strategies can be employed to enhance personalization? 5. Can the email ignore rate be used as a predictor of overall subscriber satisfaction and loyalty? 6. What is the relationship between the email ignore rate and subsequent open and click-through rates? 7. How do external factors, such as changes in industry trends, influence the email ignore rate over time?
nodes.slices: - Email Content Type - Sender Reputation - Target Audience - Time of Day

nodes.description: An overall health score for your email list, incorporating various factors like engagement, deliverability, and cleanliness.
nodes.formula: (Delivery Rate + Open Rate + CTOR) / 3
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: This holistic metric incorporates engagement, deliverability, and user interaction. Monitoring this score guides marketers in maintaining a healthy email list by identifying areas for improvement. It serves as a valuable benchmark for optimizing campaigns and ensuring sustained positive interactions with subscribers.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the email list health score provide a holistic view of the performance of an email marketing strategy? 2. What factors contribute to the calculation of the email list health score, and how are they weighted? 3. How can improvements in engagement, deliverability, and cleanliness positively impact the email list health score? 4. Are there specific benchmarks or thresholds for a "healthy" email list health score in the industry? 5. How does the email list health score align with the organization's broader marketing and business goals? 6. Can the email list health score be used as a key performance indicator (KPI) for email marketing success? 7. What measures can be taken to address areas of weakness identified by the email list health score?
nodes.slices: - List Size - List Segment - Email Content Type

nodes.description: Email marketing costs refer to the expenses associated with planning, creating, and executing an email marketing campaign. These costs can vary depending on various factors, including the size and scope of the campaign, the number of emails sent, the complexity of the email design, the use of additional tools or services, and the overall strategy employed. Often only the cost of email marketing software or service with a subscription or usage-based pricing model is included in the costs.
nodes.formula: Total Expenses Associated with Email Marketing Campaign
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking Email Marketing Costs is crucial for budget management and assessing the ROI of email marketing initiatives. This metric guides strategic decisions, helping marketers allocate resources effectively, optimize campaign elements, and maximize the overall efficiency of email marketing efforts.
nodes.questions: 1. How do email marketing costs impact the overall return on investment (ROI) of marketing campaigns? 2. What components are typically included in the calculation of email marketing costs? 3. How can cost efficiencies be achieved without compromising the effectiveness of email campaigns? 4. What role does the complexity of email design and content play in influencing email marketing costs? 5. How do changes in email marketing strategy or frequency impact total email marketing costs? 6. Can the analysis of email marketing costs provide insights into the cost-effectiveness of different campaign elements? 7. How do email marketing costs compare to other digital marketing channels, and what factors contribute to these differences?
nodes.slices: - Campaign Size - Additional Tools or Services - Campaign Type

nodes.description: The average number of emails a subscriber receives from you over a period.
nodes.formula: (Emails Sent / Total Subscribers)
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Monitoring this metric guides marketers in balancing communication to avoid subscriber fatigue. It ensures a strategic approach to content distribution, optimizing engagement and preventing potential negative impacts on user experience or deliverability. This metric contributes to maintaining a healthy and engaged subscriber base
nodes.questions: 1. How does the average number of emails per subscriber impact subscriber satisfaction and retention? 2. Are there optimal frequencies for email communications based on the analysis of emails per subscriber? 3. How does the average number of emails per subscriber vary across different audience segments or demographics? 4. Can the analysis of emails per subscriber inform the development of targeted and segmented email campaigns? 5. What is the correlation between the average number of emails per subscriber and overall engagement metrics? 6. How can the organization strike a balance between maintaining communication and avoiding email fatigue for subscribers? 7. Are there industry benchmarks for emails per subscriber, and how does the organization compare to these benchmarks?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Increased Email Frequency. Sending a higher volume of emails to subscribers within a short period may lead to subscriber fatigue, resulting in a decrease in emails per subscriber. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Irrelevant Content. If the content of the emails is not tailored to the interests of the subscribers, they may lose interest, causing a decline in the average number of emails per subscriber. 🔹 Opt-out Rate Increase. A sudden increase in subscribers opting out of emails may lower the average number of emails per subscriber, impacting this metric negatively. 🔹 Changes in Subscriber Behavior. Shifts in subscriber behavior, such as reduced engagement or changing preferences, can result in a decline in the average number of emails per subscriber. 🔹 Lack of Personalization. Failing to personalize emails based on subscriber preferences and behaviors may lead to decreased interest, affecting the average number of emails per subscriber. 🔹 Competitive Marketing Noise. Increased competition for attention in the subscribers' inboxes can lead to decreased engagement, influencing the average number of emails per subscriber. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Email Platform Changes. Changes in the email platform's algorithms or policies may impact engagement levels, affecting the average number of emails per subscriber.
nodes.slices: - Subscriber Engagement Level - Time Since Subscription - Email List Segment - Content Type

nodes.description: The number of emails that were sent.
nodes.formula: Number of Emails Sent
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Monitoring "Emails Sent" metric is fundamental for assessing the scale of outreach. It guides decisions on resource allocation, campaign strategy, and audience segmentation. Tracking the number of emails sent provides insights into campaign scope, facilitating efficient planning and execution of email marketing initiatives.
nodes.questions: 1. How do fluctuations in the number of emails sent impact the overall success of an email marketing campaign? 2. What factors contribute to variations in the number of emails sent across different campaigns or time periods? 3. How can the analysis of emails sent inform decisions about budget allocation and resource planning for future campaigns? 4. What role does email list segmentation play in determining the appropriate number of emails to be sent? 5. How can historical data on emails sent be used to predict future campaign volumes and optimize marketing strategies? 6. How does the size of the email subscriber list impact the scalability of email campaigns and associated costs? 7. Can the number of emails sent be used as a key performance indicator (KPI) for the efficiency of email marketing processes?
nodes.slices: - Time of Day - Day of the Week - Target Audience - Email Content Type - Email List Health Score

nodes.description: The Forward Rate measures the percentage of email recipients who forward a given email to others.
nodes.formula: (Forwards / Opened) * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: A higher forward rate indicates content resonance and potential list growth. This metric guides marketers in crafting valuable content that encourages recipients to share, contributing to organic audience expansion. Monitoring forward rates provides insights into content effectiveness and the potential for increased brand reach.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the forward rate metric contribute to the organic growth of the email subscriber list? 2. What types of content or campaigns are more likely to be forwarded by recipients? 3. Are there correlations between the forward rate and the overall engagement or satisfaction of email subscribers? 4. How can the forward rate be used to identify content that resonates well with the audience and potential new subscribers? 5. What role do social sharing and integration play in influencing the forward rate of emails? 6. Can the analysis of the forward rate provide insights into the virality and shareability of email content? 7. How does the forward rate metric align with the organization's goals for audience growth and brand visibility?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Uninspiring Content. If the content lacks shareability or fails to evoke interest, recipients are less likely to forward the email to others, reducing the forward rate. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Complex or Lengthy Content. Emails with overly complicated or lengthy content may discourage recipients from taking the time to forward them, impacting the forward rate. 🔹 Lack of Clear Call-to-Action. If the email doesn't provide a clear and compelling reason or call-to-action to forward, recipients may not feel motivated to share it with others. 🔹 Inappropriate Timing. Sending emails at times when recipients are less likely to engage or share content, such as during non-peak hours, can reduce the likelihood of forwarding. 🔹 Inadequate Incentives. Failing to offer incentives or rewards for forwarding emails may diminish the recipients' motivation to share the content with others, affecting the forward rate. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Mobile-Unfriendly Design. Emails that are not optimized for mobile devices may discourage sharing, as many users access and interact with emails on mobile platforms. 🔹 Overly Promotional Content. Content that is overly promotional or sales-oriented may be less likely to be shared, as recipients may perceive it as less valuable to their network.
nodes.slices: - Email Content Type - Time Since Email Delivery - Target Audience - Social Media Integration

nodes.description: Email Forwards is a count of the number of times an email was forwarded. This metric gives your marketing team insight into how useful, informative, and relevant your email content is to your users. It is also an indicator of potential list growth as recipients may then sign up for your email campaigns.
nodes.formula: Number of Email Forwards
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: This metric provides insights into the perceived value and relevance of email content. Tracking forwards guides marketers in understanding content virality and its impact on list growth. It serves as an indicator of user engagement and can inform strategies to enhance content quality and encourage subscribers to share valuable information.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the number of forwards correlate with the overall success and reach of an email marketing campaign? 2. Are there specific types of content or campaigns that tend to generate a higher number of forwards? 3. How can the analysis of forwards inform decisions about content strategy and campaign planning? 4. What role do incentives or promotions play in encouraging recipients to forward emails? 5. How does the number of forwards vary based on the timing or frequency of email communications? 6. Can the tracking of forwards be used as a leading indicator for potential viral marketing opportunities? 7. What measures can be taken to increase the number of forwards and leverage this metric for audience growth?
nodes.slices: - Email Content Type - Target Audience - Time Since Email Delivery - Social Media Integration

nodes.description: The number of emails that weren’t delivered to recipients’ inboxes. A hard bounce indicates a permanent reason an email cannot be delivered. In most cases, bounced email addresses are cleaned from your audience automatically and immediately. Cleaned addresses will be excluded from all future campaign sends. Here are some common reasons an email may hard bounce. - Recipient email address doesn't exist. - Recipient email server has completely blocked delivery.
nodes.formula: Number of Hard Bounces
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Monitoring this metric is crucial for maintaining list hygiene and sender reputation. Addressing hard bounces promptly ensures a clean and responsive subscriber base. This metric guides marketers in automating list cleaning processes, enhancing deliverability, and optimizing overall email campaign performance.
nodes.questions: 1. How do hard bounces impact the overall health of an email subscriber list, and what measures can be taken to minimize them? 2. What are the common reasons for emails to hard bounce, and how can these issues be addressed proactively? 3. How does the process of cleaning bounced email addresses contribute to maintaining list hygiene? 4. Are there specific trends or patterns in hard bounces that can help identify potential issues with email content or delivery? 5. How does the handling of hard bounces align with best practices for email deliverability and sender reputation? 6. Can the analysis of hard bounces provide insights into the overall quality of the email subscriber acquisition process? 7. What role does the distinction between hard and soft bounces play in optimizing email list management strategies?
nodes.slices: - Bounce Reason - Email Content Category - Sender Reputation - Geographical Location - Device or Email Client

nodes.description: Inactive subscribers are people on your email list who aren't engaging with your emails anymore. They're not opening, clicking, or taking any other actions. These “sleepy” subscribers can hurt your email performance metrics and even affect deliverability if you're not careful. Many marketers try re-engagement campaigns to wake them up and get them interested again.
nodes.formula: Number of Inactive Subscribers
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Inactive Subscribers signify disengagement and potential deliverability challenges. This metric's importance lies in identifying and reactivating dormant subscribers to improve overall email performance. Implementing re-engagement campaigns based on this metric can awaken inactive subscribers, enhancing engagement, and mitigating the negative impact of dormant contacts on deliverability and sender reputation.
nodes.questions: 1. How do inactive subscribers impact the overall performance and effectiveness of email campaigns? 2. What strategies can be employed to re-engage inactive subscribers and improve their participation? 3. Are there specific triggers or indicators that signal subscriber inactivity, and how can they be monitored effectively? 4. How does the presence of inactive subscribers affect email deliverability and sender reputation over time? 5. Can the analysis of inactive subscribers inform decisions about email content, frequency, or targeting? 6. What role do re-engagement campaigns play in mitigating the impact of inactive subscribers on email metrics? 7. How can the identification of inactive subscribers contribute to a more streamlined and efficient email marketing strategy?
nodes.slices: - Subscriber Engagement Level - Time Since Last Interaction - Email List Health Score

nodes.description: The percentage of total recipients (successful deliveries) who opened the email campaign.
nodes.formula: (Opened / Successful deliveries) * 100
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: Open Rate is a key metric indicating the effectiveness of an email campaign. Monitoring this percentage provides insights into audience engagement and the relevance of content. A higher open rate signifies successful communication, guiding marketers to optimize subject lines, content, and timing for enhanced campaign performance and improved overall user experience.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the open rate metric reflect the level of engagement and interest among the email audience? 2. What factors influence the open rate, and how can they be optimized to improve overall campaign performance? 3. Are there differences in open rates based on the type of email content or the purpose of the campaign? 4. How can A/B testing be leveraged to identify strategies for increasing the open rate? 5. What role does the email subject line play in driving variations in open rates across different campaigns? 6. Can the open rate metric be used as a predictor of overall campaign success and audience satisfaction? 7. How does the open rate compare to industry benchmarks, and what insights can be gained from benchmarking?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Poor Email Subject Lines. Subject lines that fail to capture attention or are misleading may discourage recipients from opening the email, leading to a decrease in the open rate. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Unengaging Preview Text. Preview text is often visible before opening an email. If it fails to entice recipients, they may decide not to open the email, impacting the overall open rate. 🔹 Email Frequency. Over-sending emails can result in subscriber fatigue, causing them to ignore or skip over emails, thus lowering the open rate. 🔹 Email Content Relevance. Irrelevant or mismatched content with subscriber expectations can reduce the likelihood of recipients opening the email, affecting the open rate negatively. 🔹 Timing Issues. Sending emails at times when recipients are less likely to check their inboxes can lead to lower open rates. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Inactive Subscriber Base. A significant portion of inactive subscribers may contribute to a lower open rate, as they are not engaged or interested in the content. 🔹 Changes in Email Platform. Updates or changes in email platforms may affect how emails are displayed or delivered, influencing the open rate.
nodes.slices: - Email Subject Lines - Day of the Week - Subscriber Segmentation - Device Type

nodes.description: The number of recipients who opened the email campaign
nodes.formula: Number of Recipients who opened the email campaign
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: The "Opened" metric is fundamental for evaluating the success of email content and campaign strategy. Tracking the number of opens guides marketers in understanding audience preferences, facilitating targeted content creation, and optimizing future campaigns for increased open rates and overall user engagement.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the number of opens correlate with the overall success and impact of an email marketing campaign? 2. How does the timing of email sends impact the number of opens, and are there optimal times for different audience segments? 4. What role do personalization and segmentation play in driving variations in the number of opens across campaigns? 5. How does the number of opens align with the overall goals and objectives of the email marketing strategy? 6. Can the analysis of opened metrics provide insights into audience preferences and content resonance? 7. What measures can be taken to encourage higher open rates and enhance the overall impact of email campaigns?
nodes.slices: - Email Content Type - Email Subject Lines - Day of the Week - Subscriber Segmentation - Device Type

nodes.description: The average number of opens per subscriber.
nodes.formula: Total Opens / Successful Deliveries
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Opens per Successful Delivery offers a granular view of subscriber engagement. A higher average indicates a more engaged audience. Monitoring this metric helps marketers tailor content to individual preferences, maximizing the impact of each campaign. It guides strategies to enhance personalization and relevance, contributing to improved overall campaign effectiveness and subscriber satisfaction.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the average number of opens per subscriber reflect the engagement levels and responsiveness of the email audience? 2. What role does the frequency of email communications play in influencing the opens per successful delivery metric? 3. Are there trends in opens per successful delivery based on different devices (desktop, mobile)? 4. How can personalization and targeted content contribute to variations in the opens per successful delivery metric? 5. How does the opens per successful delivery metric align with overall goals for audience engagement and retention? 6. Can the analysis of opens per successful delivery help identify areas for improvement in email content or segmentation? 7. What strategies can be employed to increase the opens per successful delivery and enhance the overall user experience?
nodes.slices: - Email Content Type - Time Since Email Delivery - Target Audience - Email List Health Score

nodes.description: The percentage of people who willingly subscribe or opt in to receive emails from a particular sender or marketing campaign.
nodes.formula: (Opt-Ins / Number of visitors or impressions) * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This percentage is crucial for building a responsive and engaged subscriber base. Tracking opt-ins guides marketers in evaluating the appeal of campaigns and refining strategies to attract quality subscribers. It contributes to list health, deliverability, and overall campaign success, ensuring that communication reaches an interested audience.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the opt-in rate metric contribute to the assessment of the effectiveness and ethicality of email subscriber acquisition? 2. What strategies can be employed to increase the opt-in rate and build a more engaged and willing subscriber base? 3. How does transparency and clear communication impact the opt-in rate in email marketing? 4. Are there differences in opt-in rates based on the source of subscriber acquisition (e.g., website, events)? 5. How can the opt-in rate metric be used as an indicator of the overall quality of the subscriber list? 6. Can the opt-in rate metric provide insights into the success of lead generation strategies and campaigns? 7. What role does the alignment between subscriber expectations and delivered content play in optimizing the opt-in rate?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Poorly Designed Opt-in Forms. Complicated or unappealing opt-in forms may discourage visitors from subscribing, resulting in a decrease in the opt-in rate. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Lack of Incentives. Not providing compelling incentives for visitors to opt in can reduce the overall opt-in rate as people may not see the value in subscribing. 🔹 Misleading Sign-Up Prompts. If the sign-up prompts are misleading or unclear about the type of content subscribers will receive, it can impact trust and reduce the opt-in rate. 🔹 Website User Experience. A poor website user experience, including slow loading times or navigation issues, may deter visitors from completing the opt-in process. 🔹 Ineffective Call-to-Action. Failing to guide visitors with a clear and compelling call-to-action can result in a lower opt-in rate, as visitors may not understand how to subscribe. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Competition and Saturation. Increased competition or saturation in the market may make it challenging to attract subscribers, affecting the opt-in rate. 🔹 Lack of Trustworthy Branding. Visitors may hesitate to subscribe if the brand lacks trustworthiness or credibility, impacting the opt-in rate.
nodes.slices: - Sign-up Source - Time of Day - Offer or Incentive - Campaign Type - Device Type

nodes.description: Total email opt-ins you get for the period
nodes.formula: Total number of Email Opt-Ins
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: The "Opt-Ins" metric is essential for measuring the success of acquisition efforts. Tracking opt-ins guides marketers in assessing the effectiveness of various channels and campaigns, informing strategies to optimize lead generation, expand the subscriber base, and enhance the overall impact of email marketing initiatives.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the total number of opt-ins correlate with the success and growth of an email subscriber list? 2. Are there specific incentives or promotional strategies that tend to generate more opt-ins? 3. How can the analysis of opt-ins inform decisions about future marketing strategies and campaigns? 4. What is the relationship between opt-ins and subsequent engagement levels and conversion rates? 5. How do external factors, such as industry trends or changes in consumer behavior, impact the number of opt-ins? 6. Can the tracking of opt-ins be used as a leading indicator for potential list growth and increased audience reach? 7. What measures can be taken to maximize the number of opt-ins and build a more robust and responsive email subscriber base?
nodes.slices: - Email List Health Score - List Growth Over Time - Geographical Location - Target Audience - Social Media Integration

nodes.description: List churn is when you lose subscribers from your email list over time. People might unsubscribe, change their email, or get bounced off for other reasons. It's a normal thing, but if you're losing subscribers faster than you're adding new ones, it's time to take a closer look at what's going on with your emails.
nodes.formula: (Number of Opt-Ins / Number of Unsubscribers)
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Opt-Ins per Unsubscriber provides insights into list churn and the potential for recovering lost subscribers. This ratio guides marketers in understanding the balance between gaining and losing subscribers. Monitoring this metric informs strategies to minimize churn, improve engagement, and implement targeted re-engagement campaigns, ultimately contributing to sustained list growth and health.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the ratio of opt-ins to unsubscribers contribute to understanding list churn and subscriber attrition over time? 2. Are there specific triggers or patterns that indicate an increase in opt-ins or unsubscribes, and how can they be analyzed? 3. How can the analysis of opt-ins per unsubscriber guide decisions about list management and audience targeting? 4. What strategies can be employed to balance the ratio and minimize the impact of list churn on overall list health? 5. Can the opt-ins per unsubscriber metric be used to identify areas for improvement in email content or subscriber engagement? 6. What role do re-engagement campaigns play in influencing the opt-ins per unsubscriber ratio? 7. How does the opt-ins per unsubscriber metric align with industry benchmarks, and what insights can be gained from this?
nodes.slices: - Time Since Unsubscription - Email Content Type - Subscriber Engagement Level

nodes.description: The number of emails that weren’t delivered to your recipients’ inboxes. Soft bounces typically indicate a temporary delivery issue
nodes.formula: Number of Soft Bounces
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking this number is crucial for identifying and resolving issues promptly. Monitoring soft bounces guides marketers in maintaining list hygiene, optimizing content and targeting strategies, and ensuring that email campaigns reach recipients' inboxes effectively. This contributes to improved deliverability and overall campaign success.
nodes.questions: 1. How do soft bounces differ from hard bounces, and what are the typical causes of soft bounces in email marketing? 2. What strategies can be employed to minimize the occurrence of soft bounces and improve overall email deliverability? 3. How do fluctuations in the number of soft bounces correlate with changes in email content, frequency, or audience segments? 4. Are there patterns in soft bounces that can help identify potential issues with the email infrastructure or sender reputation? 5. How can the analysis of soft bounces contribute to a more proactive approach to list management and email distribution? 6. Can the distinction between hard and soft bounces inform decisions about the urgency of addressing email delivery issues? 7. What role does the identification and resolution of soft bounces play in maintaining a positive sender reputation over time?
nodes.slices: - Bounce Reason - Email Content Category - Sender Reputation - Geographical Location - Device or Email Client

nodes.description: The percentage of emails that recipients mark as spam.
nodes.formula: (Spam Complaints / Successful Deliveries) * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: A higher Spam Complaint rate can harm sender reputation and deliverability. Tracking this percentage guides marketers in identifying and addressing content or targeting issues promptly. It ensures better engagement and reduces the risk of being marked as spam, contributing to improved email campaign performance and overall sender reputation.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the spam complaint rate impact email deliverability and the overall reputation of the sender's domain? 2. What strategies can be implemented to minimize the spam complaint rate and maintain a positive relationship with recipients? 3. How does the spam complaint rate correlate with the content, frequency, or targeting of email campaigns? 4. Are there specific triggers or content elements that tend to result in higher spam complaint rates? 5. How can the analysis of spam complaint rates guide decisions about email content, segmentation, and subscriber engagement? 6. Can the spam complaint rate be used as an early warning system for potential deliverability issues and list hygiene concerns? 7. What role does transparency and clear communication play in minimizing the likelihood of recipients marking emails as spam?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Misleading Content. Content that is deceptive or does not match the expectations set by the subject line can lead to recipients marking emails as spam, increasing the spam complaint rate. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Overly Aggressive Marketing. Aggressive or pushy marketing tactics can annoy recipients, prompting them to mark emails as spam, negatively impacting the spam complaint rate. 🔹 Frequency of Email Sends. Sending emails too frequently may irritate subscribers, leading to an increase in spam complaints. 🔹 Poorly Maintained Email List. An outdated or poorly maintained email list may result in sending emails to uninterested recipients, leading to more spam complaints. 🔹 Lack of Transparency. Failing to clearly communicate the nature of emails during the opt-in process may result in recipients marking emails as spam due to a lack of transparency. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Third-party List Purchases. Purchasing email lists from third parties can lead to sending emails to recipients who did not explicitly opt in, increasing the likelihood of spam complaints. 🔹 Email Platform Issues. Technical issues with the email platform, such as misconfigurations or server problems, may result in emails being marked as spam, affecting the spam complaint rate.
nodes.slices: - Email Content Type - Sender Reputation - Target Audience - Email List Health Score - Time of Day

nodes.description: Spam score is a rating that predicts the likelihood of an email being marked as spam by email providers. It's based on various factors like email content, sender reputation, and compliance with email best practices. A lower score means your email is less likely to end up in the spam folder, while a higher score increases that risk. Email marketers use this score to tweak their campaigns and improve deliverability.
nodes.formula: No specific formula (Rating based on factors like email content, sender reputation, and compliance)
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Though lacking a specific formula, it influences email placement and sender reputation. Monitoring this score guides marketers in adjusting campaigns based on content, sender reputation, and compliance. A lower score enhances deliverability, while a higher score may trigger spam filters. This metric contributes to improved campaign effectiveness and inbox placement.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the spam score contribute to the overall success and deliverability of an email marketing campaign? 2. What specific factors influence the spam score, and how can marketers address these factors to improve their score? 3. How does the spam score correlate with the content, design, and formatting of email campaigns? 4. Are there industry benchmarks for acceptable spam scores, and how does the organization compare to these benchmarks? 5. Can the analysis of spam scores help identify areas for improvement in email content, sender reputation, or compliance? 6. How frequently should marketers monitor and reassess their spam scores to ensure ongoing deliverability success? 7. What role does the transparency of email practices and adherence to best practices play in maintaining a low spam score?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Frequent Changes in Content. Rapid or frequent changes in email content may trigger spam filters, increasing the spam score and the risk of emails being marked as spam. 🔹 Negative Sender Reputation. A poor sender reputation, characterized by a history of spam complaints or low engagement, can significantly contribute to a higher spam score. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Non-compliance with Best Practices. Failing to adhere to email best practices, such as proper formatting and authentication, can increase the spam score, impacting email deliverability. 🔹 High Volume of Email Sends. Sending a large volume of emails in a short period can trigger spam filters, raising the spam score and reducing email deliverability. 🔹 Inconsistent Sending Patterns. Drastic changes in sending patterns, such as irregular frequencies, may raise suspicions and contribute to a higher spam score. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Use of Certain Keywords. The use of specific words or phrases commonly associated with spam may contribute to a higher spam score, affecting email deliverability. 🔹 Inaccurate Subscriber Targeting. Sending emails to recipients who are not part of the target audience may lead to a higher spam score as it suggests non-compliance with best practices.
nodes.slices: - Email Content Type - Sender Reputation - Email Authentication - Target Audience

tactics.tactic: A/B Testing for Content and Formatting
Minimize Use of Spam Trigger Words

nodes.description: A qualitative measure of subscriber satisfaction gathered through surveys or feedback mechanisms.
nodes.formula: Sum of Feedback / Number of Feedback
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: This metric, gathered through surveys or feedback mechanisms, guides marketers in understanding user perceptions. Analyzing feedback helps refine content, enhance personalization, and address subscriber concerns. This contributes to improved overall user satisfaction, engagement, and the success of future email campaigns.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the subscriber feedback score provide insights into the overall satisfaction and engagement of email subscribers? 2. What methods are effective in collecting subscriber feedback, and how can this feedback be used to inform email strategies? 3. How does the subscriber feedback score vary across different types of email campaigns (e.g., promotional, informational)? 4. Are there specific trends or patterns in subscriber feedback that can help identify areas for improvement in email content? 5. Can the subscriber feedback score be used as a leading indicator for potential issues with subscriber retention and loyalty? 6. How does the timing and frequency of feedback collection impact the accuracy and reliability of the subscriber feedback score? 7. What role does the transparency of feedback mechanisms and the responsiveness to subscriber concerns play in shaping the score?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Lack of Feedback Mechanisms. Insufficient avenues for subscribers to provide feedback can result in a lower subscriber feedback score, limiting insights into their satisfaction. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Ignoring Subscriber Feedback. Not taking action or responding to subscriber feedback promptly may lead to dissatisfaction and a lower subscriber feedback score. 🔹 Unreliable Survey Methods. If the methods used to gather feedback, such as surveys, are unreliable or biased, the resulting subscriber feedback score may not accurately reflect satisfaction. 🔹 Inconsistent Survey Frequency. Irregular or infrequent surveying may lead to outdated or incomplete feedback, affecting the accuracy of the subscriber feedback score. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Lack of Personalization in Surveys. Generic or non-personalized surveys may yield less insightful feedback, impacting the overall subscriber feedback score. 🔹 Changes in Customer Expectations. Shifts in customer expectations that are not reflected in survey questions may lead to a mismatch between perceived satisfaction and actual experience.
nodes.slices: - Survey Methodology - Frequency of Feedback - Feedback Categories - Target Audience

nodes.description: The metric tracking the rate at how rapidly your entire subscriber list is growing.
nodes.formula: ((Total Subscribers at End of Period - Total Subscribers at Start of Period) / Total Subscribers at Start) * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking Subscribers List Growth rate is essential for assessing the success of acquisition efforts. A positive growth rate indicates effective strategies in attracting and retaining subscribers. Monitoring this metric guides marketers in evaluating the impact of campaigns, optimizing acquisition channels, and ensuring sustained list growth for successful long-term email marketing initiatives.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the subscribers list growth rate contribute to the assessment of the overall health and vitality of an email subscriber base? 2. What strategies can be employed to accelerate or optimize the growth rate of the email subscriber list over time? 3. How does the subscribers list growth rate vary across different acquisition channels or marketing campaigns? 4. Are there external factors, such as industry trends or changes in consumer behavior, that impact the list growth rate? 5. Can the analysis of list growth rates help identify effective channels or campaigns for acquiring new subscribers? 6. How does the list growth rate align with the organization's goals for audience reach and market penetration? 7. nodes.description: The percentage of recipients who opened the email campaign and unsubscribed.
nodes.formula: (Number of Unsubscribed / Total Subscribers) * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Monitoring this rate is crucial for understanding subscriber sentiment and content relevance. A low unsubscribe rate suggests positive engagement, while a high rate may indicate issues with content or targeting. This metric guides marketers in refining content and strategies to minimize opt-outs, ensuring sustained subscriber growth.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the unsubscribe rate metric reflect the level of audience satisfaction and engagement with email content? 2. What factors contribute to variations in the unsubscribe rate, and how can these factors be addressed proactively? 3. Are there specific triggers or content elements that tend to result in higher unsubscribe rates? 4. How does the unsubscribe rate correlate with the frequency and timing of email communications? 5. Can the analysis of unsubscribe rates provide insights into potential improvements in email content or targeting? 6. What role does the transparency of the unsubscribe process and clear communication play in minimizing unsubscribe rates? 7. How does the unsubscribe rate metric align with industry benchmarks, and what insights can be gained from benchmarking?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Overwhelming Email Frequency. Sending emails too frequently can lead to subscriber fatigue, prompting them to unsubscribe from future communications. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Irrelevant Content. If the content of the emails is not aligned with the subscribers' interests, they may choose to unsubscribe, resulting in a higher unsubscribe rate. 🔹 Lack of Email Preferences. Failing to provide options for subscribers to customize their email preferences may lead to dissatisfaction and a higher likelihood of unsubscribing. 🔹 Poorly Timed Campaigns. Sending campaigns at inconvenient times, such as during holidays or important events, may contribute to a higher unsubscribe rate. 🔹 Changes in Subscriber Needs. If the products or services offered no longer align with subscriber needs or expectations, it may lead to a higher unsubscribe rate. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Difficulty in Unsubscribing. Complicated or unclear unsubscribe processes may frustrate subscribers, leading to a higher unsubscribe rate. 🔹 Lack of Subscriber Engagement. Low engagement levels, measured by opens and clicks, may indicate disinterest and contribute to a higher unsubscribe rate.
nodes.slices: - Email Content Type - Frequency of Emails - Subscriber Segmentation - Time of Day - Target Audience

nodes.description: The average monetary value of abandoned carts, providing insights into potential revenue loss from incomplete purchases.
nodes.formula: (Sum of values in abandoned carts) / (Number of abandoned carts)

nodes.description: The average duration users spend viewing their shopping carts, offering insights into user engagement with the cart contents.

nodes.description: The average percentage discount applied to orders using promotional codes during the checkout process.
nodes.formula: (Sum of discount percentages from all promo code uses) / (Number of promo code uses)
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric reveals the effectiveness of promotional campaigns and helps assess the impact of discounts on user behavior. It informs decisions on promotional strategies and helps optimize discount offerings for better results.
nodes.questions: 1. How do discount percentages vary between different promo codes, and which codes result in higher overall discounts for users? 2. Is there a correlation between promo code usage and average cart value, and how can strategic promotions impact both metrics positively? 3. What is the frequency distribution of discount percentages, and are there outliers or irregularities that require attention? 4. How do promo code usage patterns differ among user segments, and are there opportunities to tailor promotions to specific customer profiles? 5. Can seasonality in promo code usage be identified, and how does it align with broader marketing campaigns or external events? 6. What is the impact of expired or invalid promo codes on average discount, and how can this be mitigated to enhance user satisfaction? 7. How does average discount by promo code align with customer loyalty or retention efforts, and are there opportunities to integrate discounts strategically?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Increased Use of High-Value Promo Codes. Frequent use of high-value promo codes can result in a higher average discount, impacting the metric. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Limited Restrictions on Promo Codes. If promo codes have minimal restrictions, users may apply them more frequently, leading to a higher average discount. 🔹 Promo Code Leakage. Promo codes being shared or leaked outside the intended audience can lead to increased use and higher average discounts. 🔹 Highly Competitive Market. In a highly competitive market, businesses may offer higher discounts to attract customers, impacting the average discount. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Ineffective Promo Code Campaigns. Poorly executed promo code campaigns may result in users applying them more frequently, influencing the average discount. 🔹 Seasonal Sales and Special Events. Seasonal sales and special events can lead to increased promo code usage, impacting the average discount.
nodes.slices: - Promo Code Type - Customer Segmentation - Product Category - Order Value Threshold - Promo Code Usage Frequency

nodes.description: Checkout Conversion Rate (CCR) is the percentage of customers who complete the checkout process after they initially start it.
nodes.formula: (Number of placed orders) / (Number сheckouts) * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Checkout Conversion Rate (CCR) is a pivotal metric for assessing the efficiency of the checkout process. Monitoring CCR provides businesses with insights into the percentage of customers who successfully complete transactions after initiating the checkout. This metric is crucial for optimizing the checkout flow, reducing abandonment, and improving overall conversion rates.
nodes.questions: 1. What are the common reasons for customers not completing the checkout process? 2. How does Checkout Conversion vary across different marketing channels? 3. Is there a correlation between Checkout Conversion and product prices? 4. What impact do trust signals (e.g., security badges) have on Checkout Conversion? 5. How can the checkout process be streamlined to improve Checkout Conversion? 6. Are there specific days of the week or times of day with higher Checkout Conversion rates? 7. How do discounts and promotions influence Checkout Conversion?

nodes.description: Checkout Engagement Rate (CER) is the percentage of customers who started the checkout process after reaching the cart page.
nodes.formula: (Checkouts / Cart Views) * 100
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: Checkout Engagement Rate (CER) indicates the level of interest customers have in the checkout process after reaching the cart page. Monitoring this metric helps businesses understand user engagement and identify areas for improvement in the checkout journey.
nodes.questions: 1. What elements of the checkout page contribute to higher Checkout Engagement? 2. How does Checkout Engagement differ between first-time and repeat customers? 3. Are there particular devices or browsers that show variations in Checkout Engagement? 4. What is the impact of offering guest checkout on Checkout Engagement? 5. Does the length of the checkout process affect Checkout Engagement? 6. Are there geographical differences in Checkout Engagement rates? 7. How does Checkout Engagement correlate with overall site engagement metrics?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Lack of Incentives or Offers. Absence of incentives or offers during the checkout process may result in reduced engagement and interest from users. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Complicated Checkout Process. A complex checkout process may discourage users from actively engaging during the checkout, impacting the engagement rate. 🔹 Technical Glitches in Checkout Process. Technical issues during the checkout process can disrupt the user experience and reduce engagement with the checkout. 🔹 Insufficient Payment Options. Limited payment options may lead to decreased engagement during the checkout if users cannot choose their preferred method. 🔹 Lack of Personalization. Personalized elements during the checkout, such as tailored recommendations, can enhance user engagement and interaction. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Seasonal Peaks in Traffic. High traffic during peak seasons or events may lead to increased checkout abandonment, impacting the engagement rate.

nodes.description: The number of times users started the checkout process.
nodes.formula: Count of initiated checkouts
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: The number of checkouts initiated by users is crucial for evaluating user interest in completing transactions. Tracking checkouts provides insights into user behavior and allows businesses to optimize their checkout strategies for increased conversions.
nodes.questions: 1. What are the most common entry points for users into the checkout process? 2. How do Checkouts vary based on the source of traffic (organic, paid, referral)? 3. Are there product categories with higher or lower Checkouts? 4. How does the checkout frequency change during holiday seasons? 5. What role do product recommendations play in driving users to initiate Checkouts? 6. Are there differences in Checkouts between desktop and mobile users? 7. How does the effectiveness of marketing campaigns impact the number of Checkouts?
nodes.slices: - User Segmentation - Device Comparison - Time-Based Analysis - Product Category - Geographic Location

nodes.id: Conversion to Adding a Payment Method
nodes.description: The percentage of users who added a payment method out of those who selected a delivery method.
nodes.formula: (Payment Method Added / Shipping Method Added) * 100
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: Understanding the percentage of users adding a payment method out of those selecting a delivery method is vital for optimizing the payment process. This metric guides efforts to streamline payment steps, reduce friction, and enhance overall checkout completion rates.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the user journey differ between selecting a delivery method and adding a payment method? 2. Are there specific payment methods that show higher conversion rates? 3. Does the presentation of payment options impact the conversion to adding a payment method? 4. What role does the checkout page design play in the conversion to adding a payment method? 5. Are there security concerns that affect the conversion to adding a payment method? 6. How does the conversion to adding a payment method change for registered vs. guest users? 7. What strategies can be implemented to encourage users to add a payment method?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Limited Payment Options. Users may be less likely to add a payment method if they have limited options or cannot use their preferred method. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Lack of Clear Instructions. Unclear instructions or a complicated process for adding a payment method may discourage users from completing this step. 🔹 Technical Glitches in Payment Process. Technical issues during the payment method addition process can create obstacles and reduce the likelihood of completion. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Seasonal Peaks in Traffic. High traffic during peak seasons or events may lead to increased user hesitation or difficulties in adding payment methods. 🔹 Changes in User Behavior. Shifts in user behavior, such as reduced interest in adding payment methods, can contribute to variations in conversion to payment method.
nodes.slices: - User Type Analysis - Device Comparison - Geographic Breakdown - Order History - Payment Method Options

nodes.id: Conversion to Adding a Shipping Method
nodes.description: The percentage of users who added a shipping method out of those who logged in.
nodes.formula: (Shipping Method Added / User Logged in) * 100
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: The percentage of users adding a shipping method out of those who log in provides insights into user preferences. It helps optimize shipping options to align with customer expectations, ultimately improving the checkout experience and increasing the likelihood of completed transactions.
nodes.questions: 1. How do users' shipping preferences impact the conversion to adding a shipping method? 2. Are there seasonal trends in the conversion to adding a shipping method? 3. What is the impact of providing multiple shipping options on conversion? 4. Do shipping costs influence the conversion to adding a shipping method? 5. How does the conversion to adding a shipping method differ for one-time shoppers vs. repeat customers? 6. Are there specific geographies where users are more likely to complete the shipping method step? 7. How can the user interface be optimized to facilitate the conversion to adding a shipping method?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Lack of Clear Instructions. Unclear instructions or a complicated process for adding a shipping method may discourage users from completing this step. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Limited Shipping Options. Users may be less likely to add a shipping method if they have limited options or cannot choose their preferred method. 🔹 Technical Glitches in Checkout Process. Technical issues during the shipping method addition process can create obstacles and reduce the likelihood of completion. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Seasonal Peaks in Traffic. High traffic during peak seasons or events may lead to increased user hesitation or difficulties in adding shipping methods. 🔹 Changes in User Behavior. Shifts in user behavior, such as reduced interest in adding shipping methods, can contribute to variations in conversion to shipping method.
nodes.slices: - User Type Analysis - Device Comparison - Geographic Breakdown - Checkout Flow Experience - Previous Shipping Preferences

nodes.description: The percentage of users who completed the authorization step, which could involve creating an account or confirming access, out of those who initiated the checkout process.
nodes.formula: (User Logged in / Checkouts) * 100
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: The percentage of users completing the authorization step out of those who initiated the checkout process is crucial for enhancing user trust and streamlining account creation. Businesses can use this metric to optimize the authorization process, contributing to improved conversion rates.
nodes.questions: 1. What factors contribute to a higher conversion rate to authorization? 2. How does the complexity of the authorization process impact conversion rates? 3. Are there differences in conversion to authorization between desktop and mobile users? 4. What role does the clarity of authorization instructions play in the conversion process? 5. How does the conversion to authorization vary for users with existing accounts vs. new users? 6. Are there legal or regulatory requirements that affect the conversion to authorization? 7. What strategies can be employed to reduce friction and improve conversion to authorization?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Limited Trust in Website. Users may be hesitant to complete the authorization step if they lack trust in the website's security or credibility. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Complicated Authorization Process. A complex or unclear authorization process may lead to higher abandonment rates and lower conversion to authorization. 🔹 Technical Glitches in Checkout Process. Technical issues during the authorization process can create obstacles and reduce the likelihood of completion. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Seasonal Peaks in Traffic. High traffic during peak seasons or events may lead to increased user hesitation or difficulties in completing the authorization step. 🔹 Changes in User Behavior. Shifts in user behavior, such as reduced interest in completing the authorization step, can contribute to variations in conversion to authorization.
nodes.slices: - User Type Analysis - Device Comparison - Geographic Breakdown - Checkout Flow Experience - Previous Account Creation

nodes.description: The percentage of users who viewed their shopping cart out of those who added something to their cart.
nodes.formula: (Cart Views / Users who added something to their cart) * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric is essential for evaluating the effectiveness of cart engagement strategies and optimizing the user experience. It guides efforts to enhance cart visibility, potentially increasing overall conversion rates.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the presentation of recommended products impact the conversion to cart view? 2. Are there specific product categories with higher or lower conversion to cart view rates? 3. What role do personalized promotions play in driving conversion to cart view? 4. How does the user's browsing history influence the conversion to cart view? 5. Are there differences in conversion to cart view between logged-in and guest users? 6. How can the website layout and design be optimized to enhance the conversion to cart view? 7. Does the speed of the website or app impact the conversion to cart view?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Lack of Clear Call-to-Action. If the call-to-action for viewing the shopping cart is unclear or not prominent, users may overlook or miss the opportunity to view their carts. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Limited Visibility of Cart View Button. A poorly visible or hidden cart view button may reduce the number of users initiating the action to view their shopping carts. 🔹 Technical Glitches in Website. Technical issues related to website performance or functionality may discourage users from initiating the cart view action. 🔹 Lack of Cart Engagement Features. The absence of engaging features or incentives in the cart may lead to fewer users initiating the action to view their shopping carts. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Seasonal Trends and Events. External factors such as seasonal trends or events can influence user initiation of the cart view action, impacting the metric. 🔹 Changes in User Behavior. Shifts in user behavior, such as reduced interest in initiating the cart view action, can contribute to variations in conversion to cart view.
nodes.slices: - User Type Analysis - Device Comparison - Geographic Breakdown - Shopping Behavior - Product Category

nodes.description: The percentage of users who successfully placed an order out of those who selected a payment method.
nodes.formula: (Placed Orders / Payment Method Added) * 100
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: The percentage of users successfully placing an order out of those who selected a payment method is crucial for assessing the effectiveness of the order placement process. This metric guides strategies to optimize the order completion flow and increase overall conversion rates.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the conversion to placing an order vary across different marketing channels? 2. Are there specific demographics that show higher conversion to placing an order? 3. How does the conversion to placing an order change during peak shopping seasons? 4. What role do customer reviews and ratings play in the conversion to placing an order? 5. Are there differences in conversion to placing an order between desktop and mobile users? 6. How does the ease of the return process impact the conversion to placing an order? 7. What strategies can be employed to enhance the overall user experience and improve conversion to placing an order?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Complicated Checkout Process. A complex and lengthy checkout process may deter users from successfully placing orders, impacting the conversion rate. 🔹 Technical Glitches in Checkout Process. Technical issues during the checkout process can create obstacles and reduce the likelihood of users placing orders. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Lack of Trust in Website Security. Users may hesitate to place an order if they have concerns about the security of the website. 🔹 Insufficient Payment Options. Limited payment options may lead to lower conversion rates if users cannot choose their preferred payment method. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Seasonal Peaks in Traffic. High traffic during peak seasons or events may lead to increased order placement abandonment, impacting the conversion rate. 🔹 Changes in User Behavior. Shifts in user behavior, such as reduced interest in completing order placements, can contribute to variations in conversion.
nodes.slices: - User Type Analysis - Device Comparison - Geographic Breakdown - Checkout Flow Experience - Product Category

nodes.description: The average cost incurred for each successfully placed order, providing insights into the efficiency of the marketing and sales efforts.
nodes.formula: Marketing Costs / Placed Orders
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Cost per Order is a critical metric for evaluating the efficiency of marketing and sales efforts. Monitoring this metric provides insights into the average cost incurred for each successfully placed order. Businesses can use this information to assess the return on investment (ROI) of their marketing strategies and optimize spending for improved profitability.
nodes.questions: 1. How do fluctuations in marketing costs impact the Cost per Order? 2. Are there specific marketing channels with a lower or higher Cost per Order? 3. How does seasonality affect the average Cost per Order? 4. What strategies can be implemented to reduce the Cost per Order? 5. Is there a correlation between the Cost per Order and customer lifetime value? 6. How can the efficiency of sales efforts be improved to lower the Cost per Order? 7. Are there external factors, such as economic trends, influencing the Cost per Order?
nodes.slices: - Marketing Channel - Campaign Performance - User Acquisition Source - Geographic Breakdown - Time-Based Analysis

nodes.description: Mobile Abandonment Gap (MAG) indicates the difference in effectiveness between your CAR for mobile and your CAR for desktop.
nodes.formula: Cart Abandonment Rate on Mobile - Cart Abandonment Rate on Desktop
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Mobile Abandonment Gap (MAG) highlights the difference in cart abandonment rates between mobile and desktop users. Monitoring MAG helps businesses identify disparities and optimize the mobile checkout experience to reduce abandonment and increase conversions.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the Mobile Abandonment Gap change during different marketing campaigns? 2. Are there specific aspects of the mobile checkout process that contribute to the Mobile Abandonment Gap? 3. How does the Mobile Abandonment Gap vary across different device types (iOS, Android)? 4. Is there a correlation between website/app performance and the Mobile Abandonment Gap? 5. How can the user experience on mobile devices be optimized to minimize the Mobile Abandonment Gap? 6. Are there particular times of the day or days of the week with higher Mobile Abandonment Gap? 7. What strategies can be employed to bridge the gap between mobile and desktop Cart Abandonment Rates?
nodes.slices: - User Type Analysis - Device Comparison - Geographic Breakdown - Marketing Channel - Time-Based Analysis

nodes.description: The number of mandatory form fields users need to fill out during the checkout process.
nodes.formula: Count of required form fields
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: The number of mandatory form fields during the checkout process is crucial for assessing user experience and potential friction points. Monitoring this metric helps businesses identify areas for optimization, streamline the checkout process, and ultimately improve conversion rates.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the number of required form fields impact the completion rate of the checkout process? 2. Are there specific types of form fields that contribute to higher abandonment rates? 3. How does the length of the checkout process influence the completion of required form fields? 4. What role does user feedback play in optimizing the number of required form fields? 5. Are there differences in completion rates based on user demographics? 6. How do required form fields impact the overall user satisfaction with the checkout process? 7. What strategies can be implemented to streamline and optimize the required form fields for a better user experience?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Excessive or Complicated Form Fields. A checkout process with too many or overly complicated required form fields may lead to user frustration and abandonment. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Lack of Clear Instructions. Unclear instructions or guidance on how to fill out the required form fields may result in user confusion and abandonment. 🔹 Technical Glitches in Checkout Process. Technical issues during the form-filling process can create obstacles and reduce the likelihood of users completing the required fields. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Seasonal Peaks in Traffic. High traffic during peak seasons may exacerbate user frustration with lengthy or complicated form-filling processes. 🔹 Changes in User Behavior. Shifts in user behavior, such as reduced tolerance for lengthy checkout processes, can contribute to variations in this metric.

nodes.id: Share of Checkouts with Promo Code
nodes.description: The percentage of checkouts where a promotional code was applied out of the total number of checkouts.
nodes.formula: (Number of checkouts with promo code) / (Total number of checkouts) * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: The percentage of checkouts where a promotional code was applied out of the total number of checkouts is valuable for evaluating the impact of promotions. This metric helps businesses gauge the success of promotional efforts and make data-driven decisions for future campaigns.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the Share of Checkouts with Promo Code vary across different product categories? 2. Are there specific times or events when the Share of Checkouts with Promo Code is higher? 3. What impact do different types of promotional codes have on the Share of Checkouts? 4. How does the Share of Checkouts with Promo Code correlate with customer loyalty metrics? 5. Are there differences in the Share of Checkouts with Promo Code between new and returning customers? 6. How can the presentation of promo codes be optimized to increase their usage? 7. What strategies can be employed to promote the use of promo codes without affecting profit margins?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Limited Availability of Promotions. If there are fewer promotional campaigns or codes available, the percentage of checkouts with promo codes applied may decrease. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Lack of Awareness of Promo Codes. Users may not apply promo codes if they are unaware of ongoing promotions or do not have easy access to promo code information. 🔹 Technical Glitches in Promo Code Application Process. Technical issues during the promo code application process can hinder users from successfully applying promo codes. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Seasonal Peaks in Traffic. High traffic during peak seasons may result in increased competition for promo code application, impacting the share of checkouts with promo codes. 🔹 Changes in User Behavior. Shifts in user behavior, such as reduced interest in applying promo codes, can contribute to variations in this metric.
nodes.slices: - User Type Analysis - Device Comparison - Geographic Breakdown - Campaign Performance - Time-Based Analysis
