nodes.description: Return on Customer Experience (ROCX) measures the financial benefit derived from investments in improving the customer experience, such as customer service enhancements or product quality improvements. This metric reflects the impact of customer-centric initiatives on revenue and profitability.
nodes.formula: (Additional Revenue from Improved CX - Investment in CX) / Investment in CX
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Monitoring ROCX helps assess the impact of customer-centric initiatives on revenue and profitability, guiding decisions on resource allocation, customer service enhancements, and product quality improvements to enhance customer satisfaction, loyalty, and long-term profitability.
nodes.questions: 1. How does ROCX contribute to revenue growth and profitability, and what proportion of additional revenue is typically attributed to improvements in customer experience? 2. Can you identify any specific customer experience initiatives or investments that have led to measurable improvements in revenue and profitability? 3. What impact does ROCX have on customer retention, loyalty, and lifetime value, and how should customer experience strategies be aligned with business objectives to maximize return on investment? 4. How does ROCX compare to industry benchmarks or competitors' metrics, and what insights can be gained from benchmarking against leaders in customer experience excellence? 5. Are there any risks or challenges associated with measuring and improving ROCX, and what best practices can be adopted to overcome these challenges and achieve sustainable improvements in customer experience and financial performance?
nodes.slices: - Customer Service Initiatives - Product Quality Improvements - CX Technology Adoption - Marketing Personalization - Omnichannel Experience - Employee Training & Development

nodes.description: Return On Investment (ROI) represents the profitability of an investment relative to its cost. This metric measures the financial return generated from efforts relative to their cost.
nodes.formula: (Gross Profit - Total (Marketing) Costs) / Total (Marketing) Costs
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Monitoring ROI helps assess the financial return generated from efforts, guiding decisions on resource allocation, investment prioritization, and strategic planning to maximize returns and achieve business objectives.
nodes.questions: 1. How does ROI contribute to assessing the profitability and effectiveness of investments, and what factors influence its calculation and interpretation? 2. Can you identify any trends or patterns in ROI across different investment opportunities or business initiatives, and what insights can be derived from analyzing the drivers of ROI? 3. What is the relationship between changes in ROI and strategic decision-making regarding resource allocation, capital investment, and business expansion? 4. How does ROI impact shareholder value, investor confidence, and market perception of the company's financial performance and growth potential? 5. Are there any limitations or challenges associated with calculating and interpreting ROI, and what measures can be taken to ensure accurate ROI assessment and effective decision-making?
nodes.slices: - Investment Type - Time Frame - Marketing Channel - Product Line - Geographic Region - Customer Segment

nodes.description: RPS, or revenues per 1000 sessions, is a metric that measures how much revenue a website generates for every 1000 visitors or sessions. It is an important indicator for businesses that generate revenue through website visits. ‍ Also, note that If RPS is increasing over time, it suggests that the company is successfully drawing higher-quality visitors to its website or that the user experience on its website is improving. If RPS is decreasing, businesses may need to modify their marketing strategies or improve the user experience of their website.
nodes.formula: (Revenue / Sessions) * 1000
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Monitoring RPS helps assess website performance, user engagement, and revenue generation efficiency. It guides decisions on website optimization, marketing strategies, and user experience enhancements to drive revenue growth and improve overall business performance.
nodes.questions: 1. How does RPS vary across different time periods or marketing campaigns, and what factors contribute to fluctuations in revenue per session? 2. Can you identify any correlations between changes in RPS and shifts in website traffic sources, user demographics, or conversion rates, and how can these insights inform marketing strategies and website optimization efforts? 3. What impact does RPS have on overall revenue generation and profitability, and how should businesses interpret changes in RPS to make informed decisions about resource allocation and revenue forecasting? 4. How does RPS compare to industry benchmarks or competitors' metrics, and what strategies can be implemented to improve RPS performance relative to market standards? 5. Are there any limitations or challenges associated with interpreting RPS, and what measures can be taken to ensure accurate analysis and actionable insights from this metric?
nodes.slices: - Marketing Campaign - User Experience - Traffic Source - Conversion Funnel - Seasonality

nodes.description: Sales represent the total income generated from selling goods. This metric reflects the primary source of revenue for the business and is essential for assessing financial performance and growth potential.
nodes.formula: Total Sales
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Monitoring sales is crucial for assessing financial performance, identifying revenue trends, and evaluating the effectiveness of sales strategies to drive growth and profitability. By tracking sales data, businesses can make informed decisions about inventory management, pricing strategies, and market expansion opportunities.
nodes.questions: 1. How does sales revenue contribute to the overall financial performance and growth potential of the business, and what factors influence its fluctuations? 2. Can you identify any trends or patterns in sales revenue across different product categories, geographic regions, or customer segments, and what insights can be derived from analyzing the drivers of sales? 3. What is the relationship between changes in sales revenue and marketing efforts, pricing strategies, and customer satisfaction, and how can businesses optimize sales performance based on these relationships? 4. How does sales revenue compare to industry benchmarks or competitors' metrics, and what strategic opportunities or challenges does this comparison reveal? 5. Are there any risks or uncertainties associated with sales revenue fluctuations, and how should businesses mitigate these risks while capitalizing on sales growth opportunities?
nodes.slices: - Product Category - Sales Channel - Customer Segment - Time Frame - Pricing Strategy

nodes.description: Sales Taxes represent the taxes collected by a business on the sale of goods or services. This metric reflects the amount of taxes owed to the government based on sales transactions and is essential for regulatory compliance and financial planning.
nodes.formula: Total Sales Tax Collected
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking sales taxes collected is essential for regulatory compliance and financial planning. It ensures accurate tax reporting and payment, minimizing the risk of penalties or legal issues related to tax obligations. Additionally, analyzing sales tax data can provide insights into consumer purchasing behavior and regional sales trends.
nodes.questions: 1. How do sales taxes impact the financial statements and profitability of the business, and what proportion of total revenue do they represent? 2. Can you identify any regulatory or compliance considerations associated with collecting and remitting sales taxes, and what measures can be taken to ensure accurate reporting and compliance with tax laws? 3. What is the relationship between changes in sales taxes and overall sales revenue, and how can businesses manage the impact of sales tax changes on pricing strategies and customer purchasing behavior? 4. How do sales tax rates and regulations vary across different jurisdictions or geographic regions, and what implications does this have for businesses operating in multiple markets? 5. Are there any opportunities for optimizing sales tax collection processes or minimizing tax liabilities through strategic tax planning or jurisdictional considerations?
nodes.slices: - Tax Compliance - Financial Planning - Pricing Strategy - Customer Communication - Tax Optimization

nodes.description: Service Subscription Costs represent the expenses associated with subscribing to services necessary for business operations, such as software subscriptions or cloud services. This metric reflects the ongoing expenses incurred for maintaining essential services and infrastructure.
nodes.formula: Total Expenses for Service Subscriptions
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Monitoring these costs is vital for managing budget allocation effectively, assessing the cost-effectiveness of service subscriptions, and optimizing resource utilization to support operational efficiency and productivity. By understanding subscription costs, businesses can make informed decisions about service providers, negotiate better rates, and identify opportunities for cost savings.
nodes.questions: 1. How do service subscription costs contribute to the overall cost structure of the business, and what proportion of total expenses do they represent? 2. Can you identify any opportunities for optimizing service subscription costs through renegotiation, consolidation of services, or alternative service providers? 3. What impact do changes in service subscription costs have on operational efficiency, productivity, and technological capabilities, and how should businesses balance service quality with cost optimization? 4. How do service subscription costs compare to industry benchmarks or competitors' metrics, and what insights can be gained from benchmarking against leaders in service efficiency and technology adoption? 5. What are the strategic implications of service subscription costs on budget allocation, resource planning, and long-term business sustainability, and how should these factors be considered in strategic decision-making?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Adoption of new software solutions or tools. Implementing new software solutions or tools may require subscription fees, increasing service subscription costs. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Increased usage of cloud services or platforms. Expanding reliance on cloud services or platforms for business operations may lead to higher subscription costs due to increased usage or data storage. 🔹 Additions or upgrades to service plans. Adding new features or upgrading service plans may incur additional subscription fees, contributing to higher service subscription costs. 🔹 Inefficient utilization of subscribed services. Poor utilization of subscribed services or underutilization of features may result in wasted expenses, driving up service subscription costs. 🔹 Bundling of services with unnecessary features. Bundling services with unnecessary features or subscriptions may lead to higher overall costs, increasing service subscription expenses.
nodes.slices: - Subscription Type - Vendor Management - Usage Metrics - Cost Allocation - Renewal Strategy

nodes.description: Shipping Expenses represent the costs associated with shipping products to customers. This metric reflects the operational costs incurred to fulfill customer orders and deliver products to their destination.
nodes.formula: Total Expenses for Shipping
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking shipping expenses is essential for optimizing logistics processes, managing transportation costs, and enhancing profitability by streamlining shipping operations and improving customer experience. By analyzing shipping data, businesses can identify areas for cost reduction, negotiate favorable shipping rates, and implement strategies to minimize shipping delays and errors.
nodes.questions: 1. How do shipping expenses contribute to the overall cost structure of the business, and what proportion of total expenses do they represent? 2. Can you identify any opportunities for optimizing shipping expenses through carrier negotiation, packaging efficiency, or order fulfillment strategies? 3. What impact do changes in shipping expenses have on customer satisfaction, delivery speed, and operational efficiency, and how should businesses balance cost control with service quality? 4. How do shipping expenses compare to industry benchmarks or competitors' metrics, and what insights can be derived from benchmarking against leaders in logistics and supply chain management? 5. Are there any environmental or sustainability considerations associated with shipping expenses, and what measures can be taken to minimize the environmental footprint of shipping operations while optimizing cost-effectiveness?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Increase in shipping rates or carrier fees. Rising shipping rates or carrier fees can directly impact shipping expenses, leading to higher total shipping costs. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Expansion into remote or distant markets. Expanding into remote or distant markets may require longer shipping distances, resulting in higher shipping expenses. 🔹 Changes in packaging materials or dimensions. Changes in packaging materials or dimensions may affect shipping costs due to variations in package weight, size, or handling requirements. 🔹 Inefficient order fulfillment processes. Inefficient order fulfillment processes, such as delays in processing or shipping, may result in higher costs associated with rush orders or expedited shipping. 🔹 Fluctuations in fuel prices or transportation costs. Fluctuations in fuel prices or transportation costs can impact shipping expenses, leading to variability in total shipping costs.
nodes.slices: - Shipping Methods - Order Characteristics - Carrier Negotiations - Packaging Materials - Fulfillment Efficiency

nodes.description: Shipping Paid by Customer represents the portion of shipping costs borne by the customer when purchasing products. This metric reflects the amount charged to customers for shipping services and is essential for determining pricing strategies and profitability margins.
nodes.formula: Total Shipping Costs Paid by Customer
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Monitoring these costs helps determine competitive shipping rates, assess customer willingness to pay for shipping, and optimize pricing strategies to maximize revenue and customer satisfaction. By analyzing shipping cost data, businesses can adjust pricing models, offer shipping incentives, and improve overall profitability while maintaining customer loyalty.
nodes.questions: 1. How do shipping costs paid by customers impact pricing strategies, purchase decisions, and overall profitability margins? 2. Can you identify any correlations between changes in shipping costs paid by customers and changes in purchase behavior or order values, and how can businesses leverage this insight to optimize pricing and shipping policies? 3. What is the relationship between shipping costs paid by customers and customer satisfaction, loyalty, and repeat purchase rates, and how should businesses balance shipping charges with customer value perception? 4. How do shipping costs paid by customers compare to industry standards or competitors' practices, and what strategies can be implemented to remain competitive while ensuring fair and transparent shipping policies? 5. Are there any legal or regulatory considerations associated with charging shipping fees to customers, and what measures can be taken to ensure compliance with consumer protection laws and regulations?
nodes.slices: - Pricing Strategy - Shipping Options - Promotional Campaigns - Customer Communication - Shipping Policy Optimization

nodes.description: Total Advertising Cost of Sales (TACOS) represents the sum of all advertising expenses relative to total sales, encompassing all sales, not just those generated from advertising efforts. This metric provides a comprehensive view of the advertising costs in relation to overall revenue.
nodes.formula: Total Advertising Cost / Total Sales
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Monitoring TACOS helps evaluate the ROI of advertising campaigns, allocate advertising budgets effectively, and optimize marketing strategies to enhance sales performance and profitability. By analyzing advertising cost data, businesses can identify high-performing marketing channels, target relevant audiences, and refine advertising tactics to maximize return on investment.
nodes.questions: 1. How does TACOS contribute to the overall advertising cost structure and profitability of the business, and what proportion of total sales do advertising expenses represent? 2. Can you identify any trends or patterns in TACOS across different advertising channels, campaigns, or target audience segments, and what insights can be derived from analyzing the effectiveness of advertising efforts? 3. What impact does TACOS have on overall marketing ROI, sales revenue, and customer acquisition, and how can businesses optimize advertising spend to maximize return on investment? 4. How does TACOS compare to industry benchmarks or competitors' metrics, and what strategic opportunities or challenges does this comparison reveal? 5. Are there any risks or uncertainties associated with high TACOS, and how should businesses mitigate these risks while maintaining competitive advertising strategies and revenue growth objectives?
nodes.slices: - Advertising Channels - Campaign Type - Customer Segments - Time Frame - Competitive Landscape

nodes.description: Transaction Fees represent the charges incurred by a business for processing transactions, such as credit card fees or payment gateway charges. This metric reflects the cost of facilitating payments and is essential for managing financial transactions and profitability.
nodes.formula: Total Transaction Fees
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Monitoring transaction fees is essential for managing financial transactions efficiently, assessing payment processing costs, and optimizing payment methods to minimize fees and enhance profitability. By analyzing transaction fee data, businesses can identify cost-saving opportunities, negotiate lower processing rates, and implement strategies to streamline payment processes and improve overall financial performance.
nodes.questions: 1. How do transaction fees impact the financial statements and profitability of the business, and what proportion of total revenue do they represent? 2. Can you identify any trends or patterns in transaction fees across different payment methods, transaction volumes, or sales channels, and what insights can be derived from analyzing transaction cost dynamics? 3. What is the relationship between changes in transaction fees and overall sales revenue, customer payment preferences, and payment processing efficiency, and how can businesses optimize transaction costs to improve profitability? 4. How do transaction fees compare to industry averages or competitors' metrics, and what strategic opportunities or challenges does this comparison reveal? 5. Are there any opportunities for minimizing transaction fees through negotiation, optimization of payment processing systems, or alternative payment solutions, and what are the potential cost savings or benefits of implementing these strategies?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Increase in credit card processing fees or gateway charges. Higher credit card processing fees or gateway charges can directly impact transaction fees, increasing total transaction costs. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Adoption of premium payment processing services. Utilizing premium or enhanced payment processing services may involve higher transaction fees compared to standard services. 🔹 Higher chargeback rates or disputes. Increased chargeback rates or disputes may result in additional fees or penalties imposed by payment processors, elevating transaction fees. 🔹 Integration of additional payment methods or platforms. Integrating additional payment methods or platforms may incur setup fees or transaction charges, contributing to higher transaction fees. 🔹 Compliance with payment regulations or security standards. Compliance requirements or security standards may necessitate investments in secure payment processing systems or audits, increasing transaction fees.
nodes.slices: - Payment Methods - Sales Volume - Payment Processors - Fee Optimization - Compliance and Security

nodes.description: Variable Expenses are costs that fluctuate with the level of production or sales, such as raw materials or sales commissions. This metric represents the costs directly tied to business activity and revenue generation and is essential for assessing cost structures and profitability.
nodes.formula: Sum of all variable costs
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking variable expenses helps assess cost structures, manage cost variability, and optimize pricing strategies to ensure profitability margins while maintaining competitiveness in the market. By analyzing variable expense data, businesses can identify cost drivers, implement cost-saving measures, and make informed decisions about pricing, production, and resource allocation to enhance profitability and sustain long-term growth.
nodes.questions: 1. How do variable expenses contribute to the overall cost structure and profitability of the business, and what proportion of total expenses do they represent? 2. Can you identify any trends or patterns in variable expenses relative to changes in production levels, sales volumes, or revenue streams, and what insights can be derived from analyzing the drivers of variable costs? 3. What impact do changes in variable expenses have on gross profit margins, pricing strategies, and overall business performance, and how should businesses manage variable costs to maintain profitability? 4. How do variable expenses compare to industry benchmarks or competitors' metrics, and what strategies can be implemented to optimize variable cost management and improve cost-effectiveness? 5. Are there any risks or challenges associated with managing variable expenses, and what measures can be taken to mitigate these risks while optimizing operational efficiency and financial performance?
nodes.slices: - Production Volume - Sales Performance - Cost Drivers - Efficiency Metrics - Product Development

nodes.description: Withdrawn Net Profit represents the portion of net profit that is withdrawn from the business by owners or shareholders as dividends or distributions. This metric reflects the profits distributed to owners and is essential for assessing the financial returns to stakeholders.
nodes.formula: Total Net Profit - Reinvested Net Profit
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: By analyzing withdrawn net profit data, businesses can evaluate shareholder returns, plan for future financial obligations, and optimize capital allocation strategies to drive sustainable business growth and shareholder value.
nodes.questions: 1. How does withdrawn net profit impact the financial statements and cash flow of the business, and what proportion of total profits is typically distributed to owners or shareholders? 2. Can you identify any trends or patterns in withdrawn net profit distributions over time or in relation to changes in business performance or profitability? 3. What is the relationship between withdrawn net profit and shareholder returns, investor confidence, and business valuation, and how should distribution policies be aligned with shareholder expectations and business objectives? 4. How do withdrawn net profit distributions compare to industry practices or competitors' dividend policies, and what insights can be gained from benchmarking against industry leaders or peers? 5. Are there any legal or regulatory considerations associated with withdrawing net profit, and what measures can be taken to ensure compliance with corporate governance standards and tax regulations?
nodes.slices: - Distribution Strategy - Dividend Policy - Capital Investment - Shareholder Communication - Tax Planning

nodes.description: This metric represents the percentage of tickets that cannot proceed to the next stage of resolution due to some form of blockage or impediment.
nodes.formula: (Blocked Tickets / Ticket Volume) * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking the percentage of blocked tickets is crucial as it identifies bottlenecks in the support process, enabling proactive measures to remove obstacles and enhance efficiency. High percentages indicate potential issues hindering ticket resolution, prompting investigations and process improvements.
nodes.questions: 1. What are the most common reasons for ticket blockages and how can they be mitigated? 2. How does the percentage of blocked tickets vary by support channel (e.g., email, chat, phone)? 3. Which product categories or services experience the highest percentage of blocked tickets? 4. How does the percentage of blocked tickets correlate with customer satisfaction scores? 5. What is the impact of blocked tickets on overall resolution times and customer experience? 6. How does the percentage of blocked tickets trend over different time periods (e.g., daily, weekly, monthly)? 7. Are there specific agents or teams that have a higher incidence of blocked tickets, and what factors contribute to this?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Poor communication between teams. Information silos can prevent tickets from being resolved efficiently. 🔹 Lack of access to necessary resources. Agents may be unable to proceed without the required tools or information. 🔹 System downtime or technical issues. Technical problems can halt the resolution process. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 High dependency on third-party vendors. Delays from third-party vendors can block ticket progress. 🔹 Inefficient ticket prioritization system. Incorrect prioritization can cause important tickets to be delayed.
nodes.slices: - Reason for Blocking - Ticket Category - Time of Day - Channel - Customer Segment

nodes.description: This metric indicates the percentage of time during which customer support experiences the highest volume of incoming requests, typically reflecting busier periods.
nodes.formula: (Peak Hours / Total Hours) * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Knowing the percentage of peak hours allows for resource allocation optimization during busy periods, ensuring adequate staffing to handle increased ticket volumes efficiently. This metric aids in strategic planning, helping support teams anticipate workload fluctuations and adjust staffing levels or schedules accordingly to maintain service quality.
nodes.questions: 1. What are the busiest hours for customer support, and how do they vary across different days of the week? 2. How does the percentage of peak hours affect agent workload and utilization rates? 3. What strategies can be implemented to better manage peak hours and improve customer support efficiency? 4. How do peak hours correlate with the volume of sales and conversions generated through support interactions? 5. How does the distribution of peak hours differ between various customer segments or regions? 6. What impact do peak hours have on customer wait times and satisfaction levels? 7. How can predictive analytics be used to forecast peak hours and optimize staffing levels accordingly?
nodes.slices: - Day of the Week - Time of Day - Seasonality - Geographic Location - Product or Service Lifecycle Stage

nodes.description: This metric measures the percentage of customer service interactions that result in sales, demonstrating the effectiveness of customer support in driving revenue.
nodes.formula: (Converted into a Purchase Tickets / Resolved Tickets) * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Monitoring the percentage of service interactions that lead to sales provides insights into the revenue-generating potential of customer support. It guides strategies for enhancing support interactions to drive conversions, contributing directly to business growth and profitability. Understanding this metric helps align support efforts with sales objectives, maximizing the value of customer interactions.
nodes.questions: 1. Which types of customer service interactions are most likely to result in sales conversions? 2. How does the service-to-sales conversion rate vary across different product categories or services? 3. What impact does agent training and incentives have on the service-to-sales conversion rate? 4. How does the service-to-sales conversion rate trend over different time periods, and what factors influence these trends? 5. What is the correlation between customer satisfaction scores and service-to-sales conversion rates? 6. How does the service-to-sales conversion rate differ between new and returning customers? 7. What specific strategies can be employed to enhance the service-to-sales conversion rate in customer support interactions?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Ineffective training of support agents. Poorly trained agents may struggle to convert service interactions into sales. 🔹 Insufficient integration of customer support and sales processes. Poor integration can hinder seamless transitions from support to sales. 🔹 Low customer satisfaction. Unhappy customers are less likely to make additional purchases. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Lack of incentives for agents to upsell or cross-sell. Without motivation, agents may not focus on sales opportunities. 🔹 Product or service issues. Defective or subpar products reduce the likelihood of conversion from support interactions.
nodes.slices: - Customer Journey Stage - Product or Service Category - Support Channel - Customer Segment - Support Resolution Time

nodes.description: This metric shows the percentage of tickets resolved within the agreed Service Level Agreement (SLA) timeframe, indicating the team's adherence to service standards.
nodes.formula: (Tickets with SLA Compliance / Resolved Tickets) * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Monitoring SLA compliance ensures customer satisfaction by meeting delivery time expectations. High compliance rates indicate efficient operations and reliability, which can enhance customer trust and loyalty.
nodes.questions: 1. Which support channels have the highest and lowest SLA compliance rates, and why? 2. How does SLA compliance vary across different types of customer inquiries or issues? 3. What is the impact of SLA compliance on overall customer satisfaction and retention rates? 4. How does SLA compliance trend over time, and what operational changes can improve it? 5. How do different teams or agents perform in terms of SLA compliance, and what best practices can be shared? 6. What role does technology (e.g., automation, AI) play in improving SLA compliance rates? 7. How do external factors (e.g., holiday seasons, promotions) affect SLA compliance, and how can these impacts be mitigated?
nodes.slices: - SLA Type - Customer Priority Level - Support Channel - Ticket Category - Time of Day

nodes.description: This metric represents the percentage of tickets that remain open and unresolved within a given time period, indicating the backlog or efficiency of the support team.
nodes.formula: (Unresolved Tickets / Ticket Volume) * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: The percentage of unresolved tickets highlights inefficiencies or backlogs in the support system, impacting customer experience and retention. It prompts actions to streamline processes, reduce resolution times, and prioritize critical issues, ultimately improving service quality and customer satisfaction. Monitoring this metric enables proactive management of ticket queues, ensuring timely resolution and minimizing customer frustration.
nodes.questions: 1. What are the primary causes for tickets remaining unresolved, and how can these be addressed? 2. How does the percentage of unresolved tickets vary by support channel or issue type? 3. What is the impact of unresolved tickets on customer satisfaction and loyalty? 4. How does the percentage of unresolved tickets trend over different time periods, and what can be done to improve resolution rates? 5. How do unresolved ticket rates compare between different teams or agents, and what factors contribute to these differences? 6. What strategies can be implemented to reduce the percentage of unresolved tickets and improve overall support efficiency? 7. How does the complexity of customer issues influence the rate of unresolved tickets?
nodes.slices: - Ticket Priority - Ticket Age - Ticket Category - Customer Segment - Support Channel

nodes.description: This metric measures the deviation from the defined SLA timeframe, providing insights into how consistently tickets are resolved within the expected time frame.
nodes.formula: (Average Resolution Time - SLA) / SLA * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: SLA deviation percentage offers insights into the consistency of meeting service level agreement timeframes, identifying areas for process optimization and resource allocation adjustments. It facilitates performance evaluation and improvement efforts, enhancing operational efficiency and customer satisfaction by aligning resolution times with agreed-upon service standards. Regular monitoring of SLA deviation helps maintain service quality and strengthens customer trust in support capabilities.
nodes.questions: 1. Which types of tickets or issues experience the highest SLA deviations, and why? 2. How does SLA deviation impact customer satisfaction and retention? 3. What operational changes can be implemented to reduce SLA deviations and improve service consistency? 4. How do different teams or agents perform in terms of SLA deviation, and what can be learned from high performers? 5. What is the correlation between SLA deviation and overall resolution times? 6. How does SLA deviation trend over different periods, and what factors contribute to these trends? 7. What role does training and development play in reducing SLA deviation rates among support agents?
nodes.slices: - Ticket Priority - Ticket Category - Customer Segment - Support Channel - Time of Day

nodes.description: This metric counts the number of customer conversations that were abandoned before reaching a resolution, reflecting potential issues with service quality or response times. For instance, a customer might abandon a conversation if they're put on hold for an extended period or if they're dissatisfied with the initial response provided by the agent.
nodes.formula: Number of Abandoned Сonversations
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: High abandonment rates signal dissatisfaction or frustration among customers, necessitating immediate attention to enhance engagement and resolution experiences. This metric aids in identifying pain points in the support journey, enabling tailored solutions to improve customer satisfaction and retention.
nodes.questions: 1. What are the main reasons for customers abandoning conversations before resolution? 2. How does the rate of abandoned conversations vary by support channel (e.g., chat, phone, email)? 3. What impact do abandoned conversations have on overall customer satisfaction and perception of the support team? 4. How do abandoned conversation rates trend over different time periods, and what measures can be taken to reduce them? 5. How does agent response time correlate with the rate of abandoned conversations? 6. What strategies can be implemented to re-engage customers who have abandoned conversations? 7. How does the complexity of customer issues affect the likelihood of conversation abandonment?
nodes.slices: - Reason for Abandonment - Support Channel - Customer Segment - Time of Day - Agent Handling the Conversation

nodes.description: This metric gauges the satisfaction level of customer support agents, typically through surveys or feedback mechanisms, reflecting their morale and engagement levels.
nodes.formula: Survey or Feedback Form
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Regular assessments of agent satisfaction inform initiatives to boost morale, enhance training, or improve work environments, fostering a positive culture and delivering better customer experiences. This metric serves as a vital indicator of team health and effectiveness, guiding leadership decisions to create a supportive and empowered workforce.
nodes.questions: 1. What factors contribute most to agent satisfaction, and how can they be enhanced? 2. How does agent satisfaction impact overall team performance and customer satisfaction? 3. What is the correlation between agent satisfaction and turnover rates within the support team? 4. How do different management practices or team structures affect agent satisfaction rates? 5. How does agent satisfaction vary across different support channels or roles? 6. What role does professional development and training play in improving agent satisfaction? 7. How does agent satisfaction trend over time, and what organizational changes can sustain high satisfaction levels?
nodes.slices: - Team or Department - Tenure - Performance - Workload - Shift Timing

nodes.description: This metric measures the percentage of time agents spend actively handling customer inquiries or tasks compared to their total available working time.
nodes.formula: (Total Active Time / Total Available Time) * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Optimal utilization rates indicate effective workload management and resource allocation, leading to improved response times and service quality. This metric enables support managers to identify underutilized or overburdened agents, optimizing staffing levels to meet demand and enhance overall operational efficiency.
nodes.questions: 1. How does agent utilization rate vary across different support channels or shifts? 2. What is the optimal agent utilization rate to balance workload and prevent burnout? 3. How does agent utilization rate correlate with customer satisfaction and resolution times? 4. What strategies can be implemented to improve agent utilization without compromising service quality? 5. How do agent utilization rates differ between experienced and new agents? 6. What impact does automation and AI have on agent utilization rates? 7. How does agent utilization trend over different periods, and what factors contribute to these trends?
nodes.slices: - Skill Level - Shift Timing - Support Channel - Ticket Category - Team or Department

nodes.description: This metric indicates the percentage of tickets that are resolved automatically without requiring manual intervention from support agents, showcasing system efficiency.
nodes.formula: (Auto-Resolved Tickets / Ticket Volume) * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: High auto-resolution rates indicate robust automation capabilities, streamlining support processes and improving response times. Monitoring this metric informs investments in automation technologies and guides enhancements to self-service options, enhancing customer experiences while reducing operational costs.
nodes.questions: 1. What types of tickets or issues are most commonly resolved through automation? 2. How does the auto-resolution rate impact overall agent workload and productivity? 3. What is the correlation between auto-resolution rates and customer satisfaction? 4. How do auto-resolution rates vary by support channel or ticket category? 5. What improvements can be made to increase the effectiveness and accuracy of automated resolutions? 6. How does the implementation of new technologies (e.g., AI, machine learning) affect auto-resolution rates? 7. How does the auto-resolution rate trend over time, and what factors influence these trends?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Outdated or poorly designed automated systems. Ineffective automation can fail to resolve issues adequately. 🔹 Increase in complex customer queries. Complex issues often require human intervention. 🔹 Insufficient or inaccurate knowledge base. An inadequate knowledge base hampers the auto-resolution process. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Lack of continuous improvement of automation rules and algorithms. Automation needs regular updates to remain effective. 🔹 Inadequate initial categorization of tickets. Incorrect categorization can lead to incorrect automated responses.
nodes.slices: - Ticket Category - Support Channel - Customer Segment - Ticket Complexity - Time of Day

nodes.description: This metric represents the number of tickets resolved automatically by predefined processes or automated systems, reducing the workload on support agents.
nodes.formula: Number of Auto-Resolved Tickets
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking the number of auto-resolved tickets provides insights into the effectiveness of automation systems in handling support requests, reducing the workload on agents and improving operational efficiency. This metric reflects investments in automation technology and guides enhancements to self-service options, ultimately enhancing customer experiences while optimizing resource utilization.
nodes.questions: 1. What types of tickets are most frequently auto-resolved and why? 2. How does the rate of auto-resolved tickets impact overall customer satisfaction? 3. How does the implementation of new automation technologies affect the number of auto-resolved tickets? 4. What is the trend of auto-resolved tickets over the past year, and what factors influence this trend? 5. How do auto-resolved tickets compare in terms of resolution time versus manually resolved tickets? 6. What are the most common failure points in the auto-resolution process, and how can they be addressed? 7. How does the percentage of auto-resolved tickets vary between different support channels?
nodes.slices: - Ticket Category - Support Channel - Customer Segment - Ticket Complexity - Time of Day

nodes.description: This metric calculates the average number of agents actively handling customer inquiries or tickets during a specific time period, indicating staffing requirements.
nodes.formula: (Sum of Active Agents per Interval) / Total Intervals
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Calculating the average number of active agents helps determine staffing requirements for handling customer inquiries or tickets effectively. By understanding agent availability trends, support managers can optimize workforce allocation, ensuring adequate coverage during peak periods and minimizing wait times for customers. This metric enables proactive resource planning and allocation, enhancing service quality and responsiveness to customer needs.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the number of active agents correlate with the volume of incoming tickets? 2. What are the peak periods for active agent engagement, and how do these align with customer demand? 3. How does agent productivity change with varying numbers of active agents? 4. What impact does agent training and development have on the average number of active agents? 5. How does the average number of active agents vary between different shifts or times of day? 6. What is the relationship between average active agents and customer satisfaction scores? 7. How does the introduction of new tools or technologies affect the average number of active agents?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 High turnover rate among agents. Frequent changes in staff can disrupt service continuity. 🔹 Inadequate staffing models. Poor planning can result in either understaffing or overstaffing. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Seasonal demand fluctuations. Peaks and troughs in demand affect the average number of active agents needed. 🔹 Insufficient cross-training. Agents who are not well-rounded may not be able to handle a variety of tickets. 🔹 Changes in support policies or procedures. New policies might require more or fewer agents to handle tickets.
nodes.slices: - Time of Day - Weekday vs. Weekend - Shift Timing - Department or Team - Customer Segment

tactics.tactic: Invest in Chatbots for Tier 1 Support

nodes.description: This metric calculates the average number of agents actively engaged in customer support activities during daytime hours on a daily basis. It provides insights into staffing needs and trends specifically for daytime shifts, helping optimize resource allocation and ensure adequate coverage during peak periods.
nodes.formula: (Sum of Active Agents per Day) / Total Days
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Insights from this metric enable support managers to adjust schedules and allocate resources effectively, maximizing agent productivity and responsiveness during peak hours and improving overall support experiences for customers.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the average number of daily active agents vary throughout the week? 2. What factors influence the fluctuation of daily active agents? 3. How does the average daily active agents correlate with daytime ticket volume? 4. What impact do holidays and special events have on the average daily active agents? 5. How does agent scheduling affect the average daily active agents during peak daytime hours? 6. What is the impact of remote working policies on the average daily active agents? 7. How does the average daily active agents trend over different seasons?
nodes.slices: - Weekday vs. Weekend - Seasonal Trends - Peak vs. Off-Peak Hours - Daytime vs. Nighttime - Shift Timing

nodes.description: This metric calculates the average time it takes for the support team to respond to initial customer inquiries, reflecting responsiveness and service quality.
nodes.formula: Total Response Time / Ticket Volume
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Monitoring FRT enables timely interventions to improve response times, enhancing customer satisfaction and loyalty. This metric guides efforts to streamline communication channels, prioritize tickets, and optimize workflows, ensuring prompt and efficient handling of customer inquiries from the moment they are received.
nodes.questions: 1. What is the impact of average first response time on customer satisfaction and retention? 2. How does average first response time vary across different support channels? 3. What strategies can be implemented to reduce the average first response time? 4. How does the average first response time correlate with ticket resolution rates? 5. What are the main factors causing delays in the first response time? 6. How does the average first response time trend over different time periods? 7. How does agent experience and training affect the average first response time?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Increase in ticket volume. Higher volume can lead to longer wait times before the first response. 🔹 Insufficient number of active agents. Not enough agents can delay initial responses. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Complex or technical issues requiring longer time to provide initial response. Difficult problems may require more time to understand before responding. 🔹 Inefficient ticket assignment processes. Poor assignment can lead to delays in responding to tickets. 🔹 Agent burnout or low morale. Burned-out agents may take longer to respond to tickets.
nodes.slices: - Ticket Priority - Ticket Category - Support Channel - Agent or Team - Time of Day

nodes.description: This metric represents the average duration it takes for agents to handle and resolve customer inquiries or tickets from the moment they connect with the customer to the moment they provide a solution, excluding any waiting time in the queue. It measures the efficiency and productivity of agents in resolving customer issues once they are actively engaged in the interaction.
nodes.formula: Total Handling Time / Resolved Tickets
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Understanding handle time helps identify opportunities for process optimization, agent training, or automation, leading to faster resolution times and improved service quality. This metric guides efforts to streamline support workflows, reduce customer wait times, and enhance agent effectiveness in resolving customer issues, ultimately driving higher satisfaction levels.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the average handle time differ between simple and complex tickets? 2. What is the impact of average handle time on overall agent productivity? 3. How does the average handle time correlate with customer satisfaction scores? 4. What strategies can be implemented to reduce the average handle time without compromising service quality? 5. How does the average handle time vary by support channel (e.g., phone, email, chat)? 6. What is the trend of average handle time over the past year, and what factors influence this trend? 7. How does the implementation of new support tools or technologies affect the average handle time?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Increase in ticket complexity. More complex issues take longer to resolve. 🔹 Lack of agent training. Untrained agents may take longer to handle tickets. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Inefficient internal processes. Cumbersome processes can slow down resolution times. 🔹 Poor knowledge base. Without quick access to information, agents take longer to handle issues. 🔹 System or technical issues. Technical problems can slow down handling times.
nodes.slices: - Ticket Priority - Ticket Category - Support Channel - Agent or Team - Ticket Complexity

nodes.description: This metric calculates the percentage of customer conversations that are abandoned before reaching a resolution, indicating potential issues with service quality or response times.
nodes.formula: (Abandoned Conversations / Ticket Volume) * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: High abandonment rates signal dissatisfaction or frustration among customers, necessitating immediate attention to enhance engagement and resolution experiences. This metric aids in identifying pain points in the support journey, enabling tailored solutions to improve customer satisfaction and retention.
nodes.questions: 1. What are the main reasons for customers abandoning conversations before resolution? 2. How does the abandonment rate vary across different support channels or types of inquiries? 3. What is the impact of conversation abandonment on customer satisfaction and loyalty? 4. How does the abandonment rate trend over different time periods, and what factors influence these trends? 5. How does agent response time correlate with the rate of conversation abandonment? 6. What strategies can be implemented to reduce the abandonment rate and improve customer engagement? 7. How does the complexity of customer issues affect the likelihood of conversation abandonment?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Long waiting times. Customers are more likely to abandon conversations if they wait too long. 🔹 Poor initial responses or agent performance. Inadequate responses can frustrate customers, leading to abandonment. 🔹 Low customer satisfaction. Dissatisfied customers are more likely to abandon conversations. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Technical issues during conversation. Technical problems can disrupt communication, causing customers to abandon the interaction. 🔹 Lack of proactive customer engagement. Without proactive engagement, customers might lose interest and abandon the conversation.

nodes.description: This metric assesses the accuracy of forecasts made by the support team regarding future support demand or ticket volumes, helping improve resource planning and allocation.
nodes.formula: Forecast Error / Forecast of Ticket Volume x 100
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: Accurate forecasts help businesses anticipate support needs, optimize staffing levels, and ensure adequate coverage during peak periods, improving operational efficiency and customer service quality. Monitoring forecast accuracy enables businesses to refine forecasting models, adjust resource allocation strategies, and mitigate risks associated with over or underestimating support demand.
nodes.questions: 1. What are the primary factors contributing to forecast inaccuracies, and how can they be mitigated? 2. How does forecast accuracy impact resource planning and allocation? 3. What methods can be implemented to improve the accuracy of support demand forecasts? 4. How does forecast accuracy trend over different time periods and operational changes? 5. How does forecast accuracy correlate with overall support team performance? 6. What role does historical data play in improving forecast accuracy? 7. How do unexpected events or anomalies affect forecast accuracy, and how can the support team prepare for them?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Poor historical data analysis. Inaccurate analysis of past data can lead to incorrect forecasts. 🔹 Unexpected market or demand changes. Sudden changes can disrupt previously accurate forecasts. 🔹 Ineffective demand prediction models. Flawed models can result in inaccurate forecasts. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Insufficient data collection or analysis tools. Lack of proper tools can lead to poor forecasting accuracy. 🔹 Delays in data processing. Late data can cause forecasts to be outdated or inaccurate.

nodes.why: A high NPS indicates satisfied customers who are likely to advocate for the brand, leading to increased referrals and business growth. Tracking NPS helps businesses identify promoters, address detractors' concerns, and improve overall service quality to drive customer retention and loyalty. This metric guides decisions related to customer experience management, service improvement initiatives, and brand building strategies to enhance customer satisfaction and loyalty.

How does NPS correlate with overall customer retention and loyalty? 4. What strategies can be implemented to improve NPS? 5. How does NPS trend over different time periods and in response to specific company actions? 6. How does NPS compare between customers who have used support services and those who have not? 7. What is the relationship between NPS and other customer satisfaction metrics?

nodes.description: This metric represents the total count of agents available in the customer support team. It reflects the team's capacity to handle customer inquiries and support tasks.
nodes.formula: Count of Agents
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Understanding agent numbers helps businesses allocate resources, optimize staffing levels, and ensure adequate support coverage to meet customer demand. By aligning agent capacity with support needs, businesses can improve service responsiveness, minimize wait times, and enhance customer satisfaction. This metric guides decisions related to workforce planning, capacity management, and service level agreements to optimize support delivery and improve operational efficiency.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the number of agents correlate with the overall volume of tickets and inquiries? 2. What is the impact of the number of agents on customer satisfaction and resolution times? 3. How does agent turnover affect the number of agents and overall support team performance? 4. What strategies can be implemented to optimize the number of agents based on support demand? 5. How does the number of agents vary across different support channels and time periods? 6. How does agent training and onboarding processes affect the number of agents needed? 7. What is the relationship between the number of agents and the efficiency of support operations?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 High turnover rate. Frequent staff changes can affect the overall number of agents. 🔹 Inadequate hiring practices. Poor hiring strategies can result in either a shortage or surplus of agents. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Budget constraints. Limited budgets may prevent hiring sufficient agents. 🔹 Seasonal demand fluctuations. Seasonal changes can temporarily affect the number of agents needed. 🔹 Policy changes affecting work conditions. Changes in policies may impact the number of agents willing to stay or join the team.
nodes.slices: - Department - Shift - Tenure - Location - Team Size

nodes.description: This metric calculates the percentage of time that agents work beyond their regular scheduled hours to handle customer inquiries or support tasks, reflecting workload and resource allocation.
nodes.formula: (Overtime Hours / Total Regular Hours) * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Monitoring overtime rates helps businesses assess staffing adequacy, identify workload imbalances, and mitigate agent burnout risks. By optimizing workload distribution and resource allocation, businesses can reduce overtime costs, improve agent morale, and maintain service quality standards. This metric guides decisions related to workforce management, capacity planning, and workload distribution to enhance operational efficiency and employee satisfaction.
nodes.questions: 1. How does the overtime rate impact overall agent satisfaction and morale? 2. What are the primary factors contributing to high overtime rates, and how can they be addressed? 3. How does the overtime rate correlate with customer satisfaction and support quality? 4. What strategies can be implemented to reduce overtime rates without compromising service quality? 5. How does the overtime rate trend over different time periods and operational changes? 6. How does the workload distribution affect the overtime rate? 7. What is the impact of overtime rate on overall support team productivity and efficiency?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Insufficient number of agents. Too few agents can lead to increased overtime. 🔹 High ticket volume. More tickets can necessitate overtime to handle them. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Poor workload management. Inefficient distribution of tasks can result in more overtime. 🔹 Lack of proper scheduling. Inadequate scheduling practices can cause overtime to cover gaps. 🔹 Unexpected absenteeism. Unexpected leave can increase the need for remaining agents to work overtime.

nodes.description: This metric calculates the percentage of customer support interactions where the same customer contacts support again for the same issue within a specific period. It reflects the rate of recurring problems or unresolved issues that require repeated contact with the support team.
nodes.formula: (Customers Contacting Multiple Times / Total Customers) * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Monitoring repeat contact rates helps businesses identify trends, address underlying issues, and improve service efficiency. By reducing repeat contact rates, businesses can streamline support processes, enhance issue resolution, and improve customer satisfaction levels. This metric guides decisions related to process optimization, agent training, and customer feedback management to minimize customer effort and enhance service quality.
nodes.questions: 1. What are the primary reasons for customers making repeat contacts for the same issue? 2. How does the repeat contact rate impact overall customer satisfaction and loyalty? 3. What strategies can be implemented to reduce the repeat contact rate? 4. How does the complexity of customer issues affect the repeat contact rate? 5. How does agent performance and training influence the repeat contact rate? 6. What is the trend of repeat contact rates over different time periods and operational changes? 7. How does the repeat contact rate vary across different customer segments or demographics?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Poor initial resolution of issues. Inadequate resolutions can lead to customers contacting support again. 🔹 Low agent training or performance. Poor performance can result in unresolved issues requiring multiple contacts. 🔹 Incomplete or inaccurate information provided. Incorrect information can cause customers to seek further clarification or resolution. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Technical issues reoccurring. Persistent technical problems can lead to repeated contacts. 🔹 Lack of follow-up after initial contact. Without follow-up, issues may remain unresolved, leading to repeat contacts.

nodes.description: This metric is calculated as the standard deviation of response times for customer inquiries or tickets. It measures the variability or consistency of response times provided by the support team across different interactions or channels. A lower standard deviation indicates more consistent response times, while a higher standard deviation suggests greater variability in response times.

nodes.description: This metric represents the commitments between the support team and customers regarding response and resolution timeframes for customer inquiries or issues. An SLA can be assigned for any length of time, such as Waiting Time, First Response Time, Handle Time, or Resolution Time. For example, an SLA may specify the maximum acceptable time for resolving customer issues.
nodes.formula: SLA Definitions and Commitments
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Ensuring compliance with SLAs is essential for meeting customer expectations and maintaining service levels. It provides clear guidelines for order processing time frames, allowing for efficient order management and timely delivery. Tracking SLA adherence helps identify areas for improvement and ensures that customer commitments are met consistently.
nodes.questions: 1. How do different SLA definitions impact customer satisfaction and service quality? 2. What are the most common causes of SLA breaches, and how can they be addressed? 3. How does adherence to SLAs influence overall support team performance and efficiency? 4. How do SLAs vary across different types of customer inquiries or support channels? 5. What strategies can be implemented to improve SLA compliance? 6. How does SLA compliance trend over different time periods and operational changes? 7. How does SLA performance correlate with customer satisfaction and retention?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Changes in customer expectations. Higher expectations can make it harder to meet existing SLA commitments. 🔹 Insufficient staffing levels. Not enough staff can result in missed SLA targets. 🔹 System outages or technical issues. Technical problems can prevent timely responses and resolutions, impacting SLAs. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Increased ticket complexity. More complex issues can lead to longer resolution times, affecting SLA compliance. 🔹 Poor process management. Inefficient processes can lead to delays in meeting SLAs.

nodes.description: The average time a picker spends moving between picks
nodes.formula: Total Travel Time / Number of Picks
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Monitoring travel time per pick ensures that pickers spend minimal time traveling between picks, optimizing picking processes and reducing overall order processing time. nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Inefficient warehouse layout. Poor layout design can increase the distance and time taken to pick items 🔹 Poor inventory placement strategies. Frequently picked items placed far from packing areas can increase travel time 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Lack of picking automation. Manual picking processes take longer compared to automated systems 🔹 High volume of orders. More orders can lead to congestion and longer travel times within the warehouse 🔹 Inadequate staffing levels. Not enough pickers to handle the volume of orders efficiently 🔹 Inconsistent training for pickers. Poorly trained pickers may not use the most efficient routes or methods 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Environmental factors within the warehouse. Poor lighting, temperature, or other environmental factors can slow down pickers

nodes.description: The ratio of how often customers click your ad when it’s shown. Amazon provides you with the CTR for each campaign and keyword.

nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Irrelevant Keywords. Ads shown for non-relevant keywords reduce click likelihood. 🔹 Poor Ad Copy. Unengaging or unclear ad text leads to fewer clicks. 🔹 Inadequate Targeting. Ads not shown to the right audience result in lower CTR. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Ad Fatigue. Users become tired of seeing the same ad repeatedly. 🔹 Competitor Ads. More attractive competitor ads steal clicks. 🔹 Negative Reviews. Poor product reviews discourage clicks on the ad. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Seasonal Variations. Seasonal changes affect user interest and click behavior.

nodes.description: The number of times your ad was considered viewable. In Sponsored Display, this metric is available when you choose Optimize for reach as your bid optimization strategy. In Sponsored Brands, this metric is available when you choose Grow brand impression share as your campaign goal. An image is counted as viewable when at least 50% is visible for at least 1 second. A video is counted as viewable when at least 50% is visible while playing for at least 2 seconds. This is the Media Rating Council's (MRC) definition of viewability.

nodes.description: New-to-brand metrics let you measure attributed orders from first-time customers of your brand on Amazon. Amazon Ads determines if a purchase is new to your brand by reviewing the last 12 months of shoppers purchase history. If shoppers haven’t purchased from your brand within the previous 12 months, the order is considered new-to-brand. Purchase history is based on all shopping activity within a given brand on Amazon and isn’t limited to attributed purchases. New-to-brand metrics are available for Sponsored Brands and Sponsored Display.
nodes.formula: Number of new-to-brand orders

nodes.description: Top-of-search impression share is the percentage of top-of-search impressions that the campaign earned of all the top-of-search impressions it’s eligible to receive for a given date range. Various factors determine the eligibility for an impression including campaign status and targeting status. This metric is available for the trailing 90-days. It can take up to 12 hours for your metrics to populate. Top-of-search IS is available for Sponsored Brands and Sponsored Products campaigns in the campaign manager. For Sponsored Products, this metric gives the top-of-search IS for the first page only. You can use top-of-search IS to understand how effective your campaign is at driving top-of-search impressions. Depending on your objectives for the campaign, this metric can be used as a measure to set and monitor top-of-search impression share targets. For Sponsored Products, you can adjust the bids by placement in the campaign’s placement tab to influence this metric.
nodes.formula: Top-of-search impressions earned / Eligible top-of-search impressions * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Top-of-search impression share indicates ad visibility at the top of search results, crucial for maximizing exposure and driving high-intent clicks. It informs bid adjustments and targeting strategies to dominate key search positions.

nodes.description: This metric determines how much you've paid for the ads placed on websites or social media, based on the number of viewable impressions the ad receives. This metric is available when you use the bidding option to optimize for viewable impressions. For this option, you’ll enter a bid in terms of “cost per 1000 viewable impressions” or vCPM. This means you only pay when shoppers actually viewed your ad. In Sponsored Display, this metric is available when you choose Optimize for reach as your bid optimization strategy. In Sponsored Brands, this metric is available when you choose Grow brand impression share as your campaign goal.
nodes.formula: Total spend / (Number of viewable impressions / 1000)
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: vCPM measures the cost for viewable impressions, indicating efficiency in paying for actual ad views rather than mere placements. It helps optimize ad spend towards more effective impressions and brand exposure.
nodes.questions: How does changing the bid optimization strategy (e.g., from Optimize for reach to Grow brand impression share) impact vCPM? How does vCPM vary between different ad placements (e.g., Sponsored Brands vs. Sponsored Display)? What is the trend in vCPM over different time periods within the same ad campaign? How does seasonality impact the vCPM metric in Amazon advertising campaigns? How effective are adjustments in ad creative formats in lowering the overall vCPM?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Low Viewability. Ads are not in view long enough to be considered viewable, wasting spend. 🔹 High Competition. Increased bidding competition raises the cost of viewable impressions. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Poor Ad Placement. Ads placed in less visible areas result in higher costs for fewer impressions. 🔹 Ineffective Creative. Ads that don’t capture attention lead to lower viewability. 🔹 Campaign Targeting Issues. Targeting less relevant audiences can lead to higher costs per viewable impression. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Technical Issues. Issues like slow loading times or poor ad rendering can affect viewability. 🔹 Seasonal Demand Fluctuations. Changes in demand during certain seasons can affect ad costs.
nodes.slices: - Campaign Type - Product Category - Geographic Region - Time Period - Ad Performance

nodes.description: Click-through rate for views (clicks divided by views).
nodes.formula: Clicks / Views * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: vCTR indicates the effectiveness of video ads in generating clicks relative to the number of views, guiding optimizations to enhance engagement and ad performance. It informs strategies for improving ad content and placement.
nodes.questions: What factors contribute to fluctuations in vCTR across different ad campaigns? How does vCTR vary between different demographic segments targeted in Amazon advertising? How does seasonality impact the vCTR metric in Sponsored Brands and Sponsored Display campaigns? How effective are changes in ad copy and call-to-action in improving vCTR? How does the vCTR compare between video ads and static image ads on Amazon?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Unengaging Content. Viewers don't find the ad compelling enough to click. 🔹 Misleading Ad Thumbnail. Thumbnails that don't match ad content lead to lower clicks. 🔹 Inappropriate Audience Targeting. Targeting an audience not interested in the ad content results in fewer clicks. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Competing Content. High-quality competing content distracts viewers from clicking your ad. 🔹 Ad Placement Issues. Poor ad placement affects visibility and clickability. 🔹 Negative Brand Perception. Prior negative experiences with the brand can deter clicks. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Changes in Viewing Habits. Shifts in how and when users view content can impact CTR.

How does seasonality impact the video 5 second view rate in Sponsored Brands campaigns? How effective are different video content strategies in improving the 5 second view rate? How does the video 5 second view rate vary between different audience segments?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Weak Opening Seconds. If the first few seconds aren't engaging, viewers skip the video. 🔹 Irrelevant Content. Video content not relevant to the audience leads to lower completion rates. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 High Ad Frequency. Repeated exposure to the same video ad can lead to viewer fatigue. 🔹 Technical Issues. Problems like buffering or poor video quality cause viewers to skip. 🔹 Competing Distractions. Viewers distracted by other content or notifications skip the video. 🔹 Misleading Thumbnails or Titles. Misleading initial impressions cause viewers to lose interest quickly. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Changes in Viewer Behavior. Shifts in how users consume video content can affect view rates.

nodes.formula: Number of unmutes / Total video impressions * 100

What is the relationship between the unmute rate and overall video ad engagement metrics?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Uninteresting Visuals. Poor visual content may not motivate users to unmute the video. 🔹 Low Relevance of Content. If the video content is not relevant, viewers won't unmute. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Intrusive or Annoying Ads. Ads perceived as intrusive are less likely to be unmuted. 🔹 Competing Sound Sources. Background noise or other audio distractions can deter users from unmuting. 🔹 Unclear Value Proposition. If the video doesn’t quickly convey value, viewers won’t bother unmuting. 🔹 Negative Prior Experiences. Previous negative experiences with similar ads can reduce unmute rates. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Mute Preferences. Increasing preference for consuming content silently, especially in public or shared spaces.

nodes.description: View-through rate (vtr). Views divided by impressions.
nodes.formula: Number of views / Number of impressions * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: VTR measures viewer engagement with ads by calculating the rate of views relative to impressions, guiding optimizations to enhance ad content and placement effectiveness.
nodes.questions: What is the trend in view-through rate across different campaigns and ad formats? How does the view-through rate vary between different audience demographics? What factors contribute to fluctuations in the view-through rate across different time periods? How does seasonality impact the view-through rate in Sponsored Display and Sponsored Brands campaigns? How effective are changes in ad creatives in improving the view-through rate?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Unengaging Content. Videos that fail to capture interest lead to lower view-through rates. 🔹 Misleading Thumbnails or Titles. If the initial impression doesn’t match the content, viewers stop watching. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Lengthy Videos. Longer videos can deter full views, especially if not engaging throughout. 🔹 Poor Video Quality. Technical issues like low resolution or buffering can cause viewers to abandon the video. 🔹 Inadequate Targeting. Ads shown to an uninterested audience lead to lower view-through rates. 🔹 Competitive Distractions. Viewers distracted by other content or multitasking reduce full view rates. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Seasonal or Temporal Factors. Viewer engagement can vary based on time of year or other external factors.

nodes.description: Percentage of loyalty members who have complete profiles, including all required information.
nodes.formula: (Members with Complete Profiles / Total Members) * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Monitoring profile completeness ensures that the program has accurate and detailed information about its members. Complete profiles enhance personalization and targeted marketing efforts, leading to improved member experiences
nodes.questions: 1. How has the member profile completeness rate evolved over the past year? 2. What factors influence members to complete their profiles? 3. How does profile completeness impact member engagement and satisfaction? 4. What is the correlation between profile completeness and program participation rates? 5. How do different communication strategies affect profile completeness rates? 6. What incentives can be offered to improve profile completeness? 7. How does profile completeness vary across different member segments?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Time-consuming process. If the profile completion process is too lengthy, members may not finish it. 🔹 Privacy concerns. Members may be reluctant to share personal information. 🔹 Poor user interface. A difficult-to-navigate profile completion process can discourage members. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Lack of clear benefits. Members may not see the value in completing their profiles. 🔹 Infrequent reminders. Not reminding members to complete their profiles can lead to lower completion rates. 🔹 Technical issues. System glitches or errors can prevent profile completion. 🔹 Changes in required information. Updates to required fields can result in previously complete profiles becoming incomplete.

nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.

nodes.description: Percentage of points earned per dollar spent, showing how quickly members can accumulate points.
nodes.formula: Points Earned / Dollars Spent * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: The point accrual rate shows how quickly members can accumulate points based on their spending. This metric is important for evaluating the attractiveness of the earning structure and its impact on member spending behavior.
nodes.questions: 1. How has the point accrual rate changed over the past year? 2. What factors most significantly affect the point accrual rate? 3. How does the point accrual rate vary across different product categories? 4. What is the relationship between point accrual rate and member engagement? 5. How do changes in the point accrual rate influence member behavior? 6. What is the impact of point accrual rate on overall program costs? 7. How does our point accrual rate compare to industry standards?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Reduced member spending. Lower spending by members leads to fewer points earned. 🔹 Changes in accrual rules. Modifications to how points are earned can affect the accrual rate. 🔹 Technical errors. Issues with the point tracking system can lead to incorrect accrual rates. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Increased point value. If points are worth more, fewer points may be given per dollar spent. 🔹 Changes in product categories. Shifts in member purchases to lower-point categories can reduce the accrual rate. 🔹 Reduction in promotional offers. Fewer promotional point-earning opportunities can lead to a decrease in the accrual rate. 🔹 Increased competition. Competitors offering better accrual rates can divert member spending.
