nodes.questions: How does the type of content (e.g., product reviews, guides, blog posts) influence its ability to drive sales? What is the relationship between content recirculation and content-driven sales? How do content-driven sales differ based on the customer’s location or demographics (e.g., age, interests)? How does the timing of content publication (e.g., product launches, promotions) impact sales driven by content? How does the number of content interactions a customer has before making a purchase influence the likelihood of content-driven sales? What is the correlation between content performance metrics (e.g., engagement, shares) and content-driven sales? How does content-driven sales performance vary across different traffic sources (e.g., organic search, social media)?
nodes.slices: - Content type - Sales funnel stage - Traffic source - Product type - Time of interaction

nodes.description: The average time it takes for a content-heavy page to fully load for visitors.
nodes.formula: Page Load Time = Total Page Load Time / Total Content Page Views
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Page load time is crucial for user experience, as slow-loading pages can drive visitors away. Monitoring this metric helps improve site performance, ensuring that content pages load quickly and reducing bounce rates.
nodes.questions: How does the page load time differ between content-heavy pages (e.g., videos, infographics) and text-based content? What is the correlation between page load time and user engagement metrics (e.g., time on page, bounce rate)? How does page load time vary based on the user’s location or internet connection speed? How does the device used to access content (e.g., mobile vs. desktop) impact page load times? What is the relationship between page load time and content conversion rates (e.g., sign-ups, purchases)? How does the inclusion of interactive elements (e.g., quizzes, videos) impact the overall page load time? How does optimizing image or video sizes affect the total load time for content-heavy pages?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Large or unoptimized multimedia elements. Content with large, uncompressed images or videos will take longer to load, increasing page load time. 🔹 Server performance or hosting issues. Slow server response times or inadequate hosting solutions can increase content page load times. 🔹 Excessive use of tracking or third-party scripts. Too many tracking scripts or ads can slow down page load time, especially on mobile devices. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Poor website optimization for mobile devices. Content that isn’t optimized for mobile browsing can result in slower load times on smartphones and tablets. 🔹 Outdated website or content management system. An outdated CMS or website architecture can negatively impact page load times, especially with content-heavy pages.
nodes.slices: - Device type - Content format - Page complexity - Geographical location - Traffic volume

tactics.tactic: Optimize image and video files for web

nodes.description: The total number of content ideas that were placed in the backlog but were never moved to production.
nodes.formula: Dead Content Ideas = Total Abandoned Ideas in Backlog
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: A high number of dead content ideas may indicate inefficiencies in content planning or a lack of prioritization. Tracking this metric helps identify which ideas are abandoned and allows you to focus on more feasible, impactful content creation.
nodes.questions: What factors contribute to content ideas being abandoned in the backlog for extended periods? How does the type of content idea (e.g., evergreen vs. time-sensitive) affect the likelihood of it being abandoned? What is the relationship between the complexity of a content idea and its chances of being moved out of the backlog? How does the number of new content ideas being generated impact the overall size of the backlog and the abandonment rate? What role does team capacity or resource allocation play in the likelihood of content ideas being abandoned? How does content priority or relevance change over time, leading to more content ideas being abandoned in the backlog? What is the correlation between the introduction of new tools or processes and the reduction in dead content ideas?
nodes.slices: - Content type - Idea complexity - Team capacity - Content priority - Idea origin

nodes.description: The number of content pieces created internally by the company’s employees.
nodes.formula: In-House Content = Total Number of Content Created Internally
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking in-house content helps assess the internal team’s productivity and content output. It also helps compare the effectiveness and cost-efficiency of internally produced content versus outsourced content.
nodes.questions: How does the type of content (e.g., blog posts, videos, infographics) produced in-house differ from content outsourced to freelancers or agencies? What is the correlation between in-house content creation and overall content quality or performance (e.g., engagement, conversions)? How do production timelines for in-house content compare to outsourced content production? What is the relationship between in-house content team size and overall content output or diversity of content formats? How does the cost of producing content in-house compare to outsourcing content creation to external agencies or freelancers? How do different content formats (e.g., articles, videos) produced in-house impact brand consistency and alignment with messaging? What is the impact of in-house content production on overall content strategy (e.g., speed of production, creative control)?
nodes.slices: - Content type - Creator role - Production cost - Content complexity - Team size

nodes.description: The total number of external links pointing to your content, which can improve SEO and site authority.
nodes.formula: Inbound Links = Total Number of Links from External Websites
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Inbound links are essential for improving SEO and boosting content authority. Tracking this metric shows how well your content attracts external attention, improving search engine rankings and increasing organic traffic.
nodes.questions: How does the number of inbound links differ between content types (e.g., blog posts, videos, case studies)? What is the relationship between content performance metrics (e.g., engagement, shares) and the number of inbound links? How does the domain authority of linking websites impact the SEO benefits of inbound links to content? How do link-building strategies (e.g., outreach, guest blogging) affect the volume and quality of inbound links? How does the age of the content influence its ability to attract inbound links over time? What is the impact of content updates or refreshes on attracting new inbound links to older content? How does the presence of multimedia elements within the content (e.g., images, videos) affect the likelihood of attracting inbound links?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Decline in external outreach or promotion efforts. If fewer efforts are made to promote content externally (e.g., guest posts, outreach campaigns), fewer inbound links will be generated. 🔹 Lower-quality or less shareable content. If content quality drops or is less relevant, fewer websites will link to it. 🔹 Lack of focus on link-building strategies. A failure to prioritize link-building initiatives can result in fewer inbound links over time. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Increased competition for backlinks. Competitors creating more valuable or higher-quality content may attract more backlinks, reducing the number of links to your content. 🔹 Changes in SEO algorithms or link-building best practices. Algorithm updates may reduce the effectiveness of certain types of links, leading to fewer inbound links over time.
nodes.slices: - Content type - Source type - Domain authority - Content age - Link acquisition method

tactics.tactic: Outreach to industry influencers and thought leaders
Refresh and repurpose older high-performing content

nodes.description: The percentage of visitors who subscribe to a newsletter after interacting with content.
nodes.formula: Sign-Up Rate = (Newsletter Sign-Ups / Total Content Visitors) * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric indicates how effective your content is at driving newsletter subscriptions. A higher sign-up rate means your content is compelling enough to encourage ongoing engagement through email communication, boosting retention.
nodes.questions: How does the newsletter sign-up rate vary by content type (e.g., blog posts, landing pages, videos)? What is the relationship between the placement of the sign-up form (e.g., pop-up, sidebar, footer) and the sign-up rate? How does the timing of content publication (e.g., during promotions or product launches) affect newsletter sign-up rates? What is the effect of different traffic sources (e.g., social media, organic search, referral) on newsletter sign-up rates? How do content engagement metrics (e.g., time on page, shares) correlate with higher newsletter sign-up rates? How does the use of lead magnets (e.g., e-books, free trials) impact the overall newsletter sign-up rate? What is the effect of audience segmentation (e.g., demographics, interests) on the newsletter sign-up rate?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Irrelevant or unengaging content. If the content doesn’t align with the audience’s interests, visitors are less likely to sign up for newsletters. 🔹 Poorly positioned or designed sign-up forms. If sign-up forms are difficult to find or not visually appealing, visitors may overlook them, reducing the sign-up rate. 🔹 Lack of a compelling value proposition for the newsletter. If the benefits of signing up for the newsletter aren’t clear or valuable, users are less likely to subscribe. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Decrease in traffic from high-converting sources. If traffic from channels that typically result in higher newsletter sign-ups (e.g., organic search, email marketing) decreases, the sign-up rate may decline. 🔹 Overuse of pop-ups or aggressive sign-up prompts. Excessive use of pop-ups can frustrate users, causing them to abandon the site before subscribing to the newsletter.
nodes.slices: - Content type - CTA placement - Audience segment - Traffic source - Content performance

tactics.tactic: Personalize sign-up forms based on user behavior
Include testimonials or social proof near sign-up forms

nodes.description: The total number of users who subscribe to your newsletter, driven by content efforts.
nodes.formula: Newsletter Sign-Ups = Total Sign-Ups from Content
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Newsletter sign-ups measure the success of your content in building a loyal audience. Tracking the number of sign-ups provides insight into how well your content generates leads and grows your email marketing list.
nodes.questions: How do newsletter sign-ups vary by content distribution channel (e.g., email, social media, organic search)? What is the relationship between newsletter sign-ups and content engagement metrics (e.g., shares, comments)? How does the use of gated content (e.g., white papers, webinars) influence the total number of newsletter sign-ups? How do external factors (e.g., seasonality, promotions) impact the volume of newsletter sign-ups? What is the correlation between content performance (e.g., traffic, time on page) and the number of newsletter sign-ups? How do different lead generation strategies (e.g., pop-ups, CTAs) affect the overall number of newsletter sign-ups? What role does audience segmentation (e.g., new vs. returning visitors) play in influencing newsletter sign-up rates?
nodes.slices: - Content type - Campaign type - Timing of publication - User behavior - Referral traffic

nodes.description: The number of content pieces created by freelancers or external agencies.
nodes.formula: Outsourced Content = Total Number of Content Created Externally
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking outsourced content helps you understand the balance between internal and external content creation. It informs decisions about resource allocation and whether to increase in-house capabilities or continue outsourcing content production.
nodes.questions: How does the performance of outsourced content (e.g., engagement, conversions) compare to in-house content? What is the cost difference between producing outsourced content and in-house content, and how does this impact ROI? How does the turnaround time for outsourced content compare to that of in-house production? What is the effect of outsourcing specific content types (e.g., videos, infographics) on the overall quality and consistency of content? How do outsourced content creators adhere to brand guidelines and messaging compared to in-house creators? What role does outsourcing play in increasing overall content output or production capacity during peak periods? How does the geographical location of outsourced content creators affect the production timeline and cost of content?
nodes.slices: - Content type - Freelancers vs. agencies - Production cost - Content performance - Deadline adherence

nodes.description: The return on investment from content marketing efforts, based on revenue or leads generated compared to costs.
nodes.formula: ROC = (Revenue from Content – Content Costs) / Content Costs * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: ROC measures the effectiveness of your content marketing by comparing the revenue or leads generated to the costs of content production. It helps assess whether content investments are yielding positive returns and informs budget allocation decisions.
nodes.questions: How does the return on content differ based on content format (e.g., blog posts, case studies, videos)? What is the relationship between content production cost and the overall ROI of content marketing efforts? How does ROC vary across different traffic sources (e.g., paid, organic, referral)? What is the impact of content length or complexity on the return on content investment? How do changes in the content distribution strategy (e.g., organic promotion vs. paid ads) affect the return on content investment? What role does content performance (e.g., engagement, conversions) play in determining ROC across different content types? How does ROC fluctuate during specific campaigns, promotions, or product launches?
nodes.slices: - Content type - Traffic source - Audience segment - Conversion type - Content production cost

nodes.description: Measures how far visitors scroll through a content page, showing their level of engagement.
nodes.formula: Scroll Depth = (Average Scroll Position / Total Content Height) * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Scroll depth indicates how engaged visitors are with your content by showing how far they scroll through a page. A higher scroll depth suggests that users are finding the content interesting and valuable, while a low scroll depth may indicate content or layout issues.
nodes.questions: How does scroll depth vary by content type (e.g., articles, long-form guides, videos)? What is the correlation between scroll depth and user engagement metrics (e.g., time on page, shares)? How does the presence of multimedia elements (e.g., images, videos) within content affect the average scroll depth? How does scroll depth differ between new visitors and returning visitors to the site? How does scroll depth correlate with conversion metrics (e.g., purchases, sign-ups)? How does content length (e.g., short-form vs. long-form) impact the overall scroll depth for different user segments? How do different traffic sources (e.g., organic search, social media) influence the scroll depth of visitors?
nodes.slices: - Content type - Page length - Device type - Traffic source - User engagement level

nodes.description: The total number of visual content pieces (e.g., infographics, videos) produced alongside written content.
nodes.formula: Visual Production = Total Number of Visual Content Pieces
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking visual content production helps gauge the output of non-written content, such as videos and infographics, which can enhance audience engagement. This metric is crucial for understanding the balance of different content types and their contribution to user interaction.
nodes.questions: How does the production of visual content vary across different formats (e.g., videos, infographics, animations)? What is the relationship between the cost of visual content production and its overall performance (e.g., engagement, shares)? How does the time required for visual content production compare to that of written content production? What is the correlation between team size and the overall volume of visual content produced? How does the performance of visual content (e.g., in terms of conversions, engagement) vary by content distribution channel (e.g., social media, website)? What role does the use of visual content play in increasing the overall engagement rates of written content it accompanies? How does the complexity of visual content (e.g., simple graphics vs. detailed infographics) impact the total production timeline?
nodes.slices: - Content format - Team size - Project complexity - Production cost - Content performance

nodes.description: The average number of referrals generated by each referrer.
nodes.formula: Total Referrals / Number of Unique Referrers
nodes.frequency: Annually. Try to check this metric each year.
nodes.why: This metric helps identify which referrers are most effective in driving new business. Tracking it can provide insight into the productivity of referrers and allow businesses to optimize their referral programs.
nodes.questions: How does the average number of referrals differ between referrers from various geographic regions? What is the correlation between referrer engagement (e.g., email open rates, interaction with referral tools) and the number of referrals generated? How does the average number of referrals change depending on the referrer’s activity level (e.g., frequent vs. occasional participants)? How does the average referral rate vary across different promotional campaigns or time periods? Are referrers with multiple referral sources (email, social media) generating more referrals than those using a single source? What is the impact of the type of incentive on the average number of referrals per referrer (e.g., cash vs. non-cash rewards)?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Incentive structure is not appealing enough.. Referrers may not feel motivated to refer more if the rewards aren’t significant or meaningful to them. 🔹 Lack of program awareness among new customers.. New customers may not be fully aware of the referral program, so fewer new referrers join. 🔹 Complex referral process.. A complicated or time-consuming referral process may discourage referrers from making more referrals. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Saturation of referral base.. Existing referrers may have already referred most of their contacts, leading to fewer new referrals being generated. 🔹 Poor communication or follow-up from the brand.. Inconsistent communication and reminders can result in referrers losing interest and not promoting the referral program actively.
nodes.slices: - Referral source - Referrer loyalty - Geographical region of referrer - Referrer engagement level - Industry type

nodes.description: The rate at which suspicious referrals stop engaging with the program.
nodes.formula: (Number of Suspicious Referrals that Churned / Total Suspicious Referrals) * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking this rate helps businesses monitor potential fraud and disengagement. It provides a signal of when to investigate and address suspicious referral activity.
nodes.questions: Which referral sources (e.g., social media, direct) exhibit the highest churn rates for suspicious referrals? Is there a relationship between the time spent in the program before churn and the likelihood of a suspicious referral? How does the churn rate for suspicious referrals compare across regions or countries? Does the churn rate increase when referrals are flagged by automated detection tools versus manual reviews? What impact do different referral program changes or rule modifications have on the churn rate of suspicious referrals? Are there certain times of year or specific campaigns that lead to an increase in suspicious referral churn?
nodes.slices: - Referral method - Referral validation status - Source of suspicion - Time since referral - Country of referral

nodes.description: The percentage of accounts closed due to confirmed fraudulent activity.
nodes.formula: (Number of Closed Accounts for Fraud / Total Accounts) * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Monitoring this rate is crucial for fraud prevention and overall platform security. A high closure rate may indicate systemic issues or areas that require stronger controls.
nodes.questions: How does the fraudulent account closure rate vary based on the age of the account (new vs. established)? What percentage of accounts closed for fraud had prior suspicious activity flagged before closure? How do different fraud detection systems (AI-based vs. manual reviews) affect the fraudulent account closure rate? Is there a correlation between the frequency of referrals and the likelihood of an account being closed for fraud? Do accounts from specific geographic regions have higher closure rates due to fraudulent activity? Are high-revenue-generating accounts less likely to be closed for fraud than lower-revenue accounts?
nodes.slices: - Account age - Fraud detection mechanism - Account activity level - Frequency of referrals made - Account location

nodes.description: The total number of referrals flagged as potentially fraudulent.
nodes.formula: Count of Fraudulent Referrals
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: This metric helps businesses detect and prevent fraudulent activity within the referral program. Tracking the number of fraudulent referrals can help refine fraud detection systems and improve trust in the program.
nodes.questions: What is the trend of fraudulent referrals over time and how does it align with changes in referral policies? How does the rate of fraudulent referrals compare across different referral channels (e.g., email, social media)? Is there a relationship between the referrer’s history and the likelihood of generating fraudulent referrals? How does the fraud detection rate change when different incentives are offered (high-value vs. low-value rewards)? Do certain campaigns or promotions see spikes in fraudulent referrals compared to others? How does the fraudulent referral rate vary across geographic regions or demographic groups?
nodes.slices: - Referral type - Campaign-specific referrals - Referrer account activity - Time of referral - Referrer incentive value

nodes.description: The percentage of referrers who misuse or abuse incentives for profit.
nodes.formula: (Number of Abusive Claims / Total Incentive Claims) * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Incentive abuse rate reveals the extent to which users exploit the referral system for profit. Lower rates indicate a healthier, more sustainable program that isn’t being manipulated.
nodes.questions: How does the abuse rate vary between referrers who make frequent vs. infrequent referrals? What types of incentives (e.g., cash, discounts, gifts) show higher instances of misuse or abuse? How does the abuse rate differ across various geographical regions? Are there specific campaigns or referral periods where incentive abuse spikes? How does the abuse rate correlate with the referrer’s lifetime value (LTV)? Is there a higher incidence of abuse among certain demographic segments of referrers?
nodes.slices: - Incentive type - Number of claims per referrer - Time of referral - Geographical location of referrer - Referrer demographic

nodes.description: The total number of referrals deemed invalid due to non-compliance.
nodes.formula: Count of Invalid Referrals
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking invalid referrals ensures that referral programs operate within compliance standards. High invalid referral rates can indicate problems with program clarity or participant misuse.
nodes.questions: What percentage of invalid referrals are associated with specific non-compliance issues (e.g., incomplete data, referral policy violations)? How do invalid referral rates vary across different referral channels? Are invalid referrals more common from new or experienced referrers? What is the trend in invalid referrals following program policy updates? How does the invalid referral rate compare between different incentive types? Are there geographical regions that have significantly higher rates of invalid referrals?
nodes.slices: - Referral verification process - Source of referral - Region of referral - Referrer history - Time of referral

nodes.description: The percentage of customers who abandon the referral process.
nodes.formula: (Number of Abandoned Referrals / Total Initiated Referrals) * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: A high abandonment rate indicates friction in the referral process that may be discouraging users. Reducing this rate helps improve program engagement and effectiveness.
nodes.questions: Which step in the referral process shows the highest rate of abandonment? How does the abandonment rate vary between different referral platforms (e.g., mobile vs. desktop)? Are abandonment rates higher during specific campaigns or promotional periods? What effect does the complexity of the referral form have on abandonment? How do different types of incentives (e.g., cash rewards vs. discounts) impact abandonment rates? How does the abandonment rate differ based on the demographic profile of the referrer?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Complex or long referral form.. Customers may abandon the referral process if the form is too lengthy or requires too much personal information. 🔹 Slow or delayed rewards for referrers.. If referrers don’t see rewards quickly, they may lose interest in completing the process. 🔹 Website or platform technical issues.. Technical glitches, slow loading pages, or broken links can cause users to give up on the referral process. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Lack of clear instructions on how the referral process works.. Customers may abandon if they don’t understand the next steps or the benefits clearly. 🔹 Unattractive incentive for the referrer or referee.. If the reward doesn’t seem worthwhile, customers may not complete the referral process.
nodes.slices: - Stage of abandonment - Referral platform - Complexity of referral process - Time of referral - Referrer incentive type

tactics.tactic: Offer instant feedback on form submission
Offer a concierge service for high-value referrals

nodes.description: The average time taken for invited customers to accept a referral invitation.
nodes.formula: Average time from invitation sent to acceptance
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Lag time measures how quickly recipients engage with referral invitations. Shorter lag times suggest a more responsive audience and can inform timing and content strategies.
nodes.questions: What is the relationship between the lag time and the method of communication used (e.g., email vs. SMS)? How does referral acceptance lag vary by geographical region? Does the timing of the referral (e.g., weekdays vs. weekends) impact lag time? How does the referral lag time compare across different referrer demographic groups? Does sending reminders to invitees reduce the overall acceptance lag time? How does the incentive value affect the lag time for referral acceptance?
nodes.slices: - Communication channel - Referrer type - Geographical region of invited customers - Referral timing - Number of referral reminders sent

nodes.description: The average amount spent per order by referred customers.
nodes.formula: Total Revenue from Referrals / Number of Orders from Referrals
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Referral AOV highlights the value of referred customers. Tracking this metric helps businesses understand the purchasing behavior of referred customers, contributing to revenue forecasts and marketing strategies.
nodes.questions: How does the AOV of referred customers compare between various product categories? Is there a difference in AOV for referrals generated via different channels (e.g., email vs. social)? How does AOV vary based on the referrer’s lifetime value? What is the impact of seasonal promotions on the AOV of referred customers? How does AOV differ between new vs. returning referred customers? Is there a correlation between the time to first purchase and the AOV of referred customers?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Targeting customers with lower purchasing power.. If referrals are being directed toward customers with less disposable income, the average order value will decrease. 🔹 Referral discounts or offers tied to low-priced products.. Offering referral incentives for low-priced products can lead to smaller order values. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Low-value incentives for referees.. If the referee incentive is not compelling, they may only make small purchases, reducing AOV. 🔹 Ineffective upselling or cross-selling efforts.. A lack of promotions, bundles, or upselling tactics may result in lower value purchases from referred customers. 🔹 Changes in product mix or availability.. A shift towards selling more low-priced products can reduce the AOV for referred customers.
nodes.slices: - Referrer tier - Customer segment - Product category - Seasonality - Referral channel

tactics.tactic: Promote bundles or upsell opportunities during referral checkout
Tie referral discounts to a minimum purchase amount
Highlight bestsellers and high-value products on referral landing pages
Offer referral-specific deals on premium products
Provide dynamic pricing based on customer behavior

nodes.description: The percentage of referred customers who stop purchasing over time.
nodes.formula: (Number of Lost Customers from Referrals / Total Referred Customers) * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Referral churn rate provides insight into customer retention. A high churn rate may suggest issues with product satisfaction or long-term engagement with referred customers.
nodes.questions: How does the churn rate differ between customers who were referred through different channels? What is the relationship between the number of purchases and the likelihood of churn among referred customers? How does churn rate vary across different product categories? Does participation in a loyalty program affect the churn rate of referred customers? How does the churn rate differ based on the timing of the referral (e.g., during or after promotional campaigns)? Is there a significant difference in churn rates between high-spending vs. low-spending referred customers?
nodes.slices: - Referral source - Purchase frequency of referred customer - Customer loyalty program participation - Customer support interaction - Product category purchased

nodes.description: The percentage of referred recipients who click on the referral link.
nodes.formula: (Number of Clicks on Referral Links / Total Links Sent) * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Referral CTR shows how effective referral links are at driving engagement. A higher CTR indicates that referral messaging is compelling and well-targeted.
nodes.questions: How does CTR vary across different referral channels (e.g., email, social media)? What is the impact of different types of referral content (e.g., video vs. text) on CTR? How does the timing of sending referral links (morning vs. evening) affect CTR? Is there a relationship between referrer demographics and the CTR of their links? What is the impact of personalized vs. generic referral messages on CTR? How does the placement of referral links within an email or web page influence CTR?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Poorly designed or unengaging referral emails or messages.. Unappealing email design or lack of a strong call to action can reduce the likelihood of recipients clicking on referral links. 🔹 Referral links not optimized for mobile devices.. A poor mobile experience may prevent users from clicking on referral links. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Irrelevant or generic referral messaging.. If the referral message doesn’t resonate with the recipient, they may ignore the referral link. 🔹 Over-saturation of referral invitations.. Sending too many referral invites can cause recipients to ignore or dismiss them, leading to lower CTR. 🔹 Referral links buried in long or complicated messages.. If the referral link is not easy to find, recipients may not click on it.
nodes.slices: - Link placement - Device type - Time of day - Link type - Source of referral

tactics.tactic: Personalize referral messages based on referrer history
Optimize referral content for mobile devices
Test different CTAs with A/B testing

nodes.description: The percentage of referred visitors who make a purchase.
nodes.formula: (Number of Purchases from Referrals / Total Referrals) * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Referral conversion rate measures how many referred visitors complete a purchase. Tracking this helps assess the overall effectiveness of the referral program in driving sales.
nodes.questions: How does conversion rate differ between first-time and repeat referred customers? What is the impact of different referral incentives on the conversion rate? Does the length of time between receiving a referral link and making a purchase affect conversion rates? How do conversion rates vary across different product categories or services? What is the relationship between referral source (e.g., social media, direct email) and conversion rate? How does the conversion rate change during promotional periods compared to non-promotional times?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Misalignment between referred product and customer expectations.. If the product being referred doesn’t meet the needs or expectations of the customer, they may not make a purchase. 🔹 Inadequate landing page experience.. If the landing page after clicking the referral link is poorly designed or lacks clarity, it may lead to a drop in conversions. 🔹 Poor product availability or shipping options.. Customers who are interested in purchasing may not convert if products are out of stock or shipping options are limited. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Referral rewards too small to motivate purchases.. If the incentives offered are not enough to encourage conversions, customers may abandon the purchase. 🔹 Lack of urgency in referral offers.. Referral campaigns without time-limited offers may not motivate customers to act quickly, reducing conversion rates.
nodes.slices: - Referrer demographic - Referral method - Product type - Traffic source - Referral touchpoints

tactics.tactic: Reduce steps in the referral purchase process
Provide a money-back guarantee for referred purchases

nodes.description: The total cost incurred to acquire a new customer through referrals.
nodes.formula: Total Referral Program Costs / Number of New Customers Acquired
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: CAC indicates how much it costs to acquire a customer through referrals. Keeping this cost low ensures the referral program remains profitable and scalable.
nodes.questions: How does CAC differ between various referral channels (e.g., social media vs. direct email)? What is the impact of increasing referral program spending on CAC? How does the CAC for referral programs compare to other customer acquisition methods? How does referrer activity level (high vs. low) affect the overall CAC? What is the effect of incentive type (monetary vs. non-monetary) on CAC? How does the CAC fluctuate depending on the geographical region of the referrers or customers?
nodes.slices: - Acquisition channel - Referrer incentive type - Referrer activity level - Region of referred customers - Marketing spend per channel

nodes.description: The percentage of referred customers who continue to make purchases.
nodes.formula: (Number of Customers who Purchase Again from Referrals / Total Referred Customers) * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: This rate helps track how well referred customers are retained. Retaining referred customers increases their lifetime value and improves long-term profitability.
nodes.questions: How does retention rate differ between customers referred through different channels? What is the relationship between the frequency of purchases and the retention rate of referred customers? How does the retention rate of referred customers change over time? What impact do loyalty programs have on the retention rate of referred customers? How does product category affect the retention rate of referred customers? Is there a correlation between referral incentives and long-term customer retention?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Low customer satisfaction with referred product or service.. Customers who are dissatisfied with the product they were referred to are unlikely to return and make future purchases. 🔹 Lack of follow-up or customer engagement after the initial purchase.. If there is no post-purchase engagement or communication, referred customers may not feel compelled to make repeat purchases. 🔹 High competition or better alternatives.. Customers may find better products or services elsewhere, causing them to stop making repeat purchases from the referred brand. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Referrers not providing ongoing support or advocacy.. Referred customers may churn if the referrer does not remain engaged or continues to advocate for the brand 🔹 Changes in the referral program or incentives.. If the referral program becomes less attractive or changes, customers may lose interest in staying loyal.
nodes.slices: - Loyalty status - Purchase history - Outreach frequency - Product type - Support interaction

tactics.tactic: Implement a loyalty program for referred customers
Send replenishment reminders for consumable products
Offer special rewards for subsequent purchases

nodes.description: The percentage of referral invitation emails that are opened.
nodes.formula: (Number of Opened Emails / Total Emails Sent) * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Email open rate reflects the effectiveness of referral invitation emails. A high open rate indicates that the subject line and sender reputation are working well to capture attention.
nodes.questions: How does the time of day (morning vs. evening) impact email open rates for referral invitations? What is the impact of personalized subject lines on referral email open rates? How do open rates vary across different email providers or devices (mobile vs. desktop)? Is there a correlation between the referrer’s activity level and the open rates of their referral emails? How does email design (plain text vs. HTML) influence referral open rates? What is the effect of sending follow-up referral emails on overall open rates?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Unappealing subject lines.. Boring or irrelevant subject lines can cause recipients to ignore the referral emails. 🔹 Referral emails marked as spam.. If too many emails are sent or the content triggers spam filters, email open rates can decline. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Sending referral emails at suboptimal times.. Emails sent during busy times or in inappropriate time zones may get ignored. 🔹 Inconsistent or infrequent email communications.. If referral emails are sent irregularly, customers may not anticipate them and may fail to open them. 🔹 Poor personalization of referral emails.. Generic or non-personalized emails may not resonate with recipients, reducing open rates.
nodes.slices: - Subject length - Send time - Referrer type - Email design - Recipient demo

Send referral emails from a recognizable sender address
Send referral emails at optimal times

What is the correlation between the incentive value and the likelihood of fraud in referrals? How does fraud detection rate change during promotional periods or high-volume campaigns? Does the geographical region of the referrer influence the fraud detection rate?

nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.

How does referrer engagement level influence the likelihood of utilizing incentives? Is there a correlation between the size of the incentive and the referrer’s likelihood of using it? How does the utilization rate vary across different demographic groups of referrers? Does the timing of when the referral was made (seasonal vs. off-season) affect the utilization rate?

What is the relationship between referrer activity level and the rate of invalid referrals? How does the invalid referral rate differ across different regions or countries? Are there specific campaigns or periods that tend to generate higher invalid referral rates? How do different referral program rules or restrictions affect the invalid referral rate? What is the correlation between referral incentive size and invalid referral rates?

nodes.description: The percentage of invitations that result in a successful referral.
nodes.formula: (Number of Accepted Referrals / Total Invitations Sent) * 100
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Acceptance rate tracks how often invitations lead to successful referrals. A high rate indicates that the referral message resonates well with recipients and motivates them to take action.
nodes.questions: How does acceptance rate differ based on the communication method (email vs. SMS)? What impact does sending multiple reminders have on the referral acceptance rate? How does the acceptance rate vary between different demographic segments? What is the relationship between the time elapsed since the invitation was sent and the acceptance rate? How do different incentive types impact the overall invitation acceptance rate? Does the day of the week or time of day affect the acceptance rate for referral invitations?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Lack of trust between the referrer and referee.. If the referee doesn’t fully trust the referrer, they may not accept the referral invitation. 🔹 Referral incentives not compelling enough.. If the reward offered for accepting the referral is too small or irrelevant, invitees may not act on it. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Poor timing of referral invitations.. Sending referral invitations at inconvenient times (e.g., holidays, busy periods) can reduce acceptance rates. 🔹 Overwhelming or unclear referral invitation content.. Invitations that are too long or complicated may deter recipients from accepting. 🔹 Negative past experiences with referral programs.. If recipients have had poor experiences with referral programs in the past, they may be less likely to accept invitations.
nodes.slices: - Communication method - Incentive value - Time since invite - Reminders - Region

Offer referral incentives that benefit both parties
Highlight social proof in referral invitations

nodes.description: The percentage of referrers who follow up with their invited friends.

nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Follow-up rate measures how often referrers remind invitees to complete the referral. Tracking this helps assess the commitment and persistence of referrers, impacting referral success.
nodes.questions: How does follow-up rate differ between referrers who have made multiple vs. single referrals? What impact does offering an additional incentive in the follow-up have on the follow-up rate? How does the timing of follow-up (immediate vs. delayed) affect the response rate? How does the follow-up rate vary across different referral channels? What is the relationship between referrer engagement and their follow-up behavior? How does the follow-up rate differ across various demographic or geographical segments?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Lack of engagement or interest from referrers.. Referrers may not be motivated to follow up with their friends if they lose interest in the referral program. 🔹 No easy follow-up tools provided.. Without simple tools or templates to follow up, referrers may feel it’s too much effort to remind their friends. 🔹 Referrers disengaged due to program complexity.. If the referral process or platform is too complex, referrers may abandon the program, leading to fewer follow-ups. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Referrers unaware of the importance of follow-ups.. If referrers don’t realize that following up increases success, they may not take action. 🔹 Poor timing of follow-up reminders.. Follow-ups sent too late after the initial referral can reduce the likelihood of success.

nodes.description: The projected revenue that a referred customer will generate over their lifetime.

nodes.questions: How does referral LTV differ across product categories or services? What is the impact of the referral source (e.g., email vs. social media) on LTV? How does the frequency of referrals influence the LTV of referred customers? What is the relationship between customer demographic (e.g., age, gender) and referral LTV? How does referral LTV compare between customers acquired via referrals vs. other marketing channels? How does the duration between referral and first purchase impact LTV?

How does the network effect evolve over time during different marketing campaigns? What geographic regions show the strongest referral network effect? Frequent participants may become tired of the referral process and stop sending as many invitations. 🔹 Referral platform or tools too complex.. nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.

nodes.description: The return on investment for the referral program.
nodes.formula: (Referral Revenue - Total Referral Program Costs) / Total Referral Program Costs
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Referral program ROI measures the financial return of the program. It helps assess whether the referral program is cost-effective and generating profit.
nodes.questions: How does referral program ROI compare to other customer acquisition strategies? What is the relationship between referral incentive costs and ROI? How does the ROI of the referral program vary across different product categories? How does ROI differ between high-spending and low-spending customers referred through the program? How does program ROI change over time as more customers participate in the referral program? What is the impact of geographical regions on the overall ROI of the referral program?
nodes.slices: - Time period - Channel - Customer segment - Incentive type - Region

nodes.description: The percentage of users satisfied with the referral program experience.

nodes.description: The average time required to load orders onto delivery vehicles. This metric indicates the efficiency of the loading process in the warehouse.
nodes.formula: Total Loading Time / Total Orders Loaded
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Average loading time reflects the efficiency of warehouse operations. Reducing loading time can speed up the delivery process and improve overall logistics performance.
nodes.questions: How does loading time vary across different product categories (fragile, bulky items)? What is the impact of shift changes on average loading time in the warehouse? How do different loading equipment types (manual vs. automated) affect average loading time? Does team experience level correlate with shorter average loading times? Which warehouse locations exhibit the longest loading times, and what are the contributing factors? How does the average loading time change with variations in the number of items per order? What are the effects of different work shifts (morning, evening) on average loading time?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Insufficient training of warehouse staff. Workers take longer to complete loading due to lack of expertise. 🔹 Outdated or malfunctioning loading equipment. Equipment issues can slow down the loading process significantly. 🔹 Shortage of loading staff. Fewer staff members available leads to longer loading times. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 High number of SKUs with complex packing requirements. More complex products require extra time for safe loading. 🔹 Inefficient warehouse layout. Poorly planned layout increases travel time between storage and loading areas. 🔹 Sudden increase in order volume. Unanticipated order surges can overwhelm existing resources and delay loading. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Quality control checks at loading. Additional quality checks can add time to the loading process.
nodes.slices: '- Warehouse location - Product type (small, large, fragile) - Shift time (morning, afternoon, night) - Loading equipment used - Team experience level

How does seasonality (e.g., holidays) influence average time in customs?
nodes.slices: '- Country of import/export - Product type (regulated, non-regulated) - Customs clearance method (manual, electronic) - Shipping partner - Time of year (holiday period, regular days)

nodes.description: Average time taken to unload orders from delivery vehicles at their destination points.
nodes.formula: Total Unloading Time / Total Orders Unloaded
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Average unloading time is crucial for ensuring quick turnover at delivery points. Shorter unloading times can enhance delivery capacity and optimize the distribution network.
nodes.questions: Which delivery destinations (residential, commercial) have longer average unloading times? How does the number of items per delivery impact average unloading time? What effect does vehicle type have on unloading efficiency? How do changes in the unloading team size affect average unloading time? Are there regional differences in unloading times based on delivery location? How does unloading time vary by product type (perishable, durable)? What time of day typically experiences longer average unloading times, and why?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Congested delivery points. Delivering to locations with limited space or high congestion can slow down unloading. 🔹 Insufficient unloading staff. A shortage of personnel leads to longer unloading times. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Unfamiliar delivery locations. Drivers and staff take longer at new or unfamiliar sites. 🔹 Complex product handling requirements. Fragile or large items need careful handling, extending unloading time. 🔹 Traffic and delays upon arrival. Arrival delays can cause unloading to overlap with peak times at the destination. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Weather-related disruptions. Bad weather can make unloading more difficult, especially for outdoor drop-offs. 🔹 Inefficient coordination between drivers and unloading staff. Poor communication slows the unloading process.
nodes.slices: '- Destination type (warehouse, retail store, home delivery) - Time of day - Team size at unloading - Product type (perishable, durable) - Delivery vehicle type

nodes.questions: How does container utilization rate vary between different product categories? What impact does shipment type (full, partial) have on container utilization? nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Poor container packing practices. Inefficient packing leads to unused container space. 🔹 Manual packing methods. Manual processes are less efficient compared to automated solutions. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Unbalanced order sizes. Large variations in order sizes can make it difficult to optimize space. 🔹 Limited availability of standardized container sizes. Mismatched container sizes lead to suboptimal utilization. 🔹 High volume of small or irregularly shaped items. These items can leave gaps in container space. 🔹 Inconsistent inbound shipment schedules. Unpredictable delivery times make it harder to maximize container use.

nodes.description: The percentage of deliveries that were damaged during transit, affecting customer satisfaction and increasing potential costs.
nodes.formula: Damaged Orders / Total Deliveries * 100
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

nodes.questions: Which product types are most frequently associated with a higher delivery damage rate? How does delivery vehicle type impact the rate of damaged deliveries? What is the relationship between packaging quality and delivery damage rates? Are certain delivery routes linked with higher instances of damaged deliveries? How do weather conditions affect the delivery damage rate? What time of year sees an increase in delivery damage rates, and why? nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Insufficient packaging quality. Poor packaging fails to protect items during transit. 🔹 Inadequate training for delivery staff. Improper handling increases the risk of damage. 🔹 Use of unsuitable vehicles. Using non-specialized vehicles for fragile items can result in higher damage rates. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Poor road conditions on delivery routes. Bad roads or rough handling can lead to higher damage rates. 🔹 Overloading delivery vehicles. Excessive load increases the likelihood of damage during transport. 🔹 Lack of quality control checks. Fewer checks before shipping can allow for damaged items to be sent out. 🟡 Low impact 🔹 Weather-related issues. Severe weather conditions can impact the safety of deliveries.

nodes.slices: '- Supplier type (primary, secondary) - Product type (fragile, durable) - Time frame (monthly, yearly) - Region (domestic, international suppliers) - Delivery method (express, standard)

nodes.description: The total number of different stock-keeping units (SKUs) currently in stock.
nodes.formula: Count of SKUs in Stock
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: The number of SKUs in stock reflects the diversity of available inventory, aiding in assortment planning and stock management.
nodes.questions: Which categories have the highest number of SKUs in stock, and what does this indicate about product diversity? How do the number of SKUs in stock impact inventory management complexity? What operational challenges arise from managing a large number of SKUs in stock? How do changes in customer demand affect the number of SKUs stocked? What is the impact of seasonal changes on the number of SKUs in stock? How does SKU management relate to overall warehouse space utilization? What strategies can improve SKU management efficiency to optimize stock levels?
nodes.slices: '- Product category (electronics, apparel) - Sales channel (online, in-store) - Warehouse location - Season (pre-season, post-season) - Supplier reliability

nodes.description: The total number of units supplied to the inventory over a period.
nodes.formula: Sum of Supplied Units
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking the total number of units supplied helps assess supply chain reliability and inventory flow. It ensures that stock levels align with demand and avoids disruptions.
nodes.questions: What product categories receive the highest number of supplied units, and why? How does supplier reliability affect the total number of units supplied over a period? What is the impact of seasonal demand on the total number of units supplied? How do changes in lead times impact the total units supplied? What role does supply chain efficiency play in maintaining a consistent number of units supplied? How does the volume of units supplied correlate with inventory holding and storage costs? What strategies can be implemented to optimize the total units supplied for high-demand products?
nodes.slices: '- Product category (bulk, perishable) - Supplier type (local, international) - Season (peak, off-peak) - Region (warehouse location) - Time frame (monthly, quarterly)

nodes.description: Frequency at which inventory is restocked, showing how often inventory is replenished.
nodes.formula: Total Replenishments / Period
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric indicates how often inventory is restocked, helping manage inventory turnover and ensuring that stock levels are maintained efficiently to meet customer demand.
nodes.questions: How does the replenishment rate vary across different product categories, and what does this indicate? What is the relationship between replenishment rate and sales velocity for high-demand products? How does seasonality affect inventory replenishment rate, and what strategies can optimize it? What impact does supplier lead time variability have on the frequency of inventory replenishments? How does inventory replenishment rate affect warehouse space utilization and holding costs? What are the operational challenges of maintaining a high replenishment rate, and how can they be mitigated? How does replenishment rate correlate with customer satisfaction and order fulfillment speed?
nodes.slices: '- Product category (fast-moving, slow-moving) - Warehouse location - Season (holiday, regular) - Supplier reliability - Demand variability

nodes.description: The total count of suppliers a company sources inventory from.
nodes.formula: Count of Suppliers
nodes.frequency: Annually. Try to check this metric each year.
nodes.why: Monitoring the number of suppliers provides insight into supply chain diversity and risk management. A diverse supplier base can increase flexibility and reduce dependency on single sources.
nodes.questions: How does the number of suppliers impact the risk and reliability of the supply chain? What is the effect of having a diverse supplier base on inventory replenishment and lead times? How do supplier count and quality affect overall procurement and inventory costs? What strategies can be implemented to optimize the number of suppliers for different product types? How does the number of suppliers correlate with product availability and stockouts? How do supplier relationships (long-term vs. new) influence the overall supply chain performance? What is the impact of increasing or consolidating the number of suppliers on operational efficiency?
nodes.slices: '- Product type sourced (raw materials, finished goods) - Region (local, international) - Reliability level (high, low) - Frequency of orders (weekly, monthly) - Product category (essential, non-essential)

nodes.description: The total number of deliveries received at the warehouse within a specific period.
nodes.formula: Count of Deliveries Received
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking warehouse deliveries helps measure the volume of incoming inventory, aiding in operational planning and resource allocation within the warehouse.
nodes.questions: How does the volume of warehouse deliveries vary throughout different days of the week or month? What product categories contribute most to the total number of warehouse deliveries? How do different delivery methods (e.g., bulk, individual) affect warehouse delivery volumes? What is the relationship between warehouse deliveries and inventory holding costs? How does the efficiency of warehouse operations impact the processing of incoming deliveries? How do seasonal trends influence the number of deliveries received at the warehouse? What measures can be implemented to streamline warehouse deliveries and reduce congestion?
nodes.slices: '- Product type (bulk, specialty) - Time of day (morning, evening) - Supplier type (primary, secondary) - Warehouse location - Season (peak, regular)

nodes.description: The amount of inventory needed to cover demand during the lead time period.
nodes.formula: Average Daily Demand * Lead Time
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: This metric ensures that sufficient inventory is available to meet demand during lead times, preventing stockouts and supporting continuous operations.
nodes.questions: What product types have the highest lead time demand, and how can this impact stock management? How does lead time demand change with fluctuations in supplier reliability? What operational strategies can be employed to manage high lead time demand? How does lead time demand impact the calculation of safety stock levels? What is the effect of seasonal demand on lead time requirements for different products? How does lead time demand correlate with customer order fulfillment rates? What adjustments can be made to reduce lead time demand and ensure smoother inventory flow?
nodes.slices: '- Product type (standard, custom) - Supplier type (local, international) - Demand consistency (high, low variability) - Time of year (peak season, off-peak) - Sales channel (online, direct)

nodes.description: The average lead time across all products from order to warehouse receipt.
nodes.formula: Sum of Product Lead Times / Total Products
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Monitoring average product lead time helps identify potential delays in supply chains. Shorter lead times improve responsiveness to market demands and enhance customer satisfaction.
nodes.questions: Which product categories have the longest average lead time, and what factors contribute to this? How does average product lead time affect overall inventory management and order planning? What is the impact of supplier location (domestic vs. international) on average lead time? How do changes in supplier performance influence the average product lead time? What measures can be taken to reduce the average product lead time and improve supply chain efficiency? How does average lead time correlate with stockout and backorder rates? How do seasonal trends affect the average lead time across different product lines?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Supplier production delays. Delays in production lead to longer lead times for products. 🔹 International shipping complications. Customs and international transport delays extend lead times. 🔹 Dependence on single-source suppliers. Relying on one supplier increases vulnerability to delays. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Limited transportation options. Fewer shipping options result in longer wait times for delivery. 🔹 Poor supplier management and coordination. Inefficient communication with suppliers can lead to longer lead times. 🔹 High order customization requirements. Custom orders take longer to process, extending lead times. 🔹 Seasonality and peak periods. High demand during peak seasons increases lead times.
nodes.slices: '- Product category (electronics, perishables) - Supplier origin (domestic, international) - Order volume (high, low) - Season (holiday, non-holiday) - Warehouse processing efficiency

nodes.description: The number of times your video was viewed for at least 15 seconds, or viewed in full if it is less than 15 seconds long. For each video impression, plays are counted separately and replays are excluded. This metric is only available for advertisers using focused view products.
nodes.formula: 15-second focused views (paid views) = Sum of views for at least 15 seconds
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric indicates how effectively your video captures attention for a meaningful duration. It helps assess video quality, audience engagement, and ad relevance.
nodes.questions: What is the correlation between video length and the percentage of viewers who watch for at least 15 seconds? How do 15-second views vary across different ad placements (e.g., in-feed, pre-roll)? What is the impact of ad creative (e.g., design or message) on achieving focused views? Are there time-of-day patterns where 15-second views are consistently higher? How does audience segmentation (e.g., age, interest groups) affect the likelihood of achieving focused views?
nodes.slices: - Device type - Time of day - Ad placement - Audience demographics - Content category

nodes.description: The number of times your video received at least one positive interaction (likes, shares, follows, or clicks) without being viewed for 15 seconds. This metric is only available for advertisers using focused view products. For each video impression, paid interactions are counted separately and replays are excluded.
nodes.formula: 15-second focused views (paid interactions) = Sum of positive interactions without 15-second views
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking this metric helps understand how your audience interacts with your content even if they don’t watch the full video. It highlights the potential for early-stage engagement.
nodes.questions: Which type of positive interaction (e.g., likes, shares) is most strongly associated with videos viewed for less than 15 seconds? How does interaction volume change depending on the placement of the video ad? Is there a trend between interaction rate and video completion rate? How does the interaction-to-view ratio compare across different audience demographics? Are interactions higher for specific video lengths under 15 seconds?
nodes.slices: - Interaction type - Video format - Audience behavior - Campaign goal - Platform language

nodes.description: Number of times your video was played for at least 2 seconds. For each video impression, plays are counted separately and replays are excluded.
nodes.formula: 2-second video views = Sum of views ≥ 2 seconds
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric measures initial audience interest in your video. It provides insights into the effectiveness of the video thumbnail and the first few seconds of your content.
nodes.questions: Which ad placement generates the highest number of 2-second video views consistently? How does video length affect the number of 2-second views? Are viewers who watch for 2 seconds likely to interact with the ad in other ways? How do 2-second view rates vary by campaign type (e.g., brand awareness vs. lead generation)? What percentage of 2-second viewers progress to longer viewing durations?
nodes.slices: - Content length - Scroll speed - Video preview - Internet speed - Frequency of exposure

nodes.description: The number of times your video was viewed for at least 6 seconds, or viewed in full if it is less than 6 seconds long. For each video impression, plays are counted separately and replays are excluded. This metric is only available for advertisers using focused view products.
nodes.formula: 6-second focused views = Sum of views ≥ 6 seconds or full video if shorter
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric gauges mid-level engagement and helps determine if your video is holding viewers' attention long enough to convey a partial or complete message.
nodes.questions: How does the inclusion of an early call-to-action (CTA) impact the likelihood of achieving 6-second focused views? What is the relationship between ad frequency and focused views? Are 6-second focused views higher for specific video genres or themes? How does video resolution (e.g., HD vs. SD) impact engagement at the 6-second mark? How does engagement at 6 seconds differ between returning and new viewers?
nodes.slices: - Region - Ad copy - Targeting criteria - Viewing environment - Video quality

nodes.description: The number of times your video received at least one positive interaction (likes, shares, follows, or clicks) without being viewed for 6 seconds. This metric is only available for advertisers using focused view products. For each video impression, paid interactions are counted separately and replays are excluded.
nodes.formula: 6-second focused views (paid interactions) = Sum of positive interactions without 6-second views
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Monitoring this metric highlights the ability of your video to drive engagement (likes, shares, clicks) even with short viewing durations, indicating high content relevance.
nodes.questions: Which demographic groups are more likely to interact positively within the first 6 seconds of viewing? How does the interaction rate within 6 seconds compare for different ad objectives (e.g., conversions vs. awareness)? Are videos with an interactive element (e.g., polls) more effective at generating interactions within 6 seconds? What is the interaction-to-cost ratio for 6-second focused views? How does ad placement influence early interaction rates?
nodes.slices: - Interaction timing - Overlay elements - Frequency capping - User interests - Engagement-to-click ratio

nodes.description: Number of times your video was played for at least 6 seconds. For each video impression, plays are counted separately and replays are excluded.
nodes.formula: 6-second video views = Sum of views ≥ 6 seconds
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric helps evaluate how many viewers stay engaged beyond the first few seconds, offering insights into the quality of your video content and targeting strategy.
nodes.questions: Are 6-second views more likely for videos with a dynamic opening compared to a static one? How does device type impact the percentage of users watching at least 6 seconds? Which traffic source (e.g., social, referral) contributes most to 6-second views? How does ad targeting precision affect 6-second video view rates? Are 6-second views higher for specific campaign timeframes, such as weekends?
nodes.slices: - Video pacing - Previous interactions - Ad bidding strategy - Seasonality - Competitor presence

nodes.description: The total value of onsite add to cart actions attributed to your ads.
nodes.formula: Add to cart value (onsite) = Sum of product values added to cart

nodes.questions: Which product categories contribute the most to the total add-to-cart value onsite? How does cart value vary by user segmentation (e.g., first-time visitors vs. returning customers)? What is the correlation between average session duration and total cart value? How does the availability of discounts impact cart value? Is there a significant difference in cart value by traffic source (e.g., organic, paid)?

nodes.description: The total value of onsite add to wishlist actions attributed to your ads.

How does wishlist value correlate with user engagement metrics like time on site? Is wishlist value higher for users who interact with reviews or ratings before adding an item? How does the total wishlist value differ between mobile and desktop users?

nodes.description: The number of onsite add to cart actions attributed to your ads.
nodes.formula: Adds to cart (onsite) = Count of add-to-cart actions onsite

nodes.why: This metric provides insights into how well your ads drive purchase intent. It helps measure the success of targeting strategies and product appeal.

Are users more likely to add items to the cart when product recommendations are displayed? How does the number of add-to-cart actions vary by device type? What is the relationship between product availability rate and add-to-cart actions?

nodes.description: The number of page add to cart actions attributed to your ads.

nodes.description: The number of onsite add to wishlist actions attributed to your ads.

What is the relationship between wishlist additions and subsequent purchases onsite? How do seasonal trends (e.g., holiday periods) affect wishlist activity onsite? Does the visibility of a "wishlist" button on product pages impact onsite wishlist additions?

nodes.description: The number of page add to wishlist actions attributed to your ads.
nodes.formula: Adds to wishlist (page) = Count of add-to-wishlist actions on page
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking wishlist additions on specific pages helps evaluate the appeal of individual products. It highlights which items are resonating with your audience for targeted promotions.

How do wishlist addition rates vary across different ad campaigns driving traffic to product pages?

nodes.description: Average time your video was played per user, including any time spent replaying the video.
nodes.formula: Average play time per user = Total play time / Unique users
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric helps gauge user engagement by showing how long, on average, users are spending on your video content. It reflects the quality and relevance of your videos.
nodes.questions: Which user segments (e.g., age, location) have the longest average play time per session? How does the inclusion of interactive elements (e.g., clickable CTAs) impact average play time? What is the difference in play time between users accessing content via mobile versus desktop? How does average play time vary by traffic source (e.g., organic, paid)? Are users more likely to replay videos with a high average play time?
nodes.slices: - Content type - Playback platform - Viewer engagement - Frequency of exposure - Audience demographics

nodes.id: Average play time per video view
nodes.description: Average time your video was played per single video view, including any time spent replaying the video.
nodes.formula: Average play time per video view = Total play time / Total video views
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Monitoring this metric shows how effectively your videos hold attention for each play. It provides insights into optimizing video length and structure for better engagement.
nodes.questions: How does video length affect the average play time per view? Which ad placements (e.g., in-feed, pre-roll) result in the longest average play time per view? How does average play time differ across platforms (e.g., YouTube, Instagram)? Does including subtitles in videos impact average play time per view? How does audience segmentation (e.g., interest groups) influence the average play time per video view?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Poor video quality. Low-resolution or laggy videos may drive users to stop watching early. 🔹 Misaligned audience targeting. The content may not align with viewer interests, leading to early drop-off. 🔹 Lack of engaging opening content. Weak openings fail to capture user interest, reducing overall play time. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Overly long videos. Viewers may lose interest if the video is unnecessarily long. 🔹 Viewing issues on mobile devices. Poor mobile compatibility can shorten play times.
nodes.slices: - Video duration - Video placement - Ad creative - Internet speed - Interaction types

nodes.description: Average time that users spent on the Instant Form.
nodes.formula: Average time on Instant Form = Total time on form / Total form views
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric measures how long users spend on your forms, reflecting their ease of use and the level of user interest. High time spent may indicate complexity or strong engagement.
nodes.questions: How does the number of form fields impact the average time spent on the form? Which ad creatives are associated with the longest time spent on the form? How does average time vary by traffic source leading to the Instant Form? Are users more likely to complete forms that they spend longer average time on? How does the inclusion of auto-fill options impact time spent on the form?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Overly complex forms. Forms with too many fields discourage users from spending time on them. 🔹 Slow load times. If forms load too slowly, users may exit before interacting. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Lack of engaging content on the form. Forms without descriptions or visual aids fail to capture attention. 🔹 Unclear or misleading CTAs. If users are confused about the form's purpose, they may abandon it quickly. 🔹 Targeting low-intent audiences. Users without genuine interest are less likely to spend time filling out forms.
nodes.slices: - Form length - Completion rate - Entry device - Form design - Audience source

nodes.description: The number of page initiate checkout actions attributed to your ads.
nodes.formula: Checkouts initiated (page) = Count of checkout initiation actions
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric tracks how effectively your ads drive purchase intent. It helps identify conversion potential and the impact of specific product page layouts or offers.
nodes.questions: Which product categories see the highest number of checkout initiations? How does page load time correlate with checkout initiation rates? Does the presence of multiple payment options influence the number of checkouts initiated? What is the impact of product recommendations on the checkout initiation rate? How do checkout initiation rates vary by user demographics (e.g., age, location)?

nodes.description: Number of clicks from your ads. This includes clicks that lead to destination as well as those for social and interaction purposes.
nodes.formula: Clicks (all) = Count of all ad clicks
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: This metric provides an overall measure of how well your ads are driving user interaction. It includes destination and social clicks, offering a broad view of ad performance.
nodes.questions: How does click behavior vary across different ad placements (e.g., stories, search results)? Which ad creative types drive the highest click engagement (e.g., static image, carousel)? What is the relationship between ad frequency and click engagement? How do click rates differ between broad and highly targeted audiences? Are clicks higher during specific campaign timeframes, such as sales events?
nodes.slices: - Click type - Ad creative - Campaign type - User intent - Device type

nodes.description: Number of clicks from your ads to a specified destination.
nodes.formula: Clicks (destination) = Count of clicks to specified destination
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: This metric measures how effectively your ads drive traffic to specific landing pages. It is crucial for evaluating click-through success and audience targeting accuracy.
nodes.questions: Which destination pages (e.g., product, category) receive the highest number of clicks? How does the quality of ad CTAs impact destination clicks? What is the relationship between ad copy relevance and destination click rates? How do destination click rates vary by device type (e.g., mobile, desktop)? Which audience segments are most likely to click on destination links?
