nodes.description: Percentage of results you received out of all destination clicks on your ads.
nodes.formula: CVR (clicks) = Conversions / Clicks × 100%
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric evaluates the effectiveness of your ads in converting destination clicks into results. It highlights the quality of your landing page and overall ad relevance.
nodes.questions: How does conversion rate vary across different campaign objectives (e.g., awareness, sales)? What is the impact of page speed on conversion rates from clicks? How do conversion rates differ between new users and returning users? Which ad placements (e.g., feed, search) yield the highest click-to-conversion rates? What is the relationship between product pricing and click-to-conversion rates?
nodes.slices: - Ad spend - User journey - Campaign timing - Seasonality - Creative testing

nodes.description: Percentage of results you received out of all impressions on your ads.
nodes.formula: CVR = Conversions / Impressions × 100%
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking this metric shows the overall efficiency of your ad impressions in generating desired results. It provides a holistic view of campaign performance.
nodes.questions: How does conversion rate vary by geographic region? What is the impact of ad frequency on conversion rates from impressions? Are conversion rates higher for ads featuring user-generated content versus professional creatives? How do seasonal trends affect overall conversion rates? What is the correlation between campaign budget and conversion rate?
nodes.slices: - Industry benchmarks - Ad placement - Product lifecycle - Geographic regions - User retention

nodes.description: Number of times your ad resulted in the optimization event you selected.
nodes.formula: Conversions = Count of selected optimization events
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: This metric directly measures the success of your ads in achieving their intended goal (e.g., purchases, sign-ups). It is critical for assessing ROI and optimization opportunities.
nodes.questions: Which conversion events (e.g., purchases, sign-ups) are the most common across campaigns? How do conversions vary by traffic source (e.g., organic, paid)? What is the relationship between ad spend and total conversions? How do user demographics influence conversion rates for specific campaigns? Are conversions higher for campaigns optimized for specific actions, such as add-to-cart or wishlist?
nodes.slices: - Conversion type - Audience stage - Cross-device activity - Ad channels - Loyalty programs

nodes.description: Sum of your total ad spend.
nodes.formula: Cost = Total ad spend
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.
nodes.why: Tracking total ad spend is essential for monitoring budget usage and managing overall campaign costs. It provides a foundation for evaluating the return on investment (ROI).

How does ad spend allocation differ between brand awareness and direct response campaigns? Are campaigns with higher total spend achieving better engagement metrics (e.g., CTR)? How does total spend differ by audience segment (e.g., age group, location)?

nodes.description: Average cost to reach 1,000 unique users.

What is the correlation between CPM and campaign objectives (e.g., brand awareness vs. lead generation)? How does the platform (e.g., Instagram, YouTube) impact the cost to reach 1,000 users? Are CPMs higher for campaigns with more narrowly defined audiences?
nodes.slices: - Audience density - Time of day - Ad format - Campaign objective - Seasonal trends

Are add-to-cart costs lower during promotional periods or flash sales?

nodes.description: The average cost per page add to cart action attributed to your ads.

Are campaigns with product recommendations achieving lower add-to-cart costs? How does page load speed impact the cost efficiency of add-to-cart actions? What is the effect of ad creative types (e.g., video, static) on reducing add-to-cart costs?

Are wishlist additions onsite more cost-effective for returning customers than for new ones? What is the impact of personalized product recommendations on reducing wishlist addition costs? nodes.description: The average cost per page add to wishlist action attributed to your ads.
nodes.formula: Cost per add to wishlist (page) = Cost / Adds to wishlist (page)
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: This metric highlights the cost effectiveness of driving wishlist actions for specific products. It aids in refining ad strategies for high-value or popular items.
nodes.questions: How does cost efficiency for wishlist additions differ between high-demand and niche products? What is the effect of seasonal campaigns (e.g., holidays) on wishlist addition costs? How does user intent (e.g., browsing vs. buying) impact cost per wishlist addition?

nodes.description: The average cost per page initiate checkout action attributed to your ads.

nodes.description: The average cost per page complete payment action attributed to your ads.

nodes.description: Average amount you spent per 1,000 impressions.
nodes.formula: CPM = Cost / (Impressions / 1,000)
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Cost per 1,000 impressions is key for understanding the cost efficiency of your campaign in reaching a broad audience. It helps optimize budget allocation for brand awareness.
nodes.questions: How does CPM vary across campaigns with different objectives (e.g., awareness, conversions)? Are CPMs consistently higher during specific times of the day or week? What is the impact of audience size on CPM for highly targeted campaigns? How does ad creative quality (e.g., resolution, engagement) influence CPM? Which platforms yield the most cost-effective CPMs for specific audience segments?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 High competition during ad placement auctions. Increased demand for ad slots can raise costs. 🔹 Broad targeting strategies. Targeting larger, less refined audiences can lead to higher CPMs. 🔹 Seasonal competition. During peak times (e.g., holidays), ad costs typically increase. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Inefficient ad creatives. Poorly optimized creatives may result in less effective ad performance and higher costs. 🔹 Poor ad placement. Ads placed in less engaging spots may drive higher costs per 1,000 impressions.
nodes.slices: - Platform - Audience density - Campaign objective - Ad creative type - Seasonal trends

nodes.description: Percentage of impressions that resulted in a destination click out of all impressions.
nodes.formula: CTR (destination) = Clicks (destination) / Impressions × 100%
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric evaluates the percentage of impressions that lead to clicks on your ad’s destination. It is crucial for understanding ad relevance and targeting effectiveness.
nodes.questions: Which ad placements achieve the highest destination click-through rates? How does the relevance of ad copy impact destination CTR across campaigns? What is the relationship between audience segmentation (e.g., broad vs. narrow) and destination CTR? Are CTRs higher during specific seasons or promotional periods? How does the design of destination landing pages influence the CTR of linked ads?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Irrelevant ad copy. Ad content not matching user intent results in fewer clicks. 🔹 Poor creative design. Unappealing visuals fail to capture user attention and clicks. 🔹 Mismatched audience targeting. Ads shown to disinterested audiences lead to lower click rates. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Slow landing page load time. Users may not click on links if they expect delays after clicking. 🔹 Excessive ad frequency. Ad fatigue reduces user interest in clicking on ads.
nodes.slices: - Ad placement - Target audience - Creative type - Campaign timing - Page relevance

nodes.description: The percentage of times your video was viewed for 15 seconds, or in full from paid ad impressions received through focused view ads. This metric is only available for advertisers using focused view products.
nodes.formula: Focused view 15-second view rate = 15-second focused views (paid views) / Impressions × 100%
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking this metric helps assess how well your video captures and retains audience attention for a meaningful duration. It indicates video relevance and quality.
nodes.questions: How does video content type (e.g., tutorial, product demo) influence the rate of 15-second views? Are view rates higher for videos with dynamic intros compared to static openings? What is the effect of demographic targeting on 15-second view rates? How does ad placement impact the percentage of viewers watching for 15 seconds or more? Are view rates higher for shorter videos that can be fully viewed within 15 seconds?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Poor initial content. Viewers disengage quickly if the first few seconds are unengaging. 🔹 Misaligned targeting. Content not relevant to the audience reduces engagement. 🔹 Low-quality video resolution. Poor visuals discourage viewers from continuing. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Long loading times. Viewers may abandon videos that take too long to load. 🔹 Overly aggressive CTAs. Viewers may feel pressured and disengage quickly.
nodes.slices: - Video category - Viewer intent - Playback platform - Video length - Audience segmentation

nodes.description: The percentage of times your focused view paid ad was viewed for 6 seconds, or in full from paid ad impressions received through focused view ads. This metric is only available for advertisers using focused view products.
nodes.formula: Focused view 6-second view rate = 6-second focused views / Impressions × 100%
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric shows the percentage of viewers who engage with your video for at least 6 seconds. It helps determine mid-level engagement and ad effectiveness.
nodes.questions: Which types of video creatives drive the highest percentage of 6-second focused views? How does audience segmentation impact 6-second view rates? Are view rates higher for campaigns targeting mobile users versus desktop users? How do video resolutions (e.g., HD, SD) affect 6-second focused view rates? What is the relationship between ad frequency and 6-second view rates?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Irrelevant targeting. Ads shown to the wrong audience result in low engagement. 🔹 Unengaging openings. The first 6 seconds are crucial; if they fail to grab attention, viewers drop off. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Distracting ad elements. Overcrowded or irrelevant visuals reduce engagement. 🔹 Poor mobile experience. Videos not optimized for mobile discourage viewers. 🔹 Lengthy buffering times. Viewers may abandon videos due to delays.
nodes.slices: - Ad placement - Engagement type - Device type - Region - Video thumbnails

tactics.tactic: Hook audience in the first 3 seconds

nodes.description: Percentage of unique users who exited the form without effective engagement, such as scrolling or editing.
nodes.formula: Instant form bounce rate = Exited forms / Instant form unique views × 100%
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric evaluates how effectively your Instant Form engages users. A high bounce rate suggests usability issues or a mismatch between the ad and audience expectations.
nodes.questions: How does the bounce rate vary for forms with different numbers of fields? Are bounce rates higher for mobile users compared to desktop users? What is the relationship between page load time and the bounce rate of Instant Forms? Does the inclusion of auto-fill options reduce the bounce rate of forms? How do bounce rates differ across different traffic sources (e.g., organic, referral)?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Long load times. Slow forms frustrate users, leading to higher bounce rates. 🔹 Overly complex forms. Complicated forms with too many fields deter users from engaging. 🔹 Misleading ad copy. Users leave when forms don’t match ad expectations. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Poor mobile compatibility. Users on mobile may find forms difficult to interact with, leading to abandonment. 🔹 Low user intent. Users not ready to engage are more likely to bounce.
nodes.slices: - Form length - Entry source - Device segmentation - Form design - Ad targeting

nodes.description: Average loading time of the Instant Form.
nodes.formula: Instant form load time = Total load time / Count of loads
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Monitoring this metric helps ensure a smooth user experience. Slow load times can lead to user drop-off, negatively impacting campaign performance.
nodes.questions: How does the number of form fields affect the average load time? Are load times slower for mobile users compared to desktop users? How does geographic location influence the average load time of Instant Forms? What is the relationship between server response time and Instant Form load times? Are faster-loading forms associated with higher submission rates?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Large media files. Forms with heavy images or videos take longer to load. 🔹 Poor server performance. Slow backend infrastructure increases load times. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Excessive scripts. Forms with unnecessary third-party scripts load slower. 🔹 Geographic server issues. Users accessing forms from regions far from the server may experience delays. 🔹 Browser compatibility issues. Older browsers may struggle to load modern form designs.
nodes.slices: - Device type - Traffic source - Page complexity - Geographic region - Ad format

tactics.tactic: Enable lazy loading for non-critical elements

nodes.formula: Registrations completed (page) = Count of completed registrations
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: This metric measures how many users completed a registration process, providing insights into campaign performance and lead-generation efficiency.
nodes.questions: Which registration incentives (e.g., discounts, exclusive content) lead to the highest number of completed registrations? How do registration completions differ across different traffic sources, such as social media versus organic search? Are campaigns targeting younger audiences achieving higher registration completion rates? What is the relationship between registration form complexity and completion rates? How does campaign timing (e.g., weekdays vs. weekends) influence completed registrations?
nodes.slices: - Registration type - Campaign objectives - User demographics - Traffic source - Time of day

nodes.description: The percentage of onsite add to cart actions from all clicks attributed to your ads.
nodes.formula: Unique add to cart rate (onsite) = Unique adds to cart (onsite) / Clicks (all) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric shows the percentage of ad-driven clicks that resulted in add-to-cart actions onsite. It helps assess how effectively your campaign generates purchase intent.
nodes.questions: Which product categories have the highest unique add-to-cart rates? How does user behavior, such as average session time, impact the unique add-to-cart rate? Are unique add-to-cart rates higher for campaigns offering personalized recommendations? What is the relationship between product availability (e.g., in-stock vs. out-of-stock) and unique add-to-cart rates? How do unique add-to-cart rates differ by traffic source, such as organic, paid, or referral?
nodes.slices: - Product categories - Price range - Campaign type - Traffic source - Audience intent

nodes.description: The percentage of page add to cart actions from all clicks attributed to your ads.
nodes.formula: Unique add to cart rate (page) = Unique adds to cart (page) / Clicks (all) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric reflects how effectively clicks to a specific product page lead to add-to-cart actions. It is useful for identifying the performance of targeted product campaigns.
nodes.questions: How does the unique add-to-cart rate vary by page type (e.g., product detail, category)? Are unique add-to-cart rates higher for products with detailed descriptions compared to minimal descriptions? What is the relationship between page load time and unique add-to-cart rates? Do users from specific traffic sources (e.g., social media, paid search) have higher unique add-to-cart rates? How does user engagement (e.g., time spent on page) influence the unique add-to-cart rate?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Unclear CTAs. Poorly designed "Add to Cart" buttons reduce user engagement. 🔹 Lack of product descriptions. Users may hesitate to add products to their cart without sufficient information. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 High shipping costs. Unexpected costs discourage cart additions. 🔹 Slow page load speeds. Users abandon the process if pages take too long to load. 🔹 Poor mobile optimization. Users on mobile may face difficulties adding items to their cart.
nodes.slices: - Landing page design - Device segmentation - Seasonal trends - Ad creative - Demographics

nodes.description: The percentage of onsite add to wishlist actions from all clicks attributed to your ads.
nodes.formula: Unique add to wishlist rate (onsite) = Unique adds to wishlist (onsite) / Clicks (all) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: This metric measures how effectively your campaign drives users to add products to their wishlist from the website. It indicates product interest and future purchase potential.
nodes.questions: Which product categories have the highest onsite unique add-to-wishlist rates? How do unique add-to-wishlist rates vary by device type (e.g., mobile, desktop)? Are wishlist rates higher for users exposed to ads with social proof (e.g., reviews, ratings)? What is the correlation between wishlist additions and discounts offered onsite? How does user intent (e.g., browsing vs. purchase-focused) impact unique add-to-wishlist rates?
nodes.slices: - Product demand - User segments - Geographic segmentation - Traffic type - Seasonality

nodes.description: The percentage of page add to wishlist actions from all clicks attributed to your ads.
nodes.formula: Unique add to wishlist rate (page) = Unique adds to wishlist (page) / Clicks (all) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking this metric helps evaluate the performance of individual product pages in converting clicks into wishlist actions, highlighting popular items or campaigns.
nodes.questions: Which page elements (e.g., product images, reviews) influence unique add-to-wishlist rates the most? How do wishlist rates differ across various traffic sources driving users to product pages? What is the effect of seasonal promotions on unique add-to-wishlist rates? Are wishlist rates higher for product categories with limited availability or exclusivity? How does page speed optimization influence unique add-to-wishlist rates?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Weak product imagery. Poor visuals discourage users from adding items to their wishlist. 🔹 Lack of wishlist visibility. A hidden or hard-to-find wishlist button reduces usage. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Limited product availability. Products marked as out-of-stock may reduce wishlist activity. 🔹 Inadequate personalization. Generic suggestions fail to inspire users to save products for later. 🔹 Poor mobile experience. Users on mobile may face usability issues with wishlists.
nodes.slices: '- Page speed - Call-to-action (CTA) - Product availability - Referral sources - User device

tactics.tactic: Showcase wishlists prominently on the site

nodes.description: The percentage of page initiate checkout actions from all clicks attributed to your ads.
nodes.formula: Unique checkout initiation rate (page) = Unique checkouts initiated (page) / Clicks (all) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric reflects how efficiently your campaign converts clicks into checkout initiations. It identifies the effectiveness of your call-to-action and page design in driving sales.
nodes.questions: Which checkout page designs (e.g., single-step vs. multi-step) yield higher unique checkout initiation rates? How do unique checkout initiation rates vary by product price tier? Are checkout rates higher for campaigns targeting returning customers compared to new users? What is the relationship between time spent on the page and checkout initiation rates? How does the presence of express checkout options affect unique checkout initiation rates?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Unclear payment options. If payment methods are not clearly displayed, users may hesitate to initiate checkout. 🔹 High unexpected costs. Additional fees or taxes revealed at checkout can discourage users from proceeding. 🔹 Lack of trust indicators. Missing trust badges or reviews may deter users from initiating checkout. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Poor page navigation. Users may struggle to navigate to the checkout page due to unclear CTAs or page design. 🔹 Mobile-unfriendly design. Users on mobile devices may find it challenging to initiate checkout if the page isn’t optimized for smaller screens.
nodes.slices: - Checkout design - Product categories - Payment options - User demographics - Ad timing

tactics.tactic: Show a summary of charges upfront

nodes.description: The percentage of onsite submit form actions from all clicks attributed to your ads.
nodes.formula: Unique form submission rate (onsite) = Unique form submissions (onsite) / Clicks (all) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric shows the percentage of clicks that lead to form submissions onsite. It provides insights into user interest, the relevance of your offer, and the usability of your form.
nodes.questions: How does the complexity of form fields affect unique onsite form submission rates? Are submission rates higher for forms embedded on specific pages (e.g., landing, product)? What is the relationship between time spent on forms and unique submission rates? How do submission rates differ for users from different geographic regions? Are submission rates higher for campaigns offering incentives, such as discounts or freebies?
nodes.slices: - Form type - Audience segment - Device type - Traffic source - Campaign objective

nodes.description: The percentage of page place order actions from all clicks attributed to your ads.
nodes.formula: Unique orders placed rate (page) = Unique orders placed (page) / Clicks (all) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking this metric helps assess how well your campaign converts clicks into orders on specific product pages. It provides insights into the effectiveness of product-focused campaigns.
nodes.questions: Which product categories have the highest unique order placement rates? How does traffic source (e.g., referral, paid) influence the rate of orders placed? Are order placement rates higher for campaigns optimized for high-intent users? What is the relationship between discounts offered and unique order placement rates? How does device type impact the unique order placement rate on pages?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Unoptimized checkout process. Complicated or lengthy checkout forms can lead to cart abandonment, reducing order placements. 🔹 Out-of-stock products. Users may be unable to complete their purchases if desired products are unavailable. 🔹 High cart abandonment rate. Lack of incentives, unclear return policies, or unexpected costs may cause users to abandon their carts. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Limited payment options. Users may not place orders if preferred payment methods are unavailable. 🔹 Poor promotional visibility. Users may not be aware of discounts or offers, leading to fewer placed orders.
nodes.slices: - Product category - Pricing strategy - Landing page optimization - Traffic source - Audience intent

nodes.description: The percentage of page complete payment actions from all clicks attributed to your ads.
nodes.formula: Unique payment completion rate (page) = Unique payments completed (page) / Clicks (all) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric indicates how effectively your ads drive completed payments relative to clicks. It is critical for evaluating the overall ROI of your campaigns.
nodes.questions: Which payment methods (e.g., credit card, PayPal) contribute to the highest unique payment completion rates? How does geographic segmentation impact unique payment completion rates? Are campaigns with limited-time offers achieving higher payment completion rates? What is the relationship between page load time and unique payment completion rates? How do payment completion rates differ across different audience demographics (e.g., age, gender)?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Security concerns. Users may abandon payment if they feel the site lacks adequate security measures. 🔹 Slow payment processing. Delays in processing payments may lead users to abandon the transaction. 🔹 Technical issues with payment gateways. Payment gateway failures or errors can hinder transaction completions. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Poor customer support availability. Users may abandon the process if they encounter issues and cannot get immediate assistance. 🔹 Lack of payment confirmation feedback. Users may hesitate to complete payment if they are unsure whether the transaction is successful.
nodes.slices: - Payment methods - Checkout design - Audience segmentation - Campaign timing - Geographic region

tactics.tactic: Optimize load times for payment pages

nodes.description: The percentage of page add payment info actions from all clicks attributed to your ads.
nodes.formula: Unique payment info add rate (page) = Unique payment info adds (page) / Clicks (all) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: This metric measures the percentage of clicks that result in users entering their payment details. It highlights the strength of your checkout process and audience intent.
nodes.questions: How does the presence of multiple payment options affect unique payment info addition rates? What is the impact of discounts or promotional codes on payment info addition rates? Are unique payment info add rates higher for campaigns targeting loyal customers versus new customers? How does the complexity of the checkout process influence payment info addition rates? Are payment info addition rates higher for ads that feature product urgency or exclusivity?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Distrust of platform. Users may hesitate to provide payment info if the site doesn’t appear credible or secure. 🔹 Poor form usability. Difficult or unclear payment info forms can deter users from proceeding. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Lack of pre-fill options. Users may be less likely to add payment info if they have to input all details manually. 🔹 Lack of incentives. Without rewards or discounts, users may not feel motivated to proceed to payment. 🔹 Mobile device compatibility issues. Payment forms not optimized for mobile devices may discourage users.
nodes.slices: - Product price - Device type - Traffic segmentation - Ad creative - User loyalty

tactics.tactic: Pre-fill payment information for logged-in users
Allow secure saving of payment details
Add trust indicators near payment fields
Provide contextual help for payment fields

nodes.description: The percentage of page complete registration actions from all clicks attributed to your ads.
nodes.formula: Unique registration completion rate (page) = Unique registrations completed (page) / Clicks (all) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking this metric shows how well your campaign drives users to complete registration forms. It is essential for assessing lead generation effectiveness.
nodes.questions: What is the relationship between form simplicity and unique registration completion rates? How do registration completions differ for campaigns with incentives (e.g., free trials, discounts)? Are registration rates higher for mobile users compared to desktop users? How does time spent on the registration page impact completion rates? What is the effect of ad frequency on unique registration completion rates?
nodes.causes: 🔴 High impact 🔹 Privacy concerns. Users may hesitate to complete registration if data usage policies are unclear or untrustworthy. 🔹 Overly lengthy forms. Long or detailed registration forms may cause users to abandon the process. 🔹 Unclear benefits of registration. If users don’t see the value in registering, they may not complete the form. 🟠 Medium impact 🔹 Missing autofill options. Lack of autofill for personal information increases friction in the registration process. 🔹 Unintuitive registration design. Poorly designed forms that lack clear instructions or flow can confuse users.
nodes.slices: - Registration type - Audience targeting - Form complexity - Referral sources - Campaign goal

nodes.description: The average value per onsite add to cart action attributed to your ads.
nodes.formula: Value per add to cart (onsite) = Add to cart value (onsite) / Adds to cart (onsite)
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric provides the average monetary value of add-to-cart actions onsite. It helps identify high-value product interest and refine promotional strategies.
nodes.questions: Which product categories generate the highest average value per add to cart onsite? How does value per add to cart vary by customer segment (e.g., high spenders vs. average spenders)? What is the relationship between discounts offered and value per add to cart onsite? Are higher-value products more likely to be added to the cart by mobile users or desktop users? How does the time of day affect the value of items added to carts onsite?
nodes.slices: - Product category - Traffic source - Discounts - Customer segments - Page design

nodes.description: The average value per onsite add to wishlist action attributed to your ads.
nodes.formula: Value per add to wishlist (onsite) = Add to wishlist value (onsite) / Adds to wishlist (onsite)
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking this metric helps measure the average value of wishlist actions, providing insights into user preferences and potential future revenue opportunities.
nodes.questions: Which product types (e.g., premium, everyday essentials) contribute to the highest value per wishlist addition? Are wishlist addition values higher for users exposed to remarketing campaigns? What is the impact of offering discounts on the value per wishlist addition? How does wishlist addition value differ between first-time and returning visitors? Are users more likely to add high-value items to their wishlist during promotional events or standard periods?
nodes.slices: - Product demand - User demographics - Seasonal trends - User engagement - Traffic segmentation

nodes.description: The average value per page complete payment action attributed to your ads.
nodes.formula: Value per complete payment (page) = Payment completion value (page) / Payments completed (page)
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: This metric shows the average value of completed payments attributed to your ads. It is a key indicator of the profitability of your campaigns.
nodes.questions: Which product categories generate the highest average value per completed payment? How does value per completed payment vary by traffic source (e.g., organic, paid)? What is the relationship between the availability of discounts and the value per completed payment? Are higher payment values more likely for desktop users compared to mobile users? How does customer segmentation (e.g., new vs. returning) impact the value per completed payment?
nodes.slices: - Pricing tiers - Payment methods - Geographic segmentation - Seasonal patterns - Campaign objectives

nodes.description: The average value per onsite submit form attributed to your ads.
nodes.formula: Value per form submitted (onsite) = Form submission value (onsite) / Form submissions (onsite)
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: This metric reflects the average monetary value of form submissions. It helps evaluate the quality of leads generated through your ads and informs decisions on campaign effectiveness.
nodes.questions: Which types of forms (e.g., lead generation, surveys) generate the highest average submission value? How does the use of incentives (e.g., discounts, free trials) affect the value per form submission? Are submission values higher for campaigns targeting high-intent users compared to broad audiences? What is the impact of form field complexity on the average submission value? How does geographic segmentation influence the value per form submission?
nodes.slices: - Form type - Audience segmentation - Traffic source - Time on page - Region

nodes.description: Number of times your video started to play. For each video impression, plays are counted separately and replays are excluded.
nodes.formula: Video views = Count of video plays
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking video views provides insights into how many users engaged with your video content. It indicates the overall reach and initial interest in your message.
nodes.questions: Which ad placements (e.g., feed, stories) generate the highest number of video views? How does video content type (e.g., tutorial, testimonial) impact the total number of views? Are video views more likely to occur during specific times of the day or week? What is the relationship between video length and total views? How do video views vary across different audience demographics (e.g., age, interests)?
nodes.slices: - Platform - Audience segmentation - Ad placement - Time of day - Video length

nodes.description: Number of times your video was played 100% of its length. For each impression, views are counted separately and replays are excluded.
nodes.formula: Video views at 100% = Count of views to 100% of video length
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric measures how many viewers watched your video to completion. It reflects the effectiveness of your content in holding attention and delivering the full message.
nodes.questions: How does the inclusion of a strong CTA impact the percentage of viewers who watch the video to completion? Are videos with shorter lengths more likely to achieve 100% views compared to longer videos? How do different platforms (e.g., YouTube, Facebook) influence the rate of 100% video views? What is the relationship between ad frequency and the percentage of 100% video views? Are 100% video view rates higher for specific video genres (e.g., storytelling, product demos)?
nodes.slices: - Audience retention - Video category - Video resolution - Playback devices - Time of day

nodes.description: Number of times your video was played at least 25% of its length. For each impression, views are counted separately and replays are excluded.
nodes.formula: Video views at 25% = Count of views to at least 25% of video length
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric shows how many viewers engaged with at least the first quarter of your video. It helps assess if your content captures initial attention effectively.
nodes.questions: What is the correlation between video length and the percentage of viewers who watch at least 25% of the video? Are 25% view rates higher for videos with dynamic intros compared to static openings? How do different ad placements (e.g., feed, stories) influence the percentage of viewers reaching 25% of the video? What is the effect of audience segmentation (e.g., age, location) on 25% video view rates? How do engagement metrics (e.g., likes, comments) correlate with 25% video views?
nodes.slices: - Video pacing - Ad creative - Viewer demographics - Internet speed - Seasonality

nodes.description: Number of times your video was played at least 50% of its length. For each impression, views are counted separately and replays are excluded.
nodes.formula: Video views at 50% = Count of views to at least 50% of video length
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: This metric indicates mid-level engagement by showing how many viewers watched at least half of your video. It helps optimize content for retaining viewer interest.
nodes.questions: Which ad creative types (e.g., animation, testimonials) result in the highest 50% view rates? How does video resolution (e.g., HD vs. SD) impact the likelihood of reaching 50% views? Are 50% video view rates higher for campaigns targeting specific time slots (e.g., evenings)? What is the relationship between the use of captions and the percentage of viewers watching 50% of the video? How do traffic sources (e.g., organic, paid) influence the percentage of viewers reaching 50% of the video?
nodes.slices: - Audience interest - Video duration - Playback platform - Ad placement - Traffic segmentation

nodes.description: Number of times your video was played at least 75% of its length. For each impression, views are counted separately and replays are excluded.
nodes.formula: Video views at 75% = Count of views to at least 75% of video length
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.
nodes.why: Tracking this metric reveals how many viewers watched most of your video. It provides insights into the effectiveness of your video in sustaining engagement.
nodes.questions: How does the inclusion of engaging mid-video content affect the likelihood of reaching 75% video views? Are viewers more likely to reach 75% of the video on shorter videos compared to longer ones? What is the impact of ad placement (e.g., pre-roll, mid-roll) on 75% video views? How do engagement elements (e.g., polls, clickable overlays) influence 75% video view rates? Are 75% view rates higher for videos with recurring branding elements throughout the video?
nodes.slices: - Viewer intent - Campaign type - Region - Ad copy - Video type

nodes.description: The number of conversions which are attributed to an ad impression. (The total count is based on impression time)
nodes.formula: VTA Conversions = Count of conversions attributed to impressions
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: This metric measures the number of conversions attributed to ad impressions. It is important for evaluating the impact of passive ad exposure on audience actions.
nodes.questions: Which campaigns optimized for impressions generate the highest number of VTA conversions? How does the type of ad creative (e.g., video, static image) impact VTA conversions? Are VTA conversions higher for ads with longer impression durations? What is the relationship between audience size and the number of VTA conversions? How do VTA conversion rates vary across different regions or geographic locations?
nodes.slices: - Ad placement - Impressions frequency - Campaign duration - Audience segmentation - Ad creative

nodes.description: The number payment events that are attributed to an ad impression. (The total count is based on impression time)
nodes.formula: VTA Purchase = Count of purchase events attributed to impressions
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking VTA purchases highlights how effectively your ads influence buying decisions without direct clicks. It helps measure the ROI of impression-based campaigns.
nodes.questions: Which product categories yield the highest number of purchases attributed to impressions? Are VTA purchases higher for campaigns targeting returning customers versus new customers? How does the timing of campaigns (e.g., holidays, sales events) impact the number of VTA purchases? What is the relationship between ad spend and the number of purchases attributed to impressions? Are VTA purchases more likely for ads with interactive features (e.g., swipe-ups, polls)?
nodes.slices: - Product type - Geographic segmentation - Ad spend - Shopping season - Campaign goals

nodes.description: The number of registration events that are attributed to an ad impression. (The total count is based on impression time)
nodes.formula: VTA Registration = Count of registration events attributed to impressions
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: This metric measures how many registrations are attributed to impressions. It provides insights into the effectiveness of ad placements in generating interest and capturing leads.
nodes.questions: Which campaigns designed for lead generation achieve the highest number of VTA registrations? How does ad creative design (e.g., color scheme, CTA placement) affect VTA registrations? Are VTA registrations higher for audiences with broader targeting criteria compared to narrower criteria? How do VTA registration rates vary across different platforms (e.g., Instagram, LinkedIn)? What is the effect of campaign timing (e.g., weekday vs. weekend) on VTA registrations?
nodes.slices: - Registration incentives - Traffic source - Platform segmentation - Campaign timing - Audience behavior

nodes.description: The total fees charged by the marketplace platform for selling on their platform, including referral fees, closing fees, and any other platform-specific charges. These fees are deducted from gross revenue.
nodes.formula: Marketplace Fees = Referral Fee + Closing Fee + Other Platform Fees
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.

nodes.description: The percentage of gross revenue that becomes net profit after all costs are deducted. It shows how efficiently the business converts sales into actual profit.
nodes.formula: Profit Margin = (Net Profit ÷ Gross Revenue) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.

nodes.description: The total cost of picking, packing, and shipping products to customers, including fees paid to the marketplace fulfillment network (e.g., FBA) or third-party logistics providers. Does not include storage.
nodes.formula: Fulfillment Costs = Per-Unit Fulfillment Fee × Units Shipped + Any Fixed Fulfillment Overheads
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.

nodes.description: The fees charged by the marketplace (e.g., Amazon FBA) for storing your products in their warehouses. These fees typically increase significantly during peak periods and for long-term stored inventory.
nodes.formula: Storage Costs = Monthly Storage Rate (per cubic foot) × Cubic Feet Occupied
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.

nodes.description: Advertising Cost of Sales — the ratio of ad spend to the revenue directly attributed to those ads. Shows how efficiently advertising dollars generate sales revenue.
nodes.formula: ACoS = (Ad Spend ÷ Ad Revenue) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

nodes.description: The total amount of money spent on marketplace advertising across all campaign types during a given period. This is the direct out-of-pocket advertising expenditure.
nodes.formula: Ad Spend = Sum of All Ad Campaign Costs in the Period
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

nodes.description: Total Advertising Cost of Sales — the ratio of ad spend to total gross revenue (not just ad-attributed revenue). Shows the true advertising burden on all business revenue, including organic sales.
nodes.formula: TACoS = (Ad Spend ÷ Total Gross Revenue) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

nodes.description: The direct costs associated with producing or purchasing the goods that were sold during a period. Includes product cost, manufacturing, and inbound shipping to the fulfillment center.
nodes.formula: COGS = (Unit Cost + Inbound Shipping per Unit) × Units Sold
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.

nodes.description: The average price at which a product is actually sold to customers, accounting for discounts, promotions, coupons, and price variations across the catalog. Reflects real realized revenue per unit.
nodes.formula: ASP = Total Gross Revenue ÷ Total Units Sold
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

tactics.tactic: A/B test price increases on top SKUs
Improve listing quality to justify premium pricing
Shift ad spend toward higher-ASP SKUs
Reduce marketplace participation on low-ASP channels
Train staff to lead with premium recommendations
Monitor and control staff discount authority
Introduce value-add bundles instead of price reductions
Shift promotional cadence toward fewer, deeper events
Analyze customer segments by ASP and target higher-ASP profiles

nodes.description: The total revenue generated from all product sales before any costs, fees, returns, or deductions. This is the top-line revenue figure representing all money collected from customers.
nodes.formula: Gross Revenue = Average Selling Price × Units Sold
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

nodes.description: The percentage of total gross revenue that was generated through paid advertising campaigns, as opposed to organic (non-ad-attributed) sales. Shows how dependent the business is on advertising for revenue.
nodes.formula: % Ad Sales = (Ad Revenue ÷ Total Gross Revenue) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.

nodes.description: The absolute monetary amount refunded to customers due to product returns during a given period. This is the total sum of money paid back to buyers — not a percentage, but a raw dollar figure.
nodes.formula: Return Cost = Sum of all individual refund amounts paid to customers for returned products in the period
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.

nodes.description: The number of times the total average inventory is sold and replaced over a given period. Measures how efficiently inventory is being converted into sales.
nodes.formula: Inventory Turnover = Units Sold ÷ Average Inventory Level
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.

nodes.description: The average star rating of a product based on customer reviews on the marketplace, typically on a scale of 1 to 5. Reflects overall customer satisfaction and perceived product quality.
nodes.formula: Product Rating = Sum of All Star Ratings ÷ Total Number of Reviews
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

tactics.tactic: Resolve top complaint issues in the product
Use Amazon Vine for new listings
Update listing to set accurate expectations
Use product inserts to encourage positive feedback
Report fake negative reviews for removal

nodes.description: The total count of customer reviews a product has accumulated on the marketplace. Higher review counts increase social proof, buyer confidence, and algorithmic ranking strength.
nodes.formula: Number of Reviews = Total Count of Customer Reviews on the Listing
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

tactics.tactic: Time review requests for peak satisfaction moments
Consolidate variants under one parent listing
Run targeted promotions to boost review-to-sales ratio
Monitor and appeal wrongly removed reviews

nodes.description: The total number of customer orders placed during a period. Each order may contain one or multiple units, making it the primary count of customer purchase transactions.
nodes.formula: Orders = Total Number of Customer Purchase Transactions in the Period
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.

nodes.description: The percentage of time a product is available for purchase on the marketplace — i.e., not out of stock, suppressed, or unavailable. A measure of inventory and listing reliability.
nodes.formula: In-Stock Rate = (Days In Stock ÷ Total Days in Period) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

nodes.description: The percentage of customers who purchase a product and subsequently leave a review. Measures how effectively post-purchase engagement converts buyers into reviewers.
nodes.formula: Review Rate = (Number of Reviews Received ÷ Units Sold) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.

tactics.tactic: Enroll qualifying products in Amazon Vine
Improve product quality to create advocacy
Use packaging inserts with compliant review nudge
Segment review requests by order value
Test different review request subject lines

nodes.description: The current number of units physically held in warehouse or fulfillment center inventory available for sale. This is a snapshot metric representing available sellable stock.
nodes.formula: Inventory Level = Units Received − Units Sold − Units Returned as Unsellable
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

nodes.description: The percentage of time a seller's offer appears in the featured offer (Buy Box) position on a product listing. Winning the Buy Box is critical because the vast majority of marketplace sales go through this position.
nodes.formula: Buy Box % = (Hours in Buy Box ÷ Total Hours) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.

tactics.tactic: Maintain FBA fulfillment on key ASINs
Monitor and enforce against unauthorized sellers
Set competitive price floors with repricing rules
Enroll in brand registry to protect listings

nodes.description: The total number of units physically received at the fulfillment center or warehouse during a given period. Represents inbound inventory flow and is compared to units ordered from suppliers.
nodes.formula: Units Received = Sum of All Units Confirmed as Received at Fulfillment Center in the Period
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

nodes.description: The average time from when an order is placed by a customer to when it is delivered to their door. A key driver of customer satisfaction and marketplace ranking for FBM sellers.
nodes.formula: Delivery Time = Average (Delivery Date − Order Placement Date) across all orders
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

tactics.tactic: Switch high-volume FBM SKUs to FBA
Use multiple carriers with route optimization
Improve packaging efficiency for faster processing

nodes.description: The percentage of ad impressions (times the ad was shown) that resulted in a click. Measures how compelling and relevant an ad creative is to the target audience.
nodes.formula: CTR = (Clicks ÷ Impressions) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

tactics.tactic: Upgrade main product image for ad thumbnail
Add negative keywords for irrelevant terms
Test multiple product images via A/B testing
Optimize product title with high-intent keywords
Test price competitiveness on high-impression keywords
Use Sponsored Brands with lifestyle imagery

nodes.description: The total number of times customers viewed a product listing page during a given period. Includes both organic and paid traffic sources.
nodes.formula: Product Page Views = Sum of All Sessions on the Product Detail Page
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

nodes.description: The position at which a product appears in search results for a specific keyword on the marketplace. Rank 1 means first result shown; higher numbers indicate lower visibility.
nodes.formula: Keyword Ranking = Position Number in Search Results for a Specific Keyword
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

tactics.tactic: Run targeted Sponsored Products on ranking keywords
Ensure keyword appears in title and bullets
Maintain inventory to protect earned rankings
Use Search Query Performance data to prioritize
Drive external traffic with UTM tracking to target keywords
Build ranking on long-tail variants first
Monitor competitor ranking changes and react quickly

nodes.description: The number of times a product's paid advertisements were shown to shoppers on the marketplace. Reflects the reach and scale of the advertising campaigns.
nodes.formula: Paid Impressions = Sum of All Ad Impressions Across All Paid Campaigns
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

nodes.description: The amount of revenue generated per square meter of retail floor space. Measures how productively the physical store space is being used to generate sales.
nodes.formula: Revenue per Square Meter = Total Revenue ÷ Total Sales Floor Area (in square meters)
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.

nodes.description: The percentage of sales transactions where a staff member actively helped the customer during the purchase process. Measures staff engagement and consultative selling effectiveness.
nodes.formula: Assisted Sales Rate = (Transactions with Staff Assistance ÷ Total Transactions) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

nodes.description: The percentage of total revenue that comes from products sold at a promotional price (discount, markdown, or special offer) rather than full list price.
nodes.formula: % Promotional Sales = (Revenue from Promotional Transactions ÷ Total Revenue) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.

nodes.description: The estimated revenue lost due to products being out of stock when customer demand existed. Represents the opportunity cost of inventory gaps and stockouts in the retail environment.
nodes.formula: Lost Sales = Expected Daily Sales Rate × Number of Out-of-Stock Days × Average Selling Price
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

nodes.description: The percentage of people who enter the store and make at least one purchase. The fundamental measure of how effectively the store converts visitor traffic into paying customers.
nodes.formula: Store Conversion Rate = (Transactions ÷ Store Visitors) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.

tactics.tactic: Improve on-shelf availability with better replenishment
Train staff on proactive customer engagement
Optimize store layout for product discovery
Redesign product displays for impulse purchase triggers
Reduce checkout wait time to prevent abandonment
Implement loyalty program to reward return visits

nodes.description: The total number of completed purchase transactions in the retail store during a given period. Each transaction represents one customer checkout event, regardless of how many items were purchased.
nodes.formula: Transactions = Total Number of Completed Checkout Events in the Period
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.

nodes.description: The average monetary value of each customer transaction at the retail store. A key measure of basket size and the effectiveness of upselling and cross-selling strategies.
nodes.formula: ATV = Total Revenue ÷ Number of Transactions
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.

tactics.tactic: Train staff on upselling and cross-selling scripts
Introduce product bundles with bundle pricing
Add impulse purchase zone at checkout

nodes.description: The average number of different product SKUs or line items included in each customer transaction. Measures how many distinct products customers buy per visit.
nodes.formula: Items per Transaction = Total Line Items Sold ÷ Total Transactions
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.

tactics.tactic: Train staff on product add-on recommendations
Use POS receipt coupons for cross-category purchases
Offer a discount on the second item during quiet hours

nodes.description: The average amount of time a customer spends inside the retail store from entry to exit. A proxy for engagement, browsing depth, and likelihood of purchase.
nodes.formula: Dwell Time = Average (Store Exit Time − Store Entry Time) across all visitors
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

tactics.tactic: Redesign layout to create natural browsing paths
Position seating areas in high-browsing zones
Train staff to extend customer interactions
Use in-store digital content to create discovery moments

nodes.description: The share of store visitors who move beyond the entrance zone into the main sales floor. Reflects how effectively the entrance converts incoming foot traffic into engaged shoppers.
nodes.formula: Store Penetration Rate = (Visitors Who Passed the Entrance Zone ÷ Total Store Visitors) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.

tactics.tactic: Place high-demand or high-interest products just beyond the entrance zone
Improve sightlines and visual anchors deeper in the store
Add clear wayfinding from the entrance
Redesign entrance zone layout to remove movement barriers
Introduce a greeter or staff presence at the entrance
Use lighting and sensory design to create depth

nodes.description: The average number of individual units (items) purchased per transaction in the retail store. Similar to items per transaction but counts total unit quantity rather than distinct SKU count.
nodes.formula: Units per Transaction = Total Units Sold ÷ Total Transactions
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.

tactics.tactic: Ensure baskets are easily accessible throughout the store
Train staff to suggest quantity upgrades in consumable categories
Add shelf signage promoting multi-unit value
Use POS prompts for quantity add-ons

nodes.description: The average number of store zones or departments a customer visits during a single store visit. Measures how deeply customers navigate through the store and explore the full assortment.
nodes.formula: Path Depth = Average Number of Distinct Store Zones Visited per Customer
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

tactics.tactic: Move destination categories to the back of the store
Add discovery zones and cross-category displays
Create visual interest and discovery moments throughout the store
Redesign traffic flow to create a natural loop
Add loyalty program rewards for zone exploration
Run zone-specific promotions to pull traffic

nodes.description: The percentage of store locations or shelf bays that correctly display products according to the agreed planogram, merchandising guidelines, or brand standards. Measures execution quality in-store.
nodes.formula: Merchandising Compliance = (Correctly Executed Display Points ÷ Total Audited Display Points) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

tactics.tactic: Make planograms accessible digitally on the shop floor
Create accountability through regular compliance audits
Simplify planograms to make compliance more achievable
Add visual reference labels to shelves
Tie store manager performance review to compliance scores
Use periodic supplier merchandising support visits

nodes.description: The percentage of time that products are physically available and correctly positioned on retail shelves during store trading hours. Combines in-stock status with correct shelf placement.
nodes.formula: On-shelf Availability = (Shelf-Present SKU-Hours ÷ Total Possible SKU-Hours) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.

nodes.description: The percentage of SKUs in the planned assortment that are completely out of stock (no units available to sell) at a given point in time. A direct measure of inventory gap severity.
nodes.formula: Out-of-Stock Rate = (Number of SKUs with Zero Stock ÷ Total Number of SKUs in Assortment) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.

tactics.tactic: Review and raise reorder points for top-velocity SKUs
Build safety stock buffers for top 20% of SKUs
Use sales data for automated replenishment suggestions
Conduct weekly full inventory cycle counts
Set up low-stock alerts in the POS/inventory system

nodes.description: The average time it takes for a staff member to serve a single customer, from the start of the interaction to transaction completion. Measures service efficiency and throughput capacity.
nodes.formula: Service Time = Total Customer Service Minutes ÷ Number of Customers Served
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

tactics.tactic: Streamline POS workflows to reduce transaction steps
Separate returns and complex queries from main checkout
Invest in staff training and checkout practice
Introduce contactless payment and mobile payment options
Move loyalty enrollment and lookup to pre-checkout
Use self-checkout for simple single-item purchases
Pre-scan high-volume items at busy periods

nodes.description: The average time a customer spends waiting before being served or receiving service at the checkout or service counter. Measures customer-facing queue efficiency.
nodes.formula: Wait Time = Average (Service Start Time − Queue Join Time) across all customers
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.

nodes.description: The average number of customers waiting in the checkout or service queue at a given point in time. A real-time operational metric indicating whether staffing and throughput are keeping up with demand.
nodes.formula: Queue Length = Average Number of Customers Waiting in Queue During the Measurement Period
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.

tactics.tactic: Open additional registers proactively during peak periods
Optimize shift scheduling to match traffic patterns
Reduce service time per transaction through process improvement
Implement a queue management alert system
Use flexible staff from other departments during peaks
Separate high-complexity transactions to a dedicated service desk
Introduce express lane for small basket sizes
Use mobile POS to check out customers on the floor

nodes.description: The total revenue generated per sales floor employee, typically calculated on a per-day, per-week, or per-shift basis. Measures labor productivity and commercial output per person.
nodes.formula: Sales per Employee = Total Revenue ÷ Number of Active Sales Floor Employees
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

nodes.description: The total number of employees on the staffing list who are available for work — the overall headcount that can be scheduled for shifts on the sales floor.
nodes.formula: Sales Floor Staff = Total number of employees available for scheduling in the period
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.

tactics.tactic: Build a rolling hiring pipeline ahead of demand
Optimise the part-time / full-time mix for scheduling flexibility
Improve onboarding speed to reduce the time-to-floor gap
Track and address turnover by root cause

nodes.description: The average number of employees working simultaneously during a shift. Shows typical staffing density on the floor at any given point in time.
nodes.formula: Active Staff per Shift = Average number of employees scheduled to work simultaneously per shift
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.

tactics.tactic: Align shift scheduling with historical traffic and transaction data
Increase Sales Floor Staff headcount if simultaneous coverage is structurally insufficient
Stagger shift start and end times to maintain coverage across the day
Reserve non-floor tasks for low-traffic periods

nodes.description: The percentage of people passing by or near the store entrance who actually enter it. Measures how effectively the store exterior, window displays, and signage attract passersby into the store.
nodes.formula: Capture Rate = (Store Visitors ÷ Passing Foot Traffic) × 100%
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

tactics.tactic: Invest in high-quality, regularly refreshed window displays
Use A-frame sidewalk signs with rotating offers
Train a greeter or position staff near entrance
Use sampling or demonstration activities outside the store
Partner with neighboring stores for cross-promotional traffic

nodes.description: The total number of individuals who enter the retail store during a given period, as measured by door counters or footfall tracking technology. The raw inbound traffic metric.
nodes.formula: Store Visitors = Total Door Counter Entries in the Period
nodes.frequency: Daily. Check this metric every day so you don't miss out on any outlier.

nodes.description: The average daily number of visitors across a defined period (week, month, or season). Smooths out day-to-day variation to reveal the underlying traffic trend.
nodes.formula: Avg Store Visitors = Total Store Visitors in Period ÷ Number of Trading Days in Period
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.

nodes.description: The number of completed customer transactions processed per employee during a given period. Measures checkout and service staff productivity in terms of customer throughput.
nodes.formula: Transactions per Employee = Total Transactions ÷ Number of Active Staff in the Period
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

nodes.description: The total number of people who pass through or near the store's location, whether or not they enter. Represents the external traffic opportunity available to be captured by the store.
nodes.formula: Foot Traffic = Total People Count Measured by External Pedestrian Counter or Area Traffic Data
nodes.frequency: Weekly. Check this metric just once a week.

nodes.description: Total number of orders that include the redemption of loyalty points.
nodes.formula: Count of Orders with Redemption
nodes.frequency: Monthly. Try to check this metric each month.
nodes.why: Tracking the number of orders with redemption helps understand how frequently members use their loyalty points. This metric provides insights into the program's effectiveness in encouraging point usage and driving sales through redemptions.
nodes.questions: 1. How has the number of orders with redemption changed over time? 2. What factors influence the likelihood of orders including redemptions? 3. How does the number of orders with redemption vary across different product categories? 4. What is the correlation between orders with redemption and overall sales? 5. How do promotional campaigns impact the number of orders with redemption? 6. What is the relationship between orders with redemption and member satisfaction? 7. How does the number of orders with redemption affect inventory management?
nodes.slices: - Time of Year - Product Category - Member Tier - Geographical Region - Order Value
